topics.title: Classical Optimization Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classical Optimization Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — CLASSICAL OPTIMIZATION MODELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classical Optimization Models** are mathematically rigorous formulations that seek the **best (optimum) solution** from a set of available alternatives by systematically traversing a continuous, differentiable search space using **deterministic analytical techniques** rooted in differential calculus, convex analysis, and matrix algebra.

A general classical optimization problem is stated canonically as:

$$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} \; f(x)$$

subject to the constraints:

$$g_j(x) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

$$h_k(x) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

where:
- $f(x)$ is the **objective function** (also called cost or merit function).
- $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ is the **decision variable vector** in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- $g_j(x)$ are **inequality constraints**.
- $h_k(x)$ are **equality constraints**.
- The set $S = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(x) \leq 0, \; h_k(x) = 0\}$ is called the **feasible region**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Vocabulary Note:** The triplet $(\text{Objective}, \text{Inequality Constraints}, \text{Equality Constraints})$ is the *standard form* referenced in every KTU board paper for optimization courses.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hiking in the Kerala Western Ghats near Munnar. You want to reach the **lowest valley** (the optimum) using only a **map and a compass** (calculus tools). The terrain is smooth and well-charted (the function is differentiable). The map tells you the gradient (slope) at every point.

- A **classical optimization method** is like walking strictly downhill by reading the gradient at each step.
- You can only succeed if the terrain is **smooth, continuous, and known in advance**.
- If the terrain is rugged, with multiple valleys (multi-modal), the compass-walker may get stuck in a **local valley** instead of the deepest one (**global optimum**).

This is precisely the limitation of classical models: they are powerful, but they demand a *well-behaved mathematical landscape*. When the landscape is jagged, discontinuous, or non-differentiable, we need **metaheuristics** (covered later in the course).

> [!TIP]
> Think of classical optimization as **GPS navigation on a paved highway**, and metaheuristics as **off-road exploration**. Both reach destinations, but under different terrain conditions.

---

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants / Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Convexity Constant (Hessian Eigenvalues):** $\lambda_{\min}(H) \geq 0$ — confirms convexity.
- **Tolerance ($\epsilon$):** Standard convergence threshold is $10^{-6}$ in KTU numerical answer scripts.
- **Line Search Step Size ($\alpha_k$):** Typically $\alpha_k \in (0, 1)$ for Armijo/Wolfe conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Module 1 explicitly demands:**
> (a) Problem formulation in canonical form.
> (b) Classification of optimization models.
> (c) Classical methods — single & multivariable.
> (d) Constrained optimization — Lagrange & KKT.
> (e) Limitations leading to metaheuristics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Classical Optimization Models

The classification is hierarchical. Every problem must be mapped to a unique class before applying the appropriate solver.

**Step 1 — Nature of the Objective Function:**
- **Linear Programming (LP):** Both $f(x)$ and constraints are linear.
- **Nonlinear Programming (NLP):** $f(x)$ or constraints are nonlinear.
- **Quadratic Programming (QP):** $f(x)$ is quadratic, constraints are linear.

**Step 2 — Nature of Decision Variables:**
- **Continuous:** $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Integer Programming (IP):** $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP):** Some $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$, others $\in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Step 3 — Presence of Constraints:**
- **Unconstrained:** $S = \mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Equality Constrained:** Only $h_k(x) = 0$.
- **Inequality Constrained:** Only $g_j(x) \leq 0$.
- **Mixed Constrained:** Both present.

**Step 4 — Convexity of the Feasible Region:**
- **Convex Optimization:** $f$ is convex and $S$ is a convex set → global optimum is *guaranteed*.
- **Non-convex Optimization:** Multiple local optima possible.

**Step 5 — Dynamic Behavior:**
- **Static (Single-Stage):** One decision snapshot.
- **Dynamic Programming (DP):** Multi-stage decisions linked by Bellman's recurrence.

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Class

- **Why LP first?** Because LP has polynomial-time solvers (Simplex, Interior Point) and the KKU 2024 syllabus uses LP to introduce the concept of *basic feasible solutions* and the *vertex-enumeration principle*.
- **Why distinguish convex from non-convex?** Convexity is the *sine qua non* of guarantees — KKT conditions are both necessary and sufficient only for convex problems.
- **How does DP differ?** DP recasts a multi-stage problem into overlapping sub-problems via the **Principle of Optimality** (Bellman, 1957).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula tested in KTU 2024 ESE papers for Module 1. Memorize these as a unified sheet.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Condition** | **Notation & Remarks** |
|---|---|---|
| Canonical Form | $\min f(x)$ s.t. $g_j(x) \leq 0$, $h_k(x) = 0$ | Standard KTU form |
| Gradient Vector | $\nabla f(x) = \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right)^T$ | First-order necessary condition: $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ |
| Hessian Matrix | $H_{ij} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | Symmetric $n \times n$ matrix |
| First-Order Necessary (Unconstrained) | $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ | Stationary point |
| Second-Order Sufficient (Min) | $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ AND $H(x^*) \succ 0$ | $H$ positive definite |
| Second-Order Sufficient (Max) | $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ AND $H(x^*) \prec 0$ | $H$ negative definite |
| Lagrangian Function | $\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda, \mu) = f(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j g_j(x) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \mu_k h_k(x)$ | $\lambda_j \geq 0$ are Lagrange multipliers |
| KKT Stationarity | $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j(x^*) + \sum \mu_k \nabla h_k(x^*) = 0$ | $\nabla_x \mathcal{L} = 0$ |
| KKT Primal Feasibility | $g_j(x^*) \leq 0$, $h_k(x^*) = 0$ | Must hold at $x^*$ |
| KKT Dual Feasibility | $\lambda_j \geq 0$ for all $j$ | Sign condition |
| KKT Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_j \, g_j(x^*) = 0$ for all $j$ | Either $\lambda_j = 0$ or $g_j(x^*) = 0$ |
| Convexity Test | $f(y) \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y-x)$ | $f$ is convex iff this holds |
| Bellman Recurrence (DP) | $V_n(s) = \max_{a} \left[ r(s,a) + V_{n-1}(T(s,a)) \right]$ | Multi-stage dynamic programming |
| Simplex Ratio Test | $\theta = \min_{i : a_{ik} > 0} \left( \frac{b_i}{a_{ik}} \right)$ | LP pivot rule |
| Newton's Update | $x_{k+1} = x_k - H^{-1}(x_k) \, \nabla f(x_k)$ | Quadratic convergence |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LP in Industry:** Production planning at HMT, FACT Kochi — maximizing output under raw material constraints.
- **NLP in Aerospace:** Trajectory optimization of ISRO launch vehicles using Lagrange multipliers and Pontryagin's Maximum Principle.
- **DP in Control:** Optimal control of robotic arms using Bellman's principle.
- **Convex QP in Finance:** Portfolio optimization (Markowitz model) is a KTU-favorite QP example.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lagrange Multiplier Method (Equality Constraints)

We want to solve:

$$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad h_k(x) = 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, p$$

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**

Introduce one multiplier $\mu_k$ for each equality constraint:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \mu) = f(x) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \mu_k h_k(x)$$

This is a *scalarization* — converting a constrained problem into an unconstrained one in $(n+p)$ variables.

**Step 2 — Impose the first-order necessary condition.**

At a local optimum $x^*$, the gradient of the Lagrangian must vanish:

$$\nabla_x \mathcal{L}(x^*, \mu^*) = 0$$

Expanding component-wise:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \mu_k \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial x_i} = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n$$

In matrix form:

$$\nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \mu_k \, \nabla h_k(x^*) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Impose primal feasibility.**

The original constraints must be satisfied at the optimum:

$$h_k(x^*) = 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, p$$

**Step 4 — Solve the resulting $(n+p)$ system of equations.**

This system yields the candidate stationary point $(x^*, \mu^*)$.

**Step 5 — Apply the second-order sufficient condition.**

The bordered Hessian $B$ is formed by augmenting $H = \nabla^2 \mathcal{L}$ with the constraint gradients. For a *minimum*:

$$B = \begin{pmatrix} \nabla^2 \mathcal{L} & \nabla h \\ (\nabla h)^T & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

The leading principal minors of order $p+1, p+2, \ldots, n$ must alternate in sign (negative for last $n-p$ minors in the minimum case).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the KKT Conditions (Inequality Constraints)

We extend the Lagrangian to handle inequalities:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda, \mu) = f(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j g_j(x) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \mu_k h_k(x)$$

The four KKT conditions are derived as follows:

**Step A — Stationarity.** Differentiate $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $x$ and set to zero:

$$\nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \nabla g_j(x^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \mu_k \nabla h_k(x^*) = 0$$

This must hold at the optimum because any feasible descent direction $d$ must satisfy $d^T \nabla f \geq 0$, and the KKT stationarity ensures this can only be zero.

**Step B — Primal Feasibility.** By definition of the feasible set:

$$g_j(x^*) \leq 0, \quad h_k(x^*) = 0$$

**Step C — Dual Feasibility.** Since the objective is minimization and we are pushing against the boundary $g_j(x) \leq 0$, the multipliers must be non-negative:

$$\lambda_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, \ldots, m$$

If $\lambda_j < 0$, the penalty term $\lambda_j g_j$ would be *positive* when the constraint is violated, defeating the optimization.

**Step D — Complementary Slackness.** At the optimum, either:
- The constraint is **active** ($g_j(x^*) = 0$), in which case $\lambda_j$ may be positive.
- The constraint is **inactive** ($g_j(x^*) < 0$), in which case $\lambda_j = 0$.

Formally:

$$\lambda_j \, g_j(x^*) = 0, \quad \forall j = 1, \ldots, m$$

This is the bridge between active and inactive constraints.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern (Lagrange Multipliers)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ subject to $h(x_1, x_2) = x_1 + x_2 - 2 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L} = x_1^2 + x_2^2 + \mu(x_1 + x_2 - 2)$$

**Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 + \mu = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 + \mu = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mu} = x_1 + x_2 - 2 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system.**

From the first two equations: $2x_1 + \mu = 2x_2 + \mu \Rightarrow x_1 = x_2$.

Substitute into the constraint: $2x_1 = 2 \Rightarrow x_1 = 1, \; x_2 = 1$.

Then $\mu = -2x_1 = -2$.

**Step 4 — Verify the second-order condition.**

The Hessian is:

$$H = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \succ 0$$

So $x^* = (1, 1)$ is a **strict local minimum** (in fact, the global minimum on the constraint).

**Step 5 — State the optimal value.**

$$f(1, 1) = 1 + 1 = 2$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Bordered Hessian Test

For an equality-constrained problem with $p$ constraints, the *bordered Hessian* is:

$$B(x) = \begin{pmatrix} 0_{p \times p} & (\nabla h)^T \\ \nabla h & \nabla^2 \mathcal{L} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Theorem (Second-Order Sufficient Conditions):** Let $x^*$ satisfy the first-order Lagrange conditions. Then $x^*$ is a **local minimum** if the last $n - p$ leading principal minors of $B$ are **negative** and the leading principal minors of $B$ of order $p + 1, p + 2, \ldots, n$ have the sign pattern $(-1)^{p+1}, (-1)^{p+2}, \ldots, (-1)^n$.

This pattern ensures that the second variation $d^T \nabla^2 \mathcal{L} \, d > 0$ for all feasible tangent directions $d$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Frequently Tested Theorems

| **Theorem** | **Statement** | **Used For** |
|---|---|---|
| Weierstrass Theorem | A continuous function on a compact set attains its min and max. | Existence guarantee |
| Fermat's Theorem (Unconstrained) | If $x^*$ is local min of differentiable $f$, then $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$. | Unconstrained necessary condition |
| Lagrange Theorem (Equality) | At a regular point of constraint, $\nabla f = -\mu \nabla h$. | Equality constrained problems |
| Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) | Stationarity + Feasibility + Dual Feas. + Slackness. | General NLP problems |
| Bellman's Principle of Optimality | An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision. | Dynamic programming |
| Duality Theorem (LP) | If a primal LP has an optimal solution, so does its dual, and the optimal values are equal. | LP sensitivity & bounds |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Classical Optimization Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Classical Optimization Models] --> B[By Objective Function]
    A --> C[By Variable Type]
    A --> D[By Constraints]
    A --> E[By Convexity]
    A --> F[By Time Structure]

    B --> B1[Linear Programming LP]
    B --> B2[Nonlinear Programming NLP]
    B --> B3[Quadratic Programming QP]
    B --> B4[Geometric Programming GP]

    C --> C1[Continuous Variables]
    C --> C2[Integer Programming IP]
    C --> C3[Mixed Integer MIP]
    C --> C4[Binary Programming]

    D --> D1[Unconstrained]
    D --> D2[Equality Constrained]
    D --> D3[Inequality Constrained]
    D --> D4[Mixed Constrained]

    E --> E1[Convex Optimization]
    E --> E2[Nonconvex Optimization]
    E --> E3[Multimodal Problems]

    F --> F1[Static Single Stage]
    F --> F2[Dynamic Multi Stage DP]
    F --> F3[Stochastic Programming]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Start at node A and traverse a unique path along one branch from each of B, C, D, E, F to fully classify any classical optimization model. For example, *Quadratic, Continuous, Inequality-Constrained, Convex, Static* → a QP solved by Interior Point methods.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Solution Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Real World Problem] --> B[Mathematical Formulation]
    B --> C{Problem Classification}
    C -->|Linear| D[Simplex or Interior Point]
    C -->|Nonlinear Unconstrained| E[Gradient or Newton Method]
    C -->|Nonlinear Constrained| F[Lagrange or KKT Solver]
    C -->|Dynamic| G[Bellman Recursion DP]
    D --> H[Verify KKT and Feasibility]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Converged}
    I -->|Yes| J[Optimal Solution x star]
    I -->|No| K[Adjust Step or Penalty]
    K --> C
```

**Reading the flow:** Every classical optimization algorithm must pass through stages B → C → (Solver) → H → I. The feedback loop K → C is the iterative core of gradient-based methods.

---

### 4.3 Relationship Between Classical Methods and Metaheuristics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Optimization Landscape] --> B{Well Behaved Continuous Differentiable}
    B -->|Yes| C[Classical Methods]
    B -->|No| D[Metaheuristic Methods]
    C --> C1[Gradient Descent]
    C --> C2[Newton Method]
    C --> C3[Lagrange KKT]
    D --> D1[Genetic Algorithm]
    D --> D2[Particle Swarm]
    D --> D3[Simulated Annealing]
    D --> D4[Ant Colony]
    C1 --> E[Guaranteed Local Optimum]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> F[Approximate Global Optimum]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 closes with this exact bridge:** classical methods fail on non-differentiable, multi-modal, black-box problems — *this is the motivation* for the metaheuristic techniques covered in Modules 2–5 of PEITT632.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the canonical form of a classical optimization problem. Mention the roles of $f(x)$, $g_j(x)$, and $h_k(x)$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A classical optimization problem in canonical (standard) form is stated as:

$$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x)$$

subject to:

$$g_j(x) \leq 0, \; j = 1, \ldots, m \quad \text{and} \quad h_k(x) = 0, \; k = 1, \ldots, p$$

**Roles:**
- $f(x)$: the **objective (or cost) function** to be minimized; it quantifies the "goodness" of a candidate solution.
- $g_j(x) \leq 0$: the **inequality constraints** defining upper bounds or restrictions.
- $h_k(x) = 0$: the **equality constraints** defining exact balance conditions (e.g., resource conservation).

A point $x$ satisfying all constraints is called a **feasible point**; the set of all such points is the **feasible region** $S$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the four Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions. Why are they important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For the problem $\min f(x)$ subject to $g_j(x) \leq 0$ and $h_k(x) = 0$:

1. **Stationarity:** $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j(x^*) + \sum \mu_k \nabla h_k(x^*) = 0$
2. **Primal Feasibility:** $g_j(x^*) \leq 0$ and $h_k(x^*) = 0$
3. **Dual Feasibility:** $\lambda_j \geq 0$ for all $j$
4. **Complementary Slackness:** $\lambda_j \, g_j(x^*) = 0$ for all $j$

**Importance (1 mark):** KKT conditions generalize the Lagrange multiplier method to inequality constraints and are the *first-order necessary conditions* for optimality in smooth constrained NLPs. For convex problems, they are also **sufficient**, guaranteeing global optimality. They form the foundation of modern NLP solvers like SQP and Interior Point methods.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Full-Length with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Formulate the following engineering problem in standard optimization form: *"A manufacturing firm produces two products P1 and P2. Each unit of P1 yields ₹30 profit and requires 2 hours of machine time and 1 unit of raw material. Each unit of P2 yields ₹20 profit and requires 1 hour of machine time and 3 units of raw material. The firm has 100 hours of machine time and 60 units of raw material available. Determine the production mix to maximize profit."* Solve the problem using the graphical method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the KKT conditions for the following constrained problem:
$$\min \; (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad x_1^2 + x_2^2 \leq 5, \; x_1 + 2x_2 = 3$$
Identify the optimal solution and verify the conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Define variables.** Let $x_1$ = units of P1, $x_2$ = units of P2.

**Step 2 — Objective function (maximization).** Convert to minimization by negating:

$$\min \; -30 x_1 - 20 x_2$$

**Step 3 — Constraints.**
- Machine time: $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 100$
- Raw material: $x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 60$
- Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Step 4 — Find the corner points of the feasible region.**

- Intersection of $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ and $x_1 + 3x_2 = 60$:
Multiply the second equation by 2: $2x_1 + 6x_2 = 120$.
Subtract the first: $5x_2 = 20 \Rightarrow x_2 = 4, \; x_1 = 24$.
- Other vertices: $(0, 0)$, $(30, 0)$, $(0, 20)$.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the objective at each corner point.**

| Corner Point $(x_1, x_2)$ | Profit $30x_1 + 20x_2$ (₹) |
|---|---|
| $(0, 0)$ | 0 |
| $(30, 0)$ | 900 |
| $(0, 20)$ | 400 |
| $(24, 4)$ | $720 + 80 = 800$ |

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** Maximum profit is **₹900** at $(x_1, x_2) = (30, 0)$. The firm should produce 30 units of P1 and 0 units of P2.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L} = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2 + \lambda(x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 5) + \mu(x_1 + 2x_2 - 3)$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 2) + 2\lambda x_1 + \mu = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 1) + 2\lambda x_2 + 2\mu = 0$$

**Step 3 — Primal feasibility.**

$$x_1^2 + x_2^2 \leq 5, \quad x_1 + 2x_2 = 3$$

**Step 4 — Complementary slackness.**

$$\lambda (x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 5) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Test active and inactive cases.**

- **Case I (inactive):** $\lambda = 0$. Then $2(x_1-2) + \mu = 0$ and $2(x_2-1) + 2\mu = 0$. From the second, $\mu = 1 - x_2$. Substituting: $2x_1 - 4 + 1 - x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow 2x_1 - x_2 = 3$. Combined with $x_1 + 2x_2 = 3$, solving gives $x_1 = 9/5, x_2 = 3/5$. Then $x_1^2 + x_2^2 = 81/25 + 9/25 = 90/25 = 3.6 < 5$. ✓ Inactive constraint consistent.

- **Case II (active):** $\lambda \geq 0$ and $x_1^2 + x_2^2 = 5$. With $x_1 + 2x_2 = 3 \Rightarrow x_1 = 3 - 2x_2$. Substitute: $(3-2x_2)^2 + x_2^2 = 5 \Rightarrow 9 - 12x_2 + 4x_2^2 + x_2^2 = 5 \Rightarrow 5x_2^2 - 12x_2 + 4 = 0$. Discriminant: $144 - 80 = 64$, so $x_2 = (12 \pm 8)/10 = 2$ or $0.4$.

For $x_2 = 2$, $x_1 = -1$: objective $f = 9 + 1 = 10$.
For $x_2 = 0.4$, $x_1 = 2.2$: objective $f = 0.04 + 0.36 = 0.4$.

**Step 6 — Identify optimum.** The Case I solution $(9/5, 3/5)$ gives $f = (1/5)^2 + (2/5)^2 = 1/25 + 4/25 = 5/25 = 0.2$. The active case with $x_2 = 0.4$ gives $f = 0.4$. So the **optimum is $x^* = (9/5, 3/5)$** with $f^* = 0.2$ and $\lambda^* = 0$ (inactive), $\mu^* = 1 - 3/5 = 2/5$.

**Verification:** $\nabla f = (2(9/5-2), 2(3/5-1)) = (-2/5, -4/5)$. The KKT stationarity $-2/5 + (2/5)(9/5) + 0 = -2/5 + 18/25 = -10/25 + 18/25 = 8/25 \neq 0$ — wait, recompute:

$2(9/5 - 2) = 2(-1/5) = -2/5$. Adding $\mu = 2/5$: $-2/5 + 2/5 = 0$. ✓
$2(3/5 - 1) = 2(-2/5) = -4/5$. Adding $2\mu = 4/5$: $-4/5 + 4/5 = 0$. ✓

Both stationary conditions satisfied. **Optimum confirmed: $x^* = (9/5, 3/5)$, $f^* = 0.2$, $\lambda^* = 0$, $\mu^* = 2/5$.**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Using the method of Lagrange multipliers, find the maximum and minimum values of $f(x, y, z) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2$ subject to the constraint $x + y + z = 1$. Verify your answer using the bordered Hessian test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of optimality in Dynamic Programming. Solve the shortest path problem: From node S, reach node T using the network below, where arc costs are given by the matrix. *(Assume a 4-stage network with 3 intermediate nodes per stage.)* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L}(x, y, z, \mu) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 + \mu(x + y + z - 1)$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity conditions.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2x + \mu = 0 \Rightarrow x = -\mu/2$$

By symmetry, $y = z = -\mu/2$.

**Step 3 — Feasibility.**

$$x + y + z = 3(-\mu/2) = 1 \Rightarrow \mu = -2/3$$

Thus $x = y = z = 1/3$.

**Step 4 — Optimal value.**

$$f(1/3, 1/3, 1/3) = 3 \cdot (1/9) = 1/3$$

**Step 5 — Bordered Hessian test.**

The Hessian of $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $(x, y, z)$ is:

$$\nabla^2 \mathcal{L} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

The bordered Hessian is:

$$B = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute the leading principal minors:
- $|B_1| = 0$ (degenerate, but acceptable for equality).
- $|B_2| = \det \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = -1 < 0$.
- $|B_3| = \det \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = 0(4 - 0) - 1(2 - 0) + 1(0 - 2) = -2 - 2 = -4 < 0$.
- $|B_4| = \det(B)$. For the unconstrained $f$ on the constraint plane, since $\nabla^2 f = 2I \succ 0$, $x^* = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$ is a **strict local (and global) minimum**.

**Conclusion:** Minimum value is $1/3$ at $(1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$. There is **no maximum** since $f \to \infty$ as $\|x\| \to \infty$ on the constraint plane.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Principle of Optimality (Bellman, 1957):** *An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision.*

**Shortest Path Network (assumed KTU standard):**

Stages 1, 2, 3 connect S → {A, B, C} → {D, E, F} → {G, H, I} → T.

For brevity, assume arc costs:
- Stage 1: $S \to A = 2$, $S \to B = 4$, $S \to C = 1$.
- Stage 2: $A \to D = 3$, $A \to E = 2$, $A \to F = 5$, etc.

**Step 1 — Backward Recursion (Stage 4 → 3):** Compute shortest cost from each node in stage 3 to T.
$V(G) = 1$, $V(H) = 2$, $V(I) = 3$ (assumed last-mile costs).

**Step 2 — Stage 3 → 2:** For each node in stage 2, find the min successor.
$V(D) = \min\{3 + V(G), 4 + V(H), 2 + V(I)\} = \min\{4, 6, 5\} = 4$ (via G).

**Step 3 — Stage 2 → 1:** For each node in stage 1.
$V(A) = \min\{3 + V(D), 2 + V(E), 5 + V(F)\}$. (Continue with appropriate costs.)

**Step 4 — Stage 1 → S:** Final shortest path cost $V(S) = \min\{2 + V(A), 4 + V(B), 1 + V(C)\}$.

**Step 5 — Path Reconstruction:** Trace back the decisions that achieved the minimum at each stage.

> The numerical values depend on the network diagram provided in the actual KTU question paper. The student is expected to set up a **4 × 3 table** of stage-wise optimal values $V_j(s_j)$.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Module 1 Classical Optimization Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the dual feasibility condition ($\lambda_j \geq 0$)** in KKT answers — a full 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing maximization and minimization signs** in LP formulations — always convert to canonical minimization form before applying Simplex.
> 3. **Skipping the complementary slackness check** in KKT derivations — examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Confusing the bordered Hessian test signs** — remember: for a *minimum*, the last $n - p$ leading principal minors must be **negative**.
> 5. **Not verifying second-order conditions** in Lagrange multiplier problems — KTU 2024 marks this as a 2-mark item.
> 6. **Writing DP recursions without stating the principle of optimality** — the principle itself carries 2 marks.
> 7. **Not drawing the feasible region** in graphical LP problems — geometry is mandatory in KTU valuation.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The **canonical form** of classical optimization is $\min f(x)$ s.t. $g_j(x) \leq 0, h_k(x) = 0$.
- [x] Problems are classified along five axes: **objective linearity, variable type, constraint type, convexity, time structure**.
- [x] **Convex problems** enjoy guaranteed global optimality; non-convex problems may have multiple local optima.
- [x] **First-order necessary condition (unconstrained):** $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$.
- [x] **Second-order sufficient condition (min):** $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ AND $H(x^*) \succ 0$ (positive definite).
- [x] **Lagrangian** converts constrained problems to unconstrained ones in augmented space.
- [x] The **four KKT conditions** are: stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness.
- [x] **Complementary slackness** links active ($\lambda_j \geq 0$) and inactive ($\lambda_j = 0$) inequality constraints.
- [x] **Bellman's Principle of Optimality** is the foundation of Dynamic Programming.
- [x] **LP** is solved by Simplex or Interior Point; **NLP** by Lagrange/KKT/SQP; **DP** by backward recursion.
- [x] Classical methods **fail** on non-differentiable, multi-modal, or black-box problems — motivating **metaheuristics** (GA, PSO, SA, ACO).
- [x] Always **verify second-order conditions** before declaring a stationary point as a min or max.
- [x] The **bordered Hessian test** is the KTU-approved second-order test for equality-constrained problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Methods - Exact Methods and Approximate Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Optimization Methods - Exact Methods and Approximate Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Optimization? (The Formal Definition)

In the context of engineering and computer science, **optimization** is the discipline of finding the **best possible solution** (maximum or minimum value of an objective) from a set of available alternatives, subject to a defined set of constraints.

Mathematically, an optimization problem in its canonical form is stated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } \mathbf{x}^* \quad \text{that minimizes (or maximizes)} \quad f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to:} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad h_j(\mathbf{x}) = c_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is the **decision variable vector** in the search space $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$.
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** (also called cost function, fitness function, or energy function).
- $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ are **inequality constraints** and $h_j(\mathbf{x})$ are **equality constraints**.
- $b_i, c_j$ are the constraint bounds.
- $\mathbf{x}^*$ is the **optimal solution** (or near-optimal in the case of approximation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the course PEITT632 (Metaheuristic Optimization), optimization problems are studied primarily through the lens of combinatorial and NP-hard problems, where exhaustive search becomes computationally infeasible. Metaheuristics are designed precisely to handle such intractable problems efficiently.

## 1.2 Classification of Optimization Methods

The vast universe of optimization algorithms is broadly bifurcated into two giant families:

| Category | Description | Guarantee of Optimality | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Exact Methods** | Algorithms that traverse the entire (or systematically pruned) solution space to find the provably optimal solution. | **Guaranteed** to find the true optimum (given enough time). | Often **exponential** in worst case. |
| **Approximate Methods** | Algorithms that sacrifice the guarantee of optimality for tractability, finding "good enough" solutions quickly. | **No guarantee**; only returns a near-optimal solution. | Typically **polynomial** or sub-exponential. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you have lost your car keys somewhere in Kerala (a huge search space!).
> - An **Exact Method** is like a detective who, given infinite time, will search every single square inch of Kerala and **guarantee** finding the keys. Slow, but certain.
> - An **Approximate Method (Metaheuristic)** is like you hiring a smart friend who checks the most "likely" places first — your office, the restaurant you visited yesterday, your jacket pocket. They will likely find the keys **fast**, but there is a small chance they miss the true location.

## 1.3 Exact Methods — Detailed Overview

Exact methods (also called **classical methods** or **complete methods**) are mathematically rigorous techniques that systematically explore the solution space. They include:

1. **Brute Force (Exhaustive Enumeration):** Evaluates $f(\mathbf{x})$ for *every* feasible $\mathbf{x}$. Guaranteed optimum but only feasible for tiny problem sizes ($n \leq 20$ typically).
2. **Dynamic Programming (DP):** Breaks a problem into overlapping sub-problems, solves each once, and stores the result (Bellman's principle of optimality).
3. **Branch and Bound (B&B):** Systematically enumerates candidate solutions by means of state-space search, using bounds on the objective to discard subtrees that cannot contain the optimum.
4. **Integer Linear Programming (ILP) / Mixed Integer Programming (MIP):** Solved using techniques like the Simplex method augmented with branch-and-bound, or the cutting-plane method.
5. **Backtracking:** Incremental construction of candidates, abandoning a candidate ("pruning") as soon as it is determined that it cannot lead to a valid solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need Metaheuristics then?** Because many real-world engineering problems (e.g., Travelling Salesman with 1000 cities, VLSI circuit layout, job-shop scheduling) belong to the class **NP-hard**. For these, the time complexity of exact methods grows **exponentially** ($O(2^n)$ or worse), making them computationally intractable beyond very small instances.

## 1.4 Approximate Methods — Detailed Overview

Approximate methods (also called **heuristic methods** or **incomplete methods**) are algorithms that find "good" solutions in a reasonable amount of time without any guarantee of optimality. They are further sub-divided:

### A. Problem-Specific Heuristics
Constructed for a single problem. They exploit domain knowledge. Example: **Nearest-Neighbour heuristic** for TSP. Fast but quality varies greatly with the problem instance.

### B. Metaheuristics (The Focus of PEITT632)
**Metaheuristics** are higher-level, problem-independent *strategic frameworks* that guide underlying heuristics to search the solution space efficiently. The word "meta" means "beyond" or "higher level." They are designed to **escape local optima** and explore the global search space better than simple heuristics.

Categories of metaheuristics:
- **Single-solution based (Trajectory methods):** Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search, Iterated Local Search.
- **Population-based:** Genetic Algorithms, Differential Evolution, Particle Swarm Optimization, Ant Colony Optimization.
- **Nature-inspired / Bio-inspired:** Genetic Algorithms (GA), Artificial Bee Colony (ABC), Cuckoo Search.
- **Physics-inspired:** Simulated Annealing (SA), Gravitational Search Algorithm.
- **Evolutionary:** Genetic Algorithms, Evolution Strategies, Genetic Programming.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The Hill-Climbing Analogy):** Imagine you are blindfolded on a hilly terrain and must find the highest peak.
> - A **local search heuristic** walks uphill greedily — but if you start in a small valley, you will get stuck on a *local* peak, missing the actual Mount Everest.
> - A **metaheuristic** gives you clever tricks: sometimes walking downhill briefly (SA), remembering forbidden paths (Tabu), or coordinating with a team of searchers sharing information (GA, PSO). These tricks dramatically improve your odds of finding the global peak.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Optimization Pipeline

Regardless of whether a method is exact or approximate, every optimization algorithm follows the same conceptual pipeline:

1. **Problem Encoding:** Translate the real-world problem into a mathematical model (objective + constraints).
2. **Solution Representation:** Decide how a candidate solution $\mathbf{x}$ is encoded (binary string, real-valued vector, permutation, tree, etc.).
3. **Initialization:** Generate one or more starting candidate solutions.
4. **Search / Iteration:** Apply an update rule to improve the candidate(s).
5. **Termination Check:** Stop when a budget is exhausted (iterations, time) or convergence is detected.
6. **Return Best:** Output the best candidate found.

## 2.2 Exact Methods — Operational Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Brute Force
**Operational Logic:**
1. Enumerate every element $\mathbf{x} \in S$ (the feasible space).
2. Evaluate $f(\mathbf{x})$ for each.
3. Track the best-so-far.
4. Return the global optimum.

**Complexity:** $O(\vert S \vert)$ evaluations.

**When feasible:** Only when $\vert S \vert$ is small (e.g., $n \leq 25$).

### 2.2.2 Dynamic Programming (DP)
**Operational Logic:**
- Decompose the problem $P$ into sub-problems $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_k$ that share sub-sub-problems.
- Solve each sub-problem once and store the result in a **memoization table**.
- Combine sub-problem solutions using the **recurrence relation**.

**Bellman's Principle of Optimality:**
> *"An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."*

### 2.2.3 Branch and Bound
**Operational Logic:**
1. **Branching:** Split the feasible set $S$ into two or more non-overlapping subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots$
2. **Bounding:** For each subset $S_i$, compute a bound on the best achievable $f(\mathbf{x})$ within $S_i$ using a *relaxation* (e.g., LP relaxation of an IP).
3. **Pruning:** If the lower bound (for minimization) of a subset exceeds the current best feasible solution, **discard the entire subset** — it cannot contain the optimum.
4. **Fathoming:** A node is fathomed if (a) it is infeasible, (b) its bound is worse than incumbent, or (c) it represents a single solution.

**Advantage over Brute Force:** Pruning eliminates vast portions of the tree — often reducing exponential search to a tractable size for moderate $n$.

### 2.2.4 Backtracking
**Operational Logic:**
- Build the solution incrementally, one component at a time.
- After assigning a partial solution, check if it can possibly lead to a complete valid solution.
- If not, **backtrack** (undo the last assignment) and try the next alternative.

**Distinction from B&B:** Backtracking focuses on feasibility constraints; B&B focuses on optimality bounds.

## 2.3 Approximate Methods — Operational Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Construction Heuristics
Build a single feasible solution from scratch using greedy rules. Example: **Nearest-Neighbour TSP** — at each city, go to the closest unvisited city.

**Quality:** Typically within 10–25% of optimum for TSP. Fast: $O(n^2)$.

### 2.3.2 Improvement Heuristics (Local Search)
**Operational Logic:**
1. Start with an initial feasible solution $\mathbf{x}_0$.
2. Define a **neighbourhood** $N(\mathbf{x})$ — set of solutions reachable by a small perturbation (e.g., 2-opt swap in TSP).
3. Move to the best neighbour $\mathbf{x}^* \in N(\mathbf{x})$ if it improves $f$.
4. Repeat until no neighbour improves $f$ → reached a **local optimum**.

**Problem:** Local optimum ≠ global optimum. This is the **local optima trap**.

### 2.3.3 Metaheuristics — The Escape Strategy
Metaheuristics introduce mechanisms to **escape local optima**:

| Metaheuristic | Escape Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Simulated Annealing (SA)** | Accepts worse solutions with probability $P = e^{-\Delta f / T}$; temperature $T$ decreases over time. |
| **Tabu Search (TS)** | Maintains a short-term memory (tabu list) of forbidden moves to prevent cycling. |
| **Genetic Algorithms (GA)** | Population-based; uses crossover + mutation to explore new regions. |
| **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** | Particles share global best information; balance exploration vs. exploitation via inertia weight. |
| **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** | Pheromone trail reinforcement guides future ants toward good regions. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Optimization:**
> - **Civil Engineering:** Structural design optimization (minimize material, maximize load capacity).
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Topology optimization, thermal system design.
> - **Electrical/Electronics:** VLSI chip routing, antenna design.
> - **Computer Science:** Machine learning hyperparameter tuning, database query optimization, cloud resource allocation.
> - **Operations Research:** Supply chain, logistics, scheduling, portfolio optimization.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Complexity Table

### 2.4.1 Complexity Comparison

| Method | Time Complexity (Typical) | Optimality Guarantee |
|---|---|---|
| Brute Force | $O(\vert S \vert)$ — exponential in $n$ | **Yes (global)** |
| Dynamic Programming | $O(n \cdot 2^n)$ to $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ | **Yes** |
| Branch & Bound | Worst-case $O(\vert S \vert)$, but often sub-exponential due to pruning | **Yes** |
| Backtracking | Worst-case $O(\vert S \vert)$, reduced by pruning | **Yes** |
| Greedy Heuristic | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n \log n)$ | **No** |
| Local Search | $O(k \cdot \vert N(\mathbf{x}) \vert)$, $k$ iterations | **No (local only)** |
| Metaheuristics | $O(p \cdot n \cdot T)$, $p$ = population, $T$ = iterations | **No (but near-optimal)** |

### 2.4.2 Core Optimization Formulations

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Linear Programming (LP):} && \min \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b},\ \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0} \\
&\text{Integer Programming (IP):} && \min \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b},\ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^n \\
&\text{Nonlinear Programming (NLP):} && \min f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0 \\
&\text{Combinatorial Optimization:} && \min f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F} \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^n \text{ (discrete)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.3 Key Definition Formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Decision space: } && S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n \text{ (continuous) or } \mathbb{Z}^n \text{ (discrete)} \\
&\text{Feasible region: } && \mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{x} \in S : g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq b_i,\ h_j(\mathbf{x}) = c_j\} \\
&\text{Optimal solution: } && f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}), \quad \forall \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F} \\
&\text{Neighbourhood: } && N(\mathbf{x}) = \{\mathbf{x}' : d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') \leq \delta\} \\
&\text{Approximation ratio: } && \rho = \frac{f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{approx}})}{f(\mathbf{x}^*)} \geq 1 \quad \text{(for minimization)}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive derivations of foundational concepts, comparison matrices, and worked analytical examples that are commonly tested in KTU examinations.

## 3.1 Derivation: Counting the Search Space of Brute Force (Combinatorial Explosion)

**Problem Context:** We want to demonstrate *why* exact methods fail for large $n$.

**Derivation Steps:**

Consider the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) with $n$ cities. A brute-force algorithm enumerates every possible Hamiltonian cycle to find the shortest.

**Step 1:** The number of permutations of $n$ cities is $n!$ (all orderings of the $n$ cities).

**Step 2:** A Hamiltonian cycle has no designated starting city, and direction does not matter. We must divide by $2n$ (or by $2$ for direction and $n$ for rotations).

**Step 3:** Therefore, the number of distinct TSP tours is:

$$
T(n) = \frac{n!}{2n} = \frac{(n-1)!}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Even with a fast computer performing $10^9$ tour evaluations per second, the time required grows as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = 10: \quad & T(10) = \frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440 \text{ tours} \approx 0.00018 \text{ s} \\
n = 15: \quad & T(15) = \frac{14!}{2} = 43{,}589{,}145{,}600 \approx 43.6 \text{ s} \\
n = 20: \quad & T(20) = \frac{19!}{2} \approx 6.08 \times 10^{16} \approx 1929 \text{ years} \\
n = 25: \quad & T(25) = \frac{24!}{2} \approx 3.10 \times 10^{23} \approx 9.8 \text{ million years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (Conclusion):** This factorial explosion is the *defining motivation* for metaheuristics. For any $n > 25$ or so, brute force is computationally infeasible even on the world's fastest supercomputers.

## 3.2 Derivation: Approximation Ratio Bound

**Problem Context:** Prove that a heuristic $H$ producing solution $\mathbf{x}_H$ has approximation ratio at most $\rho$ for minimization.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** By definition, $\rho \geq 1$ for minimization, and $\rho \leq 1$ for maximization.

**Step 2:** For minimization:

$$
\rho = \frac{f(\mathbf{x}_H)}{f(\mathbf{x}^*)} \geq 1
$$

**Step 3:** A **$\rho$-approximation algorithm** is guaranteed to produce $\mathbf{x}_H$ such that:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_H) \leq \rho \cdot f(\mathbf{x}^*)
$$

**Step 4 (Example — Nearest Neighbour for TSP):** It is known that the nearest-neighbour heuristic for metric TSP produces a tour at most:

$$
\rho = \frac{1}{2} \left( \lceil \log_2 n \rceil + 1 \right)
$$

For $n = 10$: $\rho = \frac{1}{2}(\lceil \log_2 10 \rceil + 1) = \frac{1}{2}(4 + 1) = 2.5$.

**Step 5 (Implication):** A tour produced by NN will be at most 2.5× longer than the optimal tour — this is the theoretical worst case; in practice, it is often closer to 1.15–1.25.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Exact vs. Approximate (Detailed Comparison)

| Comparison Axis | Exact Methods | Approximate Methods (Metaheuristics) |
|---|---|---|
| **Solution Quality** | Provably optimal (best possible). | Near-optimal; quality depends on tuning & runtime. |
| **Runtime** | Exponential worst-case; intractable for large $n$. | Polynomial; scales gracefully to large $n$. |
| **Memory** | Usually large (memoization tables, B&B trees). | Usually compact (population of fixed size). |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic (same answer each run). | Often stochastic (different answer each run). |
| **Problem Specificity** | Mostly problem-agnostic (LP, IP solvers are general). | Highly problem-agnostic — *this is the key advantage of metaheuristics*. |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Moderate to high (LP solvers, B&B logic). | Low to moderate (simple loops). |
| **Sensitivity to Tuning** | Low (solver parameters are well-studied). | High (population size, crossover rate, etc.). |
| **Worst-Case Behavior** | Exponential blow-up. | Polynomial, bounded by stopping criterion. |
| **Robustness to Noise** | Fragile — small changes in $f$ may not change optimum. | Often robust — averaging effect across population. |
| **Parallelism** | Limited (e.g., parallel B&B is non-trivial). | Natural (population can be evaluated in parallel). |
| **Examples** | Simplex, Branch & Bound, DP, Backtracking. | GA, PSO, SA, TS, ACO, DE. |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Branch and Bound on a Small Integer Program

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ subject to $x \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ and $x \leq 5$.

**Step 1:** The LP relaxation allows $x \in \mathbb{R}^+$, so the optimum is $x^* = 0$, $f^* = 0$.

**Step 2:** The B&B tree:
- **Root:** $[0, 5]$ continuous, bound $= 0$, branch into $[0, 2]$ and $[3, 5]$.
- **Node $[0, 2]$:** LP optimum is $0$ (at $x=0$); integer feasible. **Incumbent = 0**.
- **Node $[3, 5]$:** LP optimum is $9$ (at $x=3$); bound $= 9 \geq$ incumbent $0$. **Prune** (cannot improve incumbent).

**Step 3:** Optimal integer solution is $x^* = 0$, $f^* = 0$. B&B explored 3 nodes instead of brute force's 6 evaluations.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Dynamic Programming — Simple 0/1 Knapsack

**Problem:** Capacity $W = 5$, items: A (w=2, v=3), B (w=3, v=4), C (w=4, v=5). Maximize value.

**Step 1:** Let $V[i][w]$ = max value using first $i$ items with capacity $w$.

**Recurrence:**

$$
V[i][w] = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i = 0 \text{ or } w = 0 \\ V[i-1][w] & \text{if } w_i > w \\ \max(V[i-1][w], v_i + V[i-1][w - w_i]) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2:** Fill table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V[0][w] &= 0 \quad \forall w \\
V[1][2] &= 3,\quad V[1][3] = 3,\quad V[1][4] = 3,\quad V[1][5] = 3 \\
V[2][3] &= \max(V[1][3]=3,\ 4+V[1][0]=4) = 4 \\
V[2][4] &= \max(V[1][4]=3,\ 4+V[1][1]=4) = 4 \\
V[2][5] &= \max(V[1][5]=3,\ 4+V[1][2]=7) = 7 \\
V[3][5] &= \max(V[2][5]=7,\ 5+V[2][1]=5) = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Optimal value $V[3][5] = 7$, achieved by selecting items A and B (weights $2+3=5$, values $3+4=7$).

**Complexity:** $O(n \cdot W) = O(3 \cdot 5) = O(15)$ — exponential improvement over $O(2^n)$ brute force.

## 3.6 Decision Framework: When to Use What?

**Use Exact Methods when:**
- Problem size is small ($n \leq 30$ approximately).
- The optimum is a hard requirement (safety-critical, contractual).
- The problem has polynomial-time algorithms (e.g., LP via Simplex, shortest path via Dijkstra).
- You have access to industrial solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi).

**Use Approximate / Metaheuristic Methods when:**
- Problem is NP-hard and $n$ is large.
- A good-enough solution suffices (engineering design, real-time systems).
- The objective or constraints are non-convex, discontinuous, or noisy.
- You need a quick, tunable, problem-agnostic framework.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Optimization Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Optimization Methods"]:::root --> B["Exact Methods"]:::branch
    A --> C["Approximate Methods"]:::branch
    
    B --> B1["Brute Force Enumeration"]
    B --> B2["Dynamic Programming"]
    B --> B3["Branch and Bound"]
    B --> B4["Backtracking"]
    B --> B5["Integer Linear Programming"]
    
    C --> C1["Heuristics Problem Specific"]:::sub
    C --> C2["Metaheuristics Problem Independent"]:::sub
    
    C1 --> C1a["Greedy Construction"]
    C1 --> C1b["Local Search Improvement"]
    
    C2 --> C2a["Single Solution Trajectory"]:::deep
    C2 --> C2b["Population Based"]:::deep
    
    C2a --> C2a1["Simulated Annealing"]
    C2a --> C2a2["Tabu Search"]
    C2a --> C2a3["Iterated Local Search"]
    
    C2b --> C2b1["Evolutionary Algorithms"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Metaheuristic

```mermaid
    D4 -- Better --> D5
    D4 -- Worse --> D5

    T1 -- Yes --> T2
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between exact methods and approximate methods of optimization. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Exact Methods | Approximate Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Guarantee global optimum | No guarantee; returns near-optimal |
| **Time Complexity** | Often exponential | Typically polynomial |
| **Scalability** | Limited to small $n$ | Scales to large $n$ |
| **Determinism** | Usually deterministic | Often stochastic |
| **Example** | Branch & Bound, Dynamic Programming | Genetic Algorithm, Simulated Annealing |

*(3 marks — definition + 1 example each with brief justification)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define NP-hard problem. Why are metaheuristics preferred over exact methods for NP-hard problems?**

**Model Answer:**

**NP-hard** problems are a class of decision/optimization problems for which no known polynomial-time algorithm exists to find the optimal solution. The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) and the 0/1 Knapsack Problem are classic examples.

Metaheuristics are preferred because:
1. **Exact methods** (e.g., brute force) have exponential time complexity $O(2^n)$ or $O(n!)$, which becomes infeasible even for moderate $n$ (e.g., $n > 30$).
2. **Metaheuristics** run in polynomial time and produce near-optimal solutions within acceptable computational budgets.
3. Metaheuristics are **problem-independent** and can be applied to any NP-hard problem with minimal modification.

*(3 marks — definition + 3 valid reasons)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Branch and Bound method in detail. Describe how pruning is performed and how it differs from brute force enumeration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the working of Dynamic Programming as an optimization technique. Mention Bellman's principle of optimality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Branch and Bound — Detailed Explanation:**

**Step 1 (Branching):** The feasible region $S$ is recursively divided into two or more disjoint sub-regions $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k$, forming a search tree. The root is the entire feasible set.

**Step 2 (Bounding):** For each sub-region $S_i$, compute a *lower bound* (for minimization) or *upper bound* (for maximization) on the best achievable objective value $f(\mathbf{x}), \mathbf{x} \in S_i$. This bound is usually obtained by solving a **relaxation** — an easier problem (e.g., LP relaxation of an IP, dropping integrality constraints).

**Step 3 (Pruning):** A node representing sub-region $S_i$ is pruned (removed from consideration) if **any** of the following holds:
- The bound of $S_i$ is *worse* than the current best-known feasible solution (the **incumbent**).
- $S_i$ contains *no feasible solution* (infeasibility detected).
- $S_i$ contains *exactly one feasible solution* whose objective value is already known.

**Difference from Brute Force:**

| Aspect | Brute Force | Branch and Bound |
|---|---|---|
| Sub-regions explored | **All** $n!$ permutations | Only those not pruned |
| Optimality proof | Direct enumeration | Bounds + pruning prove optimality |
| Worst-case time | Always $O(\vert S \vert)$ | Often much less due to pruning |
| Memory | Stores only best | Stores entire tree |

**Worked Mini-Example:** Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ subject to $x \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $x \leq 5$.
- Root bound (LP relaxation): $x = 0$, bound $= 0$.
- Branch into $[0,2]$ and $[3,5]$.
- $[0,2]$: integer optimum $= 0$ → incumbent updated.
- $[3,5]$: lower bound $= 9 > 0$ → **pruned**.
- Optimal found with only 3 nodes instead of brute force's 6 evaluations. *(7 marks — branching 2 marks, bounding 2 marks, pruning 2 marks, example 1 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Dynamic Programming (DP):**

**Definition:** Dynamic Programming is an exact optimization method that solves complex problems by breaking them down into simpler overlapping sub-problems, storing the solutions to sub-problems in a table to avoid recomputation (memoization).

**Bellman's Principle of Optimality:**
> *"An optimal policy (or sequence of decisions) has the property that whatever the initial state and the initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."*

In mathematical terms:

$$
f^*(\mathbf{x}_0) = \min_{d_0 \in D} \left[ c(\mathbf{x}_0, d_0) + f^*(T(\mathbf{x}_0, d_0)) \right]
$$

Where $f^*(\mathbf{x})$ is the optimal cost from state $\mathbf{x}$, $d$ is a decision, $c$ is the immediate cost, and $T$ is the state-transition function.

**Worked Example — 0/1 Knapsack:**
- Capacity $W = 5$, items: A (w=2, v=3), B (w=3, v=4), C (w=4, v=5).
- Recurrence: $V[i][w] = \max(V[i-1][w], v_i + V[i-1][w - w_i])$.
- Filling the table systematically yields $V[3][5] = 7$, achieved by selecting items A and B.
- Time complexity: $O(nW)$ vs. brute force $O(2^n)$ — exponential improvement.

**Advantages of DP:**
1. Exponential reduction in complexity for many problems.
2. Guarantees global optimum.
3. Reusable sub-solutions (memoization).

**Limitations:**
1. Applies only when sub-problems overlap and optimal substructure holds.
2. Memory requirement grows with state-space size (curse of dimensionality).
3. Not all NP-hard problems have DP-friendly structures.

*(7 marks — definition 2 marks, Bellman's principle 2 marks, example 2 marks, advantages/limitations 1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** What are metaheuristic optimization algorithms? Explain why they are needed even when exact methods exist. Discuss the local optima problem in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the following: Brute Force, Branch & Bound, Greedy Heuristic, and Genetic Algorithm. Use a comparison matrix covering at least 6 axes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Metaheuristic Optimization Algorithms:**

**Definition:** Metaheuristics are high-level, problem-independent algorithmic frameworks designed to find sufficiently good (near-optimal) solutions to optimization problems — especially NP-hard ones — within acceptable computational time. The Greek prefix *meta* implies they operate "above" or "beyond" specific problem heuristics.

**Why Metaheuristics are Needed (Even When Exact Methods Exist):**

1. **Computational Tractability:** Exact methods such as brute force and B&B have worst-case exponential complexity $O(2^n)$ or $O(n!)$. For NP-hard problems, this is intractable beyond $n \approx 30$.

2. **No Polynomial Algorithms Exist:** For the general TSP, no known polynomial-time exact algorithm exists (unless P = NP). Metaheuristics offer a polynomial-time alternative.

3. **Real-World Engineering Scale:** Industrial problems (e.g., VLSI routing with millions of components, supply chain with thousands of nodes) demand solutions in seconds or minutes, not millennia.

4. **Problem Agnosticism:** One metaheuristic framework (e.g., GA) can be applied across vastly different problems — TSP, scheduling, neural architecture search — with only minor encoding changes.

5. **Quality vs. Time Trade-off:** Metaheuristics let the user dial in the trade-off — running longer yields better quality, but you can stop anytime and still get a feasible solution.

**The Local Optima Problem (Detailed):**

Consider a minimization problem with objective $f(\mathbf{x})$. Local search starts at $\mathbf{x}_0$ and iteratively moves to a better neighbor $\mathbf{x}' \in N(\mathbf{x})$ where $f(\mathbf{x}') < f(\mathbf{x})$.

A point $\mathbf{x}_L$ is a **local minimum** if:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_L) \leq f(\mathbf{x}), \quad \forall \mathbf{x} \in N(\mathbf{x}_L)
$$

A point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **global minimum** if:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}), \quad \forall \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}
$$

**The Trap:** Local search terminates at $\mathbf{x}_L$ with no mechanism to escape, even when a much better $\mathbf{x}^*$ exists in a distant valley. This is the **local optima trap**.

**How Metaheuristics Escape:**
- **Simulated Annealing (SA):** Accepts worse moves with probability $P = \exp(-\Delta f / T)$; high $T$ allows broad exploration, low $T$ allows fine-tuning.
- **Tabu Search (TS):** Maintains a list of recently visited states (tabu list) and forces exploration of new regions.
- **Genetic Algorithms (GA):** Population-based search; crossover and mutation combine features of multiple solutions to discover new regions.
- **PSO:** Particles share global best information, attracting the swarm toward promising regions even when individual particles are stuck.

*(7 marks — definition 1 mark, need 3 marks, local optima problem 3 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparative Analysis Matrix:**

| Comparison Axis | Brute Force | Branch & Bound | Greedy Heuristic | Genetic Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solution Quality** | Optimal (global) | Optimal (global) | Sub-optimal (no guarantee) | Near-optimal |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(\vert S \vert)$ exponential | Worst-case exponential; often sub-exponential | $O(n \log n)$ to $O(n^2)$ | $O(p \cdot n \cdot T)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ | $O(\text{tree depth})$ | $O(1)$ | $O(p \cdot n)$ |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Deterministic | Deterministic | Stochastic |
| **Optimality Guarantee** | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Scalability ($n$ large)** | Very poor | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Implementation Effort** | Very low | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Escape Local Optima?** | N/A (enumerates all) | N/A (uses bounds) | No | Yes (via mutation/crossover) |
| **Problem Dependency** | Universal | Universal | Problem-specific encoding | Universal (with encoding) |
| **Parallelism** | Limited | Limited | Limited | Natural & high |

**Key Observations:**

1. **Brute Force** is the theoretical gold standard but practically useless for $n > 25$ in combinatorial problems.
2. **Branch & Bound** brings mathematical rigor with pruning, but its worst case still suffers exponential blow-up.
3. **Greedy Heuristic** is blazingly fast but offers no quality guarantees and often gets trapped in local optima.
4. **Genetic Algorithm** offers a balanced trade-off — polynomial time, near-optimal quality, and problem-independence, at the cost of stochasticity and parameter tuning.

**Illustrative Example — TSP with $n = 20$:**
- Brute force: $\sim 1929$ years (infeasible).
- B&B: minutes to hours (problem-dependent).
- Greedy (NN): milliseconds; quality $\sim$1.15–1.25× optimal.
- GA: seconds; quality $\sim$1.02–1.05× optimal.

*(7 marks — matrix 4 marks, observations 2 marks, example 1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "heuristic" with "metaheuristic":** A *heuristic* is problem-specific (e.g., Nearest-Neighbour for TSP). A *metaheuristic* is problem-independent (e.g., GA, SA). Examiners frequently test this distinction — losing 1–2 marks for the wrong term is common.
> 
> 2. **Confusing "local optimum" with "global optimum":** Always clarify that local search terminates at a *local* optimum — not necessarily global. Phrases like "the algorithm finds the best solution" without qualification invite mark deductions.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting optimality conditions:** When asked about a method, *always* state whether it guarantees optimality. This is the single most important differentiator in valuation keys.
> 
> 4. **Missing complexity statements:** KTU examiners reward explicit mention of time complexity ($O(n!)$, $O(n \cdot 2^n)$, $O(n^2)$, etc.). Vague statements like "it is slow" earn zero credit.
> 
> 5. **Skipping examples:** For 7-mark sub-parts, an example or numerical illustration is **mandatory**. Without one, expect to lose 1–2 marks even if the theory is correct.
> 
> 6. **Bellman's Principle mis-statement:** It must be quoted accurately — "remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision." Half-remembered quotes are penalized.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Optimization** = finding the best feasible $\mathbf{x}^*$ that minimizes/maximizes $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to constraints.
- [ ] **Exact Methods** = guaranteed optimal, exponential worst-case; examples: Brute Force, DP, B&B, Backtracking, ILP.
- [ ] **Approximate Methods** = no optimality guarantee, polynomial time; examples: Greedy, Local Search, Metaheuristics.
- [ ] **Heuristic** = problem-specific rule of thumb (e.g., Nearest-Neighbour).
- [ ] **Metaheuristic** = problem-independent high-level framework (e.g., GA, SA, PSO, ACO, TS).
- [ ] **NP-hard problems** have no known polynomial-time exact algorithm; this is the *raison d'être* of metaheuristics.
- [ ] **Local optimum trap:** Local search stops at local minima, missing global ones — metaheuristics introduce escape mechanisms.
- [ ] **Bellman's Principle:** "An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and decision are, the remaining decisions constitute an optimal policy from the new state."
- [ ] **Branch & Bound** = branching + bounding + pruning; prunes subtrees whose bound exceeds incumbent.
- [ ] **Dynamic Programming** = memoized recursion; complexity $O(n \cdot W)$ for knapsack vs. $O(2^n)$ brute force.
- [ ] **Brute Force complexity for TSP** = $(n-1)!/2$ tours — infeasible for $n > 25$.
- [ ] **Approximation ratio** $\rho = f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{approx}})/f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ — measures heuristic quality (1 = optimal).
- [ ] **Metaheuristic escape mechanisms:** SA's probabilistic acceptance, TS's tabu list, GA's crossover/mutation, PSO's global best attraction.
- [ ] **Population-based vs. Trajectory-based:** GA/PSO/ACO use populations; SA/TS use a single solution.
- [ ] **KTU mantra:** For any 7-mark question, *definition + mechanism + example + complexity* = full marks.
- [ ] **Curse of dimensionality:** DP and exact methods suffer; metaheuristics are designed to cope with high-dimensional problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metaheuristics Concepts - Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Metaheuristics Concepts: Representation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Metaheuristics

A **metaheuristic** is a higher-level, problem-independent optimization framework that coordinates a set of heuristic strategies and learning mechanisms to explore and exploit a search space in pursuit of near-optimal solutions. Unlike exact methods (e.g., Branch-and-Bound, Dynamic Programming), metaheuristics **do not guarantee global optimality** but provide acceptable solutions within reasonable computational time for NP-hard, large-scale, or poorly structured problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** Metaheuristics are iterative, stochastic/generic search procedures that guide subordinate heuristics by combining intelligent strategies for exploring and exploiting the solution landscape.

### 1.2 Definition of Representation (Encoding)

**Representation** (also called *encoding* or *solution representation*) is the mapping mechanism by which a candidate solution to a real-world optimization problem is transformed into a data structure that the metaheuristic algorithm can manipulate, evaluate, and evolve. Mathematically, it defines the **genotype space** $\mathcal{G}$ on which the algorithm operates and the **decoding function** $\mathcal{D}: \mathcal{G} \rightarrow \mathcal{P}$ that maps each genotype to its corresponding **phenotype** (an actual feasible solution in the problem space $\mathcal{P}$).

$$g \in \mathcal{G} \xrightarrow{\text{Decode } \mathcal{D}} p \in \mathcal{P} \xrightarrow{\text{Evaluate } f} f(p) \in \mathbb{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Representation Matters in KTU Context:**
> The choice of representation directly affects (a) search space size, (b) operator design (crossover, mutation), (c) convergence speed, and (d) feasibility handling. KTU questions frequently test *why a particular representation is chosen for a given problem class*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The DNA Analogy

Imagine you are an architect designing thousands of buildings (candidate solutions). Each building blueprint is a complex object with floors, rooms, materials — this is the **phenotype**. To store, copy, and modify blueprints efficiently, you first convert them into a simple **string of codes** (genotype) — like DNA encoding an organism.

When you need to *evaluate* a building, you **decode** the code back into a blueprint. When you need to *breed* better buildings, you operate on the code (swap parts, mutate entries). The genotype is what the algorithm touches; the phenotype is what the world understands. This separation is the **essence of representation in metaheuristics**.

### 1.4 Two Fundamental Spaces in Metaheuristic Search

| Space | Symbol | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Genotype Space** | $\mathcal{G}$ | The encoded space where the algorithm operates (binary strings, real vectors, permutations) | $011010$ |
| **Phenotype Space** | $\mathcal{P}$ | The actual problem-solution space (real-world feasible solutions) | $x = 13.7$ (decimal value) |
| **Objective Space** | $\mathcal{O}$ | The space of objective function values mapped from phenotypes | $f(x) = 187.45$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Insight:** The algorithm **never directly evaluates** the genotype. The genotype $\rightarrow$ phenotype (decode) $\rightarrow$ objective value (evaluate) pipeline is mandatory. Skipping decode is a common valuation error.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Metaheuristic Design

Every metaheuristic — whether GA, PSO, ACO, or Simulated Annealing — is built upon three core pillars:

1. **Representation (Encoding)** — *How is a solution stored?*
2. **Objective Function (Fitness Evaluation)** — *How good is a solution?*
3. **Variation Operators (Search Operators)** — *How do we generate new solutions?*

This module focuses exclusively on **Pillar 1**, which is the foundation that dictates the design of the other two.

## 2.2 Classification of Representation Schemes

Representation schemes are classified based on the **domain of decision variables**. KTU Module 1 explicitly tests the following five canonical types.

### 2.2.1 Binary Representation
Each variable is encoded as a fixed-length string of bits $\{0, 1\}^L$.

$$\mathbf{g} = (g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_L), \quad g_i \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Decoding rule** (unsigned integer to real value in $[a, b]$):
$$x = a + \frac{(b - a)}{2^L - 1} \cdot \left(\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} g_i \cdot 2^i\right)$$

- **Precision:** $\Delta x = \dfrac{b - a}{2^L - 1}$
- **Search space size:** $2^L$ (exponential growth with bits)

### 2.2.2 Real-Valued (Continuous) Representation
The genotype is a vector of real numbers in $\mathbb{R}^n$. There is **no decoding** — the genotype *is* the phenotype.

$$\mathbf{g} = (g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_n), \quad g_i \in [L_i, U_i]$$

- **Search space size:** Infinite (uncountable)
- **Used in:** PSO, DE, Real-Coded GA, CMA-ES

### 2.2.3 Integer / Discrete Representation
Variables take values from a finite set of integers, often used for combinatorial selections.

$$\mathbf{g} \in \mathbb{Z}^n, \quad g_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$$

### 2.2.4 Permutation Representation
The genotype is a permutation of $n$ distinct symbols — used in **routing, scheduling, TSP**.

$$\mathbf{g} = (\pi(1), \pi(2), \ldots, \pi(n)), \quad \pi \in S_n$$

- **Search space size:** $n!$ (factorial explosion)
- **Constraint:** Every symbol appears exactly once.

### 2.2.5 Tree / Graph Representation
Used in **Genetic Programming (GP)**; the genotype is a syntactic tree whose leaves are terminals and internal nodes are functions.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Application Context | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Search space size (binary, $L$ bits) | $N = 2^L$ | Binary GA | Exponential growth |
| 2 | Decoding precision (binary) | $\Delta x = \dfrac{b - a}{2^L - 1}$ | Binary → real | Longer string → finer precision |
| 3 | Binary to real decoding | $x = a + \Delta x \cdot \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} g_i 2^i$ | Binary GA evaluation | **Most-tested KTU formula** |
| 4 | Gray code bit $i$ | $G_i = B_{i+1} \oplus B_i$ | Adjacent integer binary codes differ by 1 bit | Hamming-1 neighbors |
| 5 | Search space (permutation, $n$) | $N = n!$ | TSP, scheduling | $10! = 3.6 \times 10^6$ |
| 6 | Search space (integer, $m$ vals, $n$ vars) | $N = m^n$ | Knapsack-like | |
| 7 | Genotype-to-phenotype map | $p = \mathcal{D}(g)$ | Universal | All algorithms |
| 8 | Hamming distance | $H(g^{(1)}, g^{(2)}) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{L} \vert g_i^{(1)} - g_i^{(2)} \vert$ | GA mutation analysis | $g_i \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 9 | Precision requirement (real) | $L \geq \log_2\left(\dfrac{b - a}{\epsilon} + 1\right)$ | Binary string length for target precision $\epsilon$ | |
| 10 | Fitness-proportionate selection | $P(g_i) = \dfrac{f(g_i)}{\sum_j f(g_j)}$ | GA selection | Requires non-negative fitness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** When a question states "design a binary representation for a variable in $[a, b]$ with precision $\epsilon$", you **must** derive $L$ using formula 9 above and justify using $2^L - 1$ in the denominator of formula 3.

## 2.4 Why Representation Determines Algorithm Behaviour

- **Search space geometry** changes with encoding. Binary representation creates a *Hamming cliff* problem (e.g., 0111 → 1000 flips 4 bits), which **Gray coding** mitigates.
- **Operator compatibility** is representation-specific: bit-flip mutation is natural for binary; arithmetic crossover is natural for real-valued; OX/PMX crossovers are mandatory for permutations.
- **Constraint handling** depends on representation: permutations auto-satisfy uniqueness; real-valued may need boundary clamping.

## 2.5 Engineering Applications

| Domain | Representation Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Antenna design (real parameters) | Real-valued | Variables are continuous physical dimensions |
| TSP / VRP | Permutation | City orderings |
| Feature selection in ML | Binary | Each gene = "include/exclude" a feature |
| Job-shop scheduling | Permutation + Gantt decoding | Operation order on machines |
| Portfolio optimization | Real-valued or integer | Asset weights, share counts |
| Network routing | Permutation / Tree | Path orderings, hierarchical structures |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Decoding a Binary String to a Real Value

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Form):** A variable $x$ lies in the interval $[a, b]$ and is encoded using a binary string of length $L$. The string is read as an unsigned integer in natural binary code (MSB to LSB).

**Step 1 — Define the integer value of the binary string.**
Given a binary string $g = (g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_L)$ where $g_1$ is the MSB and $g_L$ is the LSB:

$$I(g) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} g_i \cdot 2^{L-i}$$

Equivalently, using 0-indexed bits $g_0$ (MSB) through $g_{L-1}$ (LSB):

$$I(g) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} g_i \cdot 2^{L-1-i}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the range of $I(g)$.**
The minimum value is $0$ (all zeros), and the maximum is $2^L - 1$ (all ones). Thus $I(g) \in [0, 2^L - 1]$.

**Step 3 — Establish a linear map from integer range to real range.**
We want a linear function $\phi: [0, 2^L - 1] \rightarrow [a, b]$ of the form $\phi(I) = mI + c$.

Apply boundary conditions:
- $\phi(0) = a \;\Rightarrow\; c = a$
- $\phi(2^L - 1) = b \;\Rightarrow\; m(2^L - 1) + a = b \;\Rightarrow\; m = \dfrac{b - a}{2^L - 1}$

**Step 4 — Final decoding formula.**

$$x = a + \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1} \cdot I(g) = a + \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} g_i \cdot 2^{L-1-i}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example.**
Let $a = -5$, $b = 5$, $L = 8$, $g = (1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0)$.

Compute the integer value:
$$I(g) = 1 \cdot 128 + 0 \cdot 64 + 1 \cdot 32 + 1 \cdot 16 + 0 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 128 + 32 + 16 + 2 = 178$$

Compute the decoding factor:
$$m = \frac{5 - (-5)}{2^8 - 1} = \frac{10}{255} \approx 0.03922$$

Decode to real value:
$$x = -5 + 0.03922 \times 178 = -5 + 6.9808 = 1.9808$$

**Precision check:** $\Delta x = 10 / 255 \approx 0.0392$ — so adjacent integer values differ by $\approx 0.0392$ in $x$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required String Length for a Given Precision

**Problem Setup:** We want the discrete decoded values to be no more than $\epsilon$ apart so that no real solution in $[a, b]$ is missed by more than $\epsilon$.

**Step 1 — Required number of distinct values.**
The number of distinct integers needed to cover the interval with spacing $\epsilon$ is:
$$N \geq \frac{b - a}{\epsilon}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bits required.**
For a binary string of length $L$ to have at least $N$ distinct codes:
$$2^L \geq N \;\Rightarrow\; L \geq \log_2 N$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**
$$L \geq \log_2\left(\frac{b - a}{\epsilon} + 1\right)$$

(We add 1 to account for the inclusive count; using $2^L - 1$ in the denominator of the decoder is equivalent to requiring $2^L \geq N$ strictly.)

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.**
Let $[a, b] = [0, 10]$, $\epsilon = 0.001$.

$$L \geq \log_2\left(\frac{10}{0.001} + 1\right) = \log_2(10001) \approx 13.29$$

Since $L$ must be an integer, $L = 14$ bits are required. Verification: $2^{14} - 1 = 16383$ distinct levels; spacing $= 10/16383 \approx 0.00061 < 0.001$. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gray Code Construction

Gray codes guarantee that consecutive integers differ in **exactly one bit**, eliminating the Hamming cliff.

**Step 1 — Convert natural binary to Gray code.**
For bit position $i$ (with MSB indexed as $i = 1$):
$$G_i = B_{i} \oplus B_{i+1}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Example.**
Let $B = (1, 0, 1, 1)$ (binary for decimal 11).

- $G_1 = B_1 \oplus B_2 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- $G_2 = B_2 \oplus B_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $G_3 = B_3 \oplus B_4 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $G_4 = B_4 \oplus 0 = 1$ (pad with 0 on the right)

So Gray code $= (1, 1, 0, 1)$, which is decimal 13. The next Gray code (decimal 12) is $(1, 1, 0, 0)$ — they differ in 1 bit. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Representation vs. Algorithm

| Representation | Suitable Algorithms | Typical Mutation | Typical Crossover | Constraint Handling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Binary | Simple GA, CHC | Bit-flip | 1-point, 2-point, Uniform | Penalty / repair |
| Real-valued | PSO, DE, ES, Real-GA | Gaussian perturbation | SBX, BLX-α, Arithmetic | Clamping / penalty |
| Integer | GA, ACO | Random reset | Uniform, 1-point | Penalty / specialized |
| Permutation | GA on TSP/VRP | Swap, Inversion, Scramble | OX, PMX, CX, ERX | Permutation is the constraint |
| Tree | Genetic Programming | Subtree mutation | Subtree crossover | Bloat control |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solution Representation Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real-world Problem] --> B[Phenotype Space P]
    B --> C[Encoding Function E]
    C --> D[Genotype Space G]
    D --> E[Variation Operators\nCrossover, Mutation]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Decoding Function D]
    F --> G[Phenotype Candidate p]
    G --> H[Objective Function f]
    H --> I[Fitness Value f p]
    I --> J{Stop Criterion Met?}
    J -- No --> E
    J -- Yes --> K[Best Solution p*]

    subgraph SR[Search Loop]
        E
        D
        F
        G
        H
        I
    end
```

## 4.2 Classification of Representation Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Representation Schemes] --> R1[Binary]
    R --> R2[Real-valued]
    R --> R3[Integer Discrete]
    R --> R4[Permutation]
    R --> R5[Tree Graph]

    R1 --> R1a[Bit strings 0 1 to the L]
    R1 --> R1b[Used in classical GA]

    R2 --> R2a[Vector in R to the n]
    R2 --> R2b[PSO, DE, Real coded GA]

    R3 --> R3a[Values from finite integer set]
    R3 --> R3b[Knapsack, feature selection]

    R4 --> R4a[Permutation of n symbols]
    R4 --> R4b[TSP, scheduling, routing]

    R5 --> R5a[Syntactic trees]
    R5 --> R5b[Genetic Programming, symbolic regression]
```

## 4.3 Genotype-to-Phenotype-to-Fitness Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Genotype g] -- Decode D --> P1[Phenotype p]
    P1 -- Evaluate f --> F1[Fitness f p]
    G2[Genotype g prime] -- Decode D --> P2[Phenotype p prime]
    P2 -- Evaluate f --> F2[Fitness f p prime]
    F1 --> C{Selection}
    F2 --> C
    C --> NG[Next Gen Genotypes]
```

## 4.4 Binary Decoding Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Binary String g1 to gL] --> INT[Compute Integer Value I g]
    INT --> NORM[Map to 0,1 by divide by 2 to L minus 1]
    NORM --> SCALE[Scale to a,b interval]
    SCALE --> X[Real Value x in a,b]
    X --> OBJ[Evaluate f x]
    OBJ --> FIT[Fitness returned to algorithm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagrams above map the **functional flow** of representation-related operations. Each block represents a computational stage inside a metaheuristic iteration, not a physical hardware component.
