**Setup:** An algorithm is run 30 times on a benchmark with convergence threshold $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$. It successfully reaches the $\epsilon$-optimum in 24 out of 30 runs.

**Step 1 — Apply the SR formula:**

$$
SR = \frac{N_{\text{success}}}{N} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
SR = \frac{24}{30} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
SR = 0.80 \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Success Rate of $\geq 80\%$ is generally considered a **strong indicator of algorithm reliability** in KTU-evaluated research presentations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Tuning Method

| Criterion | Taguchi DoE | F-Race | Irace | Self-Adaptive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Search Budget** | Low | Medium | High | None (built-in) |
| **Statistical Rigor** | Low | High (Friedman test) | Very High | Algorithm-dependent |
| **Scalability to many parameters** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Algorithm-specific |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | **Very High** | Medium | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Parameter Tuning Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Algorithm A] --> B[Identify Parameter Space Theta]
    B --> C[Define Domain Di for each theta i]
    C --> D{Select Tuning Method}
    D --> D1[Taguchi DoE]
    D --> D2[F-Race Racing]
    D --> D3[Irace Sequential]
    D --> D4[Self-Adaptive Online]
    D1 --> E[Generate Candidate Configurations]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> F[Run Algorithm on Benchmark Set B]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Record Performance Metrics BF MF SD SR]
    G --> H{Apply Statistical Test}
    H --> I[Wilcoxon Signed Rank]
    H --> J[Friedman Test]
    I --> K[Select Best Configuration theta star]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Validate on Test Problems]
    L --> M{Improvement Significant?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Deploy Tuned Algorithm]
    M -->|No| O[Re-tune or Use Defaults]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow begins with algorithm selection, branches into four parallel tuning strategies, converges at performance recording, applies statistical validation, and ends at deployment or re-tuning. This mirrors the **Birattari tuning protocol** taught in standard metaheuristic textbooks.

---

## 4.2 Performance Analysis Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Tuned Algorithm theta star] --> Q1[Run N Independent Trials]
    Q1 --> R1[Compute Best Fitness BF]
    Q1 --> R2[Compute Mean Fitness MF]
    Q1 --> R3[Compute Standard Deviation SD]
    Q1 --> R4[Compute Success Rate SR]
    Q1 --> R5[Compute Average Execution Time AET]
    R1 --> S1[Convergence Curve Plot]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S2[Boxplot Comparison]
    R5 --> S3[Computational Cost Profile]
    S1 --> T1[Final Report and KTU Submission]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain how you would analyze the performance of a tuned algorithm," **always mention all three artifacts**: convergence curve, boxplot, and execution time profile. This is a guaranteed 3-mark differentiator in KTU Part A questions.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — Parameter Types and Their Tuning Zones

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph META[Metaheuristic Algorithm]
        SP[Structural Parameters N popsize L chromosome]
        CP[Control Parameters pc pm w c1 c2]
        TP[Termination Parameters Tmax FEmax]
    end
    SP -->|Fixed by Problem| ZONE1[Low Tuning Priority]
    CP -->|Heavily Tuned| ZONE2[High Tuning Priority]
    TP -->|Computed from Budget| ZONE3[Medium Tuning Priority]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define parameter tuning in metaheuristic optimization. List any two offline tuning methods.**

**Model Answer:**

Parameter tuning is the process of finding suitable values for the control parameters of a metaheuristic algorithm (e.g., population size, mutation rate, inertia weight) to optimize its performance on a class of problems.

Two offline tuning methods are:
1. **Design of Experiments (DoE)** — uses factorial or fractional factorial designs.
2. **F-Race** — a racing algorithm that statistically eliminates poor configurations iteratively.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two methods = 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: List and define any three performance metrics used to analyze metaheuristic algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Best Fitness (BF):** The minimum (for minimization) objective value obtained over $N$ independent runs.
2. **Mean Fitness (MF):** The arithmetic average of the best fitness values over $N$ runs; indicates average solution quality.
3. **Standard Deviation (SD):** Measures the spread of best fitness values across runs; lower SD = greater robustness.

*(1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Pattern]**

---

### ✦ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Explain the F-Race algorithm for parameter tuning. Describe its working with a suitable flow of operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a metaheuristic run 10 times on a benchmark, the best fitness values are:

$$
\{2.5, 2.1, 2.8, 2.3, 2.6, 2.0, 2.4, 2.7, 2.2, 2.5\}
$$

Compute the **Mean Fitness (MF)**, **Standard Deviation (SD)**, and **Coefficient of Variation (CoV)**. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a) — F-Race Algorithm (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
F-Race, proposed by **Birattari et al. (2002)**, is a racing-based offline parameter tuning method that sequentially evaluates candidate configurations on a stream of benchmark instances and statistically eliminates inferior ones as soon as sufficient evidence is gathered.

**Working Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Step 1 — Initialisation:** Generate a finite set of candidate configurations $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m\}$ from the parameter space $\Omega$ (typically via Latin Hypercube Sampling).
2. **Step 2 — First Instance:** Evaluate all surviving configurations on the first benchmark instance $b_1$.
3. **Step 3 — Statistical Test:** After each new instance, apply a **non-parametric statistical test** (Friedman test, or paired Wilcoxon with Holm correction) to rank the configurations.
4. **Step 4 — Elimination:** Discard any configuration whose rank is significantly worse than the best, using a family-wise error correction.
5. **Step 5 — Repeat:** Continue with the next benchmark instance, **racing** only the surviving candidates, until either one winner remains or all instances are exhausted.
6. **Step 6 — Output:** The surviving (winner) configuration $\theta^*$ is the recommended tuned setting.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Computationally efficient — focuses evaluation budget on promising configurations.
- Statistically rigorous — controls family-wise error rate.
- Easy to parallelize across benchmarks.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b) — Statistical Computation (7 Marks)

Given:

$$
f = \{2.5, 2.1, 2.8, 2.3, 2.6, 2.0, 2.4, 2.7, 2.2, 2.5\}, \quad N = 10
$$

**Step 1 — Sum the values:**

$$
\sum f_r = 2.5 + 2.1 + 2.8 + 2.3 + 2.6 + 2.0 + 2.4 + 2.7 + 2.2 + 2.5 = 24.1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mean Fitness:**

$$
MF = \frac{24.1}{10} = 2.41
$$

**Step 3 — Compute deviations and their squares:**

| $r$ | $f_r$ | $f_r - MF$ | $(f_r - MF)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.5 | 0.09 | 0.0081 |
| 2 | 2.1 | -0.31 | 0.0961 |
| 3 | 2.8 | 0.39 | 0.1521 |
| 4 | 2.3 | -0.11 | 0.0121 |
| 5 | 2.6 | 0.19 | 0.0361 |
| 6 | 2.0 | -0.41 | 0.1681 |
| 7 | 2.4 | -0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 8 | 2.7 | 0.29 | 0.0841 |
| 9 | 2.2 | -0.21 | 0.0441 |
| 10 | 2.5 | 0.09 | 0.0081 |

**Sum of squared deviations:**

$$
\sum (f_r - MF)^2 = 0.0081 + 0.0961 + 0.1521 + 0.0121 + 0.0361 + 0.1681 + 0.0001 + 0.0841 + 0.0441 + 0.0081 = 0.6090
$$

**Step 4 — Variance and Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{0.6090}{N - 1} = \frac{0.6090}{9} \approx 0.0677
$$

$$
SD = \sqrt{0.0677} \approx 0.2601
$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of Variation:**

$$
CoV = \frac{SD}{\vert MF \vert} \times 100\% = \frac{0.2601}{2.41} \times 100\% \approx 10.79\%
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
Since $CoV \approx 10.79\%$ (well below 15%), the algorithm exhibits **high stability and robustness** across the 10 independent runs, indicating that the tuned parameter set is reliable.

---

### ✦ Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between **offline** and **online** parameter tuning. Give one example of an algorithm for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Design of Experiments (DoE)** approach (specifically the $2^k$ full factorial design) for parameter tuning, with a worked example involving 3 parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — Offline vs Online Tuning (7 Marks)

| Feature | Offline Tuning | Online Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **When tuned** | Before the algorithm runs | During the algorithm run |
| **Adaptivity** | Static; parameters fixed | Dynamic; parameters evolve |
| **Computational overhead** | Separate tuning phase required | No extra tuning phase |
| **Algorithm modification needed** | No (algorithm used as black box) | Yes (algorithm must incorporate adaptation logic) |
| **Best suited for** | One-time deployment on a class of problems | Long-running, real-time, or unknown environments |
| **Example method** | F-Race, Taguchi, Irace | Self-Adaptive DE (SaDE), APSO |
| **Example algorithm** | Genetic Algorithm tuned offline via F-Race | PSO with linearly decreasing inertia weight $w$ |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Offline tuning is preferred when the problem class is known and computational budget permits a separate tuning phase. Online tuning is preferred for dynamic, expensive, or poorly understood problems.

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — $2^k$ Full Factorial Design (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **$2^k$ full factorial design** is a Design of Experiments (DoE) technique where each of $k$ parameters is tested at exactly two levels (low and high), giving $2^k$ total experimental runs.

**Levels Notation:**
- Low level: denoted by $-1$ (or "$-$")
- High level: denoted by $+1$ (or "$+$")

**Worked Example for $k = 3$ parameters (e.g., $p_c$, $p_m$, $N_{\text{pop}}$): (5 marks)**

| Run | $p_c$ | $p_m$ | $N_{\text{pop}}$ | Response (Mean Fitness) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-$ (0.6) | $-$ (0.01) | $-$ (50) | $f_1 = 12.3$ |
| 2 | $+$ (0.9) | $-$ (0.01) | $-$ (50) | $f_2 = 10.1$ |
| 3 | $-$ (0.6) | $+$ (0.05) | $-$ (50) | $f_3 = 11.8$ |
| 4 | $+$ (0.9) | $+$ (0.05) | $-$ (50) | $f_4 = 9.6$ |
| 5 | $-$ (0.6) | $-$ (0.01) | $+$ (100) | $f_5 = 10.5$ |
| 6 | $+$ (0.9) | $-$ (0.01) | $+$ (100) | $f_6 = 8.9$ |
| 7 | $-$ (0.6) | $+$ (0.05) | $+$ (100) | $f_7 = 9.7$ |
| 8 | $+$ (0.9) | $+$ (0.05) | $+$ (100) | $f_8 = 8.2$ |

**Main Effect of $p_c$ (1 mark):**

$$
E_{p_c} = \frac{(f_2 + f_4 + f_6 + f_8) - (f_1 + f_3 + f_5 + f_7)}{4}
$$

$$
E_{p_c} = \frac{(10.1 + 9.6 + 8.9 + 8.2) - (12.3 + 11.8 + 10.5 + 9.7)}{4}
$$

$$
E_{p_c} = \frac{36.8 - 44.3}{4} = \frac{-7.5}{4} = -1.875
$$

Since the effect is **negative and large**, increasing $p_c$ from 0.6 to 0.9 **improves** (decreases) the fitness value — so $p_c$ should be set high.

**Selection (1 mark):** The optimal configuration from this $2^3$ design is **Run 8** with the lowest mean fitness $f_8 = 8.2$, which sets $p_c = 0.9$, $p_m = 0.05$, $N_{\text{pop}} = 100$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the family-wise error correction** when describing F-Race — always mention Friedman test + Holm correction.
> 2. **Confusing "tuning" with "configuration"** — tuning is the *process*; the result is a *configuration* $\theta^*$.
> 3. **Using the wrong denominator in SD** — KTU strictly expects $N-1$ (Bessel's correction) in algorithm performance analysis, not $N$.
> 4. **Not interpreting the metric** — computing SD without commenting on "robustness" loses the application mark.
> 5. **Mixing up Success Rate and Speed of Convergence** — SR is a count-based metric; Speed is a ratio of function evaluations.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Parameter tuning = finding optimal values of control parameters; Performance analysis = measuring algorithm effectiveness.
- **Two Paradigms:** Offline (F-Race, Taguchi, Irace, DoE) vs Online (Self-Adaptive DE, APSO).
- **Structural Parameters** are not typically tuned — they are dictated by the problem.
- **Control Parameters** (e.g., $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$ in PSO; $p_c$, $p_m$ in GA) are the **primary targets** of tuning.
- **Key Metrics to Memorize:** BF, MF, SD, SR, AET, Speed of Convergence, CoV, MAE.
- **SD uses Bessel's correction** — divide by $N-1$, not $N$.
- **F-Race** uses non-parametric tests (Friedman / Wilcoxon + Holm correction) to eliminate inferior configurations.
- **DoE $2^k$ design** gives $2^k$ runs; main effects are computed as the average difference between high and low level responses.
- **Robustness** is indicated by **low SD** or **low CoV** (typically $< 15\%$).
- **Statistical Significance** is mandatory — never claim "better" without Wilcoxon or Friedman test evidence.
- **Convergence Threshold $\epsilon$** — a standard value is $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ depending on problem precision.
- **KTU Module 1 mapping:** This topic is **CO1** — "Understand the design principles of metaheuristic algorithms."
- **Common question pattern:** "Compute MF, SD, and interpret the result for $N$ runs of an algorithm on benchmark $B$." — practice this template.
- **Always end your answer** with an "Interpretation" sentence — it is a guaranteed 1-mark differentiator.
- **Avoid** the phrase "algorithm is good because of low fitness" — replace with "algorithm is **robust** because $SD < 0.05 \times MF$."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Search - Neighbor Selection and Escaping from Local Optima
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Local Search - Neighbor Selection and Escaping from Local Optima

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Local Search — Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 syllabus of **Metaheuristic Optimization (PEITT632)**, *Local Search* (LS) is defined as an **iterative trajectory-based improvement procedure** that starts from an initial candidate solution $s_0 \in S$ and moves, at each step, to a neighboring solution $s' \in N(s)$ whose objective value is *at least as good* as the current one. The process terminates when the current solution has no strictly improving neighbor — i.e., a **local optimum** has been reached.

Formally, a generic local search algorithm can be represented as the tuple:

$$
\mathcal{LS} = \langle S, N, f, \text{select}, \text{accept} \rangle
$$

where $S$ is the discrete/continuous search space, $N : S \to 2^S$ is the **neighborhood function**, $f : S \to \mathbb{R}$ is the objective function, **select** is a *neighbor selection rule*, and **accept** is an *acceptance criterion* governing the transition from $s$ to $s'$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2):**  
> *Local Search* is a fundamental building block of every metaheuristic. Understanding **how to choose a neighbor** and **how to escape from a local optimum** is the single most examinable concept of this module.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blindfolded Hiker"

Imagine a hiker dropped on a foggy mountain range with one rule: *take a step only if the ground beneath the next foothold is at least as low as the current one.*

- Each **footstep** corresponds to a **move to a neighbor**.
- **Choosing which neighbor to test first** is the *neighbor selection strategy* (best, first, random).
- The hiker will eventually settle inside a **valley** — even if it is *not* the deepest valley on the entire range. That valley is the **local optimum**.
- The hiker has *no idea* whether a deeper valley (the **global optimum**) exists beyond the fog. **Escaping local optima** is therefore the central challenge of metaheuristics.

## 1.3 Neighbor — The Building Block of Every Local Search Move

A **neighbor** of a solution $s$ is any solution $s' \in S$ that can be reached by applying a single, atomic perturbation (a *move operator*) to $s$. The set of all such neighbors is the **neighborhood** $N(s)$.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Neighborhood Size $|N(s)|$ determines the cost–diversity trade-off:**  
> - **Small neighborhood** → cheap to evaluate, but easily trapped in shallow local optima.  
> - **Large neighborhood** → richer exploration, but expensive evaluation cost.

## 1.4 Local Optimum — The "Stuck" State

A solution $\hat{s}$ is a **local optimum** with respect to neighborhood $N$ and objective $f$ if:

$$
\forall s' \in N(\hat{s}) : f(s') \geq f(\hat{s})
$$

That is, **no neighbor offers a strict improvement**. The search has converged to a *plateau top* from the perspective of $N(\cdot)$.

## 1.5 Why We Must Escape — The Fundamental Weakness

Because a local optimum depends *only* on $N$ and $f$, two structurally different neighborhoods can yield two different local optima for the *same* problem. **No local search algorithm is universally better than another** unless it can systematically leave these basins — the so-called **basins of attraction**.

> [!WARNING]  
> Pure hill-climbing with strict improvement acceptance **cannot escape** any local optimum. The rest of this section is dedicated to *metaheuristic mechanisms* that break this strictness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Local Search Loop — Generalized Pseudocode Logic

Every local search procedure (and every metaheuristic built on top of it) follows the same five-step logic:

1. **Initialize** — generate or construct an initial solution $s_0 \in S$.
2. **Generate neighbors** — apply the move operator(s) to obtain $N(s)$.
3. **Select** — pick a candidate $s' \in N(s)$ according to the selection rule.
4. **Accept/Reject** — apply the acceptance criterion $accept(s, s')$.
5. **Loop or Stop** — repeat from step 2; stop on termination condition (local optimum, time budget, or quality threshold).

## 2.2 Neighbor Selection Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Strategy | Selection Rule | Computational Cost | Convergence Speed | Risk |
|---|----------|----------------|--------------------|-------------------|------|
| 1 | **Best Improvement** | $\;s' = \arg\min_{x \in N(s)} f(x)\;$ | Highest (full scan) | Fastest | Misses trajectory diversity |
| 2 | **First Improvement** | first $s' \in N(s)$ with $f(s') < f(s)$ | Lower (partial scan) | Slower per step | Ties broken by traversal order |
| 3 | **Random Selection** | sample $s' \in N(s)$ uniformly | Lowest | Unpredictable | Avoidable bias |
| 4 | **Random + Accept** | sample $s' \in N(s)$; accept with prob. $p$ | Lowest | Controlled by $p$ | Used in SA / Threshold |
| 5 | **Sequential Variable Neighborhood** | cycle $N_1, N_2, \dots, N_k$ | Moderate | Adaptive | Used in VNS |

## 2.3 Acceptance Criteria — The Heart of Metaheuristic Differentiation

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **acceptance criterion** is what separates a pure *local search* from a *metaheuristic*. By allowing **occasional worsening moves**, a metaheuristic can leave a basin of attraction.

| Acceptance Mechanism | Worsening Move Allowed? | Probability of Acceptance | Governing Parameter | Tunes |
|----------------------|-------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|-------|
| **Strict Improvement (Hill-Climb)** | No | $0$ | — | — |
| **Random Restart** | Yes (jump) | — | restart frequency $r$ | Diversification |
| **Simulated Annealing (SA)** | Yes | $e^{-\Delta f / T}$ | temperature $T$ | Exploration / Exploitation |
| **Threshold Accepting** | Yes | $1$ if $\Delta f \leq \tau$ | threshold $\tau$ | Aggressiveness |
| **Great Deluge** | Yes | $1$ if $f \leq L$ | water level $L$ | Quality target |
| **Tabu Search** | Yes (tabu) | Best non-tabu | tenure $t$ | Memory size |
| **Iterated Local Search (ILS)** | Yes (kick) | Deterministic kick | kick strength $k$ | Perturbation |

## 2.4 Escaping Local Optima — The Five Canonical Strategies

### Strategy A — **Random Restart**
- Discard the current local optimum $s^*$ and re-seed from a fresh $s_0$.
- Effectiveness scales with **runtime budget** and **number of restarts**.
- Weakness: throws away structural information learned about the landscape.

### Strategy B — **Simulated Annealing (SA) — Probabilistic Acceptance**
- Inspired by metallurgical annealing.
- Accepts a worsening move $s \to s'$ with probability:
  $$
  P(\text{accept}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right), \quad \Delta f = f(s') - f(s) > 0
  $$
- The **temperature schedule** $T(k)$ decreases over iterations $k$ (e.g., geometric cooling $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $\alpha \in (0,1)$).

### Strategy C — **Tabu Search (TS) — Memory-Based Escape**
- Stores recently visited solutions (or move attributes) in a **tabu list** $TL$.
- Forbids revisiting them for a **tenure** of $t$ iterations, allowing *worsening* moves whenever all improving moves are tabu.
- The **aspiration criterion** overrides tabu status if a tabu move yields the **best-ever** solution found so far.

### Strategy D — **Iterated Local Search (ILS)**
- Three components: $s_0$ (initial), **LocalSearch**$(\cdot)$ (an inner LS), **Perturbation** $(\cdot)$ (a "kick" of strength $k$).
- The cycle: $s^* \to \text{perturb}(s^*) \to \text{LocalSearch}(\cdot) \to s^{*\text{new}}$.
- Acceptance of $s^{*\text{new}}$ over $s^*$ may be strict or based on a **comparison-based criterion** (e.g., BLF — Better than Last Found).

### Strategy E — **Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS)**
- Defines a sequence of neighborhoods $N_1, N_2, \dots, N_{k_{\max}}$.
- **VND (Descent)**: cycle through neighborhoods until no improvement.
- **VNS (General)**: shake (random in $N_k$) + LocalSearch + move/neighborhood-change rules.

## 2.5 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Table)

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Parameter | Notes |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|-------|
| **Neighborhood** | $N(s) = \{ s' \in S : \text{dist}(s,s') \leq 1 \}$ | move operator $\mu$ | Defines the *step size* |
| **Local Optimum** | $\forall s' \in N(s) : f(s') \geq f(s)$ | — | Depends on $N$ and $f$ |
| **Best-Improvement** | $s' = \arg\min_{x \in N(s)} f(x)$ | — | Greedy, may need full scan |
| **First-Improvement** | $s'$ first $\in N(s) : f(s') < f(s)$ | traversal order $O(N)$ | Often faster in practice |
| **SA Acceptance** | $P = \exp\!\left(-\Delta f / T\right)$ | temperature $T$ | $\Delta f > 0$ for worsening |
| **SA Cooling (Geometric)** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | $\alpha \in (0,1)$ | Common $\alpha \approx 0.95$ |
| **SA Cooling (Lundy–Mees)** | $T_{k+1} = T_k / (1 + \beta T_k)$ | $\beta > 0$ | Slower cooling |
| **Threshold Accepting** | accept iff $\Delta f \leq \tau$ | threshold $\tau$ | Deterministic cousin of SA |
| **Great Deluge** | accept iff $f(s') \leq L$ | water level $L$ | $L$ rises each iteration |
| **Tabu Tenure** | forbid $s'$ for $t$ iterations | $t \in \mathbb{N}$ | Short $t$ = flexibility |
| **ILS Kick Strength** | $k$ distinct random moves | $k$ | $k$ must exceed basin width |
| **VNS Neighborhoods** | $N_1, N_2, \dots, N_{k_{\max}}$ | $k_{\max}$ | Ordered by size |
| **Restart Budget** | $R$ independent restarts | $R$ | Probability of finding $s^*$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Utility:** Local search + metaheuristic escape mechanisms are deployed in real-world systems including **VLSI floor-planning**, **vehicle routing (UPS ORION)**, **airline crew scheduling**, **protein folding (AlphaFold’s energy minimizer)**, and **5G network slicing**.

## 2.6 Convergence Considerations (Exam-Ready Insight)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Under strict-improvement acceptance, local search **must terminate** because $f$ is bounded below and each accepted move strictly decreases $f$. Under metaheuristic acceptance (SA, TS, ILS), termination is governed by a **time budget** or **iteration cap** — these algorithms are **stochastic and may not converge in finite steps**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation — Why Best-Improvement Always Reaches a Local Optimum

**Claim:** *A local search using the best-improvement rule terminates in a finite number of iterations at a local optimum.*

**Proof (Finite Termination):**

Let $S$ be a finite search space of size $\vert S \vert$, and let $f : S \to \mathbb{R}$. Define the strictly improving move set at step $k$:

$$
M_k = \{ s' \in N(s_k) : f(s') < f(s_k) \}
$$

If $M_k = \emptyset$, the algorithm terminates at $s_k$, which is a local optimum by definition.

Otherwise, the best-improvement rule selects:

$$
s_{k+1} = \arg\min_{s' \in M_k} f(s')
$$

By construction, $f(s_{k+1}) < f(s_k)$. Hence the sequence $\{f(s_k)\}$ is **strictly decreasing**.

Because $S$ is finite, the sequence of visited solutions $\{s_0, s_1, \dots\}$ cannot revisit any solution (otherwise a cycle would produce $f(s_i) = f(s_j)$, contradicting strict decrease). The maximum length of the sequence is therefore bounded by $\vert S \vert - 1$:

$$
\text{iterations} \;\leq\; \vert S \vert - 1
$$

The algorithm must terminate. At termination $M_K = \emptyset$, so $s_K$ is a local optimum. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]  
> This is a classic **finite-convergence theorem** — the result students are expected to reproduce verbatim in KTU 14-mark derivations.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Expected Number of Accepted Moves in Simulated Annealing

**Step 1.** Consider a single transition from $s$ to a candidate $s' \in N(s)$ with $\Delta f = f(s') - f(s) > 0$.

**Step 2.** Under the Metropolis criterion, the probability of *accepting* this worsening move is:

$$
P_{\text{acc}}(T) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right)
$$

**Step 3.** Suppose $N(s)$ contains $n$ neighbors, of which $g$ are *improving* (always accepted) and $n - g$ are *worsening*. The expected number of accepted moves per iteration is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[A] = g + \sum_{i=1}^{n-g} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f_i}{T}\right)
$$

**Step 4.** Take the limit as $T \to \infty$ (pure exploration):

$$
\lim_{T \to \infty} \mathbb{E}[A] = g + (n - g) = n
$$

i.e., *all* moves are accepted — the algorithm performs a **random walk** on the search space.

**Step 5.** Take the limit as $T \to 0$ (pure exploitation):

$$
\lim_{T \to 0} \mathbb{E}[A] = g
$$

i.e., only *improving* moves survive — the algorithm reduces to **strict hill-climbing**.

**Step 6.** The **expected acceptance ratio** at temperature $T$:

$$
\rho(T) = \frac{\mathbb{E}[A]}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\!\left[\,g + \sum_{i=1}^{n-g} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f_i}{T}\right)\right]
$$

is a **monotonically increasing** function of $T$ on $(0, \infty)$, bounded by $\rho(0) = g/n$ and $\rho(\infty) = 1$. This monotonicity justifies the **cooling schedule** as a smooth knob between pure exploration and pure exploitation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Score-Card (Exam-Ready)

| Mechanism | Exploration Power | Exploitation Power | Memory | Parameters | Best Suited For |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|--------|------------|-----------------|
| **Hill-Climb** | None | Maximum | None | 0 | Convex / unimodal landscapes |
| **Random Restart HC** | Low (proportional to $R$) | Maximum | None | 1 ($R$) | Cheap, easy to implement |
| **Simulated Annealing** | Tunable | Tunable | None | 2 ($T_0$, $\alpha$) | Continuous + combinatorial |
| **Threshold Accepting** | Tunable | Tunable | None | 1 ($\tau$) | Industrial scheduling |
| **Great Deluge** | Tunable | Tunable | None | 1 ($L_0$, $\Delta L$) | Constraint satisfaction |
| **Tabu Search** | Strong | Strong | $TL$ | 2 ($t$, $|TL|$) | Routing, scheduling |
| **Iterated LS** | Strong | Maximum | $s^*$ | 1 ($k$) | TSP, VRP |
| **VNS / VND** | Strong | Strong | $k$ | 1 ($k_{\max}$) | p-median, graph problems |

## 3.4 Worked Example — 1D Numerical Landscape (Conceptual Walk-through)

Consider the objective $f(x) = (x - 4)^2 (x + 2)^2 + 1$ on $x \in [-5, 5]$, discretized at integer points. Use a **1-step neighborhood** $N(x) = \{x-1, x+1\}$.

- **Start** at $x_0 = 0$. Compute $f(0) = 16 \cdot 4 + 1 = 65$.
- **Step 1:** $f(-1) = 25 \cdot 9 + 1 = 226$ (worse), $f(1) = 9 \cdot 9 + 1 = 82$ (better). Best-improvement selects $x_1 = 1$.
- **Step 2:** $f(0) = 65$ (better than $82$), $f(2) = 4 \cdot 16 + 1 = 65$ (better than $82$). Best-improvement: both, choose the lower — $f(0) = f(2) = 65$, ties broken arbitrarily; suppose $x_2 = 2$.
- **Step 3:** $f(1) = 82$ (worse), $f(3) = 1 \cdot 25 + 1 = 26$ (better). $x_3 = 3$.
- **Step 4:** $f(2) = 65$ (worse), $f(4) = 0$ (better — global). $x_4 = 4$.
- **Stop** at $x = 4$ — incidentally the **global optimum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> If the start had been $x_0 = -3$, hill-climb would settle at $x = -2$ (a local minimum with $f(-2) = 1$). **Only a metaheuristic with escape mechanism could leave this basin.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Local Search Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize s0 in S] --> B[Compute f of s0]
    B --> C[Generate N of s]
    C --> D{Apply Neighbor Selection Rule}
    D -->|Best Improvement| E1[Scan all neighbors]
    D -->|First Improvement| E2[Scan until first better]
    D -->|Random Selection| E3[Sample uniformly]
    E1 --> F[Apply Acceptance Criterion]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Accepted?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Update s to s prime]
    G -->|No| I[Keep s unchanged]
    H --> J{Stop Condition Met?}
    I --> J
    J -->|No| C
    J -->|Yes| K[Return Local Optimum s star]
```

## 4.2 Local Optima Basins & Escape Trajectories

```mermaid
graph LR
    startA([Start A]) --> basin1[Basin 1 - Local Optimum]
    startB([Start B]) --> basin2[Basin 2 - Local Optimum]
    startC([Start C]) --> basin3[Basin 3 - Global Optimum]
    basin1 -. Hill-Climb Stuck .-> lo1((LO1))
    basin2 -. Hill-Climb Stuck .-> lo2((LO2))
    basin3 --> go((Global Optimum))
    lo1 -. SA Worsening Move .-> go
    lo2 -. Tabu Aspiration .-> go
    lo1 -. ILS Kick .-> go
```

## 4.3 Simulated Annealing — Acceptance Probability vs Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Candidate s prime] --> B{Improving Move?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Accept s prime]
    B -->|No| D[Compute delta f]
    D --> E[Generate r in 0,1]
    E --> F{r less than exp of -delta f over T}
    F -->|Yes| C
    F -->|No| G[Reject s prime]
    C --> H[Update T with cooling schedule]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Iterations less than max?}
    I -->|Yes| A
    I -->|No| J[Return best s]
```

## 4.4 Iterated Local Search — Kick + Local Search Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Generate Initial s0] --> B[Apply LocalSearch]
    B --> C[Obtain s star local optimum]
    C --> D[Perturbation Kick]
    D --> E[New starting point s prime]
    E --> F[Apply LocalSearch]
    F --> G[New local optimum s star new]
    G --> H{Acceptance Criterion}
    H -->|Better| C
    H -->|Equal| C
    H -->|Worse| I{Probabilistic Accept?}
    I -->|Yes| C
    I -->|No| C
    H -.Terminate.-> J[Return best s star]
    I -.Terminate.-> J
```

## 4.5 Variable Neighborhood Search — Multi-Scale Escape

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: s] --> B[Set k = 1]
    B --> C[Shake: pick random in N sub k of s]
    C --> D[Apply LocalSearch to s prime]
    D --> E{f of s prime new less than f of s}
    E -->|Yes| F[Move: s = s prime new, k = 1]
    E -->|No| G{k equals k max}
    G -->|No| H[k = k + 1]
    G -->|Yes| I[Stop and Return s]
    H --> C
    F --> C
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Escape Strategies Compose

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SL[Search Loop Core]
        A1[Initialization Module]
        A2[Neighborhood Generator]
        A3[Selection Module]
        A4[Acceptance Module]
    end
    subgraph EM[Escape Mechanism Layer]
        B1[Random Restart Controller]
        B2[SA Temperature Scheduler]
        B3[Tabu List Manager]
        B4[ILS Perturbation Engine]
        B5[VNS Neighborhood Sequencer]
    end
    subgraph MEM[Memory Layer]
        C1[Best Solution Buffer]
        C2[Tabu List TL]
        C3[Restart History]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A2
    A4 -. Triggers .-> B1
    A4 -. Triggers .-> B2
    A4 -. Triggers .-> B3
    A4 -. Triggers .-> B4
    A4 -. Triggers .-> B5
    B1 --> C3
    B2 -. Reads .-> A4
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> A1
    B5 --> A2
    A4 --> C1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 2]**

### Q1. Define *neighborhood structure* in local search. Why does the choice of neighborhood affect the quality of the local optimum reached?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A neighborhood structure $N$ assigns to every solution $s \in S$ a set $N(s) \subseteq S$ of solutions reachable by applying a single move operator. Formally, $N : S \to 2^S$.

The choice of $N$ directly determines the **local optimum** that local search converges to, because the definition $\forall s' \in N(s) : f(s') \geq f(s)$ is evaluated *with respect to $N$*. A **larger neighborhood** explores more of $S$ in one step, typically producing *deeper* local optima — but at higher per-iteration cost. A **smaller neighborhood** is cheaper but yields shallow optima. Thus, $N$ is a *diversity–cost trade-off* knob.

> **Marking scheme:** Definition 1, Explanation 1, Trade-off 1.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest, Module 2]**

### Q2. Differentiate between **best-improvement** and **first-improvement** neighbor selection strategies. Which one is generally preferred in practice and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Best-Improvement | First-Improvement |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Rule** | scan *all* of $N(s)$, pick the $s'$ with the lowest $f$ | scan in fixed order, pick the *first* $s'$ with $f(s') < f(s)$ |
| **Cost per step** | $O(\vert N(s) \vert)$ | $\leq O(\vert N(s) \vert)$ |
| **Determinism** | Fully deterministic | Depends on traversal order |
| **Outcome** | Stronger, deeper local optimum per step | Faster, more diverse trajectory |

In practice, **first-improvement** is often preferred because it allows the search to leave a basin *earlier* and visit a more diverse set of solutions, which helps in escaping plateaus and shallow local optima.

> **Marking scheme:** Tabular comparison 2, Practical preference + reason 1.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define **local search** and **local optimum** rigorously. With the aid of a suitable diagram, explain why a pure hill-climbing algorithm can become *trapped* at a local optimum. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **Simulated Annealing (SA)** algorithm in detail. Clearly state its acceptance criterion, cooling schedule, and justify why SA is able to escape local optima. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Definition (2 marks).** Local search is an iterative improvement procedure that, given a current solution $s \in S$, explores its neighborhood $N(s)$ and moves to a *better* neighbor until no such neighbor exists. Formally, the algorithm updates:

$$
s_{k+1} \in \{ s' \in N(s_k) : f(s') < f(s_k) \}
$$

and terminates when this set is empty.

**Local Optimum (2 marks).** A solution $s^*$ is a *local optimum* w.r.t. $N$ and $f$ if:

$$
\forall s' \in N(s^*) : f(s') \geq f(s^*)
$$

**Trapping Phenomenon (3 marks).** Pure hill-climbing permits *only* strictly improving moves. Therefore, on the moment the algorithm reaches a basin floor whose surrounding $N$ all contain equal-or-worse solutions, the improving-move set becomes empty, and the algorithm halts. It cannot cross a *ridge* or ascend out of a *valley*, even if a deeper valley lies on the other side.

**Diagram (in words).** A two-dimensional landscape with three basins — a shallow one and a deep (global) one separated by a small ridge. The hill-climber, starting on the left slope, slides down to the shallow basin and stops at $LO_1$, never reaching the global optimum at $GO$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Algorithm Skeleton (3 marks).** SA operates as follows:

1. Initialize $s_0 \in S$, set initial temperature $T_0 > 0$.
2. Repeat until termination:
   1. Generate $s' \in N(s)$ uniformly at random.
   2. Compute $\Delta f = f(s') - f(s)$.
   3. If $\Delta f \leq 0$, accept $s'$.
   4. Else, accept $s'$ with probability $e^{-\Delta f / T}$.
   5. Update $T \leftarrow \text{Cool}(T)$.

**Acceptance Criterion (2 marks).** The Metropolis criterion allows *occasional uphill moves*, controlled by the temperature $T$. When $T$ is high, almost all moves are accepted (exploration); when $T$ is low, only improving moves survive (exploitation).

**Cooling Schedule (1 mark).** A standard schedule is the **geometric cooling**:

$$
T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad \alpha \in (0,1)
$$

Typical values: $\alpha = 0.95$ to $0.99$, $T_0$ chosen so the initial acceptance ratio is ~0.8.

**Justification — Escape from Local Optima (1 mark).** Because SA accepts *worsening* moves with positive probability when $T > 0$, the algorithm can climb *out* of shallow basins. As $T$ decreases, the search *anneals* into the deepest basin reachable in the allotted time.

---

> **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Tabu Search (TS)** algorithm. Discuss the role of the **tabu list**, **tabu tenure**, and the **aspiration criterion** in enabling escape from local optima. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe **Iterated Local Search (ILS)** and **Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS)**. Compare their exploration–exploitation balance in a neat table. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Tabu Search Overview (2 marks).** Tabu Search is a memory-based metaheuristic introduced by **Glover (1986)** that uses *short-term memory* to forbid revisiting recently explored regions, allowing the algorithm to perform *worsening* moves deliberately to escape local optima.

**Tabu List & Tenure (3 marks).** A **tabu list** $TL$ stores either full solutions or *attributes* of recent moves (e.g., the edge flipped in a TSP move). Each entry is **forbidden** for a fixed number of iterations — the **tabu tenure** $t$. Short tenure → flexible; long tenure → restrictive. The list is implemented as a circular buffer of capacity $|TL|$.

**Aspiration Criterion (2 marks).** The aspiration criterion *overrides* the tabu status of a move if that move produces a solution **better than the best-known** $s^{**}$. This ensures that exceptionally good moves are never lost to history.

**Mechanism of Escape (2 marks).** Once the search reaches a local optimum $s^*$ with no improving neighbor in $N(s^*)$, TS selects the *least-bad* (or even a worsening) neighbor from $N(s^*) \setminus TL$ and moves there. Because $TL$ rotates entries, the search is gently pushed into *unvisited* regions of the landscape, breaking the trap of pure hill-climbing.

> **Marking scheme:** Overview 2, List/Tenure 3, Aspiration 2, Mechanism 2.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Iterated Local Search (3 marks).** ILS is a *meta-metaheuristic* consisting of three components:

- **Initial Solution** $s_0$,
- **Local Search** subroutine $\text{LS}(\cdot)$,
- **Perturbation** operator $\text{Perturb}(s, k)$ applying $k$ random moves to $s$.

The cycle is:

$$
s^* \xleftarrow{\text{accept}} \text{LS}(\text{Perturb}(s^*))
$$

The acceptance criterion may be *better-only*, *equal-accept*, or stochastic.

**Variable Neighborhood Search (3 marks).** VNS assumes that **different neighborhoods yield different local optima**, so systematically varying $N$ explores the space effectively. The VNS framework uses:

- a sequence of neighborhoods $N_1, N_2, \dots, N_{k_{\max}}$,
- **Shake** (random move in $N_k$),
- **Local Search** $\text{LS}(\cdot)$,
- **Move** (accept if improved) and **Neighborhood Change** (reset $k = 1$ on success, else $k \leftarrow k+1$).

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | ILS | VNS |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Diversification mechanism | Single **kick** operator | Sequence of **neighborhoods** |
| Parameters | Kick strength $k$ | $k_{\max}$, neighborhood family |
| Memory of past structure | $s^*$ (best so far) | $s^*$ (best so far) |
| Strength | Simplicity, low parameter count | Systematic coverage of $S$ |
| Weakness | May cycle if $k$ is too small | Cost grows with $k_{\max}$ |

> **Marking scheme:** ILS description 3, VNS description 3, Comparison 1.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**  
> 1. **Conflating the *selection* rule with the *acceptance* rule** — these are *two different stages*; the KTU rubric awards separate marks for each.  
> 2. **Omitting the temperature schedule** in SA answers — full 2 marks are reserved for the cooling schedule alone.  
> 3. **Writing “Tabu Search is the same as Simulated Annealing”** — TS uses *deterministic memory*; SA uses *probabilistic acceptance*. They are fundamentally different.  
> 4. **Forgetting the aspiration criterion** in TS — most students omit it; it is worth 1–2 marks consistently.  
> 5. **Stating “local search = hill climbing”** — local search is the *umbrella term*; hill climbing is a *strict* instance. Be precise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2:**

- ☐ **Local Search** is iterative improvement on a neighborhood $N(s)$.
- ☐ A **local optimum** is *defined* by $N$ and $f$: $\forall s' \in N(s) : f(s') \geq f(s)$.
- ☐ **Neighbor selection** strategies: best, first, random, sequential.
- ☐ **Acceptance** is what makes a metaheuristic: SA uses $e^{-\Delta f / T}$, TS uses tabu list + aspiration, ILS uses perturbation + BLF, VNS uses neighborhood cycling.
- ☐ **SA cooling schedules**: geometric $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, logarithmic (asymptotic convergence), Lundy–Mees.
- ☐ **Tabu Search** uses a tenure $t$, tabu list $TL$, and **aspiration criterion** to override tabu.
- ☐ **ILS** = LocalSearch ∘ Perturbation ∘ Acceptance, applied cyclically.
- ☐ **VNS** = VND (descent) + VNS (general) — assumes *systematic* neighborhood variation.
- ☐ **Convergence theorem**: best-improvement local search terminates in $\leq \vert S \vert - 1$ iterations.
- ☐ **Pure hill-climbing cannot escape** any local optimum — a metaheuristic acceptance rule is *required*.
- ☐ **Engineering applications**: VLSI, VRP (vehicle routing), scheduling, protein structure, antenna design, ML hyper-parameter tuning.
- ☐ Remember to **state explicitly which parameter controls the exploration–exploitation trade-off** in every answer.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Local Search: Neighbor Selection and Escaping from Local Optima.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulated Annealing - Move Acceptance and Cooling Schedule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simulated Annealing - Move Acceptance and Cooling Schedule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Simulated Annealing (SA)

**Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a stochastic, trajectory-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the physical process of **annealing in metallurgy**, where a metal is heated to a high temperature and then slowly cooled to reduce its internal defects and reach a low-energy crystalline state.

In the optimization context, SA searches the solution space by accepting not only **improving moves** (which lower the objective function value) but also **non-improving moves** (which worsen it) with a probability that depends on:
- The magnitude of the deterioration ($\Delta E$ or $\Delta f$), and
- A global control parameter called the **Temperature ($T$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> "Simulated Annealing is a probabilistic technique that approximates the global optimum of a given objective function. It is especially effective for large combinatorial optimization problems where the search space is discrete."

### 1.2 The Two Critical Sub-Components

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly focuses on two pillars of the SA algorithm:

**(a) Move Acceptance Criterion**
The rule that decides whether a candidate solution $s_{new}$ replaces the current solution $s_{current}$. This is governed by the **Metropolis Criterion**, derived from statistical mechanics.

**(b) Cooling Schedule**
The function $T(t)$ that governs how the temperature parameter decreases over iterations. It determines the **exploration vs. exploitation** balance of the algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping (Physics → Optimization):**
> - **Energy ($E$)** ↔ Objective Function Cost $f(s)$
> - **Temperature ($T$)** ↔ Exploration Probability / Control Parameter
> - **Ground State** ↔ Global Optimum
> - **Molecular Configuration** ↔ Candidate Solution

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Mountain Climber at Dusk

Imagine a **tired mountaineer** trapped in a foggy mountain range at dusk, trying to reach the lowest valley (the global minimum). He can only see his immediate surroundings.

- **Naïve Greedy (Hill Descent):** He *always* steps downhill. He will quickly get stuck in the nearest pit (a **local minimum**), mistaking it for the valley.
- **Simulated Annealing:** At the start (high $T$), he is willing to take **risky uphill steps** with high probability, allowing him to escape local pits. As night falls ($T$ decreases), he becomes more cautious and mostly accepts only downhill steps, refining his path into the valley.

The "daringness" to climb uphill is the **move acceptance rule**, and the gradual onset of night is the **cooling schedule**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** SA is one of the few metaheuristics with **asymptotic convergence proof** to the global optimum under a sufficiently slow logarithmic cooling schedule (Geman & Geman, 1984).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Philosophy: Why SA Works

SA operates on the principle of **Boltzmann distribution** from statistical mechanics. At thermal equilibrium, the probability of a system being in a state with energy $E$ is proportional to $e^{-E/k_B T}$.

The algorithm mimics this by:
1. Generating a neighboring solution $s_{new}$ from $s_{current}$.
2. Computing $\Delta f = f(s_{new}) - f(s_{current})$.
3. If $\Delta f \le 0$ (improvement): **Always accept**.
4. If $\Delta f > 0$ (deterioration): **Accept with probability** governed by the Metropolis criterion.

### 2.2 The Metropolis Criterion (Move Acceptance Rule)

The Metropolis Criterion is the **heart of move acceptance** in SA. For minimization problems:

$$P_{accept} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Delta f \le 0 \\ e^{-\Delta f / T} & \text{if } \Delta f > 0 \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $\Delta f = f(s_{new}) - f(s_{current})$ is the **cost increase** of the proposed move.
- $T > 0$ is the current **temperature** (control parameter).
- $e^{-\Delta f / T}$ is the **Boltzmann factor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Behavioral Properties of $P_{accept}$:**
> - As $T \to \infty$: $P_{accept} \to 1$ (pure random walk, all moves accepted).
> - As $T \to 0$: $P_{accept} \to 0$ for worsening moves (pure greedy descent).
> - For fixed $\Delta f > 0$: $P_{accept}$ is **monotonically increasing** in $T$.

### 2.3 The Cooling Schedule $T(t)$

The cooling schedule dictates how temperature evolves with iteration count $t$. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following canonical schedules:

#### (a) Geometric (Exponential) Cooling — Most Widely Used
$$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$$
where $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ is the **cooling coefficient**, typically $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$.

#### (b) Linear Cooling
$$T_k = T_0 - k \cdot \beta$$
where $\beta$ is a constant step size. Reaches zero in finite steps.

#### (c) Logarithmic Cooling — Theoretical Convergence
$$T_k = \frac{T_0}{\log(1 + k)}$$
Ensures asymptotic convergence to global optimum but is **prohibitively slow** in practice.

#### (d) Adaptive Cooling
Temperature updated based on observed acceptance ratio $\chi$:
$$T_{k+1} = T_k \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\ln(\chi_{target})}{\sigma \cdot N_{accept}}\right)^{-1}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Value** | **Unit / Range** |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|------------------|
| $f(s)$ | Objective function (cost) | Input-dependent | Problem-specific |
| $\Delta f$ | Cost change for a move | $f(s_{new}) - f(s_{current})$ | Same as $f$ |
| $T$ | Temperature (control param) | $T > 0$ | Decreases over time |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | Often absorbed into $T$ | Dimensionless (normalized) |
| $P_{accept}$ | Acceptance probability | $e^{-\Delta f / T}$ when $\Delta f > 0$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\alpha$ | Cooling rate (geometric) | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ | $\alpha \in (0.8, 0.99)$ |
| $T_0$ | Initial temperature | Accept $\sim$ 80% worsening moves | Problem-specific |
| $T_f$ | Final temperature | $T_f \approx 10^{-3}$ or $10^{-6}$ | Near zero |
| $L_k$ | Iterations per temperature | Epoch length | $\ge N$ (neighborhood size) |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of SA

- **VLSI Circuit Design:** Optimal placement and routing of components on chips.
- **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP):** Near-optimal tour generation for logistics.
- **Job-Shop Scheduling:** Production line optimization in manufacturing.
- **Image Processing:** Image segmentation, restoration, and feature selection.
- **Structural Engineering:** Truss optimization, minimum-weight design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Metropolis Acceptance Probability

#### Step 1: Start from the Boltzmann Distribution

In statistical mechanics, for a canonical ensemble at thermal equilibrium with temperature $T$, the probability of finding the system in a microstate with energy $E_i$ is:

$$P(E_i) = \frac{e^{-E_i / (k_B T)}}{Z(T)}$$

where $Z(T) = \sum_{j} e^{-E_j / (k_B T)}$ is the partition function (a normalization constant).

#### Step 2: Take the Ratio of Two States

For two states with energies $E_1$ and $E_2$ (assume $E_2 > E_1$), the ratio of their probabilities is:

$$\frac{P(E_2)}{P(E_1)} = \frac{e^{-E_2 / (k_B T)} / Z}{e^{-E_1 / (k_B T)} / Z}$$

#### Step 3: Cancel the Partition Function

The $Z$ cancels out exactly:

$$\frac{P(E_2)}{P(E_1)} = \frac{e^{-E_2 / (k_B T)}}{e^{-E_1 / (k_B T)}}$$

#### Step 4: Simplify the Exponentials

Using the law $e^a / e^b = e^{a-b}$:

$$\frac{P(E_2)}{P(E_1)} = e^{-(E_2 - E_1) / (k_B T)}$$

#### Step 5: Substitute Optimization Variables

Replace $E$ with cost $f(s)$ and absorb $k_B$ into $T$ (since $T$ is a free parameter in optimization, we can re-scale it):

$$P(E_2) = P(E_1) \cdot e^{-\Delta f / T}$$

where $\Delta f = f(s_2) - f(s_1) > 0$.

#### Step 6: Interpret as Acceptance Probability

By setting $P(E_1) = 1$ (we always accept the reference state), we obtain the **Metropolis acceptance rule**:

$$P_{accept} = e^{-\Delta f / T}$$

This is the **analytical foundation** for the move acceptance criterion in SA.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Metropolis Acceptance Computation

**Problem:** A minimization problem has $f(s_{current}) = 25$ and $f(s_{new}) = 32$. Current temperature $T = 7$. Determine whether $s_{new}$ is accepted.

**Step 1:** Compute the cost difference.

$$\Delta f = f(s_{new}) - f(s_{current}) = 32 - 25 = 7$$

**Step 2:** Check if it is an improving move.

Since $\Delta f = 7 > 0$, this is a **worsening move**. We cannot auto-accept.

**Step 3:** Apply the Metropolis criterion.

$$P_{accept} = e^{-\Delta f / T} = e^{-7 / 7} = e^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate numerically.

$$P_{accept} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

**Step 5:** Generate a uniform random number $r \sim U(0,1)$.

If $r = 0.4128$, since $r > P_{accept}$, **reject** the move.

If $r = 0.2215$, since $r \le P_{accept}$, **accept** the move.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show the comparison step explicitly. Examiners award marks for stating "since $r > P_{accept}$, reject the move" — not just the numerical evaluation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cooling Schedule Comparison

| **Property** | **Geometric** | **Linear** | **Logarithmic** |
|--------------|---------------|------------|-----------------|
| Formula | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ | $T_k = T_0 - k\beta$ | $T_k = T_0 / \ln(1+k)$ |
| Convergence Speed | Fast (practical) | Moderate | Very slow |
| Theoretical Guarantee | No finite-time guarantee | No finite-time guarantee | Asymptotic to global optimum |
| Parameter Sensitivity | Sensitive to $\alpha$ | Sensitive to $\beta$ | Low sensitivity |
| KTU Practical Use | **Most common** | Sometimes in teaching | Rarely (too slow) |
| Reaches $T = 0$? | Never (asymptotic) | Yes (in finite steps) | Never |

---

### 3.4 Determination of Initial Temperature $T_0$

A standard engineering heuristic is to choose $T_0$ such that the initial acceptance ratio $\chi_0 \approx 0.8$ (i.e., 80% of worsening moves accepted at the start).

Given a set of $N$ sample worsening moves with costs $\{\Delta f_1, \Delta f_2, \ldots, \Delta f_N\}$, $T_0$ is found by solving:

$$\chi_0 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \min\left(1, e^{-\Delta f_i / T_0}\right) = 0.8$$

A closed-form approximation uses the **average worsening cost** $\overline{\Delta f}$:

$$T_0 \approx \frac{-\overline{\Delta f}}{\ln(\chi_0)} = \frac{-\overline{\Delta f}}{\ln(0.8)} \approx 4.48 \cdot \overline{\Delta f}$$

---

### 3.5 Markov Chain Analysis: Equilibrium Condition

At each temperature $T_k$, the SA algorithm must run an inner loop long enough to reach **quasi-equilibrium**. The number of iterations per temperature $L_k$ must satisfy:

$$L_k \ge \frac{\ln(N_{states})}{\ln(1 - p_{escape})}$$

where $p_{escape}$ is the probability of escaping a local minimum at temperature $T_k$. A common simplification is $L_k = N$ (neighborhood size) for all $k$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Simulated Annealing Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Initialize T equal to T0] --> initA[Generate Initial Solution sCurrent]
    initA --> evalA[Compute f sCurrent]
    evalA --> loopA{Loop: k less than kMax}
    loopA --> innerLoop[Inner Loop: L iterations at current T]
    innerLoop --> perturbA[Generate sNew via Neighborhood Move]
    perturbA --> evalNew[Compute f sNew]
    evalNew --> deltaA[Compute Delta f = f sNew minus f sCurrent]
    deltaA --> checkDelta{Delta f less than or equal to 0?}
    checkDelta --> yesA[Accept sNew equal to sCurrent]
    checkDelta --> noA[Compute P accept = exp of minus Delta f over T]
    noA --> randA[Generate r uniform 0 1]
    randA --> checkR{r less than P accept?}
    checkR --> yesAccept[Accept sNew equal to sCurrent]
    checkR --> noReject[Reject: Keep sCurrent]
    yesA --> counterCheck{Inner loop done?}
    yesAccept --> counterCheck
    noReject --> counterCheck
    counterCheck --> noInner[Continue inner loop]
    noInner --> innerLoop
    counterCheck --> yesInner[Cooling Step: T = alpha times T]
    yesInner --> kInc[Increment k = k + 1]
    kInc --> loopA
    loopA --> noLoop[Stop: Return Best Solution]
```

### 4.2 Move Acceptance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Propose sNew from Neighborhood of sCurrent] --> compDelta[Compute Delta f = f sNew minus f sCurrent]
    compDelta --> checkSign{Is Delta f less than or equal to 0?}
    checkSign --> yesImprove[Move IMPROVES Cost]
    checkSign --> noWorsen[Move WORSENS Cost]
    yesImprove --> acceptImprove[ACCEPT sNew with Probability 1]
    noWorsen --> checkTemp{Temperature T}
    checkTemp --> highTemp[High T: Likely Accept]
    checkTemp --> lowTemp[Low T: Likely Reject]
    highTemp --> computeP[P = exp of minus Delta f over T]
    lowTemp --> computeP
    computeP --> drawR[Draw r from Uniform 0 1]
    drawR --> rCheck{r less than or equal to P?}
    rCheck --> yesWorsenAccept[ACCEPT sNew despite worsening]
    rCheck --> noWorsenReject[REJECT: Keep sCurrent]
```

### 4.3 Cooling Schedule Behavior Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX[Iteration k] --> scheduleComp[Cooling Schedule Behavior]
    scheduleComp --> geoCurve[Geometric: T_k = alpha to power k times T0]
    scheduleComp --> linCurve[Linear: T_k = T0 minus k times beta]
    scheduleComp --> logCurve[Logarithmic: T_k = T0 over ln of 1 plus k]
    geoCurve --> fastDecay[Fast initial decay then plateau]
    linCurve --> uniformDecay[Uniform straight line descent]
    logCurve --> slowDecay[Very slow decay: nearly flat]
    fastDecay --> practicalUse[Practical choice for KTU problems]
    uniformDecay --> teachingUse[Used in classroom examples]
    slowDecay --> theoreticalUse[Theoretical convergence proof only]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Components of SA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CoreSA[Simulated Annealing Engine]
        generator[Solution Generator] --> neighbor[Neighborhood Function]
        neighbor --> evaluator[Cost Evaluator f s]
        evaluator --> acceptor[Acceptance Module]
        acceptor --> scheduler[Cooling Scheduler]
        scheduler --> loopCtrl[Loop Controller]
        loopCtrl --> generator
    end
    inputBlock[Initial Solution s0] --> CoreSA
    CoreSA --> outputBlock[Best Solution sBest]
    acceptor --> metropolis[Metropolis Criterion e to power minus Delta f over T]
    scheduler --> geoCool[Geometric Cooling alpha T]
    scheduler --> altCool[Alternative Schedules]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** — 3 Marks

**"What is the Metropolis criterion in Simulated Annealing? Why is it necessary to accept worsening moves at higher temperatures?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **Metropolis criterion** is the probabilistic move-acceptance rule used in Simulated Annealing to decide whether to accept a candidate solution $s_{new}$. It is defined as:

$$P_{accept} = \begin{cases} 1, & \Delta f \le 0 \\ e^{-\Delta f / T}, & \Delta f > 0 \end{cases}$$

where $\Delta f = f(s_{new}) - f(s_{current})$ is the cost change and $T$ is the current temperature.

**Why accept worsening moves?**
At high $T$, $e^{-\Delta f / T} \to 1$, meaning nearly all moves are accepted. This enables the algorithm to **escape local minima** by traversing the search space freely. Without this stochastic acceptance, SA would degenerate into a greedy hill-descent algorithm and get trapped in the first local minimum encountered. Hence, accepting worsening moves at high $T$ is essential for **global exploration** of the solution landscape.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition with formula: 2 marks
- Justification of necessity: 1 mark

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** — 3 Marks

**"Compare geometric and logarithmic cooling schedules in Simulated Annealing with respect to convergence and practical use."**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Geometric Cooling** | **Logarithmic Cooling** |
|------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Formula | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $\alpha \in (0,1)$ | $T_k = T_0 / \ln(1+k)$ |
| Convergence | Fast; no theoretical guarantee | Asymptotically convergent to global optimum |
| Practical use | Widely used in engineering problems | Rarely used (computationally prohibitive) |
| Parameter | Sensitive to choice of $\alpha$ | Robust |

The **logarithmic schedule** provides theoretical assurance of convergence to the global optimum but is impractically slow. The **geometric schedule** is the de facto standard in industrial SA implementations due to its balance of speed and solution quality.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Any two valid comparisons with formulas: 3 marks

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** — 14 Marks

**"Explain the Simulated Annealing algorithm in detail. With suitable derivations, justify the Metropolis acceptance criterion using the Boltzmann distribution."**

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Describe the complete Simulated Annealing algorithm with all its components: initial solution, neighborhood, acceptance rule, and cooling schedule.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Initialization**
- Generate an initial feasible solution $s_0 \in S$ (solution space).
- Compute initial cost $f(s_0)$.
- Choose initial temperature $T_0 > 0$, final temperature $T_f$, cooling coefficient $\alpha$, and maximum iterations $k_{max}$.
- Set best solution $s_{best} = s_0$.

**Step 2: Outer Loop (Temperature Schedule)**
While $T > T_f$ and $k < k_{max}$:
- Execute inner loop of $L$ iterations at the current temperature.
- Update temperature: $T \leftarrow \alpha T$.

**Step 3: Inner Loop (Markov Chain)**
For $i = 1$ to $L$:
- Generate neighbor $s_{new} \in N(s_{current})$ using a neighborhood operator (e.g., bit-flip, swap, insertion).
- Compute $\Delta f = f(s_{new}) - f(s_{current})$.
- Apply Metropolis criterion to decide acceptance.

**Step 4: Update Best**
After each acceptance, if $f(s_{current}) < f(s_{best})$, update $s_{best} = s_{current}$.

**Step 5: Termination**
Return $s_{best}$ when $T \le T_f$ or $k \ge k_{max}$.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Algorithm steps with initialization: 4 marks
- Inner/outer loop structure: 2 marks
- Termination logic: 1 mark

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Derive the Metropolis acceptance probability from the Boltzmann distribution.**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a physical system at thermal equilibrium with temperature $T$. The probability of the system being in a state with energy $E_i$ is given by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$P(E_i) = \frac{e^{-E_i / (k_B T)}}{Z(T)}, \quad \text{where } Z(T) = \sum_{j} e^{-E_j / (k_B T)}$$

Now consider two states $A$ and $B$ with energies $E_A$ and $E_B$, with $E_B > E_A$. The ratio of probabilities is:

$$\frac{P(E_B)}{P(E_A)} = \frac{e^{-E_B / (k_B T)}}{e^{-E_A / (k_B T)}}$$

The partition function cancels, yielding:

$$\frac{P(E_B)}{P(E_A)} = e^{-(E_B - E_A) / (k_B T)} = e^{-\Delta E / (k_B T)}$$

Substituting $E \to f(s)$ (energy ↔ cost) and absorbing $k_B$ into $T$ (since $T$ is a free algorithmic parameter), we get:

$$P_{accept} = e^{-\Delta f / T}$$

where $\Delta f = f(s_{new}) - f(s_{current}) > 0$ is the cost increase.

**Interpretation:**
- When $\Delta f < 0$ (improvement), $P_{accept} = 1$, so we always accept.
- When $\Delta f > 0$ (worsening), acceptance decays exponentially with cost increase.

This is the **Metropolis criterion**, providing the theoretical foundation for SA's stochastic hill-climbing behavior.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Boltzmann distribution setup: 2 marks
- Ratio computation: 2 marks
- Substitution and final formula: 2 marks
- Physical interpretation: 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** — 14 Marks

**"A combinatorial optimization problem has a current solution cost $f(s_{current}) = 40$ and a neighboring solution cost $f(s_{new}) = 55$. The current temperature is $T = 10$.** **(a) Apply the Metropolis criterion to determine whether $s_{new}$ should be accepted. Repeat the analysis at $T = 2$.** **(b) Discuss the role of the cooling schedule in SA and explain why geometric cooling is preferred over linear cooling in practice."**

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Apply the Metropolis criterion at $T = 10$ and $T = 2$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the cost difference.**

$$\Delta f = f(s_{new}) - f(s_{current}) = 55 - 40 = 15$$

Since $\Delta f > 0$, this is a **worsening move**.

**Step 2: Case 1 — At $T = 10$**

Apply the Metropolis formula:

$$P_{accept}^{(T=10)} = e^{-\Delta f / T} = e^{-15 / 10} = e^{-1.5}$$

Evaluating numerically:

$$P_{accept}^{(T=10)} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

**Interpretation:** There is approximately a **22.31% probability** of accepting the worsening move at $T = 10$. Generate $r \sim U(0,1)$:
- If $r \le 0.2231$: **Accept** (escape local region).
- If $r > 0.2231$: **Reject**.

**Step 3: Case 2 — At $T = 2$**

$$P_{accept}^{(T=2)} = e^{-15 / 2} = e^{-7.5}$$

Evaluating numerically:

$$P_{accept}^{(T=2)} = e^{-7.5} \approx 0.000553$$

**Interpretation:** There is only a **0.055% probability** of accepting the worsening move at $T = 2$. This is nearly zero, meaning the algorithm behaves almost as a greedy descent at low temperatures.

**Step 4: Comparative Insight**

As $T$ decreased from 10 to 2, the acceptance probability dropped from **22.31% to 0.055%** — a reduction factor of approximately **404×**. This demonstrates the **temperature's controlling role** in exploration behavior.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct $\Delta f$: 1 mark
- Calculation at $T=10$: 2 marks
- Calculation at $T=2$: 2 marks
- Comparative interpretation: 2 marks

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Discuss the role of cooling schedule and justify the preference for geometric over linear cooling.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of the Cooling Schedule:**

The cooling schedule $T(t)$ controls the **exploration-exploitation trade-off** in SA. It governs:
- At **high $T$**: SA behaves like a random walk, exploring the entire search space and escaping local minima.
- At **low $T$**: SA behaves like a greedy local search, exploiting the best-known region to refine the solution.

The cooling schedule must be **monotonically non-increasing** and approach zero (or a sufficiently small value) to guarantee eventual convergence.

**Geometric Cooling: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $\alpha \in (0.8, 0.99)$**

**Advantages:**
- **Slow decay at low temperatures**: Since $T_{k+1}/T_k = \alpha$ is constant, the schedule maintains a meaningful exploration probability even after many iterations. For example, with $\alpha = 0.95$, it takes $\approx 90$ iterations to halve $T$.
- **Tunable**: The parameter $\alpha$ provides a single, intuitive knob to balance speed vs. quality.
- **Empirically robust**: Performs well across diverse problem classes (TSP, scheduling, VLSI).

**Linear Cooling: $T_k = T_0 - k\beta$**

**Disadvantages:**
- **Hits zero in finite steps**: $T$ reaches 0 after $k = T_0 / \beta$ iterations, abruptly terminating exploration. The algorithm loses its annealing character near the end.
- **Decay rate is too uniform**: It does not slow down at low temperatures, causing premature convergence to local minima before sufficient refinement.
- **Sensitive to step size $\beta$**: Poor choice of $\beta$ leads to either wasted computation (too slow) or poor solutions (too fast).

**Conclusion:** Geometric cooling is preferred because its **exponential decay naturally provides more iterations at low temperatures** (where fine-grained refinement happens), making it the **de facto standard** in practical SA implementations.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Role of cooling schedule: 2 marks
- Geometric advantages (2 points): 3 marks
- Linear disadvantages (2 points): 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify $\Delta f > 0$ condition:** Many students write the Metropolis formula but fail to state it applies only to worsening moves. Always include the **piecewise definition**.
> 2. **Confusing $k_B$:** KTU expects $k_B$ absorbed into $T$. Writing $e^{-\Delta f / k_B T}$ without simplification is penalized.
> 3. **Missing the random number comparison:** A numerical SA problem requires both $P_{accept}$ AND comparison with $r \sim U(0,1)$. Skipping the second step loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Cooling schedule formula errors:** Geometric is $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, NOT $T_{k+1} = T_k - \alpha$. Verify before writing.
> 5. **Confusing "always accept" rule:** Improving moves ($\Delta f < 0$) are accepted with probability **1**, not $e^{+\Delta f / T}$. This is a frequent error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **SA Origin:** Physical annealing of metals → Kirkpatrick et al. (1983).
- **Two Acceptance Cases:** Always accept improving moves; probabilistically accept worsening moves.
- **Metropolis Formula:** $P_{accept} = e^{-\Delta f / T}$ when $\Delta f > 0$.
- **Geometric Cooling:** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $\alpha \in [0.8, 0.99]$.
- **Initial Temperature:** Chosen so $\chi_0 \approx 0.8$ (initial acceptance ratio).
- **Final Temperature:** $T_f \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ or based on iteration budget.
- **Inner Loop Length:** $L_k \ge N$ (neighborhood size) for quasi-equilibrium.
- **Convergence Theorem:** Logarithmic cooling guarantees asymptotic convergence (Geman & Geman, 1984); geometric cooling has no such guarantee but is empirically effective.
- **Termination Criteria:** (i) $T \le T_f$, (ii) $k \ge k_{max}$, (iii) no improvement for $X$ consecutive iterations.
- **Boltzmann Distribution Mapping:** $P(E) \propto e^{-E / k_B T} \to P_{accept} = e^{-\Delta f / T}$.
- **Exploration ↔ Exploitation:** High $T$ = exploration; Low $T$ = exploitation.
- **Real-World Analogy:** Mountain climber who occasionally climbs uphill at the start to escape valleys.
- **Applications:** TSP, VLSI design, job-shop scheduling, image segmentation, neural network training.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tabu Search - Types of Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tabu Search - Types of Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tabu Search (TS)** is an iterative metaheuristic procedure originally proposed by **Fred Glover (1986)** for solving combinatorial optimization problems. It is designed to intelligently cross the boundaries of feasibility and local optimality by employing **adaptive memory structures** that explicitly model and exploit the history of the search process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory is the defining feature of Tabu Search.** Unlike simpler local search methods (e.g., hill climbing) that use only the current solution and its immediate neighbours, Tabu Search maintains a record of past search efforts to drive the search into new, unexplored regions of the solution space.

A **Memory** in Tabu Search is formally defined as a data structure that records attributes of solutions, moves, or regions visited during the search, used to **forbid (declare "tabu")** certain choices for a specified duration (called the **Tabu Tenure**), thereby preventing cycling and forcing the exploration of novel regions.

## 1.2 The Three Primary Types of Memory

| Memory Type | Alias | Primary Function | Storage Horizon |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Term Memory (STM)** | Recency-Based Memory | Prevents immediate cycling by forbidding recent moves | Recent iterations (t − k to t) |
| **Intermediate-Term Memory (ITM)** | Frequency-Based Memory | Penalizes moves visited too often to diversify search | Medium span (running counters) |
| **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** | Quality / Intensity-Based Memory | Reinforces elite regions containing high-quality solutions | Entire search history |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lost Traveler in a Foggy City"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into an unfamiliar city in dense fog. You must find the highest peak. Every step you take, you leave a small flag. Your **short-term memory** is the pile of flags you've planted in the last few steps — you refuse to step back on them (tabu), forcing you to move forward. Your **intermediate memory** is a notebook counting how many times you've walked down a particular street — if a street has been walked down 10 times, you probably know its height already, so you avoid it. Your **long-term memory** is a list of the highest peaks you have ever reached — if you find a path leading toward a historically high region, you are biased to take it.

In this analogy:
- **Flags** = tabu status of moves
- **Notebook** = frequency counters
- **List of peaks** = elite solution archive

This three-tier memory is exactly how Tabu Search escapes the trap of returning to previously visited local optima, which is the **fundamental failure mode** of naive local search.

## 1.4 Why Memory is Essential — The Cycling Problem

Local search terminates when no improving neighbour exists. In non-convex landscapes, this is a **local optimum**, which is *not* a guarantee of global optimality. To escape, the algorithm must occasionally accept **non-improving (worsening) moves**. Without memory, it will **cycle** — returning to the same local optimum it just left.

**Memory breaks the symmetry of time**, ensuring the algorithm cannot undo a move it just made.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Short-Term Memory (STM) — Recency-Based Memory

**Purpose:** To mark specific moves (or their attributes) as **tabu** for a fixed number of iterations, called the **Tabu Tenure** *t* (lowercase italic to avoid confusion with iteration counter).

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

1. When a move *m* is executed at iteration *i*, the attribute(s) characterizing *m* are recorded in a **Tabu List**.
2. The tabu list also stores an **expiration iteration** for each entry: $i + t$.
3. At any later iteration $i' < i + t$, the same attribute is considered **tabu** and cannot be selected.
4. When $i' \geq i + t$, the entry is released and the move becomes **admissible** again.

### 2.1.2 Tabu Tenure Strategies

| Strategy | Formula / Rule | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Tenure** | $t = \text{const}$ (e.g., $n/3$, where $n$ is the number of variables) | Simple, predictable |
| **Dynamic Tenure** | $t \in [t_{min}, t_{max}]$ sampled uniformly per iteration | Adaptive, avoids synchronisation |
| **Random Tenure** | $t = \text{rand}(a, b)$ | Introduces stochasticity |

> [!TIP]
> KTU Board Exam Favourite: *"What happens if tabu tenure is too small or too large?"* — Answer: *Too small* → cycling returns (memory too short to forbid revisit). *Too large* → excessive restriction, search becomes random.

## 2.2 Intermediate-Term Memory (ITM) — Frequency-Based Memory

**Purpose:** To keep a **running counter** of how many times each move attribute has been invoked during the search. This is a *quantitative* memory, complementing STM's *qualitative* flag.

### 2.2.1 Two Forms of Frequency

- **Residence Frequency (RF):** How often an attribute is *currently in the tabu list*.
- **Transition Frequency (TF):** How often an attribute has been *moved between solutions* (i.e., used as a move).

For an attribute $a$ over a window of $W$ iterations:

$$f_{a}^{TF} = \sum_{i=1}^{W} \mathbb{1}[\text{attribute } a \text{ used at iteration } i]$$

### 2.2.2 How Frequency Penalises Overuse

If a move $m$ has frequency $f_m > \bar{f}$ (above average), the **move value** $v(m)$ is modified as:

$$v'(m) = v(m) - \lambda \cdot f_m$$

where $\lambda$ is a **penalty coefficient**. This discourages the re-selection of heavily-used moves, **diversifying** the search.

## 2.3 Long-Term Memory (LTM) — Quality / Intensity Memory

**Purpose:** To record **elite solutions** (best-so-far and high-quality diversification candidates) and use them to *intensify* the search in promising regions.

### 2.3.1 Two Sub-Mechanisms

1. **Intensity-Based Memory:** Records how many of the best solutions contain a particular attribute. Used for **intensification** (exploit good regions).
2. **Diversity-Based Memory:** Records attributes of solutions that are *far* from the elite set. Used for **diversification** (explore new regions).

If the best objective value seen is $f^*$, an attribute $a$ has intensity:

$$I_a = \sum_{s \in S_{elite}} \mathbb{1}[a \in s]$$

where $S_{elite}$ is the set of elite solutions (e.g., those within $\epsilon$ of $f^*$).

## 2.4 Aspiration Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Aspiration Criterion** is an exception that *overrides* the tabu status of a move. The most common form: **Aspire-by-Objective**: A tabu move is permitted if it yields a solution **better than the best-known solution**.

Mathematically, a tabu move $m$ with neighbour $s'$ is admissible if:

$$f(s') < f(s^*) \quad \text{(assuming minimisation)}$$

where $s^*$ is the best-known solution.

## 2.5 Memory Classification — Explicit vs. Attributive

| Memory Form | What is Stored | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Explicit Memory** | Complete solutions | Storing $s^*$, elite archive |
| **Attributive Memory** | Solution *components* or *move attributes* | Storing "edge $e_{ij}$" used, "variable $x_k = 1$" |

Modern Tabu Search almost always uses **attributive memory** with explicit memory only for elite solutions, because storing full solutions is **memory-expensive** for large problems.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they are the bread-and-butter of Tabu Search exam questions.**

| Symbol | Meaning | Equation / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| $t$ | Tabu tenure (iterations) | $t \in [t_{min}, t_{max}]$ |
| $v(m)$ | Raw objective improvement of move $m$ | $v(m) = f(s) - f(s \oplus m)$ (minimisation) |
| $v'(m)$ | Penalised move value | $v'(m) = v(m) - \lambda \cdot f_m$ |
| $f_m^{TF}$ | Transition frequency of move $m$ | Sum of indicator over window |
| $I_a$ | Intensity of attribute $a$ | $I_a = \sum_{s \in S_{elite}} \mathbb{1}[a \in s]$ |
| Aspiration | Override condition | $f(s \oplus m) < f(s^*)$ |
| Best move rule | Selection | $m^* = \arg\min_{m \in N(s), m \notin T \text{ or } A(m)} v'(m)$ |

**Critical boundary conditions** for KTU problems:
- $t = 0$ → no memory, degenerates to local search
- $t = \infty$ → forbids moves forever, no progress
- $\lambda = 0$ → frequency memory inactive

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tabu Search's memory structures are used in:
- **VLSI circuit partitioning** (FM-style moves with recency memory)
- **Job-shop scheduling** (forbidding recent job-machine assignments)
- **Vehicle routing problems (VRP)** with time windows
- **Telecommunications: Dynamic channel assignment** in cellular networks
- **Power systems: Economic dispatch** with unit commitment

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Recency-Based Memory Update Rule

Let $L_T$ denote the tabu list at iteration $T$, storing tuples of the form $(\text{attribute } a, \text{expiration } e_a)$.
