**Step 1: A move $m$ with attribute $a$ is executed at iteration $T$.**
The system inserts a new tuple into the list:

$$L_T \leftarrow L_{T-1} \cup \{(a, T + t)\}$$

**Step 2: Expired entries are purged.**
For every tuple $(a, e_a) \in L_T$, remove it if:

$$e_a \leq T$$

**Step 3: A candidate move $m'$ with attribute $a'$ is declared tabu if:**

$$(a', e_{a'}) \in L_T \quad \text{and} \quad e_{a'} > T$$

**Step 4: Aspiration override is applied:**

$$\text{Admissible}(m') = \Big[ (a', e_{a'}) \notin L_T \ \text{or} \ f(s \oplus m') < f(s^*) \Big]$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Frequency Memory Update

Consider a **sliding window of length $W$**. Let $H_a \in \{0, 1\}^W$ be a binary history vector of attribute $a$, where $H_a[i] = 1$ if attribute $a$ was used in the last $W$ iterations ending at the current one.

**Step 1: Define the frequency of attribute $a$ at iteration $T$:**

$$f_a(T) = \sum_{j=T-W+1}^{T} H_a[j]$$

**Step 2: Update rule upon executing a move with attribute $a$ at iteration $T$:**

$$H_a[T] \leftarrow 1, \quad H_a[T-W] \leftarrow 0$$

**Step 3: Compute the average frequency across all $K$ attributes:**

$$\bar{f}(T) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{a=1}^{K} f_a(T)$$

**Step 4: Diversification penalty.**
Any move with attribute $a$ satisfying $f_a(T) > \bar{f}(T)$ is *not* explicitly forbidden (unlike tabu), but its effective value is reduced:

$$v_a^{\text{eff}} = v_a - \lambda \cdot \big( f_a(T) - \bar{f}(T) \big)$$

This is the **diversification incentive**.

## 3.3 Derivation — Intensification via Long-Term Memory

Let $S_{elite}$ be an archive of the $\kappa$ best solutions found so far. For each attribute $a$:

**Step 1: Define intensity.**

$$I_a = \sum_{s \in S_{elite}} \mathbb{1}[a \in s]$$

**Step 2: Normalise to get a probability-like weight.**

$$w_a = \frac{I_a}{\sum_{a' \in A} I_{a'}}$$

**Step 3: Bias function.**
When evaluating move $m$ with attribute $a$, add a bonus $\beta \cdot w_a$ to the move value (intensification) or subtract it (diversification):

$$v_{\text{biased}}(m) = v(m) + \sigma \cdot \beta \cdot w_a$$

where $\sigma = +1$ for intensification and $\sigma = -1$ for diversification.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A minimisation problem with 6 candidate moves at iteration $T = 10$. Tabu tenure $t = 3$. Frequency window $W = 5$. Penalty $\lambda = 2$. Current best $f^* = 50$. Current solution value $f(s) = 55$.

| Move $m_i$ | Attribute $a$ | $v(m_i) = f(s) - f(s \oplus m_i)$ | $f_{a_i}$ | In tabu list? | $f(s \oplus m_i) < f^* = 50$? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $m_1$ | $a_1$ | 4 | 1 | No | No (51) |
| $m_2$ | $a_2$ | 6 | 4 | Yes (expiry 11) | No (49 — aspired!) |
| $m_3$ | $a_3$ | 3 | 2 | No | No (52) |
| $m_4$ | $a_4$ | 5 | 3 | No | No (50 — not strictly less) |
| $m_5$ | $a_5$ | 7 | 2 | Yes (expiry 12) | No (48 — aspired!) |
| $m_6$ | $a_6$ | 2 | 5 | No | No (53) |

**Step 1:** Compute average frequency: $\bar{f} = (1+4+2+3+2+5)/6 = 17/6 \approx 2.83$.

**Step 2:** Apply frequency penalty $v'(m_i) = v(m_i) - \lambda \cdot f_{a_i}$ (with $\lambda = 2$):

$$v'(m_1) = 4 - 2(1) = 2$$
$$v'(m_2) = 6 - 2(4) = -2$$
$$v'(m_3) = 3 - 2(2) = -1$$
$$v'(m_4) = 5 - 2(3) = -1$$
$$v'(m_5) = 7 - 2(2) = 3$$
$$v'(m_6) = 2 - 2(5) = -8$$

**Step 3:** Identify admissible moves (excluding tabu unless aspired).
- $m_1, m_3, m_4, m_6$ → admissible (not in tabu list).
- $m_2, m_5$ → tabu, but $m_5$ yields 48 < 50, so **aspired**; $m_2$ yields 49 < 50, also **aspired**.

**Step 4:** Among admissible moves, pick the one with **best (lowest) penalised value**:
- $m_6$ has $v' = -8$ → strongly favoured.
- $m_3, m_4$ tied at $-1$.
- $m_5$ (aspired) has $v' = 3$ → poor despite aspiration.

**Step 5: Selected move = $m_6$.** Update best if 53 < 50? No. Update $s^* = 50$ unchanged. Record attribute $a_6$ in tabu list with expiry $10 + 3 = 13$.

This single example illustrates the interplay of **all three memory types** and **aspiration** in one selection step.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Memory | Stored Quantity | Mathematical Form | Search Effect | Risk of Overuse |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Short-Term (Recency) | Recent attribute, expiration | $(a, T + t)$ | Prevents cycling | Search gets stuck if $t$ too large |
| Intermediate (Frequency) | Visit count per attribute | $f_a(T) = \sum H_a[j]$ | Diversifies | Penalty too harsh → random walk |
| Long-Term (Intensity) | Attribute frequency in elite set | $I_a = \sum_{s \in S_{elite}} \mathbb{1}[a \in s]$ | Intensifies | Premature convergence |
| Long-Term (Diversity) | Distance from elite set | $D(s) = \min_{s' \in S_{elite}} d(s, s')$ | Explores new regions | Lost in infeasible space |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy in Tabu Search

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the three memory tiers interact during a single Tabu Search iteration, from solution evaluation to move selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current Solution s at iteration T] --> B[Generate Neighbourhood N of s]
    B --> C[Evaluate Raw Move Values v of m]
    C --> D{Check Tabu Status of each move m}
    D -- Tabu and not Aspired --> E[Disqualify m]
    D -- Not Tabu or Aspired --> F[Check Frequency Memory f sub a]
    F --> G{Is f sub a above average f bar}
    G -- Yes --> H[Apply Diversification Penalty lambda]
    G -- No --> I[Skip Penalty]
    H --> J[Compute Penalised Move Value v prime m]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Check Long Term Intensity I sub a in Elite Set}
    K -- High Intensity --> L[Apply Intensification Bonus beta]
    K -- Low Intensity --> M[Apply Diversification Bias]
    L --> N[Final Effective Value v effective m]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Select Best Move m star with lowest v effective]
    O --> P[Execute m star to obtain s prime]
    P --> Q[Update Short Term Tabu List with attribute a]
    P --> R[Increment Frequency Counter f sub a]
    P --> S[Compare f of s prime with f of s star]
    S -- Better --> T[Update s star to s prime]
    S -- Not Better --> U[Keep s star unchanged]
    T --> V[Iteration T equals T plus 1]
    U --> V
    V --> A
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of the Memory Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SM[Short Term Memory Subsystem]
        SM1[Tabu List L of T]
        SM2[Expiration Manager]
        SM3[Purging Engine]
    end

    subgraph IM[Intermediate Term Memory Subsystem]
        IM1[Frequency Vector f sub a]
        IM2[Sliding Window Buffer of length W]
        IM3[Average Calculator f bar of T]
    end

    subgraph LM[Long Term Memory Subsystem]
        LM1[Elite Archive S sub elite]
        LM2[Intensity Computer I sub a]
        LM3[Diversity Distance Estimator D of s]
    end

    subgraph ASP[Aspiration Logic]
        ASP1[Best Known Tracker s star]
        ASP2[Override Comparator f of s prime less than f of s star]
    end

    SEL[Move Selector] --> SM
    SEL --> IM
    SEL --> LM
    SEL --> ASP
    SM --> SEL
    IM --> SEL
    LM --> SEL
    ASP --> SEL
```

## 4.3 Memory Effect on Search Trajectory (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Search Start s sub 0] --> explore[Exploration Phase using STM]
    explore --> cycledetect[Cycle Detected by STM]
    cycledetect --> divert[Divert via ITM Frequency Penalty]
    divert --> promising[Promising Region Identified by LTM]
    promising --> intensify[Intensification via LTM Intensity]
    intensify --> elite[Elite Solution Found in S sub elite]
    elite --> diversify[Diversification Triggered]
    diversify --> explore
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above abstract the algorithmic flow of Tabu Search's memory mechanisms. In an examination, you may reproduce a simplified version to earn full marks on *describe-the-architecture* questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of short-term memory in Tabu Search. Why is recency-based memory alone insufficient to guarantee convergence to high-quality solutions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Short-term memory (STM) stores attributes of recently executed moves in a **tabu list** with a fixed **tabu tenure** *t*, forbidding their re-execution for *t* iterations. This breaks the immediate cycling that plagues local search.
- **(1 mark)** STM alone is *local* in time-horizon — it cannot differentiate between high-quality and low-quality revisiting. It forbids *all* revisits equally, even if a move leads to a better-than-best solution.
- **(1 mark)** Therefore, **aspiration criteria** and **long-term memory** (frequency, intensity) are essential to allow good moves through and to redirect search toward promising regions.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between explicit memory and attributive memory in Tabu Search. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Explicit memory** records *complete solutions* (e.g., the best-known solution $s^*$, the elite archive $S_{elite}$). It is memory-intensive but permits direct comparison.
- **(1 mark)** **Attributive memory** records only the *components* or *attributes* of solutions (e.g., edge $(i,j)$ used in a TSP move). It is compact and scales to large problems.
- **(1 mark)** Example — *Explicit*: storing the best TSP tour found so far. *Attributive*: storing the set of edges added/removed in the last *t* moves to forbid their re-use.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Classify the different types of memory used in Tabu Search. Explain the role of **frequency-based memory** with a suitable mathematical formulation for the transition frequency of a move attribute.

**(b) (7 marks)** Consider a 0-1 knapsack problem with 8 items. The current solution at iteration $T = 20$ has objective $f(s) = 42$, best-known $f(s^*) = 38$. Tabu tenure $t = 4$. For the 5 candidate moves listed below, decide which move is selected at iteration $T$ using recency, frequency, and aspiration criteria. Justify your choice.

| Move | Attribute | $v(m) = f(s) - f(s \oplus m)$ | $f_a$ | In Tabu List (Expiry) | $f(s \oplus m)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $m_1$ | $a_1$ | 3 | 2 | No | 39 |
| $m_2$ | $a_2$ | 5 | 3 | Yes (22) | 37 |
| $m_3$ | $a_3$ | 4 | 1 | No | 38 |
| $m_4$ | $a_4$ | 2 | 4 | No | 40 |
| $m_5$ | $a_5$ | 6 | 2 | Yes (25) | 36 |

Use $\lambda = 1.5$ for frequency penalty. $\bar{f} = 2.4$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Classification of Memory (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** Three primary types:
  - **Short-Term Memory (STM)** – Recency: tabu list with expiration iterations.
  - **Intermediate-Term Memory (ITM)** – Frequency: counters of move usage.
  - **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** – Quality/Intensity: elite solution archive, intensification/diversification.

- **(2 marks)** **Frequency-based memory role:**
  - Records the number of times each move attribute has been *executed* (transition frequency) or is *currently* in the tabu list (residence frequency).
  - Drives **diversification** by penalising moves that have been used excessively, preventing the search from getting trapped in a narrow region of the solution space.

- **(2 marks)** **Mathematical formulation of transition frequency:**
  For attribute $a$ over a sliding window of $W$ iterations:
  $$f_a^{TF}(T) = \sum_{j=T-W+1}^{T} \mathbb{1}[\text{attribute } a \text{ used at iteration } j]$$
  with update:
  $$f_a^{TF}(T+1) = f_a^{TF}(T) - H_a[T-W+1] + H_a[T+1]$$
  where $H_a[i] \in \{0, 1\}$ is the binary usage indicator.

- **(1 mark)** The penalised move value:
  $$v'(m) = v(m) - \lambda \cdot f_a^{TF}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply frequency penalty $\lambda = 1.5$, $\bar{f} = 2.4$.
Effective penalty is $\lambda \cdot f_a$:

| Move | $v(m)$ | $f_a$ | $\lambda \cdot f_a$ | $v'(m) = v(m) - 1.5 f_a$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $m_1$ | 3 | 2 | 3.0 | 0.0 |
| $m_2$ | 5 | 3 | 4.5 | 0.5 |
| $m_3$ | 4 | 1 | 1.5 | 2.5 |
| $m_4$ | 2 | 4 | 6.0 | -4.0 |
| $m_5$ | 6 | 2 | 3.0 | 3.0 |

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Check tabu status at $T = 20$ and aspiration ($f(s \oplus m) < f(s^*) = 38$).
- $m_1, m_3, m_4$ → not tabu, admissible.
- $m_2$ → tabu (expiry 22 > 20), $f(s \oplus m_2) = 37 < 38$ → **aspired**, admissible.
- $m_5$ → tabu (expiry 25 > 20), $f(s \oplus m_5) = 36 < 38$ → **aspired**, admissible.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compare effective values of admissible moves:
$$m_1: 0.0, \quad m_2: 0.5, \quad m_3: 2.5, \quad m_4: -4.0, \quad m_5: 3.0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Best (minimum) is $m_4$ with $v'(m_4) = -4.0$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Selected move = $m_4$.** Update best if 40 < 38? No. $s^* = 38$ unchanged. Record $a_4$ in tabu list with expiry $T + t = 20 + 4 = 24$.

**Step 6 (2 marks):** **Justification:** $m_4$ is selected because it has the lowest effective value ($-4.0$) due to its high frequency ($f_{a_4} = 4 > \bar{f}$), which is *heavily penalised* by frequency memory. Although $m_5$ has a high raw improvement (6) and is aspired, its lower effective value (3.0) is outweighed by $m_4$'s penalty. The interplay of recency, frequency, and aspiration is thus demonstrated in a single decision.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **three-tier memory structure** of Tabu Search. Discuss how each tier contributes to *intensification* and *diversification* of the search.

**(b) (7 marks)** For a 5-variable binary optimisation problem (minimise $f$), the current solution at $T = 5$ is $s = (1, 0, 1, 0, 1)$ with $f(s) = 15$, and $f(s^*) = 10$. Tabu tenure $t = 2$. Neighbourhood is single-bit flip. The tabu list at $T = 5$ contains $\{(x_1, 6), (x_4, 8)\}$. Three candidate moves are:
- $m_1$: flip $x_1$, $f(s \oplus m_1) = 12$
- $m_2$: flip $x_3$, $f(s \oplus m_2) = 14$
- $m_3$: flip $x_5$, $f(s \oplus m_3) = 9$

Determine the move selected and update the tabu list.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three-Tier Memory Structure (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** Diagram and description of **STM (Recency)**:
  - Stores attributes of recently executed moves; uses tabu list with expiration counter.
  - **Effect on search:** Prevents immediate cycling; primary role is *diversification* (force novel exploration).

- **(2 marks)** Diagram and description of **ITM (Frequency)**:
  - Counts how often each attribute is invoked (residence and transition frequency).
  - **Effect on search:** Penalises over-used attributes → *diversification* across attribute space.

- **(2 marks)** Diagram and description of **LTM (Quality)**:
  - Maintains elite solution archive; computes intensity $I_a$ of attributes in elite set.
  - **Effect on search:** *Intensification* (revisit good regions by biasing toward elite attributes) **or** *diversification* (escape when no improvement in many iterations, by penalising elite attributes).

- **(1 mark)** **Synthesis:** The three tiers operate simultaneously: STM is mandatory (tabu), ITM is advisory (penalty), LTM is strategic (intensify/diversify). Their interplay is the source of Tabu Search's power.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $v(m_i) = f(s) - f(s \oplus m_i)$ for each move:
$$v(m_1) = 15 - 12 = 3, \quad v(m_2) = 15 - 14 = 1, \quad v(m_3) = 15 - 9 = 6$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Check tabu status at $T = 5$:
- $m_1$ flips $x_1$; tabu list contains $(x_1, 6)$, expiry 6 > 5 → **tabu**.
- $m_2$ flips $x_3$; not in tabu list → **admissible**.
- $m_3$ flips $x_5$; not in tabu list → **admissible**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Check aspiration ($f(s \oplus m) < f(s^*) = 10$):
- $m_1$ tabu, $f(s \oplus m_1) = 12 > 10$ → not aspired, **forbidden**.
- $m_2$ admissible, $v = 1$ → candidate.
- $m_3$ admissible, $v = 6$ → candidate; also $9 < 10$ → aspired (and admissible anyway).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Best admissible move = $m_3$ (highest $v$ = 6).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Execute $m_3$. New solution $s' = (1, 0, 1, 0, 0)$, $f(s') = 9$. Update $s^* = 9$. Add $x_5$ to tabu list with expiry $T + t = 5 + 2 = 7$.

**Final tabu list:** $\{(x_1, 6), (x_4, 8), (x_5, 7)\}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing recency with frequency memory** — recency is *qualitative* (tabu/not-tabu), frequency is *quantitative* (count). Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are conflated.
> 2. **Forgetting aspiration override** — when applying tabu, always check $f(s \oplus m) < f(s^*)$. Missing this in a numerical problem costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Tabu tenure not added to current iteration** — expiration is $T + t$, not $T + t - 1$. Off-by-one errors are common and heavily penalised.
> 4. **Frequency penalty sign error** — $v'(m) = v(m) - \lambda f_a$ (subtract, not add). The penalty *reduces* attractiveness.
> 5. **Treating intensification and diversification as opposites only** — they are dual uses of the *same* long-term memory. Examiners love asking this nuance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tabu Search: Types of Memory**

- ✅ Tabu Search is a **memory-based metaheuristic** by **Fred Glover (1986)**.
- ✅ **Short-Term Memory (Recency):** tabu list of attributes with **expiration iteration** $T + t$; forbids cycles.
- ✅ **Tabu Tenure** *t*: too small → cycling; too large → random search; typically $t \approx n/3$ for $n$-variable problems.
- ✅ **Intermediate-Term Memory (Frequency):** **transition frequency** $f_a^{TF}$ and **residence frequency**; used for **diversification penalty** $v'(m) = v(m) - \lambda f_a$.
- ✅ **Long-Term Memory (Quality):** **intensity** $I_a$ in elite set; used for **intensification** (boost elite attributes) or **diversification** (escape stagnating regions).
- ✅ **Explicit memory** = full solutions; **Attributive memory** = components/attributes. Modern TS uses attributive.
- ✅ **Aspiration Criterion:** overrides tabu when $f(s \oplus m) < f(s^*)$ — allows discovery of new global bests.
- ✅ Memory is the **defining feature** distinguishing TS from local search; without it, TS degenerates to hill-climbing.
- ✅ The three memory types operate **simultaneously** — STM is mandatory, ITM is advisory, LTM is strategic.
- ✅ For KTU numericals: always apply the **order** → check tabu → check aspiration → apply frequency penalty → select best effective value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iterated Local Search - Perturbation Method and Acceptance Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Iterated Local Search - Perturbation Method and Acceptance Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Iterated Local Search (ILS)

**Iterated Local Search (ILS)** is a trajectory-based metaheuristic framework that builds a chain of locally optimal solutions by *repeatedly applying local search to perturbations of the current best solution*. It is formally defined as a 4-tuple $\{S_0, \mathcal{N}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{A}\}$ where:

- $S_0$ → procedure to generate an initial solution
- $\mathcal{N}$ → neighborhood structure used by the embedded local search
- $\mathcal{P}$ → **Perturbation mechanism** (non-trivial move to escape current local optimum)
- $\mathcal{A}$ → **Acceptance criterion** (decides whether to update the current reference solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** ILS belongs to the family of *Single-Solution Metaheuristics* (trajectory methods), alongside Simulated Annealing and Tabu Search. The two *novel components* introduced over plain local search are **Perturbation** and **Acceptance Criterion**.

The canonical KTU textbook expression for one iteration of ILS is:

$$s^{*} \leftarrow \text{LocalSearch}\big(\mathcal{P}(s^{*})\big)$$

$$s^{*} \leftarrow \mathcal{A}\big(s^{*}, s^{**}\big)$$

where $s^{*}$ is the current reference solution and $s^{**}$ is the newly optimized candidate.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mountain Climber with a Helicopter"

Imagine you are blindfolded on a misty mountain and want to reach the **highest peak**.

- **Local Search** = You walk uphill by feel until the ground is flat (you've reached a *local peak* — but maybe not the *true* summit).
- **Perturbation** = A helicopter drops you at a *nearby random spot* on the mountain (not too close to your current peak — that would just return you to the same peak; not too far — that would be a complete restart).
- **Acceptance Criterion** = You decide: *"Should I keep climbing from this new spot, or return to my previous peak?"* Sometimes you accept worse peaks (like in **Simulated Annealing acceptance**) so you can explore more terrain.
- **Local Search again** = From the new drop-off point, you walk uphill until flat again.

This cycle of *drop → climb → decide* is exactly what ILS does, thousands of times, to find very high-quality solutions.

---

## 1.3 Why ILS? The Motivation

Plain local search (e.g., Hill Climbing) gets trapped in **local optima**. ILS overcomes this by:

1. **Diversification** through perturbation (escape the basin of attraction).
2. **Intensification** through local search (refine within a basin).
3. **Memory of history** via acceptance criterion (don't cycle, don't forget good regions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Metric:** ILS is typically evaluated on *solution quality* (fitness gap to known optimum) and *computation time*. State-of-the-art ILS variants (e.g., for TSP) close the optimality gap to within **< 0.5 %** on benchmark instances like TSPLIB.

---

## 1.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ILS

| Component | Role | KTU Key Point |
|-----------|------|---------------|
| Initial Solution $s_0$ | Seed for first iteration | Generated randomly or by greedy heuristic |
| Local Search $\mathcal{N}$ | Find local optimum $s^{*}$ | Uses Hill Climbing / 2-opt / Variable Neighborhood |
| Perturbation $\mathcal{P}$ | Escape current basin | Strength must be tuned |
| Local Search (again) | Optimize perturbed solution | Produces candidate $s^{**}$ |
| Acceptance $\mathcal{A}$ | Update reference or not | Controls exploration vs. exploitation |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Perturbation Method — Formal Treatment

### 2.1.1 Definition

The **perturbation operator** $\mathcal{P}$ maps the current solution $s^*$ to a new solution $s' = \mathcal{P}(s^*)$ such that:

1. $s' \notin \mathcal{N}(s^*)$ → the perturbation must *escape the current neighborhood* (otherwise the embedded local search would just return $s^*$).
2. $s'$ remains in the feasible region $F$ of the search space.
3. The perturbation strength (typically a parameter $k$ — number of randomized moves) is **non-trivial but not destructive**.

Formally:

$$s' = \mathcal{P}_k(s^*) \quad \text{where} \quad k \geq 1$$

---

### 2.1.2 Properties of a Good Perturbation

- **Reachability:** $\mathcal{P}$ should be able to reach any region of the search space in a finite number of iterations.
- **Balance:** Strength must balance *basin escape* vs. *useful structure preservation*.
- **Determinism vs. Stochasticity:** May be random or deterministic (e.g., structured double-bridge in TSP).
- **Low cost:** Should be cheaper than a full local search (otherwise ILS degenerates into repeated random restarts).

---

### 2.1.3 Common Perturbation Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

| Strategy | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Random $k$-swap** | Swap $k$ randomly chosen elements | Permutation problems |
| **Double-Bridge (4-opt)** | A specific 4-edge reconnection in a tour | TSP (state-of-the-art) |
| **Segment Reversal** | Reverse a random contiguous segment | Permutation/sequence problems |
| **Ruin-and-Recreate** | Remove a portion, reinsert greedily | Vehicle routing, scheduling |
| **Kick move** | Apply $k$ random neighborhood moves | Generic combinatorial problems |
| **Bit-Flip (k bits)** | Flip $k$ random bits | Binary problems (e.g., MAX-SAT) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If $k = 1$ (single move), the perturbation just yields a neighbor, and local search immediately returns $s^*$ — no progress. If $k$ is too large, ILS becomes a **Multi-Start Local Search**, losing the benefit of accumulated search history.

---

### 2.1.4 Adaptive Perturbation Strength

Modern ILS variants use *adaptive perturbation*:

$$k_{t+1} = \begin{cases} k_t + \Delta_k & \text{if } s^{**} \text{ was accepted} \\ k_t & \text{if } s^{**} \text{ was rejected and } k_t < k_{\max} \end{cases}$$

This dynamically widens the perturbation when the algorithm stagnates — a key idea in **Lourenço, Martin & Stützle (2003)** style ILS.

---

## 2.2 The Acceptance Criterion — Formal Treatment

### 2.2.1 Definition

The **acceptance criterion** $\mathcal{A}$ compares the new local optimum $s^{**}$ (obtained after perturbation + local search) with the current reference $s^*$ and decides the next reference:

$$s^{*}_{\text{next}} = \mathcal{A}(s^*, s^{**})$$

---

### 2.2.2 KTU High-Yield Acceptance Criteria

| Criterion | Rule | Behavior | KTU Tag |
|-----------|------|----------|---------|
| **Better-Only** | Accept iff $f(s^{**}) < f(s^*)$ | Strict improvement | Pure intensification |
| **Better-or-Equal** | Accept iff $f(s^{**}) \leq f(s^*)$ | Non-worsening | Avoids cyclic returns |
| **Threshold Acceptance (TA)** | Accept iff $f(s^{**}) \leq f(s^*) + \tau$ | Allows small uphill steps | Tuning parameter $\tau$ |
| **Great Deluge (GD)** | Accept iff $f(s^{**}) \leq L$ | $L$ is a level that decays over time | Schedule-controlled |
| **Record-to-Record (RTR)** | Accept iff $f(s^{**}) \leq f(s^{\text{best}}) + \delta$ | Relative to global best | Explore via global reference |
| **Simulated Annealing (SA)** | Accept with probability $e^{-\Delta f / T}$ | Probabilistic uphill | Temperature-controlled |
| **Always Accept** | Always set $s^* \leftarrow s^{**}$ | Random walk | Pure diversification |

---

### 2.2.3 Key Mathematical Formulas (KTU Exam Critical)

**(a) Metropolis / SA Acceptance Probability:**

$$P_{\text{accept}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Delta f \leq 0 \\ e^{-\Delta f / T} & \text{if } \Delta f > 0 \end{cases}$$

where $\Delta f = f(s^{**}) - f(s^*)$ and $T$ is the temperature parameter.

**(b) Threshold Acceptance:**

$$\mathcal{A}: \text{ accept iff } f(s^{**}) \leq f(s^*) + \tau, \quad \tau \geq 0$$

**(c) Great Deluge Level Update:**

$$L_{t+1} = L_t - \Delta L, \quad \Delta L > 0, \quad L_0 \approx f(s^*)$$

**(d) RTR Acceptance:**

$$f(s^{**}) \leq f(s^{\text{best}}) + \delta, \quad \delta > 0$$

**(e) Cooling Schedule (Geometric):**

$$T_{t+1} = \alpha \cdot T_t, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Circuit Design:** ILS with double-bridge perturbation on routing benchmarks.
- **Logistics & TSP:** ILS holds multiple world records on TSPLIB instances (e.g., the famous **LK-H** algorithm of Helsgaun).
- **Job Shop Scheduling:** Perturbation via random critical block reversal.
- **Antenna Design / RF Engineering:** ILS optimizes antenna geometries with bit-flip perturbations on discretized parameter vectors.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein structure prediction with ruin-and-recreate perturbations.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (One-Page Rapid Revision)

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Constraint |
|------------------|---------|------------|
| $s^{*} \leftarrow \mathcal{P}_k(s^{*})$ | Apply $k$-strength perturbation | $k \geq 1$, $k \leq \vert N(s^*) \vert$ |
| $s^{**} \leftarrow \text{LS}(s')$ | Local search on perturbed solution | Until no improving neighbor |
| $P_{\text{accept}} = e^{-\Delta f/T}$ | SA-style acceptance | $T > 0$, $\Delta f > 0$ |
| $f(s^{**}) \leq f(s^*) + \tau$ | Threshold acceptance | $\tau \geq 0$ |
| $L_{t+1} = L_t - \Delta L$ | Great Deluge decay | $\Delta L > 0$ |
| $T_{t+1} = \alpha T_t$ | Geometric cooling | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| $k_{t+1} = k_t + 1$ on stagnation | Adaptive perturbation increase | Until $k_{\max}$ reached |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $f$ = objective function, $\Delta f$ = cost difference, $T$ = temperature, $\tau$ = threshold, $\delta$ = deviation, $L$ = water level, $k$ = perturbation strength.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Acceptance Probability Under SA Acceptance in ILS

We derive the expected number of accepted worsening moves per iteration when ILS uses **Simulated Annealing-style acceptance** with a fixed temperature $T$.

---

### Step 1 — Define the random variables

Let $s^*$ be the current reference and $s^{**}$ be the candidate. Define:

$$\Delta f = f(s^{**}) - f(s^*)$$

Assume $\Delta f$ is a continuous random variable with probability density function $g(\Delta f)$ supported on $\mathbb{R}$. For our analysis, assume $g(\Delta f)$ is symmetric around the *expected worsening magnitude* $\mu_w > 0$:

$$g(\Delta f) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\Delta f - \mu_w)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

---

### Step 2 — Express the acceptance probability as a function of $\Delta f$

By the Metropolis criterion:

$$P_{\text{accept}}(\Delta f) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Delta f \leq 0 \\ e^{-\Delta f / T} & \text{if } \Delta f > 0 \end{cases}$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the expected acceptance probability

$$E[P_{\text{accept}}] = \int_{-\infty}^{0} g(\Delta f) \cdot 1 \, d(\Delta f) \;+\; \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\Delta f) \cdot e^{-\Delta f / T} \, d(\Delta f)$$

---

### Step 4 — Split and evaluate each integral

**Integral 1:** The probability that the new solution is *not worse*:

$$I_1 = \int_{-\infty}^{0} g(\Delta f) \, d(\Delta f) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{0 - \mu_w}{\sigma}\right) = \Phi\!\left(-\frac{\mu_w}{\sigma}\right)$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

**Integral 2:** Using the property of Gaussian integrals:

$$I_2 = \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\Delta f) \cdot e^{-\Delta f / T} \, d(\Delta f)$$

We rewrite $g(\Delta f)$ in the exponential form:

$$g(\Delta f) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\Delta f - \mu_w)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Substituting and completing the square:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\Delta f - \mu_w)^2}{2\sigma^2} - \frac{\Delta f}{T}\right) d(\Delta f)
\end{aligned}$$

Expand the exponent:

$$-\frac{(\Delta f - \mu_w)^2}{2\sigma^2} - \frac{\Delta f}{T} = -\frac{(\Delta f)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{\Delta f \cdot \mu_w}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\mu_w^2}{2\sigma^2} - \frac{\Delta f}{T}$$

Group terms in $\Delta f$:

$$= -\frac{(\Delta f)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \Delta f \left(\frac{\mu_w}{\sigma^2} - \frac{1}{T}\right) - \frac{\mu_w^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

Let:

$$a = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}, \quad b = \frac{\mu_w}{\sigma^2} - \frac{1}{T}$$

Then:

$$= -a(\Delta f)^2 + b \cdot \Delta f - \frac{\mu_w^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

Complete the square:

$$= -a\left((\Delta f)^2 - \frac{b}{a}\Delta f\right) - \frac{\mu_w^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

$$= -a\left(\Delta f - \frac{b}{2a}\right)^2 + \frac{b^2}{4a} - \frac{\mu_w^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

Substituting back $a = 1/(2\sigma^2)$ and $b = \mu_w/\sigma^2 - 1/T$:

$$\frac{b}{2a} = \frac{\mu_w/\sigma^2 - 1/T}{1/\sigma^2} = \mu_w - \frac{\sigma^2}{T}$$

And:

$$\frac{b^2}{4a} = \frac{(\mu_w/\sigma^2 - 1/T)^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{2} = \frac{(\mu_w - \sigma^2/T)^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

So the exponent becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{exponent} &= -\frac{(\Delta f - (\mu_w - \sigma^2/T))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{(\mu_w - \sigma^2/T)^2 - \mu_w^2}{2\sigma^2} \\
&= -\frac{(\Delta f - (\mu_w - \sigma^2/T))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{-2\mu_w\sigma^2/T + \sigma^4/T^2}{2\sigma^2} \\
&= -\frac{(\Delta f - (\mu_w - \sigma^2/T))^2}{2\sigma^2} - \frac{\mu_w}{T} + \frac{\sigma^2}{2T^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Thus:

$$I_2 = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\mu_w}{T} + \frac{\sigma^2}{2T^2}\right) \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\Delta f - (\mu_w - \sigma^2/T))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) d(\Delta f)$$

The remaining integral is the **upper tail** of a Gaussian with mean $\mu_w - \sigma^2/T$:

$$I_2 = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\mu_w}{T} + \frac{\sigma^2}{2T^2}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{0 - (\mu_w - \sigma^2/T)}{\sigma}\right)\right]$$

$$I_2 = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\mu_w}{T} + \frac{\sigma^2}{2T^2}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \Phi\!\left(-\frac{\mu_w}{\sigma} + \frac{\sigma}{T}\right)\right]$$

---

### Step 5 — Combine and simplify

The expected acceptance probability is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E[P_{\text{accept}}] &= \Phi\!\left(-\frac{\mu_w}{\sigma}\right) \;+\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{\mu_w}{T} + \frac{\sigma^2}{2T^2}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \Phi\!\left(-\frac{\mu_w}{\sigma} + \frac{\sigma}{T}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 6 — Interpret the result

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** As $T \to 0$, the exponential term $\to 0$ (no worsening accepted), and $E[P_{\text{accept}}] \to \Phi(-\mu_w/\sigma)$ — only improving moves accepted. As $T \to \infty$, $E[P_{\text{accept}}] \to 1$ — all moves accepted (random walk). This justifies $T$ as the **exploration–exploitation knob**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An ILS run on a TSP instance has current reference cost $f(s^*) = 1250$. A perturbation produces a candidate with cost $f(s^{**}) = 1278$. The current SA temperature is $T = 14$.

**Step (a):** Compute the cost difference.

$$\Delta f = 1278 - 1250 = 28$$

**Step (b):** Apply the Metropolis criterion. Since $\Delta f > 0$:

$$P_{\text{accept}} = e^{-\Delta f / T} = e^{-28 / 14} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**Step (c):** Decision. A uniform random number $r \in [0,1]$ is drawn:

- If $r \leq 0.1353$: **accept** the worse solution (escape the local optimum).
- Else: **reject** and keep $s^*$.

**Step (d):** Iterative effect. Repeating this for, say, 100 iterations allows the algorithm to occasionally climb out of the basin of attraction at cost 1250 and potentially discover a better region with cost, e.g., 1190.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Acceptance Criterion for Problem Type

| Problem Property | Recommended Acceptance | Justification |
|------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Tight cost landscape, many local optima | SA / Great Deluge | Strong exploration needed |
| Smooth landscape, few local optima | Better-Only | Cheap intensification suffices |
| Real-time / fast convergence required | Threshold Acceptance | Simple parameter, no exponential |
| Long runs, high-quality desired | RTR with global $f(s^{\text{best}})$ | Anchored to global best |
| Unknown landscape | Adaptive (Kirkpatrick-style cooling) | Self-tunes exploration |

---

## 3.4 Trade-off Derivation — Perturbation Strength vs. Acceptance Rate

Let $k$ = perturbation strength. Define:

- $P_{\text{escape}}(k)$ = probability that perturbation escapes the *basin of attraction* of $s^*$.
- $P_{\text{useful}}(k)$ = probability that the new basin contains a solution better than $s^*$.

Empirically:

$$P_{\text{escape}}(k) \approx 1 - e^{-\lambda k}, \quad \lambda > 0$$

$$P_{\text{useful}}(k) \approx P_{\text{useful}}^{\max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{k}{k_{\text{destroy}}}\right), \quad 0 < k < k_{\text{destroy}}$$

The **overall progress probability** is:

$$P_{\text{progress}}(k) = P_{\text{accept}} \cdot P_{\text{escape}}(k) \cdot P_{\text{useful}}(k)$$

Differentiating and setting to zero gives the *optimal perturbation strength*:

$$k^{*} \approx \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{useful}}^{\max} \cdot P_{\text{accept}} \cdot \lambda \cdot k_{\text{destroy}}}{P_{\text{useful}}^{\max} \cdot P_{\text{accept}}}\right)$$

This confirms the empirical rule: **$k$ should be ~1/3 to 1/2 of $k_{\text{destroy}}$**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ILS Control Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Generate Initial Solution s0] --> B[Apply Local Search]
    B --> C[Set sStar = Local Optimum]
    C --> D{Perturb sStar using Pk}
    D --> E[Apply Local Search on Perturbed s]
    E --> F[Candidate sDoubleStar Obtained]
    F --> G{Apply Acceptance Criterion A}
    G -->|Accepted| H[Update sStar = sDoubleStar]
    G -->|Rejected| I[Retain sStar]
    H --> J{Stopping Criterion Met}
    I --> J
    J -->|No| D
    J -->|Yes| K[Output Best Solution sBest]
```

---

## 4.2 Perturbation Strength Trade-off (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowK[Low Perturbation k]
        L1[sStar] -->|k=1 swap| L2[s same basin]
        L2 -->|local search| L3[Same local optimum]
        L3 -->|no progress| L4[Waste of compute]
    end

    subgraph OptK[Optimal Perturbation k]
        M1[sStar] -->|balanced kick| M2[s in new basin]
        M2 -->|local search| M3[New local optimum]
        M3 -->|may be better| M4[Real progress]
    end

    subgraph HighK[Excessive Perturbation k]
        N1[sStar] -->|random restart| N2[s essentially random]
        N2 -->|local search| N3[Local optimum]
        N3 -->|forgets history| N4[Multi-start local search]
    end

    L4 -->|increase k| M1
    M4 -->|tune k| M1
    N4 -->|reduce k| M1
```

---

## 4.3 Acceptance Criterion Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Candidate sDoubleStar produced] --> Cost[Compute Delta f = f sDoubleStar - f sStar]
    Cost --> Check{Delta f}
    Check -->|Delta f < 0| Imp[Accept: strict improvement]
    Check -->|Delta f = 0| Equal[Apply Better-or-Equal: accept]
    Check -->|Delta f > 0| Up[Solution is WORSE]

    Up --> Crit{Which Criterion?}
    Crit -->|SA| SA[Accept with prob exp -Delta f / T]
    Crit -->|Threshold| TA[Accept iff Delta f <= tau]
    Crit -->|Great Deluge| GD[Accept iff f sDoubleStar <= L]
    Crit -->|RTR| RTR[Accept iff f sDoubleStar <= f sBest + delta]
    Crit -->|Better-Only| BO[Reject]

    SA --> Rand{Uniform random r}
    Rand -->|r <= P| AcceptFinal[Accept]
    Rand -->|r > P| RejectFinal[Reject]
    TA --> Decide1{Delta f <= tau?}
    Decide1 -->|Yes| AcceptFinal
    Decide1 -->|No| RejectFinal
    GD --> Decide2{f sDoubleStar <= L?}
    Decide2 -->|Yes| AcceptFinal
    Decide2 -->|No| RejectFinal
    RTR --> Decide3{f sDoubleStar <= f sBest + delta?}
    Decide3 -->|Yes| AcceptFinal
    Decide3 -->|No| RejectFinal
    BO --> RejectFinal

    AcceptFinal --> Update[Update sStar = sDoubleStar]
    RejectFinal --> Keep[Keep sStar unchanged]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — ILS Components and Their Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Init[Initialization Phase]
        I1[Random or Greedy s0] --> I2[Local Search]
        I2 --> I3[sStar initial]
    end

    subgraph Loop[Iterative Phase]
        P[Perturbation Pk] --> LS1[Local Search]
        LS1 --> S1[sDoubleStar candidate]
        S1 --> A[Acceptance Criterion A]
        A --> U[Update sStar]
    end

    subgraph Ctrl[Control]
        Param1[Perturbation Strength k]
        Param2[Temperature T or Threshold tau]
        Stop[Stopping Criterion: time or iterations]
    end

    I3 --> P
    U --> P
    Param1 -.-> P
    Param2 -.-> A
    Stop -.-> Loop

    Param1[Perturbation Strength k]
    Param2[Temperature T or Threshold tau]
    Stop[Stopping Criterion]
```
