The following Mermaid flowchart captures the full algorithmic lifecycle of Ant System. The diagram emphasizes the **cyclic nature** of the algorithm (construct → evaluate → update → repeat) and the **separation between evaporation and deposit phases** in the pheromone update.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([START])
    nodeInit["Initialize Parameters m, alpha, beta, rho, Q, tau0"]
    nodePlace["Place m ants on random starting nodes"]
    nodeTour["Each ant builds a complete tour using random proportional rule"]
    nodeEval["Compute tour length Lk for each ant"]
    nodeLocal["Optional Local Search Improvement"]
    nodeBest["Identify iteration-best tour Lbest"]
    nodeEvap["Pheromone Evaporation: tau = 1 minus rho times tau"]
    nodeDeposit["Pheromone Deposit on edges in Tbest with Q divided by Lbest"]
    nodeCheck{"Termination criterion met?"}
    nodeOutput([OUTPUT Best Solution Found])
    nodeLoop["Proceed to next iteration t plus 1"]

    nodeStart --> nodeInit
    nodeInit --> nodePlace
    nodePlace --> nodeTour
    nodeTour --> nodeEval
    nodeEval --> nodeLocal
    nodeLocal --> nodeBest
    nodeBest --> nodeEvap
    nodeEvap --> nodeDeposit
    nodeDeposit --> nodeCheck
    nodeCheck -- YES --> nodeOutput
    nodeCheck -- NO --> nodeLoop
    nodeLoop --> nodePlace
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Ant System

This diagram presents the **engineering architecture** — the three functional blocks (Environment, Ants, Coordinator) and their interactions via **pheromone (stigmergy)** and **objective feedback**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeEnv["ENVIRONMENT BLOCK\nConstruction Graph G with Cities C, Edges E, and Distance Matrix d"]
    nodePhero["PHEROMONE MATRIX BLOCK\nStores tau values for every edge; updated via evaporation plus deposit"]
    nodeAnt["ANT AGENT BLOCK\nSenses tau and eta; selects next node via random proportional rule"]
    nodeObj["OBJECTIVE EVALUATOR BLOCK\nComputes tour length Lk and identifies Lbest"]
    nodeLoop2["ITERATION COORDINATOR BLOCK\nManages t counter, termination logic, best-so-far tracker"]

    nodeEnv -- heuristic info eta --> nodeAnt
    nodePhero -- pheromone tau --> nodeAnt
    nodeAnt -- constructs solution --> nodeObj
    nodeObj -- Lk and Lbest --> nodePhero
    nodeObj -- best solution --> nodeLoop2
    nodeLoop2 -- stop or continue --> nodeAnt
```

## 4.3 Pheromone Update — Evaporation vs Deposit Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn["Input: Pheromone Matrix tau from iteration t"]
    nodeEvapStep["EVAPORATION PHASE\nFor every edge r,s:\ntau_new = 1 minus rho times tau_old"]
    nodeCheckUse{"Did ant k traverse edge r,s?"}
    nodeDepYes["DEPOSIT PHASE\nAdd Q divided by Lk to tau_new"]
    nodeDepNo["NO DEPOSIT\ntau_new remains unchanged"]
    nodeNext["Move to next edge r,s"]
    nodeDone["Output: Updated Pheromone Matrix for iteration t plus 1"]

    nodeIn --> nodeEvapStep
    nodeEvapStep --> nodeCheckUse
    nodeCheckUse -- YES --> nodeDepYes
    nodeCheckUse -- NO --> nodeDepNo
    nodeDepYes --> nodeNext
    nodeDepNo --> nodeNext
    nodeNext --> nodeDone
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: In the KTU board exam, you can reproduce this three-phase flow (Construction → Update → Termination) to score full marks on a "describe the Ant System algorithm" question. Mermaid is not required in the answer sheet, but a labelled three-block sketch is.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1. Define Ant System. List its key components. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Definition (1 mark):** Ant System is a population-based swarm intelligence metaheuristic proposed by Marco Dorigo in 1992, inspired by the pheromone-mediated foraging behaviour of real ants. It is used to solve combinatorial optimization problems.

**Key Components (2 marks):**
1. **Construction Graph** — represents the problem as a graph whose nodes are solution components and whose edges are possible connections.
2. **Pheromone Model** — a matrix $\tau(r,s)$ storing trail intensity on each edge; updated via evaporation and deposit.
3. **Heuristic Information** $\eta(r,s)$ — problem-specific greedy information (e.g., $1/d(r,s)$ for TSP).
4. **Probabilistic Transition Rule** — the random proportional rule used by each ant to build a solution.
5. **Pheromone Update Rule** — combines evaporation $(1-\rho)\tau$ and deposit $Q/L_k$.

---

### Question 2. Differentiate between Ant-Density, Ant-Quantity, and Ant-Cycle variants. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Variant | Deposit Formula | When Applied | Uses $L_k$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ant-Density** | $\Delta\tau(r,s) = Q$ | Per edge (online) | No |
| **Ant-Quantity** | $\Delta\tau(r,s) = Q/d(r,s)$ | Per edge (online) | No |
| **Ant-Cycle** | $\Delta\tau(r,s) = Q/L_k$ | After full tour (offline) | **Yes** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Ant-Cycle is preferred because it uses **global solution quality** $L_k$, leading to faster convergence and avoiding premature stagnation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Comprehensive Question **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Ant System algorithm** in detail with the **random proportional transition rule**. Discuss the role of parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\rho$ in controlling the search behaviour.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the 4-city TSP with distance matrix given below, compute the **transition probabilities** for an ant starting at city A in the **first iteration**, given $\tau_0 = 1.0$, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$.

$$
d \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} - & 2 & 4 & 5 \\ 2 & - & 3 & 6 \\ 4 & 3 & - & 1 \\ 5 & 6 & 1 & - \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Ant System Algorithm Explained

**Step 1 — Initialization (1 mark):** Set algorithm parameters $m$, $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\rho$, $Q$. Initialize pheromone trails: $\tau(r,s) = \tau_0$ for all $(r,s) \in E$. Place each of the $m$ ants on a randomly chosen starting city.

**Step 2 — Solution Construction (2 marks):** Each ant $k$ constructs a tour by repeatedly selecting the next city using the **random proportional rule**:

$$
P_k(r,s) \;=\; \frac{[\tau(r,s)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta(r,s)]^{\beta}}{\sum_{u \in J_k(r)} [\tau(r,u)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta(r,u)]^{\beta}}
$$

where $\eta(r,s) = 1/d(r,s)$ and $J_k(r)$ is the set of unvisited cities from $r$.

**Step 3 — Tour Evaluation (1 mark):** Each ant computes its total tour length $L_k$.

**Step 4 — Pheromone Update (2 marks):**

- **Evaporation** applied to every edge:

$$
\tau(r,s) \leftarrow (1-\rho)\,\tau(r,s)
$$

- **Deposit** by ants (Ant-Cycle):

$$
\tau(r,s) \leftarrow \tau(r,s) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_k(r,s), \quad \Delta \tau_k(r,s) = \frac{Q}{L_k} \text{ if } (r,s) \in T_k
$$

**Step 5 — Termination (1 mark):** If a stopping criterion (max iterations, time limit, or convergence) is met, output the best tour found; otherwise return to Step 2.

**Role of Parameters:**
- **$\alpha$ (pheromone influence)**: Higher $\alpha$ → ants rely more on **collective memory** → faster convergence but higher stagnation risk.
- **$\beta$ (heuristic influence)**: Higher $\beta$ → ants favour **short edges greedily** → faster initial improvement but may miss globally optimal tours.
- **$\rho$ (evaporation rate)**: Higher $\rho$ → faster forgetting → more exploration; lower $\rho$ → slower forgetting → more exploitation.

#### Part (b) — Computation of Transition Probabilities

**Step 1 — Compute heuristic values** $\eta(r,s) = 1/d(r,s)$:

$$
\eta(A,B) = 0.500, \quad \eta(A,C) = 0.250, \quad \eta(A,D) = 0.200
$$

**Step 2 — Compute scores** with $\tau_0 = 1.0$, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$:

$$
\text{score}(A,B) = (1.0)^1 \cdot (0.500)^2 = 0.2500
$$

$$
\text{score}(A,C) = (1.0)^1 \cdot (0.250)^2 = 0.0625
$$

$$
\text{score}(A,D) = (1.0)^1 \cdot (0.200)^2 = 0.0400
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of scores**:

$$
S = 0.2500 + 0.0625 + 0.0400 = 0.3525
$$

**Step 4 — Transition probabilities**:

$$
P(A,B) = \frac{0.2500}{0.3525} = 0.7092
$$

$$
P(A,C) = \frac{0.0625}{0.3525} = 0.1773
$$

$$
P(A,D) = \frac{0.0400}{0.3525} = 0.1135
$$

**Conclusion**: The ant at city A chooses city **B** with probability $\approx 70.9\%$, because edge A–B is shortest ($d=2$). The high $\beta = 2$ amplifies the heuristic (greedy) component.

---

### Question B — Alternative Question Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **stigmergy**? Explain the role of **pheromone evaporation and deposit** in Ant System with the Ant-Cycle update rule. Why is Ant-Cycle preferred over Ant-Density and Ant-Quantity?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of Ant System. Compare Ant System with a **genetic algorithm** (GA) on at least four dimensions.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Stigmergy and Pheromone Dynamics

**Stigmergy (1 mark):** Stigmergy is a mechanism of **indirect coordination** between agents through modifications of the environment. In AS, an ant modifies the environment by depositing pheromone on edges; this modification influences the future behaviour of other ants. No direct communication between ants is needed.

**Pheromone Evaporation (2 marks):** Evaporation is the **decay** of pheromone intensity over time, modelled as:

$$
\tau(r,s) \leftarrow (1-\rho)\,\tau(r,s)
$$

It prevents **unbounded accumulation** and encourages **forgetting** of poor historical choices. Without evaporation, the algorithm would stagnate on early-found sub-optimal solutions.

**Pheromone Deposit — Ant-Cycle (2 marks):** After each ant $k$ completes its tour of length $L_k$, deposit:

$$
\tau(r,s) \leftarrow \tau(r,s) + \frac{Q}{L_k}, \quad \forall (r,s) \in T_k
$$

Critically, deposit is **delayed until the complete tour is built**, allowing the ant to use **global solution quality** as the reward signal.

**Why Ant-Cycle is preferred (2 marks):**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Global feedback** | Uses $L_k$ (whole tour), so edges in shorter tours receive stronger reinforcement. |
| **Lower stagnation risk** | No online reward for each individual edge avoids reinforcing intermediate biases. |
| **Better empirical performance** | Dorigo's experiments on TSP showed Ant-Cycle outperforms the other two variants consistently. |
| **Closer to biological analogy** | Real ants deposit pheromone along the *complete* trail, not per-segment. |

#### Part (b) — AS vs GA Comparison

| Dimension | Ant System | Genetic Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Inspiration** | Ant foraging behaviour | Darwinian evolution |
| **Solution representation** | Constructive (build solution component-by-component) | Population of complete candidate solutions |
| **Variation operators** | Stochastic node selection + pheromone-mediated bias | Crossover + Mutation |
| **Memory mechanism** | Pheromone matrix (explicit collective memory) | None inherently; elitism is optional |
| **Handling of dynamic problems** | Excellent — pheromone adapts naturally to changing costs | Poor — needs re-initialization |
| **Convergence speed on TSP** | Fast (good for medium-scale TSP) | Slow but reliable |
| **Parallelism** | Naturally parallel (each ant is independent) | Population-level parallel |
| **Parameter sensitivity** | High ($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\rho$, $Q$) | Moderate (crossover rate, mutation rate) |

**Advantages of AS (1 mark):**
- Positive feedback → rapid discovery of good solutions.
- Distributed computation → inherent parallelism.
- Works well on dynamic and stochastic problems.

**Limitations of AS (1 mark):**
- Slower convergence than more advanced ACO variants (ACS, MMAS).
- Sensitive to parameter tuning; no universal parameter set.
- Theoretical convergence proofs are weaker than for some exact methods.
- Can suffer from **stagnation** (premature convergence on sub-optimal tours).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Ant System Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\tau$ and $\eta$** — Students often interchange pheromone and heuristic. Remember: **$\tau$ is dynamic (updated each iteration); $\eta$ is static (computed once from problem data).**
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization in transition rule** — Board answers frequently omit the denominator $\sum [\tau]^{\alpha}[\eta]^{\beta}$. Without it, the formula is worth zero marks.
> 3. **Writing Ant-Density/Quantity formulas in Ant-Cycle questions** — Examiners check the *exact* deposit formula. If the question says "Ant-Cycle", the deposit **must** be $Q/L_k$, not $Q$ or $Q/d(r,s)$.
> 4. **Not labelling units or iteration indices** — Always write $t$, $t+1$, and define each symbol at first use.
> 5. **Missing the evaporation step before deposit** — Pheromone update has **two sequential phases**: evaporation *first*, then deposit. Reversing them changes the result.
> 6. **Skipping the working for probability calculations** — Show every score, the sum, and the division. A final probability answer alone earns partial credit only.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Ant System**
>
> - **Ant System (AS)** is the **first** ACO algorithm; proposed by **Dorigo (1992)**.
> - AS is a **population-based, constructive, probabilistic** metaheuristic.
> - Three variants exist: **Ant-Density** ($Q$), **Ant-Quantity** ($Q/d$), **Ant-Cycle** ($Q/L_k$). **Ant-Cycle is the standard.**
> - **Transition rule**: $P_k(r,s) = \tau^{\alpha} \eta^{\beta} / \sum \tau^{\alpha} \eta^{\beta}$ — always normalize.
> - **Heuristic for TSP**: $\eta(r,s) = 1/d(r,s)$ — static, problem-dependent.
> - **Update rule**: evaporate first $(1-\rho)\tau$, then deposit $Q/L_k$ on edges of best tour.
> - **Parameter roles**: $\alpha$ → pheromone bias, $\beta$ → heuristic bias, $\rho$ → evaporation rate.
> - **Stigmergy** = indirect communication via environment modification (pheromone).
> - **Positive feedback** (deposit) accelerates convergence; **negative feedback** (evaporation) prevents stagnation.
> - **Typical parameter ranges**: $\alpha \in [1,2]$, $\beta \in [2,5]$, $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$, $m = n$ (one ant per city).
> - **Convergence bound**: Pheromone on best edge is bounded by $1/(1-\rho)$ in Ant-Cycle.
> - AS limitations: **stagnation**, parameter sensitivity, slower than ACS/MMAS — solved by later variants.
> - **Real-world uses**: TSP, VRP, network routing, scheduling, bioinformatics, power systems.
> - **Equation writing tip**: Use `\mid` or `\vert` for absolute value in tables; never use the broken pipe `\vert` inside LaTeX-style math in markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles of ACO - Pheromone Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of ACO - Pheromone Deposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a population-based metaheuristic introduced by **Marco Dorigo** (1992) inspired by the foraging behavior of real ants. The **Pheromone Deposition** mechanism is the central learning component of ACO, where artificial ants probabilistically construct solutions and deposit a synthetic chemical substance called **pheromone** ($\tau$) on the components (edges/nodes) of the graph they traverse.

The pheromone trail acts as a **distributed, numerical information** that encodes the collective search experience of the colony. Pheromone accumulation is governed by two opposing processes:

1. **Pheromone Reinforcement (Deposition):** Successful ants increase the pheromone on edges they traverse.
2. **Pheromone Evaporation (Decay):** All pheromone values decay uniformly over time, preventing premature convergence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, pheromone deposition is studied as the **positive feedback operator** that converts a stochastic constructive heuristic into a learning algorithm. It is classified as a **memory-based intensification strategy** within swarm intelligence.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a colony of **real ants** moving from their nest to a food source. Initially, ants wander randomly. Ants laying down a chemical called **pheromone** on the ground. Shorter paths get traversed more frequently per unit time, so pheromone accumulates faster on them. Other ants, sensing stronger pheromone, prefer shorter paths. This creates a **self-reinforcing loop** until nearly all ants follow the shortest route. 

In the digital twin (ACO), each "ant" is a software agent building a tour on a graph (e.g., cities for TSP). The "pheromone" is a number $\tau_{ij}$ stored in a table. Higher $\tau_{ij}$ means edge $(i,j)$ is "smelled" more strongly and is more likely to be chosen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Pheromone is *not* the distance itself; it is the *learned desirability* of using a component based on the **historical success** of past solutions.

### 1.3 Standard Parameters and Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Value |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $\tau_{ij}$ | Pheromone intensity on edge $(i,j)$ | $\tau_{ij} > 0$ |
| $\rho$ | Pheromone evaporation coefficient | $(0, 1)$, typically **0.5** |
| $\alpha$ | Pheromone influence exponent | $\geq 0$, typically **1.0** |
| $\beta$ | Heuristic influence exponent | $\geq 0$, typically **2.0 to 5.0** |
| $Q$ | Pheromone deposit constant | Tunable (e.g., **100** for TSP) |
| $m$ | Number of ants in colony | Typically equal to number of nodes $n$ |
| $L_k$ | Tour length of ant $k$ | Problem-dependent |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Pheromone Deposition (Step-by-Step Bulleted Logic)

The pheromone mechanism operates in a tightly coupled three-stage loop:

**Stage 1 — Initialization:**
- All edges are initialized with a small uniform pheromone value $\tau_0$ (often $\tau_0 = \frac{m}{C^{nn}}$, where $C^{nn}$ is the nearest-neighbor tour length).
- Place $m$ ants on randomly selected (or fixed) starting nodes.

**Stage 2 — Solution Construction (with implicit pheromone usage):**
- Each ant $k$ at node $i$ selects next node $j$ probabilistically using the **state transition rule** (see formula sheet).
- This stage *consumes* pheromone information but does not write to it.

**Stage 3 — Pheromone Update (the actual deposition step):**
- **Step 3a — Evaporation:** All edges lose a fraction $\rho$ of their pheromone. This prevents unlimited accumulation and allows the colony to "forget" poor historical decisions.
- **Step 3b — Deposition:** Each ant $k$ that traversed edge $(i,j)$ deposits an amount $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k$ proportional to the **quality of its solution** $L_k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Evaporation:** Without evaporation ($\rho = 0$), early random luck could lock the colony onto a suboptimal edge forever (a phenomenon called **search stagnation**). Evaporation provides the **exploration pressure** needed to escape local optima.

### 2.2 The Three Classical Ant Models (Dorigo's Original Variants)

Marco Dorigo's seminal 1996 paper defined three ant-system variants based on *when* and *how* pheromone is deposited:

1. **Ant-Density Model:** Constant deposit $Q$ per edge visited, deposited *during* the tour (online step-by-step).
2. **Ant-Quantity Model:** Deposit proportional to $\frac{Q}{d_{ij}}$ (inverse edge length), deposited *during* the tour.
3. **Ant-Cycle Model (Standard AS):** Deposit $\frac{Q}{L_k}$ proportional to the *complete tour quality*, deposited *after* the tour finishes. This is the **standard model** used in all modern ACO.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Boundary Conditions |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Global Pheromone Update** | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$ | $0 < \rho < 1$, $\tau_{ij} \geq \tau_{min}$ |
| **Ant-Cycle Deposit** | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{Q}{L_{k}}$ if $(i,j) \in T_{k}$, else $0$ | $L_k > 0$ |
| **Ant-Density Deposit** | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q$ if $(i,j) \in T_{k}$, else $0$ | Constant per step |
| **Ant-Quantity Deposit** | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{Q}{d_{ij}}$ if $(i,j) \in T_{k}$, else $0$ | $d_{ij} > 0$ |
| **State Transition (Pseudo-Random Proportional)** | $p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha}\,[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{k}(i)}[\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha}\,[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ | $j \in \mathcal{N}_k(i)$ |
| **Heuristic Visibility** | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | $d_{ij} > 0$ |
| **Evaporation Factor** | $(1-\rho)$ | $0 < \rho < 1$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pheromone-based deposition algorithms are deployed in:
- **Logistics & Routing:** UPS, FedEx, Amazon last-mile delivery and warehouse robot path planning.
- **Telecommunications:** Dynamic packet routing in **ad-hoc and sensor networks (MANETs, WSNs)** — AntHocNet protocol.
- **Manufacturing:** Job-shop scheduling and CNC tool-path optimization.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein folding and DNA sequence assembly.
- **VLSI Design:** Wire routing on integrated circuit boards.

The mathematical elegance is that pheromone deposition converts a **NP-hard combinatorial problem** into a manageable **stochastic iterative process** with provable convergence properties under specific conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Global Pheromone Update Equation

We start from first principles. The pheromone update must simultaneously achieve two goals: (a) reward good edges and (b) forget old information. This is modeled as a **linear decay-plus-reward** operator.

**Step 1 — Define the continuous-time decay model.**

If pheromone only evaporated with rate $\rho$ and no ant deposited anything, the differential equation would be:

$$\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{dt} = -\rho\,\tau_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the homogeneous equation.**

Separating variables:

$$\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{\tau_{ij}} = -\rho\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over a discrete iteration interval $[t, t+1]$.**

$$\int_{\tau_{ij}(t)}^{\tau_{ij}(t+1)} \frac{1}{\tau}\,d\tau = -\rho\int_{t}^{t+1} d\xi$$

Evaluating the integrals:

$$\ln\!\bigl(\tau_{ij}(t+1)\bigr) - \ln\!\bigl(\tau_{ij}(t)\bigr) = -\rho$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\tau_{ij}(t+1)}{\tau_{ij}(t)}\right) = -\rho$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate to isolate the decay factor.**

$$\frac{\tau_{ij}(t+1)}{\tau_{ij}(t)} = e^{-\rho}$$

For small $\rho$, the Taylor expansion $e^{-\rho} \approx 1-\rho$ gives the standard discrete decay factor used in practice:

$$\tau_{ij}^{\text{decayed}}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t)$$

**Step 5 — Add the deposition term from $m$ ants (superposition principle).**

Each ant $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ independently deposits $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$. Summing all contributions:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

This is the canonical Ant-System pheromone update rule.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: The Three Original Models

| Property | Ant-Density | Ant-Quantity | Ant-Cycle (Standard AS) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Deposit trigger** | After each edge step | After each edge step | After complete tour |
| **Deposit quantity** | $Q$ (constant) | $Q \,/\, d_{ij}$ | $Q \,/\, L_k$ |
| **Information used** | Local (edge-level) | Local + edge length | Global (tour-level) |
| **Time of update** | Online (within tour) | Online (within tour) | Offline (after tour) |
| **Performance on TSP** | Poor | Poor | **Best (baseline)** |
| **KTU exam relevance** | Definition-only | Definition-only | **Main derivable model** |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Ant-Cycle Update on a 3-Node Path)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **KTU-favorite numerical problem type**. A 3-node, 2-ant colony example appears in nearly every past paper.

**Given:**
- 3 cities: A, B, C
- 2 ants: ant-1 follows A → B → C (tour length $L_1 = 10$)
- Ant-2 follows A → C → B (tour length $L_2 = 15$)
- $Q = 30$, $\rho = 0.2$, initial pheromone $\tau_{AB}(0) = \tau_{BC}(0) = \tau_{AC}(0) = 1.0$

**Step 1 — Compute deposit by each ant (Ant-Cycle rule).**

$$\Delta\tau_{AB}^{1} = \frac{Q}{L_1} = \frac{30}{10} = 3.0$$

$$\Delta\tau_{BC}^{1} = \frac{Q}{L_1} = \frac{30}{10} = 3.0$$

$$\Delta\tau_{AC}^{2} = \frac{Q}{L_2} = \frac{30}{15} = 2.0$$

$$\Delta\tau_{CB}^{2} = \frac{Q}{L_2} = \frac{30}{15} = 2.0$$

**Step 2 — Apply evaporation to each edge.**

$$\tau_{AB}^{\text{decayed}} = (1-0.2)(1.0) = 0.8$$

$$\tau_{BC}^{\text{decayed}} = (1-0.2)(1.0) = 0.8$$

$$\tau_{AC}^{\text{decayed}} = (1-0.2)(1.0) = 0.8$$

**Step 3 — Add deposits (ant-1 traverses AB, BC; ant-2 traverses AC, CB).**

$$\tau_{AB}(1) = 0.8 + 3.0 = 3.8$$

$$\tau_{BC}(1) = 0.8 + 3.0 = 3.8$$

$$\tau_{AC}(1) = 0.8 + 2.0 = 2.8$$

$$\tau_{CB}(1) = 0.8 + 2.0 = 2.8$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

The pheromone on the shorter tour (A→B→C) is now **3.8**, larger than the longer tour's pheromone of **2.8**. The colony has correctly identified A→B→C as the more desirable path. This differential will be amplified in subsequent iterations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the Pheromone Deposition Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Graph and Pheromone Matrix tau_ij] --> B[Place m Ants on Starting Nodes]
    B --> C[Each Ant k Constructs Tour via State Transition Rule]
    C --> D[Compute Tour Length L_k for Each Ant]
    D --> E[Apply Pheromone Evaporation on All Edges]
    E --> F[Each Ant k Deposits Q / L_k on Edges of Its Tour]
    F --> G[Update Pheromone Matrix tau_ij t+1]
    G --> H{Stopping Criterion Met}
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Output Best Tour Found]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The loop from C → D → E → F → G → H → C constitutes **one ACO iteration**. Evaporation (E) is applied *before* deposition (F) to ensure all edges are penalized uniformly for being old, regardless of whether they were just visited.

### 4.2 Comparative Flowchart: Three Ant-Deposit Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Model1 [Ant-Density]
        D1[Ant moves to next node] --> D2[Deposit Q on edge traversed]
        D2 --> D3[Move to next node]
    end
    subgraph Model2 [Ant-Quantity]
        Q1[Ant moves to next node] --> Q2[Deposit Q over d_ij on edge]
        Q2 --> Q3[Move to next node]
    end
    subgraph Model3 [Ant-Cycle]
        C1[Ant completes full tour] --> C2[Compute total L_k]
        C2 --> C3[Deposit Q over L_k on every edge in tour]
    end
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Influence Map of Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pheromone tau_ij] --> R{Role in Algorithm}
    R --> S1[Intensification: Reinforces good edges]
    R --> S2[Diversification: Evaporation forgets bad edges]
    A1[Parameter alpha] --> R
    A2[Parameter beta] --> R
    A3[Parameter rho] --> R
    A4[Parameter Q] --> R
    S1 --> OUT[Solution Quality]
    S2 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** $\alpha$ and $\beta$ act on the *consumption* side (how ants read pheromone), while $\rho$ and $Q$ act on the *production* side (how pheromone is written). KTU questions often ask: *"What happens if $\alpha = 0$?"* Answer: Pheromone is ignored; only greedy heuristic $\eta_{ij}$ matters.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define pheromone deposition in the context of Ant Colony Optimization. Why is pheromone evaporation necessary even though it appears to "destroy" useful information?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Pheromone deposition is the process by which each artificial ant $k$ deposits a quantity $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$ on each edge $(i,j)$ belonging to its constructed solution. In the standard Ant-Cycle model, the deposit is $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / L_{k}$, where $L_k$ is the total tour length of ant $k$.
- **(1 mark)** Evaporation, governed by the factor $(1-\rho)$, prevents the algorithm from converging prematurely to a suboptimal solution.
- **(1 mark)** It allows the colony to "forget" outdated information and explore alternative paths, maintaining **diversification** in the search.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Compare Ant-Density and Ant-Cycle models of pheromone deposition. Which one performs better on the TSP and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Ant-Density deposits a constant amount $Q$ per edge *online* during tour construction, while Ant-Cycle deposits $Q/L_k$ per edge *offline* after the full tour is built.
- **(1 mark)** Ant-Cycle uses **global tour quality** $L_k$ as feedback, which provides a stronger and more consistent learning signal.
- **(1 mark)** Ant-Cycle performs better on TSP because it rewards complete high-quality solutions rather than individual short edges, aligning reinforcement with the global objective.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(a)* Derive the global pheromone update rule of the Ant System from the continuous-time decay model and explain each term with physical meaning. (7 marks)

*(b)* Consider 3 cities A, B, C with the following distance matrix:
$$d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 & 7 \\ 4 & 0 & 5 \\ 7 & 5 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
Two ants start at city A. Ant-1 tours A→B→C with $L_1 = 9$, Ant-2 tours A→C→B with $L_2 = 12$. Given $Q = 60$, $\rho = 0.1$, and $\tau_0 = 1.0$ on all edges, compute the updated pheromone on all edges after **one iteration** using the Ant-Cycle model. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1:** Start with continuous-time decay:
$$\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{dt} = -\rho\,\tau_{ij}$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables and integrate:
$$\int_{\tau_{ij}(t)}^{\tau_{ij}(t+1)} \frac{d\tau}{\tau} = -\rho\int_{t}^{t+1} dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\tau_{ij}(t+1)}{\tau_{ij}(t)}\right) = -\rho$$

**Step 4:** Apply Taylor approximation for small $\rho$:
$$e^{-\rho} \approx 1-\rho \implies \tau_{ij}^{\text{decayed}} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t)$$

**Step 5:** Add superposition of deposits from $m$ ants:
$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

**Physical meanings of each term** (to score full marks):
- $\tau_{ij}(t)$: learned desirability of edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$.
- $(1-\rho)$: fraction of old pheromone that *survives* evaporation.
- $\rho\,\tau_{ij}(t)$: amount evaporated (forgotten).
- $\sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$: total fresh pheromone laid by all ants using edge $(i,j)$.

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute deposits (Ant-Cycle rule):**

$$\Delta\tau_{AB}^{1} = \frac{60}{9} \approx 6.667$$

$$\Delta\tau_{BC}^{1} = \frac{60}{9} \approx 6.667$$

$$\Delta\tau_{AC}^{2} = \frac{60}{12} = 5.0$$

$$\Delta\tau_{CB}^{2} = \frac{60}{12} = 5.0$$

**Step 2 — Apply evaporation (decay factor $1-\rho = 0.9$):**

$$\tau_{AB}^{\text{decayed}} = 0.9 \times 1.0 = 0.9$$

$$\tau_{BC}^{\text{decayed}} = 0.9 \times 1.0 = 0.9$$

$$\tau_{AC}^{\text{decayed}} = 0.9 \times 1.0 = 0.9$$

$$\tau_{CB}^{\text{decayed}} = 0.9 \times 1.0 = 0.9$$

**Step 3 — Add deposits:**

$$\tau_{AB}(1) = 0.9 + 6.667 = 7.567$$

$$\tau_{BC}(1) = 0.9 + 6.667 = 7.567$$

$$\tau_{AC}(1) = 0.9 + 5.0 = 5.9$$

$$\tau_{CB}(1) = 0.9 + 5.0 = 5.9$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Shorter tour (A→B→C) accumulates **7.567** pheromone, while longer tour (A→C→B) gets only **5.9**. The ratio $\frac{7.567}{5.9} \approx 1.28$ indicates a **28% preference** for the shorter path after one iteration.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain the three original Ant System models (Ant-Density, Ant-Quantity, Ant-Cycle) for pheromone deposition. Compare their deposit formulas and information usage. (7 marks)

*(b)* In a 4-node TSP, ant $k$ produces tour A→B→D→C→A of length $L_k = 20$. Using Ant-Cycle with $Q = 100$, compute the pheromone deposited on each edge of this tour. If the evaporation rate is $\rho = 0.5$ and the initial pheromone on all edges was $2.0$, find the updated pheromone value on the edge (B,D) after one iteration. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Ant-Density:** Constant deposit per edge during tour.
$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q \quad \text{if edge used}$$

**Ant-Quantity:** Inverse-distance deposit during tour.
$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \frac{Q}{d_{ij}} \quad \text{if edge used}$$

**Ant-Cycle:** Inverse-tour-length deposit *after* complete tour.
$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \frac{Q}{L_k} \quad \text{if edge used in completed tour}$$

**Comparison Table (for KTU answer scripts):**

| Aspect | Ant-Density | Ant-Quantity | Ant-Cycle |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Information used | None (constant) | Local edge length $d_{ij}$ | Global tour length $L_k$ |
| Update timing | Step-by-step | Step-by-step | End of tour |
| Reinforcement scope | Local | Local | Global |
| Performance on TSP | Weakest | Intermediate | **Strongest** |

**Explanation (1 mark):** Ant-Cycle is best because reinforcement is aligned with the **global optimization objective** (full tour length), not a myopic local view.

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute deposit on each edge of tour A→B→D→C→A.**

Using Ant-Cycle with $Q = 100$ and $L_k = 20$:

$$\Delta\tau_{AB}^{k} = \Delta\tau_{BD}^{k} = \Delta\tau_{DC}^{k} = \Delta\tau_{CA}^{k} = \frac{Q}{L_k} = \frac{100}{20} = 5.0$$

**Step 2 — Apply evaporation to edge (B,D):**

$$\tau_{BD}^{\text{decayed}} = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{BD}(0) = 0.5 \times 2.0 = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Add deposit (only ant $k$ used this edge):**

$$\tau_{BD}(1) = 1.0 + 5.0 = 6.0$$

**Final Answer:** The updated pheromone on edge (B,D) is $\tau_{BD}(1) = 6.0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Students often write the decay factor as $\rho\,\tau_{ij}$ *added* to the sum instead of multiplied. Correct form: $(1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum \Delta\tau$. Writing $\tau_{ij} + \rho\tau_{ij} + \sum\Delta\tau$ is **wrong** — that would mean pheromone *grows* from evaporation.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Confusing Ant-Cycle with Ant-Density. If the question specifies $L_k$ in the denominator, it is **Ant-Cycle**. A constant $Q$ with no denominator is **Ant-Density**.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to apply evaporation to **all** edges, not just the ones ants visited. Evaporation is a global operator; deposition is local.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Writing $\tau_{ij} = Q/L_k$ *without* the $(1-\rho)$ decay term. This is incomplete; full marks require the full update equation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pheromone ($\tau_{ij}$):** A numeric value encoding learned desirability of an edge.
- **Two opposing forces:** Deposition (intensification) vs. Evaporation (diversification).
- **Global Update Rule:** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$.
- **Ant-Cycle is the standard:** Uses global $L_k$, not local $d_{ij}$.
- **Ant-Density:** constant $Q$, step-wise, no distance info.
- **Ant-Quantity:** $Q/d_{ij}$, step-wise, uses local distance.
- **State Transition Rule:** $\alpha$ controls pheromone weight, $\beta$ controls heuristic weight.
- **Evaporation rate $\rho \in (0,1)$:** $\rho \to 0$ means no forgetting (risk of stagnation); $\rho \to 1$ means total forgetting (no learning).
- **Constant $Q$:** Scales deposit amount; often set equal to $C^{nn}$ (nearest-neighbor tour) for TSP.
- **Convergence:** With $\rho \in (0,1)$ and $m \geq 1$, ACO converges in probability to the best-so-far solution.
- **Initialization:** $\tau_0 = m / C^{nn}$ is the canonical starting value for Ant System.
- **Exam Trick:** A numerical problem is *always* either Ant-Cycle or Ant-Density; check which formula they specify.
- **Stigmergy:** The indirect communication mechanism via environmental modification (pheromone) — a KTU-favorite 2-mark definition question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pheromone Updating Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pheromone Updating Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pheromone Updating Rules** are the mathematical mechanisms used within the **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** metaheuristic framework to adaptively modify the artificial pheromone trail values $\tau_{ij}$ associated with edges $(i,j)$ of the solution construction graph. These rules govern the dual processes of **pheromone evaporation** (forgetting) and **pheromone deposition** (reinforcement), thereby implementing the *positive feedback* and *negative feedback* loops that drive the swarm collective intelligence.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, pheromone updating rules form the **memory persistence mechanism** of any ACO variant. They are mathematically classified into two fundamentally opposing operations:

1. **Pheromone Evaporation (Decay):** A uniform multiplicative reduction of all trails to prevent premature convergence and allow the algorithm to "forget" poor historical choices.
2. **Pheromone Deposition (Reinforcement):** A reward signal added to trails proportional to the quality of solutions discovered by ants traversing those edges.

The general canonical form for any pheromone update rule is given by:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}(t)$$

where:
- $\rho \in (0, 1]$ is the **evaporation rate** (a tunable hyperparameter).
- $(1-\rho)$ is the **persistence factor**.
- $\Delta\tau_{ij}(t)$ is the **total pheromone deposit** on edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bakery District Problem.**
> Imagine 100 hungry customers (ants) trying to find the best bakery in a city full of unmarked roads. Each road has an invisible "scent trail" (pheromone). Initially, all roads are equally faint.
>
> - **Pheromone Deposition (Adding Scent):** When a customer finds a bakery with delicious cake, they leave a strong pleasant scent on the roads they walked. Future customers are more likely to follow the stronger scent.
> - **Pheromone Evaporation (Scent Fading):** Every day, all scents fade a little (just like real pheromones evaporate in nature). This ensures that bad bakeries that once seemed popular do not stay popular forever, and new bakeries can be discovered.
>
> The *combination* of these two rules is what makes the swarm intelligent — strong, popular routes get reinforced, but no single route gets monopolized indefinitely.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU-Listed Metrics for Pheromone Updating:**
> - **Evaporation Rate ($\rho$):** Typically in the range $\rho \in [0.01,\ 0.2]$; values below **0.1** are preferred for slow convergence.
> - **Pheromone Constant ($Q$):** A scaling constant, usually set to the **average edge cost** of the problem instance.
> - **Initial Pheromone ($\tau_0$):** Computed as $\tau_0 = \frac{m}{C^{nn}}$ where $m$ = number of ants and $C^{nn}$ = cost of nearest-neighbor heuristic solution.
> - **MMAS Bounds:** $\tau_{min}$ and $\tau_{max}$ constrain trails to prevent stagnation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Phase Operational Logic of Pheromone Updates

The pheromone updating process in any ACO variant follows a strict two-phase logical pipeline:

### Phase A — Pheromone Evaporation (Forgetting Phase)
- **Why?** Prevents the algorithm from getting trapped in a *local optimum*. Without evaporation, the algorithm would converge to the first reasonably good solution and never explore alternatives.
- **How?** Every pheromone trail value is multiplied by the persistence factor $(1-\rho)$ at the end of each iteration:

$$\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) \quad \forall (i,j) \in E$$

- **Engineering Utility:** Analogous to *momentum-based learning rate decay* in neural networks and *adaptive cooling* in simulated annealing.

### Phase B — Pheromone Deposition (Reinforcement Phase)
- **Why?** Implements positive feedback so that high-quality solution components are remembered and exploited in future iterations.
- **How?** Ants (or elite ants) deposit pheromone on the edges they traversed. The amount deposited is inversely proportional to the cost of their solution:

$$\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

where $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$ is the pheromone laid by ant $k$ on edge $(i,j)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates every pheromone updating rule a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the ESE (End Semester Examination).

| **ACO Variant** | **Update Type** | **Mathematical Formula** | **Key Parameters** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **Ant System (AS)** | Global (Post-Iteration) | $\tau_{ij} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$ | $\rho$, $Q$ |
| **Elitist AS (EAS)** | Global + Elite | $\tau_{ij} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} + e\cdot\Delta\tau_{ij}^{bs}$ | $e$ = elite weight, $bs$ = best-so-far |
| **Rank-Based AS (AS$_{\text{rank}}$)** | Global + Ranked | $\tau_{ij} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w-r)\cdot\Delta\tau_{ij}^{r} + w\cdot\Delta\tau_{ij}^{bs}$ | $w$ = number of ranked ants |
| **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | **Local Update** (during construction) | $\tau_{ij} = (1-\varphi)\tau_{ij} + \varphi\cdot\tau_{0}$ | $\varphi \in (0,1]$ is local decay |
| **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | **Global Update** (offline) | $\tau_{ij} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \rho\cdot\Delta\tau_{ij}^{bs}$ | Only the global-best ant updates |
| **Max-Min AS (MMAS)** | Global (Bounded) | $\tau_{ij} = \big[(1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{iter\_best}\big]_{\tau_{min}}^{\tau_{max}}$ | Bounds: $[\tau_{min},\tau_{max}]$ |
| **MMAS (Smoothed)** | Iteration Restart | $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ij} + \delta\cdot(\tau_{max} - \tau_{ij})$ | $\delta \in [0,1]$ reinitialization factor |

### 2.2.1 Pheromone Deposit Quantity Formulations

| **Deposit Model** | **Formula for $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$** | **Best Used For** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Ant Density** | $\frac{Q}{\text{constant}}$ | Discrete combinatorial |
| **Ant quantity** | $\frac{Q}{d_{ij}}$ (edge-cost weighted) | TSP, routing |
| **Ant cycle (Standard)** | $\frac{Q}{L_{k}}$ (tour-cost weighted) | Most practical ACO |

where $L_{k}$ is the total tour length constructed by ant $k$.

## 2.3 Why Different Rules Exist — Engineering Trade-offs

- **Standard AS** is the simplest but suffers from slow convergence on large instances.
- **Elitist/Rank-based AS** accelerates convergence by amplifying the best solution's contribution.
- **ACS dual-update** decouples exploration (local update) from exploitation (global update).
- **MMAS** introduces explicit bounds to prevent **stagnation behavior** (a known failure mode in AS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Perspective:** Whenever a question asks "Compare pheromone updating rules of two ACO variants," the answer MUST contain: (1) the exact formula, (2) the role of the evaporation constant, (3) who is allowed to deposit (all ants / best only / ranked ants), and (4) whether explicit bounds are applied.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Canonical Pheromone Update Rule

We start from the discrete-time pheromone dynamics equation. The pheromone level on an edge at iteration $t+1$ must reflect both:
- The memory of the past value scaled by a persistence factor.
- The cumulative new deposit made by all ants.

**Step 1:** Define the persistence-decay update (evaporation only):

$$\tau_{ij}^{\text{decayed}}(t) = (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$$

**Step 2:** Define the reinforcement term. Each ant $k \in \{1,2,\ldots,m\}$ deposits:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{Q}{L_{k}(t)} & \text{if ant } k \text{ traversed edge } (i,j) \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the contributions of all $m$ ants:

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{\text{total}}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

**Step 4:** Combine decay + deposit into the final canonical form:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)
$$

**Step 5:** Verify boundary conditions:
- If $\rho = 0$ → no evaporation (algorithmic divergence / stagnation).
- If $\rho = 1$ → total evaporation (no memory; equivalent to random search).
- If $m = 0$ → pure evaporation; pheromone decays geometrically to zero.

## 3.2 Derivation of the ACS Dual-Update Mechanism

Ant Colony System (ACS) introduces *two* distinct update rules for distinct purposes.

### 3.2.1 Local Update Rule (Exploration)

**Step 1:** When ant $k$ traverses edge $(i,j)$ during solution construction, immediately modify the trail:

$$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\varphi)\cdot \tau_{ij} + \varphi\cdot \tau_{0}$$

**Step 2:** Expand the expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{ij}^{\text{new}} &= \tau_{ij} - \varphi\cdot\tau_{ij} + \varphi\cdot\tau_{0} \\
&= \tau_{ij} - \varphi(\tau_{ij} - \tau_{0}) \\
&= \tau_{ij} - \varphi\cdot\Delta\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result: the local update *reduces* the trail by a fraction $\varphi$ of the gap between current and initial pheromone. This makes the edge *less attractive* to subsequent ants, encouraging them to explore alternative routes.

### 3.2.2 Global Update Rule (Exploitation)

**Step 1:** At the end of each iteration, only the iteration-best or best-so-far ant deposits:

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{bs} = \frac{1}{L^{bs}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the global update only on edges of the best tour:

$$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij} + \rho\cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bs}$$

**Step 3:** Notice that the global update *increases* pheromone on best-tour edges, reinforcing successful paths while evaporation reduces everything else.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The local update uses decay constant $\varphi$ (often $\approx 0.1$), while the global update uses $\rho$ (often $\approx 0.1$ as well but semantically different). The two operate on different timescales and serve opposite purposes — a classic KTU question.

## 3.3 Derivation of MMAS Bounded Pheromone Update

The Max-Min Ant System enforces explicit trail bounds to avoid stagnation.

**Step 1:** First, compute the unbounded update:

$$\tau_{ij}^{\text{raw}}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{iter\_best}(t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the bounding operator $[\cdot]_{\tau_{min}}^{\tau_{max}}$, defined as:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) =
\begin{cases}
\tau_{max} & \text{if } \tau_{ij}^{\text{raw}} > \tau_{max} \\
\tau_{min} & \text{if } \tau_{ij}^{\text{raw}} < \tau_{min} \\
\tau_{ij}^{\text{raw}} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3:** Derive the theoretical upper bound. Under convergence to the optimal solution $L^{*}$, the trail saturates at:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{(1-\rho)\cdot L^{*}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 4:** Derive the theoretical lower bound using the standard MMAS formula:

$$\tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max}\cdot (1 - \sqrt[n]{p_{best}})}{(n/2 - 1)\cdot \sqrt[n]{p_{best}}}$$

where $p_{best}$ is the probability that an ant would construct the optimal solution at convergence (typically $p_{best} = 0.05$) and $n$ is the number of nodes.

**Step 5:** Justification: The bounds prevent any single edge from dominating the probability distribution, guaranteeing a non-zero probability of selecting any edge in the transition rule:

$$p_{ij}^{k} = \frac{\tau_{ij}^{\alpha} \cdot \eta_{ij}^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_{i}^{k}}\tau_{il}^{\alpha}\cdot\eta_{il}^{\beta}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Ant System on 3-Node Graph)

Suppose we have a 3-node TSP-like graph with edges: (1,2), (2,3), (1,3). Initial trails $\tau_{12}=\tau_{23}=\tau_{13}=1.0$, $\rho=0.1$, $Q=100$, $m=2$ ants.

**Ant 1's tour:** 1 → 2 → 3 (cost $L_1 = 15$)
**Ant 2's tour:** 1 → 3 → 2 (cost $L_2 = 25$)

**Step 1:** Calculate deposits:

$$\Delta\tau_{12}^{1} = \Delta\tau_{23}^{1} = \frac{100}{15} = 6.667$$

$$\Delta\tau_{13}^{2} = \Delta\tau_{32}^{2} = \frac{100}{25} = 4.000$$

**Step 2:** Compute combined deposits per edge:

$$\Delta\tau_{12} = 6.667, \quad \Delta\tau_{23} = 6.667 + 4.000 = 10.667, \quad \Delta\tau_{13} = 4.000$$

**Step 3:** Apply evaporation:

$$(1-\rho)\cdot\tau_{ij} = 0.9 \times 1.0 = 0.9 \text{ for all edges}$$

**Step 4:** Final update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{12}^{\text{new}} &= 0.9 + 6.667 = 7.567 \\
\tau_{23}^{\text{new}} &= 0.9 + 10.667 = 11.567 \\
\tau_{13}^{\text{new}} &= 0.9 + 4.000 = 4.900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Observe that $\tau_{23}$ has the highest value, correctly reflecting that both ants traversed edge (2,3) — a strong reinforcement signal.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Pheromone Update Pipeline

The following Mermaid block maps the operational interaction between the major components of a generic pheromone-update mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Iteration Start"] --> nodeB["Solution Construction Phase"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Ant k Constructs Tour using Probabilistic Rule"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Local Pheromone Update Required"}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE["Apply ACS Local Rule tau_ij minus phi times gap"]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF["Skip to Iteration End"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Evaluate Tour Cost L_k"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Identify Best So Far Tour L_bs"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Phase A Evaporation Phase"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["tau_ij equals 1 minus rho times tau_ij for all edges"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"ACO Variant Selection"}
    nodeK -- AS --> nodeL["All m ants deposit Delta tau"]
    nodeK -- EAS --> nodeM["All ants plus Elite Ant deposit"]
    nodeK -- ACS --> nodeN["Only Best So Far deposits rho times Delta"]
    nodeK -- MMAS --> nodeO["Bounded deposit clipped to tau_min and tau_max"]
    nodeL --> nodeP["Pheromone Trail Matrix Updated"]
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ{"Convergence Check Stagnation Test"}
    nodeQ -- NOT CONVERGED --> nodeA
    nodeQ -- CONVERGED --> nodeR["Output Best Solution L_bs"]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Schematic of the Four Major Update Variants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "STANDARD ANT SYSTEM"
        AS1["All m ants deposit"]
        AS2["Uniform evaporation across all edges"]
    end

    subgraph "ELITIST ANT SYSTEM"
        EAS1["All m ants deposit"]
        EAS2["Elite ant e deposits extra weight"]
        EAS3["Uniform evaporation"]
    end

    subgraph "ANT COLONY SYSTEM"
        ACS1["Local update during construction"]
        ACS2["Global update by best only"]
        ACS3["Different decay constants phi and rho"]
    end

    subgraph "MAX MIN ANT SYSTEM"
        MMAS1["Single best ant deposits"]
        MMAS2["Explicit bounds tau_min and tau_max"]
        MMAS3["Optional smoothing on restart"]
    end

    AS1 --> AScommon["Final tau matrix"]
    AS2 --> AScommon
    EAS1 --> EAScommon["Final tau matrix"]
    EAS2 --> EAScommon
    EAS3 --> EAScommon
    ACS1 --> ACScommon["Final tau matrix"]
    ACS2 --> ACScommon
    ACS3 --> ACScommon
    MMAS1 --> MMAScommon["Final tau matrix"]
    MMAS2 --> MMAScommon
    MMAS3 --> MMAScommon
```

## 4.3 Pheromone Evolution Timeline (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT0["t equals 0 tau_ij equals tau_0 uniform across edges"]
    nodeT1["t equals 1 Evaporation reduces all by factor 1 minus rho"]
    nodeT2["Ants construct tours using stochastic rule"]
    nodeT3["Best ants deposit pheromone Delta tau"]
    nodeT4["t equals 2 Pheromone landscape shows peaks on good edges"]
    nodeT5["t equals T Final convergence tau landscape reflects optimal tour"]

    nodeT0 --> nodeT1 --> nodeT2 --> nodeT3 --> nodeT4 --> nodeT5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]
**Q: Define pheromone evaporation. Why is it essential in the Ant Colony Optimization algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Pheromone evaporation is the operation in which the pheromone trail value on every edge of the construction graph is multiplicatively reduced at the end of each iteration, governed by the persistence factor $(1-\rho)$:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$$

It is essential because it (1) prevents the algorithm from converging prematurely to a sub-optimal solution (a phenomenon called *stagnation*), (2) allows the colony to "forget" outdated or misleading pheromone information, and (3) maintains a non-zero probability of exploring all edges, ensuring algorithmic diversity. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + formula, 1 for stagnation prevention, 1 for exploration).**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between the local update rule and the global update rule in Ant Colony System (ACS).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **local update** is applied *during solution construction* by every ant on edges it just traversed, using the constant $\varphi$, and aims to **discourage** subsequent ants from taking the same edge (promotes exploration). The **global update** is applied *only after all ants have completed their tours*, only on edges of the best-so-far tour, using constant $\rho$, and aims to **reinforce** the globally best edges (promotes exploitation). The local update keeps trails close to $\tau_0$, while the global update amplifies differences. **(3 marks: 1 for local purpose, 1 for global purpose, 1 for distinguishing role).**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing the update formula without the evaporation term $(1-\rho)$. This loses **2 marks** instantly.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $\rho$ (evaporation) with $\varphi$ (ACS local decay). They serve different purposes.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the bounds $[\tau_{min},\tau_{max}]$ in MMAS. Always explicitly mention them.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not specifying *which ants* are depositing pheromone — a critical differentiator between AS, EAS, ACS, and MMAS.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Writing $(1-\rho)$ as $(1+\rho)$ or omitting parentheses — sloppy notation costs marks in KTU valuation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the pheromone update rule in the Standard Ant System (AS) with a complete mathematical formulation. Discuss the role of each parameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the modified pheromone update rule for the **Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)** and show how the bounds $\tau_{min}$ and $\tau_{max}$ are theoretically determined. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Pheromone Update in Standard Ant System (7 Marks)**

The Standard Ant System uses a single global pheromone update applied at the end of each iteration. All $m$ ants deposit pheromone proportional to the quality of their tour. The complete update is:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

where $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t) = Q/L_k$ if ant $k$ used edge $(i,j)$ and 0 otherwise, $L_k$ is the cost of ant $k$'s solution, and $Q$ is the pheromone constant.

**Role of Parameters (each worth a mark):**
- **$\rho \in (0,1]$:** Evaporation rate — controls how quickly old pheromone fades. Higher $\rho$ = faster forgetting.
- **$(1-\rho)$:** Persistence factor — fraction of last iteration's trail that survives.
- **$Q$:** Pheromone deposit constant — scales the magnitude of reinforcement.
- **$L_k$:** Normalization by tour cost ensures shorter tours deposit more pheromone (positive feedback).
- **$m$:** More ants = stronger collective reinforcement but slower per-iteration computation.

**(b) MMAS Pheromone Update with Bounds (7 Marks)**

MMAS modifies the standard update by (1) allowing only the iteration-best or best-so-far ant to deposit, and (2) enforcing explicit trail bounds:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \left[(1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{iter\_best}(t)\right]_{\tau_{min}}^{\tau_{max}}
$$

where $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{iter\_best} = 1/L^{iter\_best}$ if edge $(i,j)$ is in the best tour, else 0.

**Derivation of $\tau_{max}$:** Under convergence to optimal $L^*$, all edges in the optimal tour receive reinforcement $1/L^*$ at every iteration. The fixed-point equation $\tau_{ij} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + 1/L^*$ gives:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^*}$$

**Derivation of $\tau_{min}$:** To ensure a minimum exploration probability $p_{best}$, the standard MMAS formula is:

$$\tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max}\cdot (1 - \sqrt[n]{p_{best}})}{(n/2 - 1)\cdot \sqrt[n]{p_{best}}}$$

This lower bound prevents any trail from reaching zero, guaranteeing continuous exploration of the search space. **(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for $\tau_{max}$, 2 for $\tau_{min}$, 1 for justification).**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a clear mathematical formulation, explain the **dual pheromone update mechanism** of Ant Colony System (ACS). How does it differ from the Standard AS in convergence behavior? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following problem: Consider a 4-node TSP graph where two ants construct the following tours with initial pheromone $\tau_0 = 1.0$ on every edge, $\rho = 0.2$, and $Q = 100$.
- Ant 1 tour: 1→2→3→4→1, length $L_1 = 12$
- Ant 2 tour: 1→3→2→4→1, length $L_2 = 18$

Compute the updated pheromone on edges (1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,1), (1,3), and (2,4) after one iteration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ACS Dual Update Mechanism (7 Marks)**

ACS employs two distinct update rules:

**Local Update (during construction, applied by every ant on traversed edge):**

$$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\varphi)\cdot \tau_{ij} + \varphi\cdot \tau_{0}$$

**Global Update (offline, applied only by best-so-far ant on its tour):**

$$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij} + \rho\cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bs}, \quad \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bs} = \frac{1}{L^{bs}}$$

**Convergence Difference from Standard AS:** Standard AS uses a *single* update with all ants depositing, leading to slow convergence and high exploration. ACS achieves faster convergence because (1) the local update actively *discourages* the next ant from re-using a recently-taken edge, increasing exploration diversity, while (2) the global update *strongly reinforces* only the globally best tour edges. The use of $\varphi < \rho$ in practice makes the local effect subtle while the global effect dominates, biasing the search toward exploitation without losing all exploration. **(7 marks: 3 for local formula + role, 3 for global formula + role, 1 for convergence comparison).**

**(b) Numerical Pheromone Update (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute deposits per ant.

Ant 1 traverses: (1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,1)

$$\Delta\tau_{12}^{1} = \Delta\tau_{23}^{1} = \Delta\tau_{34}^{1} = \Delta\tau_{41}^{1} = \frac{100}{12} = 8.333$$

Ant 2 traverses: (1,3), (3,2), (2,4), (4,1)

$$\Delta\tau_{13}^{2} = \Delta\tau_{32}^{2} = \Delta\tau_{24}^{2} = \Delta\tau_{41}^{2} = \frac{100}{18} = 5.556$$

**Step 2:** Combined deposits per edge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\tau_{12} &= 8.333 \\
\Delta\tau_{23} &= 8.333 \\
\Delta\tau_{34} &= 8.333 \\
\Delta\tau_{41} &= 8.333 + 5.556 = 13.889 \\
\Delta\tau_{13} &= 5.556 \\
\Delta\tau_{24} &= 5.556 \\
\Delta\tau_{32} &= 5.556 \quad (\text{same as } \tau_{23})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply evaporation $(1-\rho) = 0.8$.

$$\tau_{ij}^{\text{evap}} = 0.8 \times 1.0 = 0.8 \text{ for all edges}$$

**Step 4:** Final updated pheromones.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{12}^{\text{new}} &= 0.8 + 8.333 = 9.133 \\
\tau_{23}^{\text{new}} &= 0.8 + 8.333 + 5.556 = 14.689 \\
\tau_{34}^{\text{new}} &= 0.8 + 8.333 = 9.133 \\
\tau_{41}^{\text{new}} &= 0.8 + 13.889 = 14.689 \\
\tau_{13}^{\text{new}} &= 0.8 + 5.556 = 6.356 \\
\tau_{24}^{\text{new}} &= 0.8 + 5.556 = 6.356
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Observation: Edges (2,3) and (4,1) have the highest pheromone — they are shared by both ants, hence maximally reinforced. Edges (1,2), (3,4) only have Ant 1's contribution, while (1,3) and (2,4) only have Ant 2's. **(7 marks: 2 for deposits, 2 for evaporation, 2 for final values, 1 for observation).**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pheromone Updating Rules**
>
> - **Canonical Formula:** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$ — memorize this first.
> - **Evaporation Role:** Prevents stagnation; must be present in every variant.
> - **Standard AS:** All ants deposit; uniform evaporation; slowest convergence.
> - **Elitist AS:** Adds elite ant contribution $e \cdot \Delta\tau^{bs}$ — accelerates convergence.
> - **Rank-Based AS:** Weighted deposit based on rank — hybrid of AS and EAS.
> - **ACS Local Update:** $\tau \leftarrow (1-\varphi)\tau + \varphi\tau_0$ — *decreases* trail (exploration).
> - **ACS Global Update:** $\tau \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau + \rho/L^{bs}$ — *increases* trail (exploitation).
> - **MMAS Bounded Update:** Apply clipping $[\tau_{min},\tau_{max}]$ — prevents stagnation behavior.
> - **MMAS $\tau_{max}$:** Equals $1/(\rho L^{*})$ where $L^{*}$ is the optimal tour cost.
> - **MMAS $\tau_{min}$:** Equals $\tau_{max}(1-\sqrt[n]{p_{best}})/((n/2-1)\sqrt[n]{p_{best}})$ with $p_{best}\approx 0.05$.
> - **Parameter Ranges:** $\rho \in [0.01, 0.2]$, $Q$ usually equals the average edge cost.
> - **Pitfall — Who Deposits:** Always clarify whether *all ants*, *best-so-far*, *iteration-best*, or *ranked ants* deposit.
> - **Pitfall — Local vs Global:** In ACS, local = during construction (every ant); global = after iteration (best only).
> - **Stagnation Behavior:** A failure mode where trails on a single tour dominate → all ants construct identical solutions.
> - **Convergence Guarantee:** MMAS and ACS are proven to converge in probability to the optimal solution under specific parameter regimes.
> - **Engineering Applications:** TSP, VRP, network routing, scheduling, protein folding, graph coloring.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of Solutions and Pheromone Evaporation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Construction of Solutions and Pheromone Evaporation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)**, **Solution Construction** refers to the probabilistic mechanism by which artificial ants incrementally build candidate solutions to a combinatorial optimization problem (e.g., TSP, VRP, scheduling). Each ant moves through a **construction graph**, applying a **state transition rule** that combines *pheromone trail intensity* (learned attractiveness) and *heuristic desirability* (greedy local preference) to choose the next component.

**Pheromone Evaporation** is the dynamic, time-decay update mechanism that reduces the pheromone value on every edge by a constant factor $\rho$ after (or during) each iteration. It implements a *simulated forgetting* process, preventing premature convergence to suboptimal solutions and allowing the colony to escape locally optimal paths.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Real Ants on a Forest Floor**
> Imagine ants searching for the shortest path between their nest and a food source. Initially, every trail is unmarked, so ants wander randomly. The first ant to return via a shorter route deposits more pheromone per unit time (because it returns faster). Other ants smell this trail and prefer it, reinforcing it further — this is **solution construction by positive feedback**.
>
> However, what if the food source moves? A *forgetting mechanism* is needed so that old, stale trails (leading nowhere) slowly fade away. This is exactly what **pheromone evaporation** models: even if no ant traverses a path, its chemical signal weakens over time. Without evaporation, the algorithm would memorize early, possibly bad, decisions forever.

## 1.3 Key Standard Parameters

- **$\rho$ (Pheromone Evaporation Coefficient)** — typically $0 \leq \rho \leq 1$ (commonly **0.1 to 0.5**).
- **$\alpha$ (Pheromone Influence Exponent)** — typically $\alpha \geq 0$ (commonly **1**).
- **$\beta$ (Heuristic Influence Exponent)** — typically $\beta \geq 0$ (commonly **2 to 5**).
- **$\tau_{ij}$** — pheromone trail on edge $(i,j)$.
- **$\eta_{ij}$** — heuristic desirability, usually $\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$ for TSP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic sits at the heart of Module 3 (ACO Variants). Examiners almost always test the **transition rule formula** and the **evaporation update formula** together. Memorizing both is non-negotiable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solution Construction — The Operational Logic

The construction of a solution proceeds in three logical phases:

1. **Initialization:** Ant $k$ is placed on a starting node $i$. The set of unvisited nodes is initialized as the candidate set $J_i^k$.
2. **Iterative Component Selection:** At each node $i$, ant $k$ selects the next node $j \in J_i^k$ using the **probabilistic state transition rule**.
3. **Termination:** The ant stops when a complete feasible solution has been built (e.g., a Hamiltonian tour for TSP).

### 2.1.1 The State Transition Rule (Ant System — AS)

$$
p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in J_i^k} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}
$$

Where:
- $p_{ij}^k(t)$ = probability that ant $k$ moves from node $i$ to node $j$ at iteration $t$.
- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ = pheromone concentration on edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$.
- $\eta_{ij}$ = static heuristic value (often inverse of distance: $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$).
- $\alpha$ = controls the influence of **pheromone** (past learning).
- $\beta$ = controls the influence of **heuristic** (greedy information).
- $J_i^k$ = set of nodes still to be visited by ant $k$ from $i$.

### 2.1.2 Ant Colony System (ACS) Variant — Pseudo-Random Proportional Rule

$$
j = \begin{cases} \arg\max\limits_{l \in J_i^k} \left\{ [\tau_{il}] \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta} \right\}, & \text{if } q \leq q_0 \text{ (exploitation)} \\ J, & \text{otherwise (biased exploration)} \end{cases}
$$

Here $q \in [0,1]$ is a uniform random number and $q_0 \in [0,1]$ is a tunable threshold (typically **0.9**). $J$ is drawn according to the AS probability distribution.

## 2.2 Pheromone Evaporation — The Operational Logic

After all ants have constructed their solutions (and deposited pheromone), an **evaporation step** reduces every pheromone trail uniformly:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}(t)
$$

Or split into two phases for clarity:

### Step 1 — Evaporation (Forgetting)
$$
\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)
$$

### Step 2 — Deposition (Reinforcement)
$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t)
$$

Where the per-ant pheromone deposit is typically:
$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k}, & \text{if ant } k \text{ uses edge } (i,j) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

- $Q$ = a constant (often set to the **number of cities** or **100**).
- $L_k$ = length of ant $k$'s tour.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Evaporation?**
> - **Prevents Stagnation:** Without evaporation, pheromone on a single good edge could grow unboundedly, freezing the colony onto one path.
> - **Enables Exploration:** Evaporation allows older, unexplored edges to "re-emerge" as attractive choices.
> - **Implements Forgetting:** Analogous to biological pheromones that diffuse and decay over time.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Component** | **Formula** | **Parameters / Units** | **Purpose** |
|---------------|-------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Transition Probability (AS)** | $p_{ij}^k = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l}[\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ | $\alpha,\beta \geq 0$ | Probabilistic component selection |
| **ACS Exploitation Rule** | $j = \arg\max \{[\tau_{il}][\eta_{il}]^{\beta}\}$ | $q_0 \in [0,1]$ | Greedy best move choice |
| **Pheromone Evaporation** | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}$ | $0 < \rho < 1$ | Forgetting / decay |
| **Pheromone Deposition** | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow \tau_{ij} + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$ | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = Q/L_k$ | Reinforcement of good edges |
| **Combined Update** | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij} + \Delta\tau_{ij}$ | — | Full iteration update |
| **Heuristic (TSP)** | $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ | $d_{ij}$ = distance | Local greedy info |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Vehicle routing, warehouse robot coordination.
- **Telecommunications:** Network routing (AntNet protocol for packet routing in IP networks).
- **Manufacturing:** Job-shop scheduling and assembly-line balancing.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein folding and DNA sequence assembly.
- **Chip Design:** VLSI wire routing on printed circuit boards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Transition Rule Has the Form It Does

The transition rule is a synthesis of **probability theory (softmax over preferences)** and **multi-criteria decision making (pheromone + heuristic)**.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Raw Attractiveness Score

Ant $k$ at node $i$ perceives each candidate edge $(i,l)$ as having an *attractiveness* proportional to both learned importance and heuristic value:

$$
A_{il}^k \;=\; \left[\tau_{il}\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}
$$

This step explicitly encodes two design intentions:
- **Past learning** ($\tau$): how successful this edge has been for previous ants.
- **Greedy preference** ($\eta$): how promising this edge looks *right now* without any learning.

The exponents $\alpha$ and $\beta$ act as **tuning knobs** to weight the two signals.

### 3.1.2 Normalization into a Valid Probability

Since the ant must select exactly one next node, the scores must be turned into probabilities that sum to 1. The standard approach is **softmax normalization** (also known as the *Boltzmann distribution* in physics):

$$
p_{ij}^k \;=\; \frac{A_{ij}^k}{\sum_{l \in J_i^k} A_{il}^k}
$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting Back

$$
p_{ij}^k(t) \;=\; \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in J_i^k} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Limiting-Case Behaviour

- **If $\alpha = 0$:** Only heuristic matters → pure greedy algorithm.
- **If $\beta = 0$:** Only pheromone matters → blind exploitation of past trails.
- **If both large:** Highly nonlinear — pheromone-dominant edges dominate.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Combined Evaporation + Deposition Update

### 3.2.1 Start with the Evaporation Step

We model evaporation as a multiplicative decay (geometric decay, not additive — this matches the biological chemistry of pheromone diffusion):

$$
\tau_{ij}^{\text{evap}}(t+1) \;=\; (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)
$$

This means: after each iteration, the pheromone on every edge keeps a fraction $(1-\rho)$ of its previous value.

### 3.2.2 Add the Deposition Step

After evaporation, ants deposit fresh pheromone proportional to the *quality* of their tours:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) \;=\; (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t)
$$

Where the individual deposit per ant is:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k(t)}, & \text{if ant } k \text{ traverses edge } (i,j) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Consider 3 nodes $A,B,C$ forming a triangle. An ant at $A$ must choose between $B$ and $C$.
- Pheromones: $\tau_{AB} = 2.0$, $\tau_{AC} = 1.0$.
- Distances: $d_{AB} = 4$, $d_{AC} = 2$.
- Parameters: $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute Heuristic Values:**

$$
\eta_{AB} = \frac{1}{d_{AB}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

$$
\eta_{AC} = \frac{1}{d_{AC}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Raw Attractiveness:**

$$
A_{AB} = [\tau_{AB}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{AB}]^{\beta} = 2.0^1 \cdot (0.25)^2 = 2.0 \cdot 0.0625 = 0.125
$$

$$
A_{AC} = [\tau_{AC}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{AC}]^{\beta} = 1.0^1 \cdot (0.5)^2 = 1.0 \cdot 0.25 = 0.25
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to Probability:**

$$
p_{AB} = \frac{0.125}{0.125 + 0.25} = \frac{0.125}{0.375} = 0.333
$$

$$
p_{AC} = \frac{0.25}{0.125 + 0.25} = \frac{0.25}{0.375} = 0.667
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Evaporation (assume $\rho = 0.2$, after iteration):**

$$
\tau_{AB}^{\text{new}} = (1 - 0.2) \cdot 2.0 = 0.8 \cdot 2.0 = 1.6
$$

$$
\tau_{AC}^{\text{new}} = (1 - 0.2) \cdot 1.0 = 0.8 \cdot 1.0 = 0.8
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The shorter edge $AC$ (with distance 2) has higher probability (0.667) despite having *less* pheromone, because $\eta_{AC}$ is twice $\eta_{AB}$. This shows the heuristic's role in **biasing toward short edges** even early in the search.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effects of $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\rho$

| **Parameter Setting** | **Algorithm Behaviour** | **Risk** |
|-----------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| $\alpha \uparrow\uparrow$, $\beta = 0$ | Pure pheromone follow, ignores distance | Stagnation |
| $\alpha = 0$, $\beta \uparrow\uparrow$ | Pure greedy, ignores learning | Local optima |
| $\alpha \approx \beta$ | Balanced exploration–exploitation | Stable (recommended) |
| $\rho \to 0$ | No forgetting → frozen trails | Premature convergence |
| $\rho \to 1$ | Aggressive forgetting | Oscillation, no learning |
| $\rho \approx 0.1 - 0.3$ | Healthy decay (recommended) | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ACO Iteration

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete interaction between **Solution Construction** and **Pheromone Evaporation** within a single ACO iteration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA[Initialize m ants on n nodes] --> initB[Set initial pheromone tau0 on all edges]
    initB --> construct
    construct[Phase 1: Solution Construction Loop]
    construct --> select{Apply transition rule at node i}
    select --> ant1[Ant k chooses next node j]
    ant1 --> tabu[Update tabu list visited nodes]
    tabu --> check{All nodes visited?}
    check -->|No| select
    check -->|Yes| tour[Ant k completes tour of length Lk]
    tour --> repeat[Repeat for all m ants]
    repeat --> deposit[Phase 2: Pheromone Deposition]
    deposit --> evap[Phase 3: Pheromone Evaporation apply 1 minus rho]
    evap --> bound{Clamp tau in tau_min tau_max}
    bound --> iter{Iteration less than N}
    iter -->|Yes| construct
    iter -->|No| best[Output global best solution]
```

## 4.2 Decomposition Diagram — Solution Construction Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node_i[Current Node i] --> calc[Compute Attractiveness]
    tau[tau_ij alpha] --> calc
    eta[eta_ij beta] --> calc
    calc --> sum[Sum over candidate set J sub i of k]
    sum --> norm[Normalize to probability p_ij sub k]
    norm --> rng{Random number r}
    rng -->|r less than q0| greedy[Greedy exploit best edge]
    rng -->|r greater than q0| prob[Probabilistic exploration]
    greedy --> move[Move ant to next node]
    prob --> move
    move --> node_i
```

## 4.3 Pheromone Lifecycle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[tau_ij at iteration t] --> evap1[Multiply by 1 minus rho]
    evap1 --> deposit{Ant k traverses i to j}
    deposit -->|Yes| add[Add Q divided by Lk]
    deposit -->|No| skip[Add 0]
    add --> clamp[Clamp to tau_min tau_max bounds]
    skip --> clamp
    clamp --> next[tau_ij at iteration t plus 1]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:**
> Examiners award marks for correctly drawing the **three-stage update cycle** (Evaporation → Deposition → Clamping) and labeling the parameters $\rho$, $Q$, $L_k$. Always include the clamp step if you draw MMAS variants.

---
