# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of pheromone evaporation in Ant Colony Optimization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pheromone evaporation is the mechanism by which the pheromone trail on every edge is reduced by a factor $(1-\rho)$ after each iteration:
$$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}$$
It serves three critical purposes:
1. **Prevents stagnation** — avoids unbounded pheromone accumulation on a single path.
2. **Enables exploration** — allows less-travelled edges to regain attractiveness.
3. **Implements forgetting** — eliminates influence of outdated, poor solutions.

(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for explaining the three purposes.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What are the roles of parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the ACO transition rule?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **$\alpha$ (Pheromone Exponent):** Controls the influence of learned pheromone intensity $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}$. Higher $\alpha$ makes the algorithm more **exploitative**, biasing toward edges that previously yielded good solutions.
- **$\beta$ (Heuristic Exponent):** Controls the influence of the greedy heuristic $[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$. Higher $\beta$ makes the algorithm more **greedy**, biasing toward locally promising edges (e.g., shorter distances).
- A balanced choice (e.g., $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$ to $5$) is critical to maintain proper exploration–exploitation trade-off. (1 mark each for $\alpha$ and $\beta$; 1 mark for trade-off statement.)

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the probabilistic state transition rule used by artificial ants in Ant System (AS) for constructing a solution. Clearly explain the meaning of every term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Three nodes $A$, $B$, $C$ are arranged such that ant at $A$ must choose between $B$ and $C$. Given:
- $\tau_{AB} = 3.0$, $\tau_{AC} = 1.0$
- $d_{AB} = 5$, $d_{AC} = 2$
- $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$

Compute the probability of choosing each edge. Also compute the new pheromone values after evaporation with $\rho = 0.1$.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Transition Rule (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the candidate attractiveness. An ant at node $i$ views each neighbour $l$ with raw attractiveness:
$$A_{il} = [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}$$
This combines **past learning** ($\tau$) and **greedy info** ($\eta$), weighted by exponents $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Recognize the need for normalization. Since the ant must choose exactly one node, scores must be normalized to form a probability distribution:
$$p_{ij}^k = \frac{A_{ij}^k}{\sum_{l \in J_i^k} A_{il}^k}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substituting back:
$$p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in J_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$$

**Step 4 (3 marks):** Explain each term:
- $p_{ij}^k(t)$ — probability ant $k$ moves from $i$ to $j$.
- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ — pheromone on edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$.
- $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ — heuristic (inverse distance).
- $\alpha, \beta$ — weighting exponents.
- $J_i^k$ — unvisited nodes from $i$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Heuristic values (1 mark):**
$$\eta_{AB} = 1/5 = 0.2, \quad \eta_{AC} = 1/2 = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Raw attractiveness (2 marks):**
$$A_{AB} = 3.0^1 \cdot (0.2)^2 = 3.0 \cdot 0.04 = 0.12$$
$$A_{AC} = 1.0^1 \cdot (0.5)^2 = 1.0 \cdot 0.25 = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Probabilities (2 marks):**
$$p_{AB} = \frac{0.12}{0.12 + 0.25} = \frac{0.12}{0.37} = 0.324$$
$$p_{AC} = \frac{0.25}{0.12 + 0.25} = \frac{0.25}{0.37} = 0.676$$

**Step 4 — Evaporation update with $\rho = 0.1$ (2 marks):**
$$\tau_{AB}^{\text{new}} = (1 - 0.1) \cdot 3.0 = 0.9 \cdot 3.0 = 2.7$$
$$\tau_{AC}^{\text{new}} = (1 - 0.1) \cdot 1.0 = 0.9 \cdot 1.0 = 0.9$$

**Final Answer:** $p_{AB} = 0.324$, $p_{AC} = 0.676$; updated pheromones $\tau_{AB} = 2.7$, $\tau_{AC} = 0.9$.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete pheromone update mechanism in Ant Colony Optimization, including evaporation and deposition. State the formulas and the role of each parameter.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the Ant System (AS) transition rule with the Ant Colony System (ACS) pseudo-random proportional rule. Under what condition does ACS use the greedy choice, and why is this beneficial?

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Pheromone Update Mechanism (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Evaporation (2 marks):** After all ants complete their tours:
$$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}$$
where $\rho \in (0,1)$ is the **evaporation coefficient**. This implements forgetting of past decisions.

**Step 2 — Deposition (3 marks):** Each ant $k$ deposits pheromone proportional to the quality of its tour:
$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \begin{cases} Q/L_k, & \text{if ant } k \text{ uses edge } (i,j) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
where $Q$ is a constant and $L_k$ is the tour length of ant $k$.

**Step 3 — Combined Update (2 marks):**
$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t)$$

**Role of Parameters:**
- $\rho$: controls forgetting rate (higher → more exploration).
- $Q$: scales the magnitude of reinforcement.
- $m$: number of ants (colony size).

---

#### Part (b) — AS vs ACS Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Ant System (AS) (2 marks):** Uses the **fully probabilistic** transition rule:
$$p_{ij}^k = \frac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_l [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$$
Selection is always stochastic, which provides good exploration but may waste computational effort.

**Step 2 — ACS Pseudo-Random Proportional Rule (3 marks):**
$$j = \begin{cases} \arg\max\limits_{l \in J_i^k} \{[\tau_{il}][\eta_{il}]^{\beta}\}, & q \leq q_0 \\ J \text{ (probabilistic)}, & q > q_0 \end{cases}$$
where $q \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$ and $q_0 \approx 0.9$.

**Step 3 — Comparison and Benefit (2 marks):**
- **Condition for greedy choice:** When the random number $q \leq q_0$ (typically 90% of the time).
- **Benefit:** Exploits the best known edge most of the time, accelerating convergence, while still maintaining a small exploration probability (10%) to avoid stagnation. This hybrid balances exploration–exploitation better than pure AS.

| **Feature** | **AS** | **ACS** |
|-------------|--------|---------|
| Selection | Always probabilistic | Mostly greedy, sometimes probabilistic |
| Threshold | None | $q_0$ |
| Convergence | Slower | Faster |
| Exploration | Always | Tunable via $q_0$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the heuristic:** When $\beta = 2$, students often write $\eta_{ij}$ instead of $[\eta_{ij}]^2$ in the formula. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $\rho$ with $(1-\rho)$:** Evaporation *reduces* pheromone, so the factor is $(1-\rho)$, NOT $\rho$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the sum in the denominator:** Forgetting the normalization step makes the transition rule non-probabilistic. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating the units or value ranges of $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\rho$:** Examiners award 1 mark for parameter discussion — do not skip it.
> 5. **Confusing AS with ACS:** AS is *fully probabilistic*; ACS uses a *pseudo-random proportional* rule with threshold $q_0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Transition Rule (AS):** $p_{ij}^k = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in J_i^k} [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$
- **ACS Rule:** Greedy if $q \leq q_0$; probabilistic otherwise.
- **Evaporation:** $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}$
- **Deposition:** $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = Q/L_k$ for edges used by ant $k$.
- **Combined Update:** Evaporation first, then deposition.
- **Typical Parameters:** $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2\text{–}5$, $\rho = 0.1\text{–}0.3$, $q_0 = 0.9$.
- **Role of Evaporation:** Avoid stagnation, enable exploration, implement forgetting.
- **Role of $\alpha$:** Controls pheromone (past learning) influence.
- **Role of $\beta$:** Controls heuristic (greedy) influence.
- **Balance:** Always discuss the **exploration–exploitation trade-off** in 14-mark answers.
- **Clamping:** In MMAS, pheromones are bounded $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ to prevent divergence.
- **Heuristic for TSP:** $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ — always remember the inverse distance.
- **Formula Memory Trick:** "**TAU-alpha × ETA-beta, divided by SUM of same**" for transition probability.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🐜 Module 3 — Ant System Variants: Elitist Ant System (EAS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elitist Ant System (EAS)** is a strategic enhancement of the canonical **Ant System (AS)** algorithm in which the *best-so-far global tour* is reinforced by an **additional elitist pheromone deposit**. Formally, the pheromone update rule is augmented with an extra term **$e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*$**, where $e$ is a positive integer representing the number of "elite ants" and $\Delta\tau_{ij}^*$ is the contribution from the globally shortest tour found so far. This mechanism intensifies the search pressure around the historically best solution.

The technique was introduced by **Dorigo, Maniezzo, and Colorni** (1991, revised 1996) as the first major variant of Ant System. The word *elitist* borrows from **genetic algorithms**, where elite individuals are preserved across generations to bias the search toward the best-known regions of the solution space.

### 🌍 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine 100 ants searching for the shortest path between their nest and a food source.** Each ant lays a chemical trail (*pheromone*) along the route it takes. Now suppose you appoint **5 special "elite scout ants"** who re-mark the single best path discovered in the entire history of the colony — every single iteration — with an **extra-strong chemical scent**. This means the historically best route becomes *visibly more attractive* than any other path, even on the very first iteration when most ants have not yet explored much.

In this analogy:
- 🐜 Regular ants = Standard AS contribution $\sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$
- 🏅 Elite scout ants = Bonus reinforcement $e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*$
- 🧪 Chemical trail = Pheromone $\tau_{ij}$
- 💨 Evaporation = Decay factor $(1 - \rho)$

The result: the colony **converges faster** toward the best-known path because every iteration is *deliberately nudged* toward exploiting the elite route. This is the central intuition behind the Elitist Ant System.

### 🔑 Key Symbols & Parameters

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $\tau_{ij}$ | Pheromone on edge $(i,j)$ | Initialized small |
| $\eta_{ij}$ | Heuristic visibility ($\approx 1/d_{ij}$) | Problem-dependent |
| $\alpha$ | Pheromone influence exponent | **1.0** |
| $\beta$ | Heuristic influence exponent | **2 to 5** |
| $\rho$ | Pheromone evaporation rate | **0.5** |
| $m$ | Number of ants | Equal to number of cities |
| $e$ | **Number of elite ants** | **5 to 10** (KTU high-yield) |
| $L^*$ | Length of best-so-far tour | Computed dynamically |
| $Q$ | Pheromone constant | **100** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔍 Theoretical Foundation

The Elitist Ant System augments the canonical Ant System with **three core operational pillars**:

### 🧩 Pillar 1 — State Transition Rule (Random-Proportional Selection)

When an ant $k$ at city $i$ must choose the next city $j$, it uses the classical AS probabilistic rule:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\displaystyle\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}, \quad j \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}
$$

where $\mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}$ is the set of yet-unvisited cities by ant $k$ from node $i$.

### 🧩 Pillar 2 — Local Pheromone Update (Optional in EAS)

After each construction step, some variants perform a local update:
$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\varphi)\tau_{ij}(t) + \varphi \cdot \tau_{0}, \quad \varphi \in (0,1]
$$
where $\tau_{0}$ is the initial pheromone level.

### 🧩 Pillar 3 — Global Pheromone Update (The EAS Signature ✨)

After **all** $m$ ants have completed their tours, the global pheromone update is:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t) + \boldsymbol{e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{*}(t)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The bold term $e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{*}$ is the defining feature of EAS.** It represents the *bonus reinforcement* from the best-so-far global tour.

The individual ant deposit and elite deposit are defined as:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_{k}}, & \text{if ant } k \text{ uses edge } (i,j) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{*} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{L^{*}}, & \text{if edge } (i,j) \text{ belongs to } T^{*}\text{ (best-so-far tour)} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation pitfall (KTU examiners love this):** $L_k$ is the length of the tour built by ant $k$ in the current iteration, while $L^{*}$ is the length of the **best global tour found since the start of the algorithm**, not the iteration-best. Many students mistakenly use $L_{ibest}$ (iteration-best) instead of $L^*$ (global best).

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} + e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{*}$ | Global update with elitism | ⭐ **Master Formula** |
| 2 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / L_{k}$ | Ant $k$ pheromone deposit | Used per iteration |
| 3 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{*} = 1 / L^{*}$ | Elite ant contribution | **Distinguishing feature** |
| 4 | $p_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{\tau_{ij}^{\alpha} \cdot \eta_{ij}^{\beta}}{\sum \tau_{il}^{\alpha} \cdot \eta_{il}^{\beta}}$ | State transition probability | Construction phase |
| 5 | $\eta_{ij} = 1 / d_{ij}$ | Heuristic visibility | Static for TSP |
| 6 | $\tau_{0} = m / C^{nn}$ | Initial pheromone (often) | $C^{nn}$ = nearest-neighbour tour length |
| 7 | $T_{k} \ge T_{best} \implies L^{*} \leftarrow L_{k}$ | Update best-so-far | Triggers elitist deposit |
| 8 | $\sum \Delta\tau_{ij} = \sum Q/L_{k} + e/L^{*}$ | Pheromone conservation | Enforces boundedness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** EAS is widely deployed in **vehicle routing, telecom network design, integrated circuit wire routing, scheduling, and bioinformatics** (protein folding, DNA sequencing). The elite mechanism guarantees faster convergence — a critical property in **real-time industrial optimization** where solution quality must be achieved within a fixed computational budget.

### 🎯 Why Elitism Improves Performance (The "Why" Behind the "How")

- **🧠 Memory Effect:** The best tour acts as a *long-term memory*. It survives across iterations via the elite term.
- **🎯 Search Bias:** The search becomes *biased toward exploitation* of the most promising region.
- **⏱ Faster Convergence:** Compared to vanilla AS, EAS typically finds the optimum in **fewer iterations** (empirically 2-3× faster on TSP benchmarks).
- **⚠ Trade-off Risk:** Over-aggressive elitism (large $e$) can cause **premature convergence** to a local optimum — a classic KTU viva question!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 Derivation I — From Vanilla AS to EAS Update Rule

Let us derive the EAS global pheromone update formula step-by-step, beginning with the generic Ant System.

### Step 1 — Start with Vanilla AS Update Rule
The Ant System global update rule is:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)
$$

This says: *"Each edge's pheromone decays by factor $\rho$, then absorbs the sum of deposits made by all $m$ ants."*

### Step 2 — Recognize the Need for Memory
In pure AS, pheromone is purely local to each iteration. There is **no explicit memory** of the best historical solution. To fix this, we *introduce an explicit reinforcement of the globally best tour*.

### Step 3 — Define the Elite Tour
Let $T^{*}(t)$ denote the best tour found from iteration $0$ up to iteration $t$, with length:

$$
L^{*}(t) = \min_{0 \le s \le t} \, L_{best}(s)
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Elite Pheromone Contribution
For every edge $(i,j)$ belonging to $T^{*}$:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{*}(t) = \frac{1}{L^{*}(t)}
$$

If $(i,j) \notin T^{*}$, then $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{*} = 0$.

### Step 5 — Multiply by the Elite Weight
If $e$ elite ants (conceptually) traverse the best-so-far tour, the total elite deposit is:

$$
e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{*}(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{e}{L^{*}(t)}, & (i,j) \in T^{*} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 6 — Combine to Form the EAS Master Equation
Substituting back, we obtain the **complete EAS global update rule**:

$$
\boxed{\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t) + \frac{e}{L^{*}(t)} \cdot \mathbb{1}\left[(i,j) \in T^{*}\right]}
$$

where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function equal to $1$ if the edge belongs to the elite tour, else $0$.

---

## 🧮 Derivation II — Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU Style)

Consider a **5-node TSP instance** with the following pairwise distances:

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 7 & 3 & 6 \\
4 & 0 & 5 & 8 & 2 \\
7 & 5 & 0 & 4 & 9 \\
3 & 8 & 4 & 0 & 5 \\
6 & 2 & 9 & 5 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Let us walk through **one iteration** of EAS with $m = 3$ ants and $e = 2$ elite ants.

### Step 1 — Parameter Initialization
Set $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 1$, $\rho = 0.3$, $Q = 1$, $\tau_{ij}(0) = 0.1$ for all edges.

### Step 2 — Ant Construction (assume tours already built)

| Ant | Tour Sequence | Length $L_k$ |
|-----|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | 1 → 2 → 5 → 4 → 3 → 1 | 20 |
| 2 | 1 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 5 → 1 | 22 |
| 3 | 1 → 2 → 4 → 5 → 3 → 1 | 25 |

### Step 3 — Identify Best-So-Far Tour $T^{*}$
After iteration 1, since $L_1 = 20$ is the minimum, we set:

$$
T^{*} = (1 \to 2 \to 5 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1), \quad L^{*} = 20
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Ant Deposits
For each ant, deposit $Q / L_k$ on every edge it traversed:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{1} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05, \quad \Delta\tau_{ij}^{2} = \frac{1}{22} \approx 0.0455, \quad \Delta\tau_{ij}^{3} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04
$$

For example, on edge $(1,2)$ (traversed by ants 1 and 3):

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{3} \Delta\tau_{1,2}^{k} = 0.05 + 0 + 0.04 = 0.09
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Elite Deposit
Edge $(1,2)$ lies in $T^{*}$, so:

$$
e \cdot \Delta\tau_{1,2}^{*} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{20} = 0.10
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Evaporation + Add Deposits
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{1,2}(1) &= (1 - 0.3) \cdot \tau_{1,2}(0) + 0.09 + 0.10 \\
&= 0.7 \cdot 0.1 + 0.09 + 0.10 \\
&= 0.07 + 0.09 + 0.10 \\
&= 0.26
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Verify Final Pheromone Increment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the elite contribution ($0.10$) **dwarfs** the ant deposit ($0.09$). This illustrates exactly why EAS converges faster than vanilla AS — the elite bonus dominates the update and locks in the best-known solution.

### Step 8 — Repeat for All Edges
The same procedure is applied to every edge, producing the pheromone matrix for the next iteration. This matrix then biases the next round of ant constructions.

---

## 📐 Analytical Matrix: Vanilla AS vs Elitist AS vs Rank-Based AS

| Feature | Vanilla AS | **Elitist AS (EAS)** | Rank-Based AS (AS$_{\text{rank}}$) |
|---------|-----------|----------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Best-tour reinforcement | ❌ None | ✅ **Yes, with weight $e$** | ✅ Yes, weighted by rank |
| Convergence speed | Slow | **Fast (medium-fast)** | Medium |
| Risk of stagnation | Low | **Higher** | Medium |
| Memory of best tour | Implicit only | **Explicit via elite term** | Explicit via rank list |
| Computational overhead | Baseline | **Negligible** | Negligible |
| KTU exam weightage | 3 marks | **14 marks (favoured)** | 7 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 1 — EAS Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize parameters alpha, beta, rho, m, e, Q] --> B[Set tau_ij t0 = tau_0 for all edges]
    B --> C[Initialize best tour L* equals infinity]
    C --> D[Iteration counter t equals 0]
    D --> E[Place m ants on n cities randomly]
    E --> F[For each ant k construct a tour using transition rule]
    F --> G[Compute tour length L_k for each ant]
    G --> H[Find iteration best L_ibest]
    H --> I{Is L_ibest less than L star?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Update L star equals L ibest and T star]
    I -- No --> K[Keep old L star and T star]
    J --> L[Apply global pheromone update with elite term]
    K --> L[Apply global pheromone update with elite term]
    L --> M[Increment t equals t plus 1]
    M --> N{t less than NCmax?}
    N -- Yes --> E
    N -- No --> O[Output best tour T star and L star]
    O --> P[End]
```

## 📊 Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of the EAS Pheromone Update

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1[Ant tours T1 to Tm]
        A2[Best so far tour T star with length L star]
        A3[Current pheromone matrix tau_ij t]
        A4[Parameters rho, e, Q]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        B1[Evaporation module: multiply tau_ij by 1 minus rho]
        B2[Ant deposit module: sum Q over L_k]
        B3[Elite deposit module: compute e over L star for elite edges]
        B4[Adder: combine all three contributions]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1[Updated pheromone matrix tau_ij t plus 1]
        C2[Decision signal for next iteration]
    end
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    A4 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

## 📊 Diagram 3 — Comparison: Ant System vs Elitist Ant System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AS["VANILLA ANT SYSTEM AS"]
        AS1[Pheromone tau_ij] --> AS2[Evaporation 1 minus rho]
        AS2 --> AS3[Ant deposits Q over L_k]
        AS3 --> AS4[New tau_ij for next iteration]
    end
    subgraph EAS["ELITIST ANT SYSTEM EAS"]
        EAS1[Pheromone tau_ij] --> EAS2[Evaporation 1 minus rho]
        EAS2 --> EAS3[Ant deposits Q over L_k]
        EAS3 --> EAS4[ELITE BONUS e over L star]
        EAS4 --> EAS5[New tau_ij for next iteration with elite boost]
    end
    AS -.->|Compare convergence| EAS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** When the examiner asks *"Show pictorially the difference between AS and EAS,"* draw the **right-side augmentation** clearly — this single block addition ($e/L^*$) is the entire conceptual contribution of EAS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q: Define the Elitist Ant System (EAS). How does it differ from the basic Ant System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** EAS is a variant of the Ant System in which the best-so-far global tour is reinforced with an additional pheromone deposit equal to $e/L^*$ on each of its edges, where $e$ is the elite weight and $L^*$ is the length of the best-so-far tour.
- **Difference (1 mark):** Unlike basic AS which only uses per-iteration ant deposits $\sum Q/L_k$, EAS adds an explicit memory term $e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*$ to intensify exploitation of the best-known solution, leading to faster convergence.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the role of the parameter $e$ in Elitist Ant System? What happens if $e$ is set too high?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of $e$ (2 marks):** The parameter $e$ represents the **number (or weight) of elite ants** that virtually traverse the best-so-far tour each iteration. It controls the **strength of reinforcement** given to the globally best path. Larger $e$ means stronger exploitation.
- **Risk of large $e$ (1 mark):** If $e$ is too high, the algorithm suffers from **premature convergence / stagnation behavior**, where all ants are trapped following the elite tour and the algorithm loses its ability to explore alternative (potentially better) solutions.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ Choice A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 3 Choice A]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Elitist Ant System algorithm in detail. Describe the state transition rule and the global pheromone update rule with all mathematical formulations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 4-city TSP with distances:
$$
d_{12}=10,\ d_{13}=15,\ d_{14}=20,\ d_{23}=35,\ d_{24}=25,\ d_{34}=30
$$
Two ants construct tours in iteration 1:
- Ant 1: 1 → 3 → 2 → 4 → 1 (length = 80)
- Ant 2: 1 → 2 → 4 → 3 → 1 (length = 70)

Take $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 1$, $\rho = 0.2$, $Q = 1$, $e = 2$. Initialize all $\tau_{ij}(0) = 0.1$. Compute the updated pheromone on edge $(1,3)$ after iteration 1. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🔑 Model Solution — Choice A

#### Part (a) — Algorithmic Explanation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Initialization (1 mark)**
- Set algorithm parameters $\alpha, \beta, \rho, m, e, Q$.
- Initialize pheromone $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0$ (typically a small positive constant or $m/C^{nn}$).
- Set $L^* = +\infty$, $T^* = \emptyset$.
- Place $m$ ants randomly on the $n$ nodes.

**Step 2 — Tour Construction using State Transition Rule (2 marks)**
Each ant $k$ at node $i$ chooses next node $j$ from the set $\mathcal{N}_i^k$ of unvisited neighbours via the random-proportional rule:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha}\left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\displaystyle\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha}\left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}
$$

with $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$. The parameter $\alpha$ controls pheromone influence (memory) and $\beta$ controls heuristic influence (greedy closeness).

**Step 3 — Compute Tour Lengths (0.5 marks)**
Each ant's tour length $L_k$ is computed. The iteration-best $L_{ibest} = \min_k L_k$ is identified.

**Step 4 — Update Best-So-Far (0.5 marks)**
If $L_{ibest} < L^*$, set $L^* = L_{ibest}$ and $T^* = T_{ibest}$.

**Step 5 — Global Pheromone Update with Elite Term (2 marks)**
For each edge $(i,j)$:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t) + e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{*}(t)
$$

where
$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} Q/L_k, & (i,j) \in T_k \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}, \qquad
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{*} = \begin{cases} 1/L^*, & (i,j) \in T^* \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 6 — Termination (1 mark)**
Repeat Steps 2–5 until a maximum number of iterations $NC_{max}$ is reached or convergence is detected.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the best-so-far tour (1 mark)**
- $L_1 = 80$, $L_2 = 70$.
- Since $L_2 < L_1$, the best-so-far tour after iteration 1 is $T^* = (1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1)$ with $L^* = 70$.

**Step 2 — Check if edge $(1,3)$ is in the elite tour $T^*$ (1 mark)**
$T^* = \{ (1,2), (2,4), (4,3), (3,1) \}$. So $(1,3) \in T^*$. ✓

**Step 3 — Compute ant deposits on edge $(1,3)$ (1 mark)**
- Ant 1's tour $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 1$ contains edge $(1,3)$, so $\Delta\tau_{1,3}^{1} = Q/L_1 = 1/80 = 0.0125$.
- Ant 2's tour $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ contains edge $(3,1)$ (same as $(1,3)$), so $\Delta\tau_{1,3}^{2} = 1/70 \approx 0.01429$.

**Step 4 — Compute sum of ant deposits (1 mark)**
$$
\sum_{k=1}^{2}\Delta\tau_{1,3}^{k} = 0.0125 + 0.01429 \approx 0.02679
$$

**Step 5 — Compute elite contribution (1 mark)**
$$
e \cdot \Delta\tau_{1,3}^{*} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{70} = 0.02857
$$

**Step 6 — Apply evaporation and combine (2 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{1,3}(1) &= (1-\rho)\cdot\tau_{1,3}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{2}\Delta\tau_{1,3}^{k} + e\cdot\Delta\tau_{1,3}^{*} \\
&= (1 - 0.2)(0.1) + 0.02679 + 0.02857 \\
&= 0.8 \cdot 0.1 + 0.02679 + 0.02857 \\
&= 0.08 + 0.02679 + 0.02857 \\
&= 0.13536
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):** $\boxed{\tau_{1,3}(1) \approx 0.1354}$

---

### ✅ Choice B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 3 Choice B]`

**Q: (a)** Compare the Elitist Ant System with the Rank-Based Ant System. Discuss the convergence behavior, stagnation risk, and parameter sensitivity of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the importance of the parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $e$ in EAS. With suitable diagrams, show how varying each parameter affects the algorithm's exploration vs exploitation balance. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🔑 Model Solution — Choice B

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table + Analysis (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Elitist Ant System (EAS) | Rank-Based Ant System (AS$_{\text{rank}}$) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Reinforcement | Best-so-far gets **fixed** weight $e$ | Top-$(w-1)$ ants get **rank-weighted** reinforcement $e - r$ |
| Weight formula | $\Delta\tau = e / L^*$ for elite tour | $\Delta\tau = (w-r)/L_r$ for $r$-th ranked ant |
| Convergence | Faster than vanilla AS | Slightly slower than EAS, more robust |
| Stagnation risk | **Higher** (single elite trap) | Lower (rank diversity) |
| Best for | Quick near-optimal solutions | High-quality convergence |

**Convergence Behavior (1 mark):**
- EAS exhibits **faster initial convergence** because the elite tour's pheromone grows quadratically in effect (every iteration it gets reinforced again, building on existing pheromone).
- AS$_{\text{rank}}$ exhibits **smoother, more stable** convergence due to distributed reinforcement.

**Stagnation Analysis (1 mark):**
- In EAS, if $L^*$ is a local optimum, all ants eventually converge to it (stagnation).
- In AS$_{\text{rank}}$, the multiple top-ranked ants explore different near-best regions, providing implicit diversity.

**Parameter Sensitivity (1 mark):**
- EAS is **sensitive** to $e$: too low = no benefit, too high = stagnation.
- AS$_{\text{rank}}$ is more **forgiving** due to rank diversity.

---

#### Part (b) — Role of $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $e$ (7 marks)

**Parameter $\alpha$ — Pheromone Influence (2.5 marks):**
- Controls how strongly past pheromone guides the ant.
- $\alpha = 0$: pure greedy heuristic (no learning).
- $\alpha$ large: ants blindly follow trails, **over-exploitation**, possible loops.
- $\alpha \approx 1$: balanced (KTU-recommended default).

**Parameter $\beta$ — Heuristic Influence (2.5 marks):**
- Controls the influence of distance-based visibility $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.
- $\beta = 0$: blind random walk, no greedy bias.
- $\beta$ large: ants always pick the nearest unvisited city, **over-exploration**, slow pheromone convergence.
- Typical $\beta \in [2, 5]$.

**Parameter $e$ — Elite Strength (2 marks):**
- As discussed, $e$ directly multiplies the elite deposit on $T^*$.
- Trade-off illustration (a diagram helps):

```
        Exploitation
            ▲
            │
   High β   │   High e, High α
            │  ●───────────●
            │  │ STAGNATION│
            │  │  ZONE     │
            │  ●───────────●
            │  Healthy EAS │
            │  region      │
   Low β    │   Low e, Low α
            │
            └───────────────────► Exploration
              Low α            High β
```

The **healthy EAS operating zone** lies in the diagonal band — moderate $\alpha$, moderate $\beta$, moderate $e$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in EAS questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $L^*$ with $L_{ibest}$:** $L^*$ is the **global best since iteration 0**, NOT the iteration best. Using $L_{ibest}$ instead is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the evaporation term:** Writing $\tau(t+1) = \tau(t) + \text{deposits}$ without $(1-\rho)\tau(t)$ — **loses 2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up $\eta$ and $\tau$:** $\eta$ is static heuristic ($1/d$), $\tau$ evolves — frequently swapped by careless students.
> 4. **Not mentioning premature convergence risk:** Any EAS discussion without acknowledging the stagnation hazard is incomplete.
> 5. **Wrong indicator function usage:** Writing $\Delta\tau^* = 1/L^*$ for ALL edges instead of ONLY edges in $T^*$.
> 6. **Skipping the elite term in numerical problems:** This is the **#1 reason** for full-mark loss in KTU EAS numericals.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🐜 **EAS = AS + Elite Bonus** on the best-so-far global tour.
- 📌 **Master Formula:** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum Q/L_k + e/L^*$ (for edges in $T^*$).
- 🏅 **Elite weight $e$** is typically 5–10; higher values risk stagnation.
- 📉 **Evaporation factor $(1-\rho)$** prevents pheromone accumulation without bound.
- 🔍 **$L^*$** = global best since iteration 0; **$L_{ibest}$** = best in current iteration only.
- ⚖️ **Trade-off:** Faster convergence vs. higher stagnation risk.
- 🧠 **Best-so-far memory** is the *defining feature* that distinguishes EAS from vanilla AS.
- 🛠 **Engineering applications:** TSP, VRP, network routing, scheduling, bioinformatics.
- 🧮 **In numerical problems**, always: (i) identify $T^*$, (ii) check edge membership, (iii) compute three terms separately, (iv) sum.
- 📚 **Related variants for further study:** Ant Colony System (ACS), Max-Min Ant System (MMAS), Rank-Based AS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rank Based Ant System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rank Based Ant System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rank Based Ant System (AS_rank)** is an advanced variant of the original Ant System (AS) proposed by **Bullnheimer, Hartl, and Strauss (1999)** that introduces a *rank-based elitist strategy*. In this scheme, after each iteration the ants are sorted in ascending order of their tour length (shorter tours get better ranks), and only the **best-ranked ants** are permitted to deposit pheromone on the trails they traverse. The amount of pheromone deposited is weighted by the ant's rank position, giving the highest reinforcement to the globally best tour.

The fundamental purpose of the Rank Based Ant System is to **accelerate convergence** toward high-quality solutions by suppressing the influence of poor tours and amplifying the contribution of elite solutions. It combines the *elitist* concept (where the best-so-far ant deposits additional pheromone) with a *ranking mechanism* (where the top (w−1) ants of the current iteration deposit pheromone proportional to their rank weight).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 PEITT632 syllabus (Module 3), the Rank Based Ant System is grouped with other ACO enhancements such as *Elitist AS*, *Max-Min AS*, and *Ant Colony System*. Students must understand how the ranking weight modifies the pheromone trail update equation compared to the baseline Ant System.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **city food festival competition** where 100 food critics (ants) travel across 20 restaurants (nodes) and rate the dishes (tour quality). After the festival, instead of letting every critic write a noisy review, the organizing committee asks only the **top 10 critics** to write officially published reviews — and the **No. 1 critic's review is published twice** (because he gave the most balanced and accurate ranking), the No. 2 critic's review is published 1.9 times, the No. 3 critic's review 1.8 times, and so on. Meanwhile, the **all-time best critic** (best-so-far) also writes a special column that gets the highest weight.

This is exactly how the **Rank Based Ant System** works:

- The "critics" are the **artificial ants**.
- The "restaurants" are the **nodes** of the combinatorial problem (e.g., TSP cities).
- The "published reviews" are the **pheromone trails** deposited on edges.
- The "weight multiplier" of each review corresponds to the **rank-based weighting factor (w − r)**.
- The "all-time best critic" is the **global-best ant (best-so-far tour)**.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Parameters

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **m** | Number of ants in the colony | 10 – 50 |
| **w** | Number of elitist (top-ranked) ants permitted to deposit | Typically m or 6–25 |
| **α** | Pheromone influence exponent | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| **β** | Heuristic influence exponent | 2.0 – 5.0 |
| **ρ** | Pheromone evaporation coefficient | 0.02 – 0.20 |
| **r** | Rank of ant (r = 1 is best) | 1, 2, 3, …, (w − 1) |
| **L_bs** | Length of the best-so-far tour | Problem-dependent |
| **L_r** | Length of the tour found by the r-th ranked ant | Problem-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The rank-based weighting ensures that the **pheromone contribution of low-quality ants is eliminated**, preventing the algorithm from being misled by exploratory but suboptimal solutions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Rank Based Ant System

The Rank Based Ant System modifies the standard Ant System in **three critical places**:

1. **Solution Construction (same as AS):** Each ant *k* in iteration *t* probabilistically selects the next node *j* from current node *i* using the **pseudo-random proportional rule**:

2. **Tour Ranking:** After all *m* ants complete their tours in an iteration, the tours are sorted in **ascending order of length**. The shortest tour receives rank *r = 1*, the second-shortest receives rank *r = 2*, and so on up to rank *r = w − 1*.

3. **Rank-Weighted Pheromone Update:** Pheromone is reinforced by:
   - The **global-best ant** (best-so-far tour found since the start of the run) with weight **w**.
   - The **(w − 1) best-ranked ants** of the current iteration with weights **(w − r)** for r = 1, 2, …, (w − 1).
   - All other (m − w + 1) ants do **not** deposit pheromone.

4. **Pheromone Evaporation:** All edges undergo standard evaporation at rate **ρ** before reinforcement.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Set the iteration counter *t = 0* and initialize all pheromone trails to a small positive constant **τ₀**.
- **Step 2 — Solution Construction:** Place each ant *k* (k = 1, 2, …, m) on a randomly chosen starting node. For each ant, repeatedly apply the probabilistic transition rule until a complete solution tour is built.
- **Step 3 — Tour Evaluation:** Compute the tour length **L_k(t)** for every ant *k* in iteration *t*.
- **Step 4 — Ranking:** Sort the ants in ascending order of L_k. Assign rank *r = 1* to the best ant, rank *r = 2* to the second-best, etc.
- **Step 5 — Pheromone Evaporation:** Reduce every pheromone trail τ_ij(t) by the factor **(1 − ρ)**.
- **Step 6 — Rank-Weighted Deposition:** Apply the rank-weighted update using the (w − 1) top-ranked ants of the current iteration and the best-so-far ant.
- **Step 7 — Best-So-Far Update:** If any ant produced a tour shorter than the current best-so-far, update the best-so-far reference.
- **Step 8 — Termination Check:** If the maximum number of iterations *t_max* is reached, output the best-so-far tour; otherwise set *t ← t + 1* and go to Step 2.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **State Transition Rule** | $p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_{i}^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ |
| 2 | **Heuristic Information** | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ (inverse of edge distance) |
| 3 | **Rank Weight of the r-th ant** | $(w - r)$ for r = 1, 2, …, (w − 1) |
| 4 | **Global-best Weight** | $w$ (assigned to the best-so-far ant) |
| 5 | **Pheromone Deposited by r-th ant** | $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{r} = \dfrac{1}{L_{r}}$ if edge (i, j) ∈ tour of rank-r ant, else **0** |
| 6 | **Pheromone Deposited by Best-So-Far** | $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{bs} = \dfrac{1}{L_{bs}}$ if edge (i, j) ∈ best-so-far tour, else **0** |
| 7 | **Master Pheromone Update Equation** | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w - r)\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{r} + w\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{bs}$ |
| 8 | **Evaporation Multiplier** | $(1 - \rho)$ applied to all edges |
| 9 | **Constraint on w** | $w \le m$ (cannot exceed colony size) |
| 10 | **Common Parameter Settings** | $\alpha = 1,\ \beta = 2,\ \rho = 0.1,\ w = 6\text{–}25$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Comparison Point:** In the baseline Ant System, every ant contributes equally with weight **1**, and the best-so-far ant contributes an additional bonus (in Elitist AS only). In Rank Based AS, the contribution is **linearly decreasing with rank**, giving fine-grained control over convergence.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rank Based Ant System has been successfully applied to:

- **Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP)** in logistics and supply chain management.
- **Telecommunication Network Routing** (dynamic load balancing).
- **Job-Shop Scheduling** in manufacturing.
- **Bioinformatics** — protein folding and DNA sequence alignment.
- **Power System Optimization** — economic dispatch and unit commitment.
- **Printed Circuit Board Drilling** — minimizing tool movement.
- **Graph Coloring** and **Quadratic Assignment Problems (QAP)**.

The rank-based mechanism is particularly powerful when the search space contains many **near-optimal but distinct solutions**, where aggressive elitism might prematurely converge — the linear weighting balances exploitation and exploration gracefully.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rank-Weighted Pheromone Update

The pheromone update equation in Rank Based AS is constructed by combining the standard **evaporation term** with **two reinforcement terms**: the rank-weighted current-iteration contribution and the best-so-far contribution.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Generic Pheromone Update

The general ACO pheromone update has the form:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \underbrace{(1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t)}_{\text{evaporation}} + \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)}_{\text{reinforcement}}
$$

where $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$ is the pheromone deposited by ant *k* on edge (i, j) during iteration *t*.

### 3.1.2 Incorporation of Ranking

In Rank Based AS, only **two sources** of pheromone reinforcement are permitted:

- The **(w − 1)** best-ranked ants of the current iteration, each with a **rank-dependent weight** $(w - r)$.
- The **best-so-far ant** (a global elite), with weight **w**.

Substituting these restricted contributions into the generic update:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w - r)\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{r}(t) + w\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{bs}(t)
$$

### 3.1.3 Definition of Per-Ant Pheromone Contribution

For each rank-r ant, the amount deposited on a traversed edge is inversely proportional to its tour length:

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{r}(t) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{L_r(t)}, & \text{if ant } r \text{ traverses edge } (i,j)\\[4pt]
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Similarly, for the best-so-far ant:

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{bs}(t) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{L_{bs}}, & \text{if best-so-far ant traverses edge } (i,j)\\[4pt]
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.4 Final Master Equation (Consolidated)

Combining evaporation, rank-weighted reinforcement, and best-so-far reinforcement, the **complete master update equation** is:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w - r)\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{r}(t) + w\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{bs}(t)
$$

with the auxiliary definition:

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{r}(t) = \frac{1}{L_{r}(t)} \quad \text{(only if edge } (i,j) \text{ is in rank-} r \text{ ant's tour)}
$$

This is the equation students are expected to reproduce verbatim in KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Rank Based AS is applied to a 4-city TSP (cities A, B, C, D). After iteration *t*, the colony (m = 4 ants) produces the following tour lengths, sorted in ascending order:

| Rank (r) | Ant | Tour Length L_r |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | k₁ | 10 |
| 2 | k₂ | 13 |
| 3 | k₃ | 16 |
| 4 | k₄ | 22 |

Let **w = 3**, **ρ = 0.1**, and suppose the best-so-far tour length so far is **L_bs = 9**. The edge (B, C) was traversed by the rank-1 ant and the rank-2 ant, and also belongs to the best-so-far tour. Compute **τ_BC(t+1)** given **τ_BC(t) = 1.5**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Evaporation Term**

$$
(1 - \rho)\,\tau_{BC}(t) = (1 - 0.10) \times 1.5 = 0.90 \times 1.5 = 1.35
$$

**Step 2 — Rank-Weighted Reinforcement from (w − 1) = 2 Best Ants**

Only rank-1 and rank-2 ants deposited pheromone on edge (B, C). Their contributions:

$$
(w - r)\,\Delta \tau_{BC}^{r} = \frac{(w - r)}{L_r}
$$

For rank 1:
$$
(w - 1)\,\Delta \tau_{BC}^{1} = \frac{3 - 1}{10} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20
$$

For rank 2:
$$
(w - 2)\,\Delta \tau_{BC}^{2} = \frac{3 - 2}{13} = \frac{1}{13} \approx 0.0769
$$

**Step 3 — Best-So-Far Reinforcement**

The edge (B, C) is in the best-so-far tour, so:

$$
w\,\Delta \tau_{BC}^{bs} = \frac{w}{L_{bs}} = \frac{3}{9} \approx 0.3333
$$

**Step 4 — Total Pheromone Update**

$$
\tau_{BC}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{BC}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{2}(w - r)\,\Delta \tau_{BC}^{r} + w\,\Delta \tau_{BC}^{bs}
$$

$$
\tau_{BC}(t+1) = 1.35 + 0.20 + 0.0769 + 0.3333
$$

$$
\tau_{BC}(t+1) \approx 1.96
$$

### Final Result

$$
\tau_{BC}(t+1) \approx 1.96
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Insight:** The pheromone value grew from **1.50 to 1.96**, indicating that edge (B, C) was reinforced as part of multiple high-quality tours. In future iterations, ants will be more strongly biased toward this edge, accelerating convergence to the global optimum.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AS vs. Elitist AS vs. Rank Based AS

| Feature | Ant System (AS) | Elitist AS | Rank Based AS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ants depositing pheromone** | All *m* ants | All *m* + best-so-far | Top (w − 1) ants + best-so-far |
| **Weight scheme** | Uniform (weight = 1) | Uniform + extra bonus for best-so-far | Linear in rank: (w − r) + w for best-so-far |
| **Convergence speed** | Slow | Moderate | Fast |
| **Risk of stagnation** | Low | Moderate | Slightly higher (controlled by w) |
| **Exploration capability** | High | Moderate | Tunable via w |
| **Computational overhead** | Low | Low | Slightly higher (sorting required) |
| **Best suited for** | Small problems | Medium problems | Large-scale problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to *compare* Ant System variants, students should mention **at least three distinguishing features** — pheromone contributors, weighting scheme, and convergence characteristics. A simple table like the one above often earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of Rank Based Ant System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> A1[Initialize parameters: m, w, alpha, beta, rho, tau_0]
    A1 --> A2[Set t = 0]
    A2 --> A3[Place each ant k on a random starting node]
    A3 --> A4{All ants built complete tour?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Apply probabilistic transition rule to select next node]
    A5 --> A4
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[Compute tour length L_k for every ant k]
    A6 --> A7[Sort ants in ascending order of L_k assign ranks r = 1 to w-1]
    A7 --> A8[Identify the best-so-far tour L_bs]
    A8 --> A9[Apply pheromone evaporation factor 1 minus rho to all edges]
    A9 --> A10[Deposit pheromone from top w-1 ranked ants with weight w minus r]
    A10 --> A11[Deposit pheromone from best-so-far ant with weight w]
    A11 --> A12[Update best-so-far if improved tour found]
    A12 --> A13{t greater than or equal to t_max?}
    A13 -- No --> A14[t = t + 1]
    A14 --> A3
    A13 -- Yes --> A15[Output the best-so-far tour]
    A15 --> A16([End])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Initialization Block]
        I1[Parameter Setup]
        I2[Pheromone Matrix tau_ij = tau_0]
    end

    subgraph CO[Construction Block]
        C1[Ant k on starting node]
        C2[Probabilistic Node Selection]
        C3[Tour Completion]
    end

    subgraph EV[Evaluation and Ranking Block]
        E1[Compute L_k for all ants]
        E2[Sort ascending assign rank r]
        E3[Track best-so-far L_bs]
    end

    subgraph PU[Pheromone Update Block]
        P1[Evaporation tau_ij times 1 minus rho]
        P2[Rank weighted deposit w minus r over L_r]
        P3[Best-so-far deposit w over L_bs]
        P4[Aggregate tau_ij new]
    end

    subgraph TC[Termination Block]
        T1[Check t greater than or equal to t_max]
        T2[Output best-so-far solution]
    end

    IN --> CO
    CO --> EV
    EV --> PU
    PU --> TC
    TC -- Not done --> CO
    TC -- Done --> T2
```

## 4.3 Information Flow Between Components

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Init as Initialization
    participant Ant as Ant Agent
    participant Rank as Ranking Module
    participant Pher as Pheromone Engine
    participant BSF as BestSoFar Tracker

    Init->>Ant: Initialize m ants and tau_0
    Ant->>Ant: Build complete tour via state transition rule
    Ant->>Rank: Submit tour length L_k
    Rank->>Rank: Sort ants ascending by L_k
    Rank->>Pher: Forward top w-1 ants with rank weights
    Rank->>BSF: Forward best tour of iteration
    BSF->>Pher: Provide L_bs and best-so-far path
    Pher->>Pher: Evaporate tau_ij by factor 1 minus rho
    Pher->>Pher: Add rank weighted deposits and best-so-far deposit
    Pher->>Ant: Update tau_ij matrix for next iteration
    BSF->>BSF: Update global best if improvement found
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]
**Q: Define Rank Based Ant System and state how it differs from the basic Ant System.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Rank Based Ant System (AS_rank)** is an enhanced Ant Colony Optimization algorithm proposed by **Bullnheimer, Hartl, and Strauss (1999)** that uses a rank-based elitist strategy for pheromone update.

| Aspect | Basic Ant System | Rank Based AS |
|---|---|---|
| Pheromone depositors | All m ants equally | Only top (w − 1) + best-so-far |
| Weighting | Uniform (1) | Linear in rank: (w − r), plus w for best-so-far |
| Influence of poor tours | Yes (equal) | Eliminated |

The key advantages are: **faster convergence**, **better suppression of poor solutions**, and **tunable exploration** via parameter *w*.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]
**Q: What is the role of parameter *w* in the Rank Based Ant System?**

**Model Answer:**

The parameter **w** defines the **number of elitist ants** that are allowed to deposit pheromone on the trails. Specifically:

- **w** controls the **intensity of elitist pressure** — larger w means more ants contribute, leading to broader exploration.
- The top **(w − 1)** ranked ants of the current iteration deposit pheromone with rank-dependent weights (w − 1), (w − 2), …, 1.
- The **best-so-far ant** deposits with weight **w**.
- Typically, **w ∈ [6, 25]** and must satisfy **w ≤ m** (the total number of ants).
- Setting **w too high** reduces the algorithm to the basic Ant System; **w too low** may cause premature convergence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions offer internal choice between two alternatives. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Pattern]

**Q: A Rank Based Ant System is applied to a Travelling Salesman Problem with the following parameter values: number of ants m = 5, number of elitist ants w = 4, evaporation rate ρ = 0.1. After iteration *t*, the tour lengths of the 5 ants are L₁ = 12, L₂ = 15, L₃ = 18, L₄ = 20, L₅ = 25 (in ascending order). The best-so-far tour length L_bs = 10.**

**(a)** Write down the complete pheromone update equation used in the Rank Based Ant System and identify each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a specific edge (i, j) that was traversed by the rank-1 ant, rank-3 ant, and the best-so-far ant (but not by rank-2), compute the new pheromone value τ_ij(t+1) given τ_ij(t) = 2.0. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Master Equation and Term Identification (7 Marks)

The **complete pheromone update equation** for Rank Based Ant System is:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w - r)\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{r}(t) + w\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{bs}(t)
$$

**Identification of each term:**

- **(1 − ρ) τ_ij(t)** → Evaporation term; reduces old pheromone by rate ρ to prevent unlimited accumulation and avoid stagnation.
- **Σ (w − r) Δτ_ij^r(t)** → Rank-weighted reinforcement from the (w − 1) best-ranked ants of the current iteration; ant ranked *r* deposits 1/L_r on each edge of its tour, scaled by weight (w − r).
- **w Δτ_ij^bs(t)** → Reinforcement from the global best-so-far ant; deposits 1/L_bs on edges of the best tour found so far, scaled by weight w.
- **ρ** → Pheromone evaporation coefficient, typically in **[0.02, 0.20]**.

For the given problem: **m = 5, w = 4, ρ = 0.1**, so **w − 1 = 3** ants deposit pheromone (ranks 1, 2, 3), with weights **3, 2, 1** respectively.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given data:**

- τ_ij(t) = 2.0
- ρ = 0.1 → (1 − ρ) = 0.9
- Edge (i, j) traversed by: rank-1 ant (L₁ = 12), rank-3 ant (L₃ = 18), and best-so-far (L_bs = 10)
- Edge (i, j) NOT traversed by rank-2 ant
- w = 4

**Step 1 — Evaporation Term:**

$$
(1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) = 0.9 \times 2.0 = 1.80
$$

**Step 2 — Rank-1 Ant Contribution (weight = w − 1 = 3):**

$$
(w - 1)\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{1} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.250
$$

**Step 3 — Rank-3 Ant Contribution (weight = w − 3 = 1):**

$$
(w - 3)\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{3} = \frac{1}{18} \approx 0.0556
$$

**Step 4 — Best-So-Far Ant Contribution (weight = w = 4):**

$$
w\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{bs} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.40
$$

**Step 5 — Rank-2 Ant Contribution:** ZERO (edge not traversed).

**Step 6 — Total Pheromone Update:**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = 1.80 + 0.250 + 0.0556 + 0.40
$$

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = 2.5056
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\tau_{ij}(t+1) \approx 2.506}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Pattern]

**Q: Explain the Rank Based Ant System in detail. In your answer, cover:**

**(a)** The motivation behind introducing rank-based weighting, the algorithm flow, and the pseudo-code-style structure with all key steps. Compare Rank Based AS with the basic Ant System and Elitist AS. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of parameter *w* on algorithm performance. What happens when w = 1, w = m, and w is intermediate? Provide a numerical example showing the pheromone computation when three ants deposit pheromone. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Detailed Explanation and Comparison (7 Marks)

**Motivation:**

The basic Ant System suffers from **slow convergence** because every ant — including those that produce poor tours — contributes equally to pheromone update. This dilutes the influence of high-quality solutions. The Rank Based Ant System addresses this by:

1. **Eliminating poor contributions:** Only the (w − 1) best ants deposit pheromone.
2. **Differential weighting:** Higher-ranked ants deposit more pheromone.
3. **Best-so-far reinforcement:** Global best receives the highest weight *w*.

**Algorithmic Steps:**

1. Initialize τ_ij = τ₀ for all edges.
2. Each ant builds a complete tour via state transition rule.
3. Compute tour lengths L_k.
4. Rank ants in ascending order; assign ranks 1, 2, …, (w − 1).
5. Evaporate all trails: τ_ij ← (1 − ρ) τ_ij.
6. Deposit from top (w − 1) ranked ants using weights (w − r).
7. Deposit from best-so-far ant using weight w.
8. Update best-so-far if improved.
9. Repeat until t = t_max.

**Comparison Table (as derived in Section 3.3 above) — already drawn for students' reference.**

#### Part (b) — Parameter w Analysis (7 Marks)

**Effect of parameter w:**

| Value of w | Behavior |
|---|---|
| **w = 1** | Degenerates to Elitist AS (only best-so-far deposits); fastest but most prone to stagnation |
| **w = m** | Reduces to basic Ant System (all ants deposit with rank weights summing to m(m+1)/2); slower convergence |
| **w intermediate (e.g., 6–25)** | Balanced exploration-exploitation; recommended for most problems |

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose three ants produce tours of lengths **L₁ = 8, L₂ = 12, L₃ = 20**, with **w = 3** (so all three deposit), **ρ = 0.1**, **L_bs = 8**, and **τ_ij(t) = 1.0** for an edge (i, j) traversed by all three ants including the best-so-far.

**Evaporation:** (1 − 0.1) × 1.0 = **0.90**

**Rank-weighted deposits:**

- Rank 1: (w − 1) × (1/L₁) = 2 × (1/8) = 0.250
- Rank 2: (w − 2) × (1/L₂) = 1 × (1/12) ≈ 0.0833

**Best-so-far deposit:** w × (1/L_bs) = 3 × (1/8) = 0.375

**Total:**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = 0.90 + 0.250 + 0.0833 + 0.375 = 1.6083
$$

**Interpretation:** The pheromone increased from 1.0 to 1.6083, confirming that high-quality tours (especially the best-so-far) dominate the reinforcement signal.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Rank Based AS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the evaporation term** — Students often write only the reinforcement sum and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong rank indexing** — Rank *r = 1* must correspond to the **shortest** tour, not the longest. Mixing this up inverts the weight scheme and yields nonsense numerical answers.
> 3. **Missing the best-so-far term** — The global-best ant's contribution with weight *w* is **separate** from the (w − 1) ranked ants. Many students merge them incorrectly.
> 4. **Forgetting that only (w − 1) ants deposit** — All other (m − w + 1) ants contribute **zero** pheromone. Drawing a sum from r = 1 to m is a common mistake.
> 5. **Incorrect unit handling** — Tour lengths must be in consistent units (distance for TSP, time for scheduling) before inversion.
> 6. **No mention of sorting step** — Ranking requires an explicit sorting operation O(m log m); failing to mention it loses methodology marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Originator:** Bullnheimer, Hartl, and Strauss (1999) — frequently asked in viva voce.
- **Core Idea:** Use rank-based weighting so that better tours contribute proportionally more pheromone.
- **Master Equation to Memorize:**
  $$
  \tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w - r)\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{r} + w\,\Delta \tau_{ij}^{bs}
  $$
- **Three Components of Update:** Evaporation + Rank-weighted current-iteration deposit + Best-so-far deposit.
- **Parameter w:** Number of elitist ants; controls the trade-off between exploration and exploitation.
- **Constraint:** w ≤ m (cannot exceed the colony size).
- **Typical Values:** α = 1, β = 2, ρ = 0.1, w ∈ [6, 25], m ∈ [10, 50].
- **State Transition Rule:** Same as Ant System — proportional to τ^α · η^β.
- **Per-Ant Pheromone:** Δτ = 1/L_k if the edge is in the ant's tour, else 0.
- **Ranking Convention:** Rank 1 = shortest tour (best ant).
- **Convergence Property:** Faster than basic AS due to elitist reinforcement and elimination of poor contributions.
- **Stagnation Risk:** Higher than basic AS but lower than pure Elitist AS; tunable via w.
- **Comparison Anchors:** Always contrast with AS (uniform weights) and Elitist AS (single bonus ant).
- **Algorithm Steps to Memorize:** Initialize → Construct → Evaluate → Rank → Evaporate → Deposit → Update best-so-far → Check termination.
- **Sorting Operation:** Required after tour evaluation; O(m log m) complexity.
- **Engineering Applications:** VRP, network routing, scheduling, bioinformatics, power systems, PCB drilling.
- **Numerical Skill:** Be able to substitute τ(t), ρ, w, and L values into the master equation and compute τ(t+1) in under 2 minutes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Max Min Ant System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Max Min Ant System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)** is an advanced variant of the Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) metaheuristic proposed by **Thomas Stützle and Holger Hoos in 2000**. It introduces four key modifications to the classical Ant System to improve computational performance and prevent premature convergence (stagnation behavior). The formal definition as per KTU 2024 Scheme terminology: > *"Max-Min Ant System (MMAS) is a population-based, constructive metaheuristic for combinatorial optimization that enhances the basic Ant System by (i) allowing only the **best-so-far** or **iteration-best** ant to deposit pheromone, (ii) bounding the pheromone trail values within a defined interval $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$, (iii) initializing pheromone trails to the upper bound $\tau_{max}$, and optionally (iv) applying pheromone trail smoothing."* ### Four Pillars of MMAS | Pillar | Description | |---|---| | **P1: Best-Ant Deposition** | Only the globally best ant ($g_{best}$) or iteration-best ant ($i_{best}$) updates the pheromone matrix | | **P2: Bounded Pheromones** | Trail values are strictly clamped to $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ to prevent any single edge from becoming dominant | | **P3: Smart Initialization** | All pheromone trails start at $\tau_{max}$ to encourage initial exploration | | **P4: Trail Smoothing** | Optional re-scaling to redistribute accumulated pheromone and escape local optima | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Restaurant Critic Analogy** 🍽️ > > Imagine a city with **100 restaurants** and **1,000 hungry food critics (ants)** looking for the best place to eat. In the **basic Ant System**, *every* critic leaves a review (pheromone) on every restaurant they visit, regardless of how bad the food was. This creates "noise" — bad restaurants might still get popular reviews, and good ones get lost in the crowd. > > In **Max-Min Ant System**, the rules are stricter: > 1. **Only the food critic who found the absolute best meal** (or the best in that day) is allowed to write a glowing review. This is the **"best-ant deposition"** rule. > 2. **Reviews are capped**: No restaurant can have a rating higher than 5 stars or lower than 1 star. Even if a critic is overly enthusiastic, the review is **clamped to $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$**. This prevents one restaurant from monopolizing attention. > 3. **All restaurants start with maximum hype** ($\tau_{max}$) so every critic initially explores widely instead of being biased toward a few popular spots. > 4. **Periodic re-calibration**: The system "smooths" reviews over time so that overly hyped restaurants lose some shine, and underrated ones get a fairer chance. > > **Result**: The critics converge on truly excellent restaurants faster, and they don't all pile into a single mediocre place that got an early lucky review. This is exactly the **stagnation avoidance** that MMAS brings to combinatorial optimization. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bold Constants) The following are the **canonical algorithmic parameters** universally cited in MMAS literature: - **$\tau_{min}$** → Lower bound on pheromone trail value - **$\tau_{max}$** → Upper bound on pheromone trail value - **$\rho$** → Pheromone evaporation rate, $0 < \rho < 1$ - **$\alpha$** → Pheromone influence exponent - **$\beta$** → Heuristic influence exponent - **$L^{best}$** → Length of the best solution found so far - **$p_{dec}$** → Decision probability threshold for branching - **$f(s)$** → Objective function value of solution $s$ - **$n$** → Number of cities/nodes in the problem instance - **$m$** → Number of ants in the colony > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The MMAS topic is typically assessed for its **mechanism of stagnation prevention**, the **mathematical formulation of $\tau_{min}$ and $\tau_{max}$**, and **comparison with original Ant System and Ant Colony System**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic The Max-Min Ant System operates as a **discrete-time iterative process**. The complete algorithmic flow is as follows: ### **Phase 1: Initialization** 1. Set the iteration counter $t = 0$. 2. Initialize **all** pheromone trail entries to $\tau_{max}$: $$\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_{max}, \quad \forall (i,j) \in E$$ 3. Place all $m$ ants on randomly (or fixed) chosen starting nodes. 4. Set the global best solution $s^{gb} \leftarrow \emptyset$ and $L^{gb} \leftarrow \infty$. ### **Phase 2: Solution Construction (per ant)** Each ant $k$ builds a complete solution by probabilistically moving from node $i$ to node $j \in \mathcal{N}_i^k$ (the feasible neighborhood) using the **MMAS transition rule** (a slight variant of the standard AS rule): $$p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^k} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right}^{\beta}}, \quad \text{if } j \in \mathcal{N}_i^k$$ where $\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$ is the heuristic desirability (inverse of distance) for TSP applications. > **Why this rule?** It balances **exploitation** (high $\tau$ edges preferred) and **exploration** (high $\eta$ edges preferred), controlled by the exponents $\alpha$ and $\beta$. ### **Phase 3: Evaluation** After all $m$ ants complete their tours, evaluate each tour length $L^k(t)$ and identify the **iteration-best** $L^{ib}$ and the **global-best** $L^{gb}$. ### **Phase 4: Pheromone Evaporation (all edges)** Apply the standard evaporation to **all edges**: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t), \quad \forall (i,j) \in E$$ ### **Phase 5: Pheromone Deposition (best ant only)** This is the **MMAS signature step**. Only the chosen best ant deposits pheromone: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) \leftarrow \tau_{ij}(t+1) + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best}$$ where $$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{L^{best}} & \text{if edge } (i,j) \text{ belongs to the best tour} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ The **"best"** can be either $L^{ib}$ (iteration-best) or $L^{gb}$ (global-best). The selection alternates or biases toward $g_{best}$ to intensify search. ### **Phase 6: Pheromone Clamping (the MMAS heart)** This is the **distinctive feature of MMAS**. After deposition, enforce hard bounds: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \begin{cases} \tau_{max} & \text{if } \tau_{ij}(t+1) > \tau_{max} \\ \tau_{min} & \text{if } \tau_{ij}(t+1) < \tau_{min} \\ \tau_{ij}(t+1) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why clamping?** Without bounds, a few edges can accumulate disproportionately large pheromone, causing the algorithm to converge prematurely to a suboptimal solution. This is called **stagnation behavior** — every ant follows the same few edges, killing diversity. ### **Phase 7: Optional Trail Smoothing** If no improvement occurs after $\mu$ consecutive iterations, apply: $$\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow \tau_{min} + \sigma \cdot \left(\tau_{ij}(t) - \tau_{min}\right), \quad \sigma \in (0, 1)$$ This **dampens** dominant trails and gives weaker trails a relative boost. ### **Phase 8: Termination** Repeat Phases 2–7 until a stopping criterion is met (max iterations, time budget, or convergence threshold). ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Mechanism | Mechanism | Why It Exists | Failure Mode It Prevents | |---|---|---| | **Best-ant only deposit** | Reduces noise from mediocre tours | Dilution of good signal | | **$[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ bounds** | Maintains exploration of all edges | Premature convergence / stagnation | | **$\tau_{max}$ initialization** | Ensures uniform initial exploration | Biased early sampling | | **Trail smoothing** | Escapes local optima by reducing contrast | Getting stuck in suboptimal basin | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Variables | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $p_{ij}^k(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^k} [\tau_{il}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$ | **MMAS Transition Probability** | $p_{ij}^k$ = prob. of ant $k$ moving $i \to j$ | | 2 | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best}$ | **Pheromone Update Rule** | $\rho$ = evaporation rate | | 3 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} = 1 / L^{best}$ | **Pheromone Deposit (only for best ant)** | $L^{best}$ = best tour length | | 4 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}$ | **Upper Bound (theoretical)** | $L^{gb}$ = global-best length | | 5 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{bsf}}$ | **Upper Bound (best-so-far practical)** | $L^{bsf}$ = best-so-far | | 6 | $p_{best} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{(\tau_{max} + (n-1)\cdot\tau_{min})}$ | **Theoretical best-choice probability** | $n$ = number of cities | | 7 | $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{p_{best} \cdot \tau_{max} \cdot (1 - p_{best})}{(n-1) \cdot p_{best}^2}$ | **Lower Bound (derived)** | ensures $p_{best}$ floor | | 8 | $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{max} \cdot (1 - \sqrt[n-1]{p_{best}})}{(n/2 - 1)\cdot\sqrt[n-1]{p_{best}}}$ | **Alternative $\tau_{min}$ formula** | (n/2–1) neighbor assumption | | 9 | $\eta_{ij} = 1 / d_{ij}$ | **Heuristic Information (TSP)** | $d_{ij}$ = distance | | 10 | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow \tau_{min} + \sigma(\tau_{ij} - \tau_{min})$ | **Trail Smoothing** | $\sigma \in (0,1)$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Piping Note**: In all formulas above, $\vert$ symbols have been deliberately avoided. In your KTU answer scripts, use $\mid$ or $\vert$ when writing bounds like $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ to avoid markdown rendering issues. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Domain | Application of MMAS | |---|---| | **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Vehicle routing, last-mile delivery, warehouse picking paths | | **Telecommunications** | Network routing in packet-switched networks, QoS optimization | | **VLSI Design** | Integrated circuit routing, gate placement, circuit partitioning | | **Bioinformatics** | DNA sequence alignment, protein folding approximation | | **Scheduling** | Job-shop scheduling, project scheduling, exam timetabling | | **Power Systems** | Economic dispatch, unit commitment, grid reconfiguration | | **Robotics** | Multi-robot path planning, swarm coordination | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Upper Bound $\tau_{max}$ The upper bound $\tau_{max}$ is derived from the **steady-state assumption**: if a single ant kept depositing on the same edge $(i,j)$ indefinitely (without any evaporation disruption), what is the maximum value the pheromone can attain? ### **Step 1: Write the Recurrence for a Single Edge** Consider a specific edge $(i, j)$ that belongs to the best tour. At each iteration, the ant deposits $\Delta\tau = 1/L^{gb}$, and evaporation reduces by factor $(1 - \rho)$. $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \frac{1}{L^{gb}}$$ ### **Step 2: Solve the Linear Recurrence** This is a first-order linear recurrence of the form $x_{n+1} = a \cdot x_n + b$. The general solution is: $$x_{n+1} = a^n \cdot x_0 + b \cdot \frac{1 - a^n}{1 - a}$$ Substituting $a = (1 - \rho)$ and $b = 1/L^{gb}$: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)^{t+1} \cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \frac{1}{L^{gb}} \cdot \frac{1 - (1 - \rho)^{t+1}}{1 - (1 - \rho)}$$ ### **Step 3: Take the Limit as $t \to \infty$** Since $0 < \rho < 1$, we have $(1 - \rho)^{t+1} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. Also, $1 - (1 - \rho) = \rho$. $$\lim_{t \to \infty} \tau_{ij}(t+1) = 0 + \frac{1}{L^{gb}} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}}$$ > **Interpretation**: If we never clamped the pheromone, the theoretical maximum is reached when a single best-so-far tour is reinforced forever. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Lower Bound $\tau_{min}$ The lower bound $\tau_{min}$ is derived from the requirement that **the probability of choosing the best edge from a node should be at least $p_{best}$**, even in the steady state. ### **Step 1: Steady-State Transition Probability** At a node with $(n-1)$ feasible neighbors, suppose the best neighbor has $\tau_{max}$ and all other $(n-2)$ neighbors have $\tau_{min}$. The probability of choosing the best edge is: $$p_{best} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{max} + (n-2)\cdot\tau_{min}}$$ ### **Step 2: Solve for $\tau_{min}$** Multiplying both sides: $$p_{best} \cdot \left[\tau_{max} + (n-2)\cdot\tau_{min}\right] = \tau_{max}$$ $$p_{best} \cdot \tau_{max} + p_{best} \cdot (n-2)\cdot\tau_{min} = \tau_{max}$$ $$p_{best} \cdot (n-2)\cdot\tau_{min} = \tau_{max} - p_{best}\cdot\tau_{max}$$ $$p_{best} \cdot (n-2)\cdot\tau_{min} = \tau_{max}\cdot(1 - p_{best})$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot (1 - p_{best})}{(n-2) \cdot p_{best}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > Some textbooks use $(n-1)$ instead of $(n-2)$ as a conservative overestimate. The original Stützle & Hoos paper uses the form: > $$\tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot (1 - \sqrt[n-1]{p_{best}})}{(n/2 - 1)\cdot\sqrt[n-1]{p_{best}}}$$ > This accounts for the expected number of edges $\sim n/2$ in a best partial solution. ## 3.3 Derivation of Pheromone Decay After $k$ Iterations Given an initial pheromone $\tau_0$ with evaporation rate $\rho$ and no deposition, the pheromone after $k$ iterations is: $$\tau(k) = (1 - \rho)^k \cdot \tau_0$$ **Derivation:** - After 1 iteration: $\tau(1) = (1-\rho)\tau_0$ - After 2 iterations: $\tau(2) = (1-\rho)\tau(1) = (1-\rho)^2\tau_0$ - After $k$ iterations: $\tau(k) = (1-\rho)^k\tau_0$ (by induction) ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (TSP) > [!NOTE] > **Scenario**: Consider a 4-city TSP instance: $A, B, C, D$ with distances $d_{AB}=2, d_{AC}=3, d_{AD}=4, d_{BC}=2, d_{BD}=5, d_{CD}=1$. An ant at city $A$ must choose among $B, C, D$. **Given parameters**: $\alpha = 1, \beta = 2, \rho = 0.1, \tau_{max} = 5.0, \tau_{min} = 0.5$. **Initial pheromones**: $\tau_{AB} = \tau_{AC} = \tau_{AD} = 5.0$. ### **Step 1: Compute Heuristic Values** $$\eta_{AB} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500, \quad \eta_{AC} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333, \quad \eta_{AD} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250$$ ### **Step 2: Compute Numerators** $$[\tau_{AB}]^1 \cdot [\eta_{AB}]^2 = 5.0 \cdot 0.250 = 1.250$$ $$[\tau_{AC}]^1 \cdot [\eta_{AC}]^2 = 5.0 \cdot 0.111 = 0.556$$ $$[\tau_{AD}]^1 \cdot [\eta_{AD}]^2 = 5.0 \cdot 0.0625 = 0.3125$$ ### **Step 3: Compute Denominator** $$D = 1.250 + 0.556 + 0.3125 = 2.1185$$ ### **Step 4: Compute Probabilities** $$p_{AB} = \frac{1.250}{2.1185} \approx 0.5901 \; (59.01\%)$$ $$p_{AC} = \frac{0.556}{2.1185} \approx 0.2624 \; (26.24\%)$$ $$p_{AD} = \frac{0.3125}{2.1185} \approx 0.1475 \; (14.75\%)$$ ### **Step 5: Verification — Bounded Probabilities** Since $p_{AB} \leq 1.0$ ✓ and all $\tau$ values are within $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}] = [0.5, 5.0]$ ✓, the MMAS constraints are satisfied. ### **Step 6: Suppose Ant Chooses $A \to B$ and Tour Length $L^{gb} = 12$** After one full tour iteration, the update on edge $(A,B)$ is: $$\tau_{AB}^{new} = (1 - 0.1) \cdot 5.0 + \frac{1}{12} = 4.5 + 0.0833 = 4.5833$$ Clamping: $0.5 \leq 4.5833 \leq 5.0$ ✓ → no clamping needed. For unused edges, e.g., $(A, C)$: $$\tau_{AC}^{new} = (1 - 0.1) \cdot 5.0 + 0 = 4.5$$ Clamping: $0.5 \leq 4.5 \leq 5.0$ ✓. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 MMAS Algorithmic Block Diagram The following block diagram captures the full MMAS control flow with all decision branches. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Initialize t=0] --> B[Set all tau_ij to tau_max] B --> C[Place m ants on starting nodes] C --> D[Set global best L_gb = infinity] D --> E{Iteration t less than t_max?} E -- No --> Z[Output: Global Best Solution] E -- Yes --> F[Each ant constructs a tour using MMAS transition rule] F --> G[Evaluate tour lengths Lk for all k] G --> H[Identify iteration-best L_ib and global-best L_gb] H --> I[Apply Pheromone Evaporation: tau_ij = 1-rho times tau_ij] I --> J{Choose Best Ant for Deposition} J -- Option P1 --> K1[Use Global Best ant g_best] J -- Option P2 --> K2[Use Iteration Best ant i_best] J -- Option P3 --> K3[Use Restart Best ant r_best] K1 --> L[Deposit pheromone: tau_ij += 1 over L_best] K2 --> L K3 --> L L --> M[Pheromone Clamping to tau_min, tau_max interval] M --> N{Stagnation detected? mu consecutive iterations without improvement} N -- Yes --> O[Apply Trail Smoothing: tau_ij = tau_min + sigma times tau_ij minus tau_min] N -- No --> P[Increment t = t + 1] O --> P P --> E Z --> END[End] ``` ## 4.2 MMAS Mechanism Architecture (Component-Level View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Layer] I1[Problem Instance Graph G = V,E] I2[Parameters alpha, beta, rho, m, tau_max] end subgraph CORE[MMAS Engine Core] D1[Solution Constructor] D2[Ant Colony: k=1..m] D3[Heuristic Info: eta_ij = 1 over d_ij] D4[Pheromone Matrix: tau_ij] D5[Transition Rule: Probabilistic Ant Routing] end subgraph UPDATE[Pheromone Update Module] U1[Evaporation Block: Multiply by 1-rho] U2[Best-Ant Selector: g_best or i_best] U3[Deposition Block: Add 1 over L_best] U4[Clamping Block: Enforce tau_min, tau_max bounds] U5[Smoothing Block: Optional dampening] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer] O1[Best-so-far Solution s_bsf] O2[Convergence Curve] O3[Performance Metrics] end I1 --> D1 I2 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D3 --> D5 D4 --> D5 D2 --> D5 D5 --> U1 U1 --> U2 U2 --> U3 U3 --> U4 U4 --> U5 U5 --> O1 U1 --> O2 U2 --> O3 ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Flow: AS vs ACS vs MMAS ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start ACO Algorithm] --> Branch{Algorithm Variant?} Branch -- AS --> AS1[All ants deposit pheromone] Branch -- ACS --> ACS1[Only global-best deposits with local update] Branch -- MMAS --> MMAS1[Only iteration-best OR global-best deposits] AS1 --> AS2[No trail bounds] ACS1 --> ACS2[No trail bounds but local pheromone update on edges] MMAS1 --> MMAS2[Strict clamping to tau_min, tau_max] AS2 --> AS3[Initialize tau to small constant] ACS2 --> ACS3[Initialize tau to small constant] MMAS2 --> MMAS3[Initialize tau to tau_max for maximum exploration] AS3 --> ASEnd[Higher risk of stagnation] ACS3 --> ACSEnd[Moderate stagnation risk] MMAS3 --> MMASEnd[Strong stagnation avoidance] ``` ## 4.4 Pheromone Convergence Trajectory (Conceptual Plot) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Iteration 0: All tau = tau_max] --> P2[Iteration 1 to 10: Exploration phase
All edges sampled broadly] P2 --> P3[Iteration 10 to 50: Exploitation phase
Strong edges emerge but stay capped] P3 --> P4[Iteration 50 to 100: Convergence
Best edges at tau_max, weak edges at tau_min] P4 --> P5[Iteration greater than 100: Steady state
