tau_ij in tau_min, tau_max interval] P5 --> PEnd[Stagnation avoided due to clamping] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. Define Max-Min Ant System and list its four key modifications over the basic Ant System.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Max-Min Ant System (MMAS) is an enhanced Ant Colony Optimization algorithm introduced by Stützle and Hoos (2000) to overcome the stagnation behavior of the classical Ant System. The **four key modifications** are: 1. **Best-Ant Deposition**: Only the best-so-far (global-best) or iteration-best ant is allowed to deposit pheromone, unlike AS where all ants deposit. 2. **Bounded Pheromone Trails**: Trail values are clamped to a fixed interval $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ to prevent premature convergence. 3. **Smart Initialization**: All pheromone trails are initialized to $\tau_{max}$ (the upper bound) to encourage initial broad exploration. 4. **Pheromone Trail Smoothing** (optional): If stagnation is detected, pheromone values are re-scaled using a smoothing factor $\sigma \in (0,1)$. --- ### **Q2. Why is pheromone clamping to $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ necessary in MMAS? What is stagnation behavior?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Stagnation behavior** is the phenomenon where all ants of a colony converge to a single (often suboptimal) tour. This happens because in classical AS, edges belonging to good early tours accumulate disproportionately high pheromone, leading all subsequent ants to choose the same edges, eliminating diversity. **Clamping is necessary** because: - It **prevents excessive accumulation** of pheromone on dominant edges (enforced by $\tau_{max}$). - It **guarantees a minimum exploration probability** for weaker edges (enforced by $\tau_{min}$). - It ensures that **the probability of choosing the best edge is bounded** by a theoretical $p_{best}$, preserving algorithmic diversity throughout the run. - It acts as a **diversity-preserving mechanism**, ensuring long-term search effectiveness. Mathematically, the bounds are: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}, \quad \tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot (1 - p_{best})}{(n-2) \cdot p_{best}}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the Max-Min Ant System (MMAS) algorithm in detail. Clearly state the pheromone update rule, the clamping mechanism, and the role of $\tau_{max}$ and $\tau_{min}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the upper bound $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}$ starting from the pheromone update recurrence. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** The **Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)** operates through the following sequence of steps: **Step 1 — Initialization:** - All pheromone trails are set to $\tau_{max}$: $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_{max}, \forall (i,j)$. - $m$ ants are placed on starting nodes; global best $L^{gb} = \infty$. **Step 2 — Solution Construction:** Each ant $k$ moves from node $i$ to node $j$ using the probabilistic transition rule: $$p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$$ **Step 3 — Pheromone Evaporation:** Apply evaporation uniformly to all edges: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$$ **Step 4 — Pheromone Deposition (only by best ant):** $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) \leftarrow \tau_{ij}(t+1) + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best}$$ where $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} = 1/L^{best}$ if $(i,j) \in T^{best}$, else $0$. **Step 5 — Clamping Mechanism:** $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \begin{cases} \tau_{max} & \text{if } \tau_{ij}(t+1) > \tau_{max} \\ \tau_{min} & \text{if } \tau_{ij}(t+1) < \tau_{min} \\ \tau_{ij}(t+1) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Role of $\tau_{max}$**: Caps the maximum pheromone on any edge, preventing excessive reinforcement and ensuring the **best edges do not become infinitely attractive**. **Role of $\tau_{min}$**: Provides a floor for pheromone, guaranteeing **non-zero probability of exploring all edges**, even the worst ones. This maintains algorithmic diversity and prevents convergence to a single suboptimal solution. --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Goal**: Derive $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}$. **Step 1**: Consider an edge $(i, j)$ that belongs to the global-best tour. Its pheromone update is: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \frac{1}{L^{gb}}$$ **Step 2**: This is a first-order linear recurrence. Expanding iteratively: $$\tau_{ij}(1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \frac{1}{L^{gb}}$$ $$\tau_{ij}(2) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(1) + \frac{1}{L^{gb}} = (1 - \rho)^2 \cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \frac{1}{L^{gb}} \cdot [1 + (1 - \rho)]$$ **Step 3**: General pattern after $t+1$ iterations: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)^{t+1} \cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \frac{1}{L^{gb}} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{t} (1 - \rho)^k$$ **Step 4**: Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{k=0}^{t} r^k = \dfrac{1 - r^{t+1}}{1 - r}$ with $r = 1 - \rho$: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)^{t+1} \cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \frac{1}{L^{gb}} \cdot \frac{1 - (1 - \rho)^{t+1}}{1 - (1 - \rho)}$$ **Step 5**: Since $1 - (1 - \rho) = \rho$: $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)^{t+1} \cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \frac{1 - (1 - \rho)^{t+1}}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}$$ **Step 6**: Take the limit $t \to \infty$. Since $0 < (1 - \rho) < 1$, we have $(1 - \rho)^{t+1} \to 0$: $$\lim_{t \to \infty} \tau_{ij}(t+1) = 0 + \frac{1 - 0}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}}$$ This is the **theoretical maximum** any pheromone value can attain under continuous best-ant reinforcement, and hence serves as the natural upper bound for MMAS clamping. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a clear block diagram, describe the working of the Max-Min Ant System. Compare MMAS with the original Ant System and Ant Colony System in terms of pheromone management. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A TSP instance has $n = 50$ cities, with the current global-best tour length $L^{gb} = 420$, evaporation rate $\rho = 0.02$, and target decision probability $p_{best} = 0.4$. Compute the values of $\tau_{max}$ and $\tau_{min}$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Block Diagram Description of MMAS Working:** The MMAS algorithm consists of the following interconnected blocks: 1. **Initialization Block**: Sets all $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{max}$, places ants on start nodes. 2. **Solution Construction Block**: Uses the transition probability rule to build tours. 3. **Evaluation Block**: Computes tour lengths and identifies $L^{ib}$ and $L^{gb}$. 4. **Evaporation Block**: Multiplies all $\tau_{ij}$ by $(1 - \rho)$. 5. **Best-Ant Selection Block**: Picks either $g_{best}$ or $i_{best}$. 6. **Deposition Block**: Adds $1/L^{best}$ to edges of the chosen ant. 7. **Clamping Block**: Enforces $\tau_{ij} \in [\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$. 8. **Smoothing Block** (conditional): Applies dampening if stagnation detected. 9. **Termination Check Block**: Decides whether to continue or stop. **Comparative Table: AS vs ACS vs MMAS** | Feature | Ant System (AS) | Ant Colony System (ACS) | Max-Min Ant System (MMAS) | |---|---|---|---| | **Pheromone deposit** | All ants | Global best only | Global best or iteration best | | **Pheromone bounds** | None (unbounded) | None (unbounded) | Strict: $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ | | **Initialization** | Small constant $\tau_0$ | Small constant $\tau_0$ | Upper bound $\tau_{max}$ | | **Local update** | None | Yes (during construction) | None | | **Stagnation handling** | None | Implicit via local update | Explicit clamping + smoothing | | **Exploration vs Exploitation** | Poor balance | Moderate balance | Strong balance via bounds | | **Performance on TSP** | Baseline | Faster convergence | Best known performance | --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given**: $n = 50$, $L^{gb} = 420$, $\rho = 0.02$, $p_{best} = 0.4$. **Step 1: Compute $\tau_{max}$** Using the formula $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}$: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{0.02 \cdot 420} = \frac{1}{8.4}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 0.1190}$$ **Step 2: Compute $\tau_{min}$** Using the formula $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{max} \cdot (1 - p_{best})}{(n-2) \cdot p_{best}}$: $$\tau_{min} = \frac{0.1190 \cdot (1 - 0.4)}{(50 - 2) \cdot 0.4} = \frac{0.1190 \cdot 0.6}{48 \cdot 0.4}$$ $$\tau_{min} = \frac{0.0714}{19.2}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{min} \approx 0.00372}$$ **Step 3: Verification** - $\tau_{max} = 0.1190 > \tau_{min} = 0.00372$ ✓ (bound condition satisfied) - $p_{best}$ check: $p_{best}^{check} = \dfrac{0.1190}{0.1190 + 48 \cdot 0.00372} = \dfrac{0.1190}{0.2976} \approx 0.40$ ✓ - This confirms the bounds are correctly calibrated. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **Forgetting the Evaporation Term**: When deriving $\tau_{max}$, students often incorrectly write $\tau_{max} = 1/L^{gb}$ without the $\rho$ factor. **Always include $\rho$** — it accounts for the steady-state balance between evaporation and deposition. > > 2. **Confusing AS, ACS, and MMAS**: Many students write the same update rule for all three. Remember: AS → all ants deposit, ACS → only $g_{best}$ with local update, MMAS → only best ant with strict bounds. > > 3. **Wrong Formula for $\tau_{min}$**: Use the **simple form** $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{max} \cdot (1 - p_{best})}{(n-2) \cdot p_{best}}$ for short-answer questions. The complex form is for research-level proofs. > > 4. **Units of $\tau$**: Pheromone values are **dimensionless quantities** (ratios of information intensity). Do not write units. > > 5. **Forgetting Initialization Detail**: A common 1-mark loss is failing to mention that MMAS initializes to $\tau_{max}$, not to a small constant like AS does. > > 6. **Confusing $\tau_{min}/\tau_{max}$ with $\sigma$ (smoothing factor)**: $\sigma$ is the smoothing coefficient, not a pheromone bound. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Max-Min Ant System** - ⭐ **MMAS = Stagnation-Free ACO**: Primary purpose is to avoid premature convergence via pheromone bounding. - ⭐ **Four Modifications**: - (i) Best-ant only deposits pheromone - (ii) $\tau_{ij} \in [\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ (clamping) - (iii) $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_{max}$ (smart init) - (iv) Optional trail smoothing - ⭐ **Key Formulas to Memorize**: - $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L^{gb}}$ - $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{max} \cdot (1 - p_{best})}{(n-2) \cdot p_{best}}$ - $p_{ij}^k = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l} [\tau_{il}]^\alpha [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$ - $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best}$ - ⭐ **Pheromone Trajectory Decay**: After $k$ iterations with no deposit, $\tau(k) = (1-\rho)^k \tau_0$. - ⭐ **Decision Probability Floor** $p_{best} \approx 0.05$ is a typical value used in practice. - ⭐ **Typical Parameter Ranges**: $\alpha \in [1, 2]$, $\beta \in [2, 5]$, $\rho \in [0.01, 0.2]$. - ⭐ **Best-Ant Selection Strategies**: - Use $g_{best}$ for **exploitation** (intensification) - Use $i_{best}$ for **exploration** (diversification) - Alternating is a common heuristic - ⭐ **Trail Smoothing** triggered when **no improvement in $\mu$ consecutive iterations** (commonly $\mu = 250$ to $1000$). - ⭐ **MMAS vs AS**: MMAS has **stronger stagnation avoidance** due to bounds. - ⭐ **MMAS vs ACS**: ACS uses local updates during construction; MMAS uses post-construction clamping. - ⭐ **Real-World Strength**: MMAS is one of the **best-performing ACO variants** on TSP and is a benchmark in the literature. - ⭐ **Proposers**: Stützle and Hoos (2000) — remember this for KTU short-answer questions. - ⭐ **KTU Exam Pattern**: Expect a 3-mark conceptual question + a 14-mark derivation/comparison question. ---

topics.title: Application of ACO to the Travelling Salesman Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of ACO to the Travelling Salesman Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)

The **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** is a classical NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem. Given a set of $N$ cities and the pairwise distances $d_{ij}$ between every pair of cities $(i, j)$, the goal is to find the **shortest possible Hamiltonian cycle** — a closed tour that visits each city exactly once and returns to the starting city, while minimizing total travel distance.

$$ \min \quad L(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{\pi(i), \pi(i+1)} $$

where $\pi$ is a permutation of $\{1, 2, \dots, N\}$ representing the order of city visits, and $\pi(N+1) = \pi(1)$ ensures the cycle closes.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)

**Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a population-based metaheuristic inspired by the foraging behavior of real ants. In ACO, artificial ants construct solutions to a combinatorial problem by stochastically traversing a **construction graph**, guided by two key factors:

- **Pheromone trails** $\tau_{ij}$ — a learned, long-term memory of past good solutions.
- **Heuristic information** $\eta_{ij}$ — a problem-specific greedy desirability measure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACO for TSP (Marco Dorigo, 1991)** is the flagship application of the metaheuristic. It maps TSP cities to graph nodes, edges to TSP routes, and the collective pheromone distribution to a probabilistic search for the optimal Hamiltonian cycle.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an ant colony searching for the shortest path from their nest to a food source. Initially, ants wander randomly, leaving a chemical called **pheromone** on the ground. When an ant chooses between two paths:

1. Shorter path → ant traverses faster → returns sooner → deposits pheromone twice before the slower ant returns.
2. Over time, the shorter path accumulates **more pheromone**, attracting even more ants.

This **positive feedback loop** (autocatalysis) leads to the emergence of an optimal path.

In the TSP-ACO analogy:
- **Ant** = a candidate TSP tour constructor.
- **Pheromone trail** $\tau_{ij}$ = learned measure of how often edge $(i, j)$ appears in good tours.
- **Heuristic** $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ = the static, inverse-distance desirability of edge $(i, j)$.
- **Evaporation** $\rho$ = mechanism that prevents premature convergence on suboptimal edges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** TSP is the canonical benchmark for ACO. Mastering ACO-TSP equips the student to apply the same framework to Vehicle Routing, Job-Shop Scheduling, Network Routing (OSPF), and Graph Coloring.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Parameters

The following **standard metrics** govern every ACO-TSP implementation:

- **Number of ants** $m$ — typically $m = N$ (one ant per city).
- **Pheromone decay coefficient** $\rho \in (0, 1)$ — usually $\rho = 0.5$.
- **Pheromone influence exponent** $\alpha \geq 0$ — controls relative weight of $\tau$.
- **Heuristic influence exponent** $\beta \geq 0$ — controls relative weight of $\eta$.
- **Pheromone deposit constant** $Q$ — total trail added by an ant per tour.

The hyperparameters $(\alpha, \beta, \rho, Q)$ are tuned empirically per problem instance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction Graph for TSP

In ACO-TSP, the **construction graph** $G = (C, L)$ is fully connected, where:
- $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_N\}$ is the set of city nodes.
- $L = \{(c_i, c_j) \mid i \neq j\}$ is the set of directed edges (for asymmetric TSP) or undirected edges (symmetric TSP).
- Each edge $(i, j)$ carries:
  - A **pheromone value** $\tau_{ij}$.
  - A **heuristic value** $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.

### 2.2 Operational Concept: The ACO-TSP Algorithm in 5 Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Initialization**
- Set $t = 0$.
- Initialize pheromone trails uniformly: $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0$ for all edges $(i, j)$.
- Place each ant $k$ on a randomly chosen starting city.

**Step 2 — Solution Construction**
- For each ant $k$, at city $i$, choose the next city $j$ from the set of unvisited cities $J_k(i)$ using the **random-proportional transition rule** (Eq. 1 below).
- Repeat until all cities are visited (forming a complete tour $T_k$).
- Compute tour length $L_k$.

**Step 3 — Pheromone Evaporation**
- Decay existing pheromone globally to discourage premature convergence:
$$ \tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij} \quad \forall (i,j) $$

**Step 4 — Pheromone Deposit (Reinforcement)**
- Each ant $k$ deposits pheromone proportional to the quality (inverse length) of its tour:
$$ \Delta \tau_{ij}^k = \frac{Q}{L_k} \quad \text{if edge } (i,j) \in T^k, \text{ else } 0 $$

**Step 5 — Update and Iterate**
- Aggregate deposits across all ants:
$$ \tau_{ij} \leftarrow \tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^k $$
- Set $t \leftarrow t + 1$, apply optional elitist/local-search refinements, and go to Step 2.

### 2.3 Why This Works: The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why heuristic $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$?** It biases ants toward short edges even before learning occurs, accelerating early-stage search.
- **Why evaporate pheromone?** Without evaporation, edges in early lucky tours would dominate forever, trapping the colony in local optima.
- **Why $\alpha$ and $\beta$ exponents?** They tune the *exploration* ($\beta$ low → more random) vs. *exploitation* ($\alpha$ high → more pheromone-driven) balance.
- **How does autocatalysis emerge?** The transition probability (Eq. 1) is proportional to $\tau_{ij}^\alpha \cdot \eta_{ij}^\beta$. Better tours reinforce $\tau_{ij}$, which in turn raises future selection probability of those edges.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Term | Formula | Purpose / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transition Probability** | $p_{ij}^k(t) = \dfrac{\tau_{ij}^\alpha(t) \cdot \eta_{ij}^\beta}{\sum_{l \in J_k(i)} \tau_{il}^\alpha(t) \cdot \eta_{il}^\beta}$ | Probabilistic rule governing ant $k$'s next-city choice at city $i$. |
| 2 | **Heuristic Information** | $\eta_{ij} = 1 / d_{ij}$ | Static greedy desirability; **inversely proportional to distance**. |
| 3 | **Evaporation Update** | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}$ | Global pheromone decay; $\rho \in (0,1)$. |
| 4 | **Ant Deposit (AS model)** | $\Delta \tau_{ij}^k = Q / L_k$ | Ant $k$ deposits $Q/L_k$ on each edge of its tour. |
| 5 | **Aggregate Update** | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^k$ | Combines evaporation + deposit in one expression. |
| 6 | **Total Tour Length** | $L_k = \sum_{(i,j) \in T^k} d_{ij}$ | Objective value to be minimized. |
| 7 | **Initial Pheromone (Max-Min)** | $\tau_0 = m / (C \cdot L_{nn})$ | $L_{nn}$ = nearest-neighbor tour length; $C$ = NN factor. |
| 8 | **Bounds (MMAS)** | $\tau_{\min} \le \tau_{ij} \le \tau_{\max}$ | Prevents stagnation by clamping trail values. |

> [!NOTE]
> Always remember: the denominator of Eq. 1 is a **normalization sum** over allowed neighbors only — not over all cities. This ensures $p_{ij}^k$ is a valid probability distribution.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

ACO-TSP has direct industrial applications in:

- **Logistics & Supply Chain** — UPS, FedEx route optimization for last-mile delivery.
- **VLSI Circuit Design** — optimal drilling and wire routing on printed circuit boards.
- **Telecommunications** — packet routing in ad-hoc and mobile networks.
- **DNA Sequencing & Bioinformatics** — fragment assembly problems.
- **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles** — multi-robot coverage and exploration paths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Transition Probability Rule

The transition probability $p_{ij}^k(t)$ must satisfy two engineering requirements:

1. **Edge desirability** — Edges with high pheromone AND short distance should be more likely selected.
2. **Normalization** — Probabilities over allowed neighbors must sum to 1.

**Step 1 — Combine attractiveness factors multiplicatively.**
Define the *attractiveness* of edge $(i, j)$ for ant $k$ as:
$$ A_{ij}^k(t) = [\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta $$

**Conversion logic:** Raising $\tau$ and $\eta$ to positive exponents allows independent tuning of *learned* ($\alpha$) vs *greedy* ($\beta$) influence, since TSP units differ (pheromone is dimensionless, distance is in km/m).

**Step 2 — Restrict to unvisited cities.**
An ant $k$ at city $i$ may only choose $j \in J_k(i)$, where $J_k(i)$ is the set of not-yet-visited cities (tabu list constraint).
$$ A_{ij}^k \text{ is defined only for } j \in J_k(i) $$

**Step 3 — Normalize to obtain a valid probability.**
$$ p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{A_{ij}^k(t)}{\sum_{l \in J_k(i)} A_{il}^k(t)} = \frac{\tau_{ij}^\alpha(t) \cdot \eta_{ij}^\beta}{\sum_{l \in J_k(i)} \tau_{il}^\alpha(t) \cdot \eta_{il}^\beta} $$

**Conversion logic:** Dividing by the sum ensures $\sum_{j \in J_k(i)} p_{ij}^k = 1$, satisfying the axioms of probability.

**Step 4 — Stochastic selection via roulette-wheel.**
The next city is sampled with probability $p_{ij}^k(t)$. If $\alpha = 0$, only heuristic applies (greedy nearest-neighbor). If $\beta = 0$, only pheromone applies (memoryless random walk biased by trails).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Combined Pheromone Update Rule

**Step 1 — Start with evaporation only.**
After all ants complete their tours, pheromone decays globally:
$$ \tau_{ij}' = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij} $$

**Step 2 — Add deposits from all $m$ ants.**
Each ant $k$ deposits $\Delta \tau_{ij}^k$ on each edge it traversed:
$$ \Delta \tau_{ij}^k = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k}, & (i,j) \in T^k \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single update equation.**
$$ \tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^k $$

**Conversion logic:** This is a discrete-time linear dynamical system. The factor $(1-\rho)$ represents exponential decay (memory half-life $\approx \log 0.5 / \log(1-\rho)$ iterations), and the summation aggregates parallel contributions from the colony.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ACO on 4-City TSP

Consider 4 cities arranged in a square with the following symmetric distance matrix:

$$ D = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 2 & 5 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 3 & 0 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 & 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix} $$

**Initial Setup:**
- $m = 4$ ants, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$, $\rho = 0.1$, $Q = 1$.
- Initial pheromone: $\tau_{ij}(0) = 0.1$ for all edges.
- Heuristic: $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.

**Iteration 1 — Compute Heuristic Values:**
$$ \eta = \begin{pmatrix} - & 0.500 & 0.200 & 0.333 \\ 0.500 & - & 0.333 & 0.250 \\ 0.200 & 0.333 & - & 0.500 \\ 0.333 & 0.250 & 0.500 & - \end{pmatrix} $$

**Iteration 1 — Tour Construction (Ant 1 starts at city 1):**

At city 1, allowed cities = $\{2, 3, 4\}$.

$$ A_{12} = (0.1)^1 (0.5)^2 = 0.025 $$
$$ A_{13} = (0.1)^1 (0.2)^2 = 0.004 $$
$$ A_{14} = (0.1)^1 (0.333)^2 = 0.0111 $$

Sum $= 0.025 + 0.004 + 0.0111 = 0.0401$.

$$ p_{12} = 0.025 / 0.0401 = 0.623 $$
$$ p_{13} = 0.004 / 0.0401 = 0.0998 $$
$$ p_{14} = 0.0111 / 0.0401 = 0.277 $$

Suppose Ant 1 draws $r = 0.45 \in [0.0998, 0.0998 + 0.623]$ → **selects city 2**.

Continuing this procedure, after all 4 ants complete their tours, suppose the best tour is:
$$ T^* = 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1, \quad L^* = 2+3+2+3 = 10 $$

**Iteration 1 — Pheromone Update:**
$$ \tau_{ij}(1) = 0.9 \cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \sum_k \Delta \tau_{ij}^k $$

For an edge in $T^*$ visited by 1 ant with $L = 10$, $Q = 1$:
$$ \tau_{12}(1) = 0.9(0.1) + 1/10 = 0.09 + 0.1 = 0.19 $$

For edges not in any tour:
$$ \tau_{13}(1) = 0.9(0.1) + 0 = 0.09 $$

**Iteration 2 — Re-weighted Transition:**
$$ A_{12} = (0.19)(0.25) = 0.0475 \quad \text{(increased!)} $$
$$ A_{13} = (0.09)(0.04) = 0.0036 $$

The probability of city 2 rises, biasing the colony toward the optimal tour — **autocatalysis confirmed**.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — ACO Variant Selection for TSP

| Algorithm | Deposit Rule | Best For | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ant System (AS)** | $Q/L_k$ | Small TSP ($N<30$) | Original, simple | Slow convergence |
| **Elitist AS (EAS)** | $Q/L_k + e \cdot Q/L^*$ | Mid-size TSP | Faster convergence on best | Bias stagnation |
| **Max-Min AS (MMAS)** | $Q/L_{best}$, clamped to $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ | Large TSP, benchmark | Best exploration balance | Parameter sensitivity |
| **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | $Q/L_{best}$, local + global update | Real-time / dynamic TSP | Fast, dual update | Complex tuning |
| **Rank-Based AS (ASrank)** | Weighted by rank | Robust applications | Stable performance | More parameters |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ACO-TSP High-Level Process Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **iterative architecture of the ACO-TSP algorithm** as it executes in software.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read TSP Instance] --> B[Initialize Pheromone tau_ij equal to tau_0]
    B --> C[Place Each Ant on Random City]
    C --> D[Set Tabu List of Visited Cities]
    D --> E{All Cities Visited?}
    E -- No --> F[Compute Attractiveness A_ij = tau^alpha times eta^beta]
    F --> G[Compute Transition Probabilities p_ij]
    G --> H[Roulette Wheel Selection of Next City]
    H --> I[Update Tabu List]
    I --> E
    E -- Yes --> J[Compute Tour Length L_k for Each Ant]
    J --> K[Apply Pheromone Evaporation 1 minus rho]
    K --> L[Apply Pheromone Deposit Q over L_k]
    L --> M[Update Best Global Tour T_star]
    M --> N{Max Iterations Reached?}
    N -- No --> D
    N -- Yes --> O[Output Best Tour T_star and Length L_star]
    O --> P[End]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ACO-TSP Interactions

This diagram shows how the **algorithmic modules interact**, mapping pheromone memory, ant colony, and TSP environment into a coherent engineering system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[TSP Environment]
        CITYDB[(City Distance Matrix D)]
        INSTANCE[TSP Instance N cities]
        CITYDB --- INSTANCE
    end

    subgraph ACO[Ant Colony System]
        COLONY[Ant Population m ants]
        CONSTRUCT[Solution Constructor]
        ROUTE[Tabu-based Tour Builder]
        DECISION[Probabilistic Transition Rule]
        COLONY --> CONSTRUCT --> ROUTE --> DECISION
    end

    subgraph MEM[Pheromone Memory]
        TAU[Pheromone Matrix tau_ij]
        EVAP[Evaporation Module rho]
        DEPOSIT[Deposit Module Q over L_k]
        UPDATE[Combined Update Rule]
        TAU --> EVAP --> UPDATE
        UPDATE --> DEPOSIT --> TAU
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Stage]
        EVAL[Tour Length Evaluator L_k]
        BEST[Global Best Tracker T_star]
        RESULT[Final Optimal Tour]
        EVAL --> BEST --> RESULT
    end

    INSTANCE --> COLONY
    DECISION -- read tau and eta --> TAU
    DECISION -- read eta --> CITYDB
    DECISION --> ROUTE
    ROUTE --> EVAL
    EVAL --> DEPOSIT
    TAU -- updated tau --> DECISION
    BEST -- terminate if converged --> RESULT
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Pheromone Reinforcement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Ant constructs tour using tau and eta] --> P2[Evaluate tour length L_k]
    P2 --> P3{Is L_k better than current best?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Increase pheromone on edges of T_k]
    P3 -- No --> P5[Add small pheromone amount]
    P4 --> P6[Evaporate all edges by factor 1 minus rho]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Pheromone matrix now encodes collective knowledge]
    P7 --> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) and state why it is classified as NP-hard.**

**Model Answer:**
The Travelling Salesman Problem seeks the shortest Hamiltonian cycle visiting each of $N$ cities exactly once and returning to the start. Formally, it minimizes $L(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{\pi(i), \pi(i+1)}$ over permutations $\pi$. It is NP-hard because the search space grows factorially as $(N-1)!/2$, and no polynomial-time exact algorithm is known for the general case. TSP belongs to the class of NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems, requiring metaheuristics such as ACO for large instances.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the role of pheromone evaporation in ACO-TSP?**

**Model Answer:**
Pheromone evaporation, governed by the decay coefficient $\rho \in (0,1)$, gradually reduces trail intensity on all edges according to $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}$. This serves two critical roles: **(i) Exploration** — it prevents the algorithm from being trapped in early suboptimal solutions by erasing over-exploited edges, and **(ii) Forgetting** — it allows the colony to adapt when the search landscape changes (e.g., dynamic TSP). Without evaporation, the algorithm would suffer from premature convergence and stagnation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Formulate the Travelling Salesman Problem mathematically. With the help of a construction graph, explain how Ant Colony Optimization can be applied to solve TSP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a.1) Mathematical Formulation of TSP (3 marks):**

Given $N$ cities with distance matrix $d_{ij}$, the symmetric TSP seeks:

$$ \min_{\pi \in \Pi} L(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{\pi(i), \pi(i+1)} $$

subject to $\pi(N+1) = \pi(1)$ and each city visited exactly once. The feasible set $\Pi$ contains $(N-1)!/2$ unique tours.

**(a.2) Construction Graph for ACO-TSP (2 marks):**
The construction graph $G = (C, L)$ is fully connected, with cities as nodes and edges labeled by:
- Pheromone $\tau_{ij}(t)$ — time-varying, learned.
- Heuristic $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ — static, greedy.

Each ant builds a tour by sequentially traversing edges.

**(a.3) ACO-TSP Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Initialize $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0$.
2. Each ant $k$ constructs a tour using transition probability $p_{ij}^k$.
3. Evaluate $L_k$ for each ant.
4. Apply global update $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$.
5. Repeat until convergence.

---

**(b)** Describe the Ant Colony System (ACS) variant and explain how the local and global pheromone update rules improve upon the original Ant System (AS) for solving TSP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(b.1) Limitations of Ant System (AS) (2 marks):**
In the original AS (Dorigo, 1991), all ants deposit pheromone globally after each iteration, and there is no explicit balance between exploration and exploitation. This leads to slow convergence and stagnation behavior on large TSP instances.

**(b.2) ACS Local Update Rule (2 marks):**
While constructing a tour, immediately after an ant traverses edge $(i,j)$, a local update is applied:
$$ \tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\xi)\tau_{ij} + \xi \cdot \tau_0 $$
where $\xi \in (0,1)$ and $\tau_0$ is the initial pheromone level. This reduces the pheromone on visited edges, encouraging **subsequent ants to explore alternative paths** and thereby increasing diversity.

**(b.3) ACS Global Update Rule (2 marks):**
After all ants finish their tours, only the **best-so-far ant** deposits pheromone on the edges of $T^*$:
$$ \tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \rho \cdot \frac{1}{L^*} \quad \text{if } (i,j) \in T^* $$

**(b.4) Pseudorandom Proportional Action Choice (1 mark):**
ACS uses a balanced rule — with probability $q_0$, exploit the best edge (greedy); otherwise apply the standard AS roulette-wheel rule. This decouples exploration and exploitation, yielding faster convergence on TSP benchmarks such as TSPLIB.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]

**(a)** Derive the transition probability rule used in ACO-TSP. Clearly explain the role of $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\eta_{ij}$, and the tabu list. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a.1) Need for a Stochastic Rule (1 mark):**
Since TSP is NP-hard, ants must use a stochastic mechanism that combines learned and heuristic information while respecting the constraint that each city is visited once.

**(a.2) Constructing the Attractiveness (2 marks):**
The attractiveness of edge $(i,j)$ for ant $k$ at iteration $t$ combines the two information sources multiplicatively:
$$ A_{ij}^k(t) = [\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta $$

**(a.3) Applying the Tabu Constraint (2 marks):**
Each ant maintains a tabu list $Tabu_k$ recording already-visited cities. The allowed next-city set is $J_k(i) = \{1, \dots, N\} \setminus Tabu_k$. The attractiveness is defined only over this set.

**(a.4) Normalization (2 marks):**
Dividing by the total attractiveness over allowed cities yields a valid probability:
$$ p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{\tau_{ij}^\alpha(t) \cdot \eta_{ij}^\beta}{\sum_{l \in J_k(i)} \tau_{il}^\alpha(t) \cdot \eta_{il}^\beta}, \quad j \in J_k(i) $$

**Role of Parameters:**
- $\alpha$ — controls the influence of pheromone (learned, collective intelligence).
- $\beta$ — controls the influence of heuristic (greedy, local knowledge).
- $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ — promotes short edges.
- Tabu list — enforces the Hamiltonian constraint (no revisits).

---

**(b)** For a 4-city symmetric TSP with the distance matrix below, perform one iteration of the Ant System algorithm with $m=2$, $\alpha=1$, $\beta=1$, $\rho=0.1$, $Q=1$, and initial pheromone $\tau_{ij}(0)=0.1$. Assume Ant 1 starts at city 1 and Ant 2 starts at city 3, and that both ants randomly select city 2 next (with probability 0.5 each from their allowed set). Compute the updated pheromone for edge (1,2) and (2,3). **(7 marks)**

$$ D = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 4 & 6 & 5 \\ 4 & 0 & 3 & 2 \\ 6 & 3 & 0 & 7 \\ 5 & 2 & 7 & 0 \end{pmatrix} $$

**Model Solution:**

**(b.1) Heuristic Matrix (1 mark):**
$$ \eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij} $$
$$ \eta_{12} = 0.25,\ \eta_{13} = 0.1667,\ \eta_{14} = 0.2,\ \eta_{23} = 0.333,\ \eta_{24} = 0.5,\ \eta_{34} = 0.1429 $$

**(b.2) Compute Tour Lengths (2 marks):**

Assume both ants construct tours (using problem-stipulated randomness):
- **Ant 1**: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$, $L_1 = 4 + 3 + 7 + 5 = 19$
- **Ant 2**: $3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 1 \to 3$, $L_2 = 3 + 2 + 5 + 6 = 16$

**(b.3) Compute Deposits (2 marks):**

For edge $(1,2)$ — only in Ant 1's tour:
$$ \Delta\tau_{12}^1 = 1/19 = 0.0526,\quad \Delta\tau_{12}^2 = 0 $$
$$ \sum_k \Delta\tau_{12}^k = 0.0526 $$

For edge $(2,3)$ — only in Ant 2's tour:
$$ \Delta\tau_{23}^1 = 0,\quad \Delta\tau_{23}^2 = 1/16 = 0.0625 $$
$$ \sum_k \Delta\tau_{23}^k = 0.0625 $$

**(b.4) Apply Global Update (2 marks):**

$$ \tau_{12}(1) = (1 - 0.1)(0.1) + 0.0526 = 0.09 + 0.0526 = 0.1426 $$
$$ \tau_{23}(1) = (1 - 0.1)(0.1) + 0.0625 = 0.09 + 0.0625 = 0.1525 $$

**Verification:** Edge (2,3) now has higher pheromone (0.1525) than edge (1,2) (0.1426), reflecting Ant 2's shorter tour — autocatalytic behavior confirmed.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the tabu list**: Writing $p_{ij}^k$ without specifying the allowed set $J_k(i)$ loses 1–2 marks. Always mention tabu constraint.
> 2. **Missing the $\alpha, \beta$ exponents**: Writing $p_{ij} = \tau_{ij} \eta_{ij} / \sum$ without exponents is technically the $\alpha=\beta=1$ case but loses generality marks.
> 3. **Confusing AS with ACS**: Local + global update is ACS only. AS has only the global update.
> 4. **Wrong evaporation placement**: Evaporation happens BEFORE deposit, not after.
> 5. **Omitting units of $L_k$**: Always specify $L_k$ in consistent distance units.
> 6. **Forgetting normalization**: If $\sum p_{ij} \neq 1$, the formula is wrong. Verify in derivations.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Application of ACO to TSP:**

- **TSP Goal**: Find shortest Hamiltonian cycle through $N$ cities; NP-hard; $(N-1)!/2$ possible tours.
- **ACO Analogy**: Real ants use pheromone to find shortest paths; artificial ants use $\tau_{ij}$ and $\eta_{ij}$ to find shortest tours.
- **Two Information Sources**: Pheromone $\tau_{ij}$ (learned, collective) and Heuristic $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ (static, greedy).
- **Transition Probability**: $p_{ij}^k = \tau_{ij}^\alpha \eta_{ij}^\beta / \sum_{l \in J_k(i)} \tau_{il}^\alpha \eta_{il}^\beta$ — over allowed (unvisited) cities only.
- **Parameters**: $\alpha$ (pheromone weight), $\beta$ (heuristic weight), $\rho$ (evaporation rate), $Q$ (deposit constant).
- **Evaporation**: $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}$ — done globally before deposit; prevents stagnation.
- **Deposit (AS)**: $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = Q/L_k$ if edge in tour; 0 otherwise.
- **Combined Update**: $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$.
- **Tabu List**: Each ant maintains a record of visited cities; enforces Hamiltonian constraint.
- **Autocatalysis**: Better tours get reinforced → attract more ants → emerge as global best.
- **Variants**:
  - **AS** — original, baseline.
  - **EAS** — elitist ant reinforces global best.
  - **MMAS** — clamps pheromone to $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$; best for benchmark TSP.
  - **ACS** — local update + global update + pseudorandom rule; fastest convergence.
- **Engineering Applications**: Logistics (UPS), VLSI drilling, telecom routing, DNA assembly, robot path planning.
- **Examiner Tip**: Always state the tabu list, show normalization, mention $\alpha,\beta$ exponents, and verify $L_k$ units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspiration from Nature - Social Behavior of Bird Flock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inspiration from Nature - Social Behavior of Bird Flock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bird Flock Social Behavior** (in the context of metaheuristic optimization) refers to the collective, decentralized movement patterns exhibited by groups of birds, which serve as the **biological inspiration** for the **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** algorithm. Formally, it describes a **self-organizing, emergent stochastic search strategy** in which a population of candidate solutions (called *particles*) navigates a multi-dimensional search space by emulating three biologically observed flocking rules originally formulated by **Craig W. Reynolds (1987)** in his *Boids* model:

1. **Separation** — Avoid crowding local neighbors.
2. **Alignment** — Steer toward the average heading of local neighbors.
3. **Cohesion** — Steer toward the average position of local neighbors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The bird-flocking metaphor bridges **biological ethology** (study of animal behavior) and **computational intelligence**. The 1995 paper by **James Kennedy** (social psychologist) and **Russell Eberhart** (electrical engineer) translated this ethological behavior into a numerical optimizer. This forms the philosophical backbone of Module 4.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **flock of 50 birds searching a foggy field for the single tallest grain of corn**. No bird can see the entire field, but every bird knows:

- **Its own best-remembered spot** (where it personally once found corn).
- **Its neighbors' best-known spot** (where the flock collectively found the best corn so far).

Each bird then adjusts its flight direction by a weighted combination of three intuitions:

| Intuition | Real Bird Behaviour | PSO Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| "Don't crash into my neighbor" | Separation | Constrain velocity magnitude |
| "Fly like my neighbors fly" | Alignment | Inherit velocity component |
| "Move toward where the flock is converging" | Cohesion | Move toward global best |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** A bird flock solves a *collective* navigation problem without any central leader. Each bird follows three ultra-simple local rules, yet the flock as a whole exhibits **intelligent, coordinated, optimal-looking behavior**. PSO copies this: simple local rules + local memory + social memory = powerful global optimization.

---

### 1.3 Why Bird Flocking Matters in Optimization

The flock is a **natural analogy for an optimization problem**:

- **Search space** → the field of corn.
- **Particle (bird)** → one candidate solution vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^D$.
- **Fitness function** → height of grain at the bird's current location.
- **pbest** → the tallest grain that bird *i* has personally ever seen.
- **gbest** → the tallest grain that *any* bird in the flock has ever seen.

By sharing only *local* information, the swarm converges to a globally excellent region. This is the **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** paradigm: **collective intelligence from decentralized, simple-agent interaction.**

---

### 1.4 Key Historical & Standard Metrics

- **Flock size (population, $N$)** — typically **20 to 50** particles.
- **Reynolds' Boids simulation (1987)** — first computational model of flocking.
- **Heppner & Grenander (1990)** — proposed a model of bird flocking with an "attractor" (the cornfield), which inspired the PSO formulation.
- **Kennedy & Eberhart (1995)** — introduced PSO at the IEEE International Conference on Neural Networks.
- **Cognitive coefficient** $c_1$ — typically **2.0**.
- **Social coefficient** $c_2$ — typically **2.0**.
- **Inertia weight $w$** — typically in **[0.4, 0.9]**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reynolds' Boids Model — The Three Steering Rules

Craig Reynolds formalized flocking in 1987 as the sum of three steering vectors applied to each agent at every simulation step.

#### Rule 1: Separation
Steer to avoid crowding neighbors within a small radius $r_s$.

$$
\mathbf{F}_{sep} = - \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_s(i)} \frac{\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j}{\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|}
$$

- $\mathcal{N}_s(i)$ = set of neighbors within separation radius.
- The negative sign indicates a *repulsive* direction.

#### Rule 2: Alignment
Steer toward the average velocity (heading) of local neighbors within radius $r_a$.

$$
\mathbf{F}_{align} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_a(i)|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_a(i)} \mathbf{v}_j
$$

- This produces the synchronized, ripple-like turns of real starling murmurations.

#### Rule 3: Cohesion
Steer toward the average position (center of mass) of local neighbors within radius $r_c$.

$$
\mathbf{F}_{coh} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_c(i)|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_c(i)} \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{x}_i
$$

#### Composite Steering Force
The total velocity update for a Boid becomes:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{v}_i(t) + \alpha \, \mathbf{F}_{sep} + \beta \, \mathbf{F}_{align} + \gamma \, \mathbf{F}_{coh}
$$

where $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ are tunable weights.

---

### 2.2 From Flocking to Optimization — Kennedy & Eberhart's Leap

Heppner & Grenander (1990) posed a beautiful question: *What if each bird is also pulled toward a hidden "roosting place" (cornfield)?* Kennedy and Eberhart converted this attractor concept into the **PSO update equations**:

**Velocity Update:**
$$
\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = w \, \mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \bigl( \mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t) \bigr) + c_2 r_2 \bigl( \mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t) \bigr)
$$

**Position Update:**
$$
\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)
$$

**Why this works — the three-term decomposition maps exactly onto flocking:**

| PSO Term | Boid Analogy | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $w \, \mathbf{v}_i(t)$ | **Inertia** (Newton's 1st Law) | Bird keeps flying in current direction |
| $c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ | **Cognitive / Nostalgia** | Bird remembers its own best spot |
| $c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ | **Social / Conformity** | Bird is pulled toward flock's best |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Swarm size (number of birds) | 20–50 | dimensionless |
| $D$ | Dimensionality of search space | Problem-specific | dimensionless |
| $t$ | Current iteration counter | $\geq 0$ | iterations |
| $\mathbf{x}_i(t)$ | Position of particle $i$ at iteration $t$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^D$ | problem-specific |
| $\mathbf{v}_i(t)$ | Velocity of particle $i$ at iteration $t$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^D$ | problem-specific |
| $\mathbf{p}_i^{best}$ | Best position particle $i$ has found (pbest) | $\in \mathbb{R}^D$ | problem-specific |
| $\mathbf{g}^{best}$ | Best position found by entire swarm (gbest) | $\in \mathbb{R}^D$ | problem-specific |
| $w$ | Inertia weight | 0.4 to 0.9 | dimensionless |
| $c_1$ | Cognitive acceleration coefficient | 1.5 to 2.0 | dimensionless |
| $c_2$ | Social acceleration coefficient | 1.5 to 2.0 | dimensionless |
| $r_1, r_2$ | Uniform random vectors | $\sim U(0,1)$ | dimensionless |
| $v_{max}$ | Velocity clamping limit | $\pm (x_{max} - x_{min})$ | problem-specific |
| $r_s, r_a, r_c$ | Reynolds neighbor radii | geometry-dependent | length |

**Convergence Boundary Conditions:**
- $0 \leq w < 1$ (typically linearly decreasing from 0.9 to 0.4).
- $0 < c_1, c_2 \leq 2.5$.
- $\vert v_{ij} \vert \leq v_{max,j}$ to prevent swarm explosion.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Use of Bird-Flock-Inspired PSO |
|---|---|
| **Power Systems** | Optimal power flow, economic dispatch |
| **Antenna Design** | Array thinning, beam-pattern synthesis |
| **Neural Networks** | Training weight optimization |
| **Robotics** | Multi-robot path planning & flocking control |
| **Image Processing** | Image segmentation, feature selection |
| **Aerospace** | Satellite constellation design, trajectory optimization |
| **Biomedical** | ECG/EEG signal parameter tuning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why flocking is special:** It balances **exploration** (global search via social term) with **exploitation** (local search via cognitive term) **without any gradient information** — making it ideal for non-differentiable, noisy, black-box problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: From Newton's Laws to PSO Velocity Update

We derive the PSO update equations from first principles by analogy with classical mechanics and Reynolds' flocking rules.

---

#### Step 1 — Start with Newton's Second Law for a Particle
A particle of mass $m$ under force $\mathbf{F}$ has acceleration $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{F}/m$.

In discrete time with step $\Delta t$, the velocity update is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{v}_i(t) + \mathbf{a}_i(t) \, \Delta t
$$

This term represents the **inertia / momentum** of the bird's flight.

---

#### Step 2 — Introduce the Cognitive Force (Personal Memory)
The bird has a memory $\mathbf{p}_i^{best}$ of its best past location. The displacement vector toward this memory is:

$$
\Delta \mathbf{x}_{cog} = \mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t)
$$

Treating this as a Hooke's-law-like spring force, the induced velocity contribution is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{cog} = c_1 \, r_1 \, (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))
$$

- $c_1$ = cognitive spring stiffness.
- $r_1$ = stochastic perturbation factor $\sim U(0,1)$.

---

#### Step 3 — Introduce the Social Force (Flock Memory)
The bird also feels attraction toward the flock's best-known location $\mathbf{g}^{best}$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{soc} = c_2 \, r_2 \, (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))
$$

- $c_2$ = social spring stiffness.
- $r_2$ = stochastic perturbation factor $\sim U(0,1)$.

---

#### Step 4 — Combine Inertia, Cognitive, and Social Forces

Summing all three contributions (a linear superposition, valid because we treat each as an independent acceleration over one time step):

$$
\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = w \, \mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i) + c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)
$$

where $w$ is the **inertia weight** controlling the persistence of the previous velocity (a damping factor $\in [0,1)$).

---

#### Step 5 — Integrate Velocity to Update Position
Position evolves by Euler integration of the velocity:

$$
\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual mapping to Reynolds' rules — final alignment check:**
>
> - $w \, \mathbf{v}_i(t)$ corresponds to **alignment** (preserve direction).
> - $c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ corresponds to **separation/individuality** (don't blindly follow the flock).
> - $c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ corresponds to **cohesion** (move toward group consensus).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each PSO Parameter

| Parameter Increased | Effect on Exploration | Effect on Exploitation | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| $w$ (Inertia) | ↑ Global search | ↓ Local refinement | Swarm diverges if $w \geq 1$ |
| $c_1$ (Cognitive) | ↑ Diversity | ↓ Convergence speed | Premature stagnation on local optima |
| $c_2$ (Social) | ↓ Diversity | ↑ Faster convergence | Premature convergence |
| $N$ (Swarm size) | ↑ Robustness | ↓ Per-iteration efficiency | Higher computation cost |
| $r_1, r_2$ randomness | ↑ Stochastic exploration | ↓ Determinism | Slower convergence |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Mini-Demonstration)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ for one particle in 1-D.

**Setup:**
- $N = 1$ bird, $D = 1$.
- $w = 0.5$, $c_1 = 1.0$, $c_2 = 2.0$.
- Initial position $x_1(0) = 5$, initial velocity $v_1(0) = 0$.
- Assume $\mathbf{p}_1^{best} = 5$ (initial best = initial position), and $\mathbf{g}^{best} = 3$ (some other bird found a better spot).
- Random samples: $r_1 = 0.4$, $r_2 = 0.7$.

**Step 1 — Velocity Update:**
$$
v_1(1) = 0.5 \times 0 + 1.0 \times 0.4 \times (5 - 5) + 2.0 \times 0.7 \times (3 - 5)
$$

$$
v_1(1) = 0 + 0 + 2.0 \times 0.7 \times (-2) = -2.8
$$

**Step 2 — Position Update:**
$$
x_1(1) = 5 + (-2.8) = 2.2
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Fitness:**
$$
f(5) = 25, \quad f(2.2) = 4.84
$$

Since $4.84 < 25$, the particle **updates its pbest**:
$$
\mathbf{p}_1^{best} \leftarrow 2.2
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The bird overshot slightly (it moved from 5 toward 3 but landed at 2.2, past the optimum at 0). On the next iteration, the cognitive term will pull it back toward 2.2, while the social term pulls it toward the (updated) gbest — yielding classic PSO damped oscillation around the optimum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bird Flock → PSO Mapper

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **biological inspiration flow** from real flocking behavior to the algorithmic PSO structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["REAL BIRD FLOCK ENVIRONMENT"]:::env --> B["REYNOLDS BOIDS MODEL 1987"]:::model
    B --> C1["SEPARATION RULE"]:::rule
    B --> C2["ALIGNMENT RULE"]:::rule
    B --> C3["COHESION RULE"]:::rule
    C1 --> D["HEPPNER AND GRENANDER ATTRACTOR MODEL 1990"]:::bridge
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E["KENNEDY AND EBERHART PSO 1995"]:::pso
    E --> F1["INERTIA TERM w times v"]:::term
    E --> F2["COGNITIVE TERM c1 r1 pbest minus x"]:::term
    E --> F3["SOCIAL TERM c2 r2 gbest minus x"]:::term
    F1 --> G["VELOCITY UPDATE EQUATION"]:::out
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["POSITION UPDATE x new equals x old plus v new"]:::out
    H --> I["CANDIDATE SOLUTION SEARCH"]:::goal

    classDef env fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef model fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef rule fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef bridge fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c,color:#000
    classDef pso fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000
```

---

### 4.2 Per-Particle Update Flow (Algorithm Pipeline)

The following flowchart represents what each particle does in a single iteration $t$, mirroring the cognitive + social decision-making of a real bird in a flock.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["START ITERATION t"]:::start --> P1["READ current position x_i t"]:::read
    P1 --> P2["EVALUATE fitness f x_i t"]:::eval
    P2 --> P3{"IS f x_i t LESS THAN f pbest_i"}:::decide
    P3 -- YES --> P4["UPDATE pbest_i equals x_i t"]:::update
    P3 -- NO --> P5["KEEP pbest_i UNCHANGED"]:::update
    P4 --> P6["CHECK gbest FROM SWARM"]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7["SAMPLE r1 r2 from U 0 1"]:::random
    P7 --> P8["COMPUTE new velocity v_i t plus 1"]:::compute
    P8 --> P9["CLIP velocity within vmax range"]:::clip
    P9 --> P10["COMPUTE new position x_i t plus 1 equals x_i t plus v_i t plus 1"]:::compute
    P10 --> P11{"CONVERGED OR t equals tmax"}:::decide
    P11 -- NO --> P0
    P11 -- YES --> P12["OUTPUT gbest AS SOLUTION"]:::out

    classDef start fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef read fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef eval fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000
    classDef decide fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef update fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef random fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#00838f,color:#000
    classDef clip fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Analogy Diagram — Information Flow in a Flock

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["BIRD 1"]:::bird -->|shares position| F["FLOCK MEMORY gbest"]
    B2["BIRD 2"]:::bird -->|shares position| F
    B3["BIRD 3"]:::bird -->|shares position| F
    B4["BIRD N"]:::bird -->|shares position| F
    F -->|broadcasts best| B1
    F -->|broadcasts best| B2
    F -->|broadcasts best| B3
    F -->|broadcasts best| B4
    B1 -->|remembers own past| M1["MEMORY pbest 1"]:::memory
    B2 -->|remembers own past| M2["MEMORY pbest 2"]:::memory
    B3 -->|remembers own past| M3["MEMORY pbest 3"]:::memory
    B4 -->|remembers own past| M4["MEMORY pbest N"]:::memory
    M1 --> B1
    M2 --> B2
    M3 --> B3
    M4 --> B4

    classDef bird fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef memory fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain briefly the biological inspiration behind Particle Swarm Optimization. Mention the three flocking rules proposed by Reynolds.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Particle Swarm Optimization is inspired by the collective social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling. James Kennedy and Russell Eberhart (1995) developed it by translating the flocking dynamics studied by Craig Reynolds (1987) into a computational optimizer.

Reynolds proposed three simple steering rules that each bird follows locally:
1. **Separation** — avoid crowding nearby flock-mates.
2. **Alignment** — steer toward the average heading of nearby flock-mates.
3. **Cohesion** — steer toward the average position of nearby flock-mates.

When applied in parallel, these three rules produce the complex, coordinated flock behavior observed in nature. PSO borrows this same "simple local rules ⇒ emergent global intelligence" principle for optimization.

*(Word count ≈ 100. Allocate ~1 mark for naming Kennedy & Eberhart + Reynolds, 1 mark for the three rules, 1 mark for the local-to-global emergent principle.)*

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the cognitive component ($c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$) and the social component ($c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$) in the PSO velocity update.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cognitive Component | Social Component |
|---|---|---|
| Source of information | Particle's **own** past experience | **Entire swarm's** best experience |
| Variable | $\mathbf{p}_i^{best}$ (personal best) | $\mathbf{g}^{best}$ (global best) |
| Coefficient | $c_1$ — controls individuality | $c_2$ — controls conformity |
| Effect | Encourages **diversification** | Encourages **convergence** |
| Real-bird analogy | "Recall where I personally found corn" | "Follow the flock to where the cornfield is" |

A high $c_1$ makes the swarm explore more (each bird trusts itself more), while a high $c_2$ speeds up convergence (each bird trusts the flock more). Balancing both prevents premature convergence and stagnation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **omit Reynolds' name and year (1987)** — examiners specifically award marks for proper attribution.
> 2. Students frequently **miss the inertia term** when writing the PSO velocity equation — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. Do **not** confuse *Reynolds' alignment* with *PSO's social term* — they are related but not identical concepts.
> 4. Always state the **role of $r_1, r_2$** (stochastic exploration) explicitly.
> 5. In numerical problems, show **every substitution step**; skipping steps costs partial marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe in detail how the social behavior of bird flocking inspired the development of the Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm. Explain Reynolds' Boids model, the Heppner-Grenander attractor concept, and the role of Kennedy & Eberhart. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the PSO velocity and position update equations from first principles, clearly identifying each term with its biological analog. State typical parameter ranges. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Reynolds' Boids Model (1987) (≈ 3 marks):**
Computer graphics researcher Craig W. Reynolds (1987) created a simulation called *Boids* in which each artificial bird obeys three local rules:

- **Separation:** steer away to avoid local crowding — prevents collisions.
- **Alignment:** steer toward the average heading of neighbors — produces synchronized turns.
- **Cohesion:** steer toward the average position of neighbors — keeps the flock together.

Although each rule is trivial, the **emergent flocking behavior** (smooth, coordinated, realistic-looking motion) arises from their simultaneous application. This demonstrated that complex global intelligence can emerge from simple local interactions.

**Step 2 — Heppner & Grenander Attractor Model (1990) (≈ 2 marks):**
Heppner and Grenander (1990) extended Reynolds' model by adding a hidden **"roosting place"** (analogous to a cornfield or attractor). Birds in their model were continuously pulled toward this attractor through a *social* force, simulating the way real starlings converge on a common landing site. This introduced the optimization-flavoured concept of a *goal* in the search space.

**Step 3 — Kennedy & Eberhart's PSO (1995) (≈ 2 marks):**
Social psychologist James Kennedy and electrical engineer Russell Eberhart (1995) translated Heppner & Grenander's attractor into a numerical search procedure. They replaced:

- the roosting place with the **best-known solution** ($\mathbf{g}^{best}$),
- the birds with **particles** carrying both position and velocity,
- and added each bird's **personal best memory** ($\mathbf{p}_i^{best}$) for individuality.

The resulting algorithm — Particle Swarm Optimization — became one of the foundational metaheuristics of swarm intelligence.

---

##### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law as Starting Point (≈ 2 marks):**
For a particle of mass $m$ under acceleration $\mathbf{a}$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{v}_i(t) + \mathbf{a}_i(t) \Delta t
$$

This **inertia term** preserves the bird's current flight direction.

**Step 2 — Cognitive Force (≈ 2 marks):**
A "personal memory spring" pulls each bird toward $\mathbf{p}_i^{best}$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{cog} = c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))
$$

**Step 3 — Social Force (≈ 2 marks):**
A "flock memory spring" pulls each bird toward $\mathbf{g}^{best}$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{soc} = c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))
$$

**Step 4 — Combined Velocity & Position Updates (≈ 1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = w \, \mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t)) + c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)
$$

**Step 5 — Typical Parameter Ranges:**
- $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ (often linearly decreased),
- $c_1, c_2 \in [1.5, 2.0]$,
- $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$,
- $\vert v_{ij} \vert \leq v_{max}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the three Reynolds flocking rules and map each rule to its corresponding PSO velocity-update term. How does the absence of any one rule affect swarm behavior? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PSO swarm has $N=3$ particles in 1-D. At iteration $t=2$, the data are:

| Particle $i$ | $x_i$ | $v_i$ | $pbest_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 1 | 3 |
| 2 | -2 | 0.5 | -1 |
| 3 | 6 | -0.5 | 5 |

Assume $w=0.6$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.5$, $r_1 = 0.5$, $r_2 = 0.8$.

Compute $\mathbf{v}_i(3)$ and $\mathbf{x}_i(3)$ for all three particles. Take $v_{max} = \pm 3$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Reynolds' Three Rules (≈ 3 marks):**

1. **Separation:** $\mathbf{F}_{sep} = - \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_s} \frac{\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j}{\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|}$ — prevents local collisions.

2. **Alignment:** $\mathbf{F}_{align} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_a|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_a} \mathbf{v}_j$ — propagates direction.

3. **Cohesion:** $\mathbf{F}_{coh} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_c|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_c} \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{x}_i$ — pulls toward local centroid.

**Mapping to PSO (≈ 2 marks):**

| Reynolds Rule | PSO Velocity Term | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Alignment | $w \, \mathbf{v}_i(t)$ | Inertia preserves direction |
| Cohesion | $c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ | Pull toward flock consensus |
| Separation | (implicit) $c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ | Pull toward personal space |

**Effect of Removing Each Rule (≈ 2 marks):**
- **No Separation** → particles collide, swarm collapses to a point.
- **No Alignment** → particles change direction chaotically, no coordinated motion.
- **No Cohesion** → swarm disperses, no convergence.

---

##### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Pre-step — Determine $\mathbf{g}^{best}$ (≈ 1 mark):**
Assuming $f(x) = x^2$:
- $f(pbest_1) = 9$,
- $f(pbest_2) = 1$ ← minimum,
- $f(pbest_3) = 25$.

Therefore $\mathbf{g}^{best} = -1$.

**Step 1 — Particle 1: $x_1 = 4, v_1 = 1, pbest_1 = 3, gbest = -1$ (≈ 2 marks):**

$$
v_1(3) = (0.6)(1) + (1.5)(0.5)(3 - 4) + (1.5)(0.8)(-1 - 4)
$$

$$
v_1(3) = 0.6 + (0.75)(-1) + (1.2)(-5) = 0.6 - 0.75 - 6.0 = -6.15
$$

Clipped: $v_1(3) = -3$ (since $\vert v_{max} \vert = 3$).

$$
x_1(3) = 4 + (-3) = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Particle 2: $x_2 = -2, v_2 = 0.5, pbest_2 = -1, gbest = -1$ (≈ 2 marks):**

$$
v_2(3) = (0.6)(0.5) + (1.5)(0.5)(-1 - (-2)) + (1.5)(0.8)(-1 - (-2))
$$

$$
v_2(3) = 0.3 + (0.75)(1) + (1.2)(1) = 0.3 + 0.75 + 1.2 = 2.25
$$

Within bounds, so no clipping.

$$
x_2(3) = -2 + 2.25 = 0.25
$$

**Step 3 — Particle 3: $x_3 = 6, v_3 = -0.5, pbest_3 = 5, gbest = -1$ (≈ 2 marks):**

$$
v_3(3) = (0.6)(-0.5) + (1.5)(0.5)(5 - 6) + (1.5)(0.8)(-1 - 6)
$$

$$
v_3(3) = -0.3 + (0.75)(-1) + (1.2)(-7) = -0.3 - 0.75 - 8.4 = -9.45
$$

Clipped: $v_3(3) = -3$.

$$
x_3(3) = 6 + (-3) = 3
$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Particle | $v_i(3)$ (after clipping) | $x_i(3)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-3.00$ | $1.00$ |
| 2 | $+2.25$ | $0.25$ |
| 3 | $-3.00$ | $3.00$ |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Bird flocking** is the **biological inspiration** for **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)**.
- [x] **Craig W. Reynolds (1987)** formulated the *Boids* model with **three rules**: **Separation, Alignment, Cohesion**.
- [x] **Heppner & Grenander (1990)** added a **hidden attractor** ("cornfield"), bridging flocking to optimization.
- [x] **Kennedy & Eberhart (1995)** introduced **PSO** at IEEE ICNN.
- [x] PSO update equations:
  - Velocity: $\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = w \, \mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i) + c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$.
  - Position: $\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)$.
- [x] **Inertia term** $w \, \mathbf{v}_i(t)$ → bird's momentum (Newton's 1st Law).
- [x] **Cognitive term** $c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ → "nostalgia" / personal memory.
- [x] **Social term** $c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g}^{best} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ → "conformity" / flock memory.
- [x] Typical values: $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$, $c_1, c_2 \approx 2.0$, $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$.
- [x] **Velocity clamping** $v_{max}$ is **mandatory** to prevent swarm explosion.
- [x] PSO is a **gradient-free, population-based, stochastic** metaheuristic.
- [x] **Exploration ↔ Exploitation** trade-off is controlled by the **balance between $c_1$ and $c_2$**.
- [x] Real-world applications: power systems, antenna design, neural network training, robotics, image processing.
- [x] Removing **Separation** → collisions; **Alignment** → chaos; **Cohesion** → dispersion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Models of PSO - Local Best PSO and Global Best PSO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Models of PSO - Local Best PSO and Global Best PSO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** is a population-based, stochastic, metaheuristic optimization technique introduced by **Kennedy and Eberhart (1995)**, inspired by the social behaviour of bird flocking and fish schooling. Each candidate solution (called a *particle*) moves through the search space with a velocity that is dynamically adjusted according to its own flying experience (cognitive component) and that of its neighbours (social component).

A **swarm** of $N$ particles explores a $D$-dimensional search space, where each particle $i$ maintains:
- A **position vector** $\vec{x}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \dots, x_{iD})$
- A **velocity vector** $\vec{v}_i = (v_{i1}, v_{i2}, \dots, v_{iD})$
- A **personal best** $\vec{p}_i^{best}$ (the best position it has visited)
- A **neighbourhood best** $\vec{n}_i^{best}$ (the best position found by its neighbours)

### 1.2 The Two Basic Models of PSO

> [!NOTE]
> **The basic models of PSO are distinguished solely by the social topology that defines "who informs whom" within the swarm.**

#### (A) Global Best PSO (gbest PSO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Best PSO (gbest PSO)** is the original, canonical model in which the social component of every particle is dictated by a *single* best-performing particle in the **entire swarm** — namely the *global best* $\vec{g}^{best}$. This corresponds to a **star-shaped (fully connected) social topology**, where information flows instantly across the whole population.

$$
\vec{n}_i^{best} \;=\; \vec{g}^{best} \quad \forall \, i \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}
$$

#### (B) Local Best PSO (lbest PSO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Best PSO (lbest PSO)** is the second basic model proposed by Kennedy and Eberhart, in which each particle is influenced only by the best position found within a small **neighbourhood** of itself. The standard implementation uses a **ring topology**, where each particle communicates with exactly two neighbours. This is denoted $\vec{l}_i^{best}$ — the *local best*.

$$
\vec{n}_i^{best} \;=\; \vec{l}_i^{best} \in \mathcal{N}_i
$$

where $\mathcal{N}_i$ is the immediate neighbourhood of particle $i$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Flock of Birds

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy (for intuition):** Imagine a flock of hungry pigeons scattered over a field, all searching for a hidden grain of corn.
>
> - **gbest (Global Best) — One bird sees the corn and shouts "EUREKA!" to the whole flock:** every single pigeon instantly knows the location and flies straight toward it. The flock converges *very fast*, but if the corn turns out to be a deceptive local patch (e.g., a piece of bread), the whole swarm gets trapped because no one is willing to explore further.
>
> - **lbest (Local Best) — Birds in small circles share info only with immediate neighbours:** each pigeon only knows the best patch that its *two immediate ring-neighbours* have found. Information propagates *slowly* around the ring. The flock explores much more thoroughly, taking longer to converge, but is far less likely to be fooled by a local deception.
>
> This single sociological difference — *global broadcast vs. local gossip* — is the essence of the two basic PSO models.

**Core parameters (in bold for emphasis):**
- **Inertia weight $w$** — controls momentum of the previous velocity.
- **Cognitive coefficient $c_1$** — pulls particle toward its own best.
- **Social coefficient $c_2$** — pulls particle toward neighbourhood/global best.
- **$V_{\max}$** — maximum allowable velocity per dimension.
- **Swarm size $N$** and **neighbourhood size $K$** (for lbest).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step by Step

#### 2.1.1 Velocity Update (the heart of PSO)

The velocity update in both basic models has the **same three-term structure**: *momentum + cognitive pull + social pull*. They differ only in the social pull.

**For gbest PSO:**

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) \;=\; w\,\vec{v}_i(t) \;+\; c_1 r_1 \bigl(\vec{p}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr) \;+\; c_2 r_2 \bigl(\vec{g}^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr)
$$

**For lbest PSO:**

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) \;=\; w\,\vec{v}_i(t) \;+\; c_1 r_1 \bigl(\vec{p}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr) \;+\; c_2 r_2 \bigl(\vec{l}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr)
$$

| Term | Symbol | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Momentum / Inertia | $w\,\vec{v}_i(t)$ | Keeps the particle moving in its current direction — encourages *exploration* of new regions. |
| Cognitive / Nostalgia | $c_1 r_1 (\vec{p}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i)$ | Pulls particle toward its *own* best memory — encourages *exploitation* of individual experience. |
| Social / Conformity | $c_2 r_2 (\vec{n}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i)$ | Pulls particle toward the best position found by its neighbours — *gbest* if global, *lbest* if local. |

#### 2.1.2 Position Update

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) \;=\; \vec{x}_i(t) \;+\; \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

#### 2.1.3 Velocity Clamping (boundary control)

$$
v_{ij}(t+1) \;=\; \text{sign}\!\bigl(v_{ij}(t+1)\bigr) \cdot \min\!\Bigl(\bigl\vert v_{ij}(t+1)\bigr\vert,\; V_{\max,j}\Bigr)
$$

This prevents particles from "exploding" out of the feasible region.

### 2.2 Topology — The Real Differentiator

| Property | gbest PSO | lbest PSO |
|---|---|---|
| Neighbourhood size $K$ | $N$ (entire swarm) | small (typically 2 in ring) |
| Network shape | Star | Ring (or other sub-graph) |
| Information propagation | Instantaneous | Slow (hops through ring) |
| Convergence speed | Fast | Slow |
| Susceptibility to local optima | High | Low |
| Diversity of swarm | Low (premature convergence) | High (sustained exploration) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU 2024 Formula Sheet — Master this table for instant marks in the exam.**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Value / Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity update (gbest) | $v_{ij}(t+1) = w v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{ij}^{best} - x_{ij}) + c_2 r_2 (g_j^{best} - x_{ij})$ | — | Global social learning |
| Velocity update (lbest) | $v_{ij}(t+1) = w v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{ij}^{best} - x_{ij}) + c_2 r_2 (l_{ij}^{best} - x_{ij})$ | — | Local ring social learning |
| Position update | $x_{ij}(t+1) = x_{ij}(t) + v_{ij}(t+1)$ | — | Particle displacement |
| Velocity clamp | $v_{ij} \in [-V_{\max,j}, \; V_{\max,j}]$ | $V_{\max} = 0.1$ to $0.2 \times$ range | Prevents explosion |
| Inertia weight $w$ | Linearly decreasing schedule (Shi & Eberhart) | $w: 0.9 \to 0.4$ | Balances exploration/exploitation |
| Cognitive coeff. $c_1$ | — | $2.0$ to $2.05$ | "Self-confidence" |
| Social coeff. $c_2$ | — | $2.0$ to $2.05$ | "Swarm confidence" |
| Constriction factor $\chi$ | $\chi = \dfrac{2\kappa}{\varphi - 2 + \sqrt{\varphi(\varphi - 4)}}$, $\varphi = c_1 + c_2$, $\kappa = 1$ | $\chi \approx 0.7298$ when $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ | Ensures convergence (Clerc-Kennedy) |
| Constriction-modified velocity | $v_{ij}(t+1) = \chi\bigl[v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1(p_{ij}^{best} - x_{ij}) + c_2 r_2(n_{ij}^{best} - x_{ij})\bigr]$ | — | Guaranteed stable PSO |
| Random vectors | $r_1, r_2 \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$ | — | Stochastic perturbation |

> **⚠ LaTeX safety note for table:** All absolute-value / magnitude notations inside table cells are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the unescaped pipe `\vert`), preserving the markdown table structure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why gbest/lbest PSO is used |
|---|---|
| **Power systems** (economic dispatch, unit commitment) | Fast convergence of gbest helps in real-time grid decisions. |
| **Antenna / RF design** | lbest PSO explores rugged design spaces without premature convergence. |
| **Neural network training (weight optimisation)** | gbest PSO replaces backpropagation in non-differentiable networks. |
| **Robotic path planning** | lbest PSO maintains diversity for obstacle-rich environments. |
| **Image segmentation (medical)** | Hybrid gbest+lbest PSO balances accuracy and robustness. |
| **Structural engineering (truss sizing)** | gbest finds near-optimal designs quickly under tight time budgets. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Constructing the Velocity Update Equation from First Principles

The original PSO velocity update is built by **superposing three behavioural tendencies**. Each tendency is derived as follows.

#### Step 1 — Pure inertia (Newton's first law analogue)

If a particle had *no* social interaction at all, it should keep moving in its current direction with the same speed. Hence the first term:

$$
\vec{v}_i^{\text{inertia}}(t+1) \;=\; \vec{v}_i(t)
$$

#### Step 2 — Cognitive / individual memory term

A particle knows its own best past position $\vec{p}_i^{best}$. The velocity contribution should be *proportional* to the displacement from its current position toward this best position, modulated by randomness. This gives:

$$
\vec{v}_i^{\text{cog}}(t+1) \;=\; c_1\, r_1 \bigl(\vec{p}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr)
$$

**Reasoning:** $r_1 \in [0,1]$ is a uniformly distributed random scalar — it represents *stochastic hesitation* in trusting one's own memory. $c_1$ scales the overall self-confidence.

#### Step 3 — Social / neighbourhood term

Similarly, the particle is attracted to the best position found by its neighbours. For gbest, this is $\vec{g}^{best}$; for lbest, it is $\vec{l}_i^{best}$. Define a generic neighbour-best as $\vec{n}_i^{best}$:

$$
\vec{v}_i^{\text{soc}}(t+1) \;=\; c_2\, r_2 \bigl(\vec{n}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr)
$$

#### Step 4 — Linearly combining all three (superposition)

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) \;=\; w\,\vec{v}_i(t) \;+\; c_1 r_1 \bigl(\vec{p}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr) \;+\; c_2 r_2 \bigl(\vec{n}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr)
$$

Here $w$ is the **inertia weight** (a multiplier on the first term) — when $w = 1$, the equation is the original 1995 form; values $0 < w < 1$ dampen momentum over time.

#### Step 5 — Position update (Euler integration)

Treating PSO as a discrete-time dynamical system with unit time step, the position is integrated via forward Euler:

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) \;=\; \vec{x}_i(t) \;+\; \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

### 3.2 Stability Analysis — Derivation of the Constriction Coefficient $\chi$

The constriction coefficient was derived by **Clerc and Kennedy (2002)** by analysing the stability of a single particle moving toward a *fixed* attractor.

#### Step 1 — Simplify the system

Assume:
- The personal best and neighbour best coincide at a fixed point: $\vec{p}_i^{best} = \vec{n}_i^{best} = \vec{p}$ (a constant).
- $r_1, r_2$ are absorbed into constants $c_1, c_2$ (deterministic analysis).

Then the velocity update (1-D form, with $w = 1$ and no constriction) becomes:

$$
v(t+1) \;=\; v(t) + c_1\bigl(p - x(t)\bigr) + c_2\bigl(p - x(t)\bigr) \;=\; v(t) + \varphi\, y(t)
$$

where $y(t) \triangleq p - x(t)$ and $\varphi = c_1 + c_2$.

#### Step 2 — Derive the recurrence for $y(t)$

$$
y(t+1) \;=\; p - x(t+1) \;=\; p - \bigl[x(t) + v(t+1)\bigr] \;=\; y(t) - v(t+1)
$$

Substitute the velocity update:

$$
y(t+1) \;=\; y(t) - \bigl[v(t) + \varphi\, y(t)\bigr] \;=\; -v(t) + (1 - \varphi)\, y(t)
$$

#### Step 3 — Write the system in matrix form

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v(t+1) \\ y(t+1) \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \varphi \\ -1 & 1 - \varphi \end{bmatrix}}_{M} \begin{bmatrix} v(t) \\ y(t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 4 — Eigenvalue analysis for stability

For convergence, both eigenvalues of $M$ must satisfy $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$. Compute the characteristic polynomial:

$$
\det(M - \lambda I) \;=\; (1 - \lambda)(1 - \varphi - \lambda) - \varphi(-1) \;=\; \lambda^2 + (\varphi - 2)\lambda + 1 \;=\; 0
$$

#### Step 5 — Stability condition

The roots of the characteristic equation are:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} \;=\; \frac{-( \varphi - 2) \pm \sqrt{(\varphi - 2)^2 - 4}}{2} \;=\; \frac{(2 - \varphi) \pm \sqrt{\varphi(\varphi - 4)}}{2}
$$

For $\varphi > 4$, the discriminant is positive (real eigenvalues). The magnitude condition $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ for both roots is satisfied when the spectral radius is bounded. Multiplying the system matrix by a **constriction factor $\chi$** modifies the characteristic equation to:

$$
\lambda^2 - \chi(\varphi - 2)\lambda + \chi^2 \;=\; 0
$$

Applying the Jury stability criterion (or Routh-Hurwitz for discrete systems), the necessary and sufficient conditions are:

$$
\chi^2 < 1, \quad \chi(\varphi - 2) < 1 + \chi^2
$$

The most commonly used value (Clerc-Kennedy 2002) sets $\kappa = 1$ and chooses $\chi$ to bring the eigenvalues precisely to the boundary of the unit circle for maximum speed:

$$
\chi \;=\; \frac{2 \kappa}{\,\varphi - 2 + \sqrt{\varphi(\varphi - 4)}\,}
$$

#### Step 6 — Numerical evaluation (canonical case)

With the canonical recommendation $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$:

$$
\varphi \;=\; c_1 + c_2 \;=\; 4.1
$$

$$
\chi \;=\; \frac{2(1)}{4.1 - 2 + \sqrt{4.1 \times 0.1}} \;=\; \frac{2}{2.1 + \sqrt{0.41}} \;=\; \frac{2}{2.1 + 0.6403} \;=\; \frac{2}{2.7403} \;\approx\; 0.7298
$$

The final constriction-modified velocity update becomes:

$$
v_{ij}(t+1) \;=\; \chi \Bigl[\,v_{ij}(t) \;+\; c_1 r_1 (p_{ij}^{best} - x_{ij}(t)) \;+\; c_2 r_2 (n_{ij}^{best} - x_{ij}(t))\Bigr]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
