> **Why this derivation matters (KTU angle):** It explains *why* $\chi \approx 0.7298$ is the "magic number" for stable PSO convergence — it emerges directly from the eigenvalue analysis, NOT from empirical tuning. Examiners love this kind of derivation question (frequently worth 7 marks).

### 3.3 Effect of Inertia Weight $w$ — Analytical Intuition

Decompose $w$ into two regimes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w > 1 & : \text{velocity diverges} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{particles explode, divergence} \\
0.9 \le w \le 1.0 & : \text{strong exploration, slow convergence} \\
0.4 \le w \le 0.9 & : \text{balanced search — recommended range (Shi \& Eberhart)} \\
w < 0.4 & : \text{behaviour becomes hill-climbing, premature convergence}
\end{aligned}
$$

A linearly decreasing schedule $w(t) = w_{\max} - (w_{\max} - w_{\min}) \frac{t}{T_{\max}}$ (typically $0.9 \to 0.4$) is the most popular KTU-style choice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Topology Comparison — gbest vs. lbest

**Diagram 1: Global Best (gbest) PSO — Star Topology**

Every particle in the swarm is directly connected to the *single* global best position $\vec{g}^{best}$. Information propagates instantaneously across the entire swarm.

```mermaid
graph TD
    GBT((Global Best gbest))
    P1[Particle 1] <--> GBT
    P2[Particle 2] <--> GBT
    P3[Particle 3] <--> GBT
    P4[Particle 4] <--> GBT
    P5[Particle 5] <--> GBT
    P6[Particle 6] <--> GBT
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the star topology of gbest PSO, all $N$ particles are direct neighbours of the global best — giving $N$ edges that form a perfect star. The graph is **fully connected through the centre**.

**Diagram 2: Local Best (lbest) PSO — Ring Topology**

Each particle communicates only with its two immediate neighbours (indices $i-1$ and $i+1$, with wrap-around). The *local best* $\vec{l}_i^{best}$ is the best among these three particles.

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1[Particle 1] <--> Q2[Particle 2]
    Q2 <--> Q3[Particle 3]
    Q3 <--> Q4[Particle 4]
    Q4 <--> Q5[Particle 5]
    Q5 <--> Q6[Particle 6]
    Q6 <--> Q1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the ring topology of lbest PSO, each particle has degree 2 — exactly two neighbours. The best position within a particle's neighbourhood is its $\vec{l}_i^{best}$. Information must hop around the ring, dramatically slowing convergence but increasing diversity.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Basic PSO Algorithm

The figure below captures the complete execution pipeline of *both* gbest and lbest PSO. The block **Update Neighbourhood Best** is the *only* architectural difference between the two models.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> A1[Initialise swarm positions x_i and velocities v_i randomly within bounds]
    A1 --> A2[Set iteration counter t = 0 and maximum iterations T_max]
    A2 --> A3[Evaluate fitness f_x_i of every particle]
    A3 --> A4[Update personal best pbest_i for each particle i]
    A4 --> A5{Choose Topology Model}
    A5 -->|Gbest| A6[Identify global best gbest across the whole swarm]
    A5 -->|Lbest| A7[For each particle identify lbest_i within its ring neighbourhood of size K]
    A6 --> A8[Compute new velocity v_i_t_plus_1 using inertia cognitive and social terms]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Clamp velocity to V_max per dimension]
    A9 --> A10[Update position x_i_t_plus_1 = x_i_t + v_i_t_plus_1]
    A10 --> A11[Apply boundary handling for x_i if outside search range]
    A11 --> A12{Termination criterion met: t equals T_max or fitness below epsilon}
    A12 -->|No| A13[Increment t = t + 1 and return to Evaluate Fitness]
    A12 -->|Yes| A14[Report gbest or lbest as the final best solution]
    A14 --> A15([End])
```

### 4.3 Vector Geometry of a Single PSO Update

The three components of the velocity update are vectorially superposed to produce the new velocity.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inertia term w times v_i_t] --> SUM((Vector Sum))
    B[Cognitive term c1 r1 times pbest_i minus x_i] --> SUM
    C[Social term c2 r2 times nbest_i minus x_i] --> SUM
    SUM --> D[New velocity v_i_t_plus_1]
    D --> E[New position x_i_t_plus_1 = x_i_t + v_i_t_plus_1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The new velocity is the *vector sum* of the inertia, cognitive, and social contributions. Each contribution is a vector in $\mathbb{R}^D$, scaled by the corresponding coefficient and random factor. The particle then translates by this new velocity vector in the search space.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Differentiate between Global Best (gbest) and Local Best (lbest) PSO in terms of social topology and convergence behaviour.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | gbest PSO | lbest PSO |
> |---|---|---|
> | Social topology | Star graph — every particle is connected to a *single* global best $\vec{g}^{best}$ | Ring (or sub-graph) — each particle has a small neighbourhood of size $K$ |
> | Information sharing | Global and instantaneous | Local; propagates slowly via neighbours |
> | Convergence speed | Fast (faster information flow) | Slow (information must hop through ring) |
> | Risk of premature convergence | High — whole swarm is pulled by one best | Low — neighbourhood diversity is maintained |
>
> Mathematically, gbest uses $\vec{g}^{best}$ in the social term, while lbest uses $\vec{l}_i^{best} \in \mathcal{N}_i$.

---

**Q2. What is the role of the inertia weight $w$ in the PSO velocity update equation?** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)]** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The inertia weight $w$ is a scalar multiplier on the previous velocity term $w \vec{v}_i(t)$ in the velocity update equation. It controls the **trade-off between exploration (global search) and exploitation (local search):**
>
> - **Larger $w$ (close to 1):** Particle retains its previous direction → encourages *exploration* of new regions, but may overshoot good optima.
> - **Smaller $w$ (close to 0):** Velocity decays quickly → encourages *exploitation* of the current region, but may cause premature convergence.
> - **A linearly decreasing $w$ from 0.9 to 0.4** over the iterations is the most common KTU-style choice, providing exploration early and exploitation later.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Paper Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically carrying 7 marks each. Either Question A or Question B (provided as alternatives) must be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Derive the velocity and position update equations of Global Best (gbest) PSO. Explain the role of inertia weight, cognitive coefficient, and social coefficient.** (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Conceptual construction (2 marks):** In gbest PSO, every particle's velocity is updated based on three superposed behavioural tendencies:
- Inertia (continuation of previous motion),
- Cognitive pull (toward its own best memory $\vec{p}_i^{best}$),
- Social pull (toward the global best $\vec{g}^{best}$ of the entire swarm).

**Step 2 — Velocity update equation (2 marks):**

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) \;=\; w\,\vec{v}_i(t) \;+\; c_1 r_1 \bigl(\vec{p}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr) \;+\; c_2 r_2 \bigl(\vec{g}^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr)
$$

where $w$ is the inertia weight, $c_1$ is the cognitive coefficient, $c_2$ is the social coefficient, and $r_1, r_2 \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$ are independent uniform random numbers.

**Step 3 — Position update equation (1 mark):**

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) \;=\; \vec{x}_i(t) \;+\; \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

**Step 4 — Role of parameters (2 marks):**
- **Inertia weight $w$:** Balances global and local search. Large $w$ → exploration; small $w$ → exploitation. A linearly decreasing schedule $0.9 \to 0.4$ is recommended.
- **Cognitive coefficient $c_1$:** Controls how strongly a particle is pulled back toward its own best position. Larger $c_1$ → more "self-confident" particles that explore individually.
- **Social coefficient $c_2$:** Controls how strongly a particle is pulled toward the global best. Larger $c_2$ → faster convergence but more risk of premature convergence to local optima.

---

**(b) Explain the Local Best (lbest) PSO model with a neat ring topology diagram. How does it differ from gbest PSO in terms of information sharing?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** In lbest PSO, each particle $i$ is assigned a small neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}_i$ (typically containing itself and its two ring neighbours $i-1$ and $i+1$, with wrap-around). The *local best* $\vec{l}_i^{best}$ is defined as the best position found by *any* particle in $\mathcal{N}_i$.

**Step 2 — Ring topology diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Particle 1] <--> R2[Particle 2]
    R2 <--> R3[Particle 3]
    R3 <--> R4[Particle 4]
    R4 <--> R5[Particle 5]
    R5 <--> R1
```

**Step 3 — Velocity update for lbest (2 marks):**

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) \;=\; w\,\vec{v}_i(t) \;+\; c_1 r_1 \bigl(\vec{p}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr) \;+\; c_2 r_2 \bigl(\vec{l}_i^{best} - \vec{x}_i(t)\bigr)
$$

Note that $\vec{l}_i^{best} \in \mathcal{N}_i$ instead of the global $\vec{g}^{best}$.

**Step 4 — Information sharing comparison (1 mark):**
- **gbest:** Information about a good region found by any particle propagates to the *entire* swarm in *one* iteration.
- **lbest:** Information propagates *one neighbour at a time* around the ring; takes up to $N/2$ iterations to traverse half the swarm. This slower propagation maintains diversity and reduces premature convergence.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Compare Global Best PSO and Local Best PSO in terms of topology, convergence speed, exploration, exploitation, and susceptibility to local optima.** (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison table (any 5 valid points for 7 marks — each point 1 mark, well-explained):**

| Criterion | gbest PSO | lbest PSO |
|---|---|---|
| **Social topology** | Star — every particle connected to one global best | Ring — each particle has $K = 2$ neighbours |
| **Convergence speed** | Fast (single-hop information flow) | Slow (multi-hop information flow along ring) |
| **Exploration capability** | Weak — swarm collapses quickly to one region | Strong — multiple sub-swarms explore different regions |
| **Exploitation capability** | Strong — once a good region is found, the whole swarm exploits it | Moderate — exploitation is distributed across neighbourhoods |
| **Diversity of swarm** | Low — particles cluster near $\vec{g}^{best}$ | High — particles remain spread across search space |
| **Susceptibility to local optima** | High — premature convergence is common | Low — diverse sub-populations avoid getting stuck |
| **Best suited for** | Smooth, unimodal landscapes or when speed matters | Rugged, multi-modal landscapes requiring robust search |
| **Parameter sensitivity** | Highly sensitive to $c_2$ | Less sensitive, more forgiving of poor parameter choices |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** gbest converges fast but is risky; lbest is robust but slow. Many practical implementations use a hybrid or dynamically adaptive topology.

---

**(b) With a neat flowchart, describe the algorithmic steps of the basic PSO algorithm. Discuss the effect of the parameters $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$, and $V_{\max}$ on algorithm performance.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Flowchart (3 marks):** Refer to the Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes. The key steps are:

1. **Initialisation:** Randomly initialise $\vec{x}_i$ and $\vec{v}_i$ for $i = 1, \dots, N$ within search bounds.
2. **Evaluation:** Compute fitness $f(\vec{x}_i)$ for every particle.
3. **Update $pbest$:** If $f(\vec{x}_i) < f(\vec{p}_i^{best})$, then set $\vec{p}_i^{best} \leftarrow \vec{x}_i$.
4. **Update $gbest$ (gbest PSO) / $lbest$ (lbest PSO).**
5. **Update velocity** using the three-term equation.
6. **Clamp velocity** to $[-V_{\max}, V_{\max}]$.
7. **Update position** using $\vec{x}_i \leftarrow \vec{x}_i + \vec{v}_i$.
8. **Boundary handling** (clamp or reflect position to feasible region).
9. **Check termination** (max iterations or fitness threshold). If not met, return to step 2.

**Step 2 — Effect of parameters (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

- **$w$ (inertia weight):** Controls momentum. $w \uparrow$ → more exploration; $w \downarrow$ → more exploitation. Recommended linearly decreasing schedule $0.9 \to 0.4$.
- **$c_1$ (cognitive coefficient):** Controls the strength of "individual learning". $c_1 \uparrow$ → particles wander more independently. Too high → poor convergence; too low → loses diversity.
- **$c_2$ (social coefficient):** Controls "social conformity" toward the best. $c_2 \uparrow$ → faster convergence to $gbest$/$\textit{lbest}$ but more risk of premature convergence. Empirical best: $c_1 + c_2 \le 4.0$ (or use Clerc's constriction with $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, $\chi \approx 0.7298$).
- **$V_{\max}$:** Clamps velocity to prevent particles from leaving the search space. $V_{\max} \uparrow$ → more exploration but risk of overshooting; $V_{\max} \downarrow$ → finer local search but possible stagnation. Typical setting: $0.1$ to $0.2$ times the dynamic range of the search space per dimension.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $w$ term:** Writing the original 1995 PSO equation without the inertia weight — the *current KTU syllabus demands the inertia-weighted form*. Deduct up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing gbest with lbest equation:** Writing $\vec{g}^{best}$ in the lbest update (or vice-versa) loses the *core distinction*. Deduct 3 marks — this is the central learning outcome.
> 3. **Skipping velocity clamping:** When asked to describe the algorithm, omitting the $V_{\max}$ clamp means missing the most common cause of particle "explosion". Deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong symbol for local best:** Some students write $\vec{g}^{best}$ instead of $\vec{l}_i^{best}$ in lbest PSO equations. The subscript $i$ matters!
> 5. **No diagram in topology questions:** A 7-mark lbest/gbest comparison without a ring/star diagram is incomplete. Examiners specifically allocate 2 marks for the topology diagram.
> 6. **Mixing up cognitive/social roles:** $c_1$ is *self* (cognitive), $c_2$ is *social* (neighbour). Reversing them is a frequently penalised error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Basic Models of PSO**

- ✅ **PSO** is a population-based metaheuristic inspired by bird flocking, using particles with position $\vec{x}_i$ and velocity $\vec{v}_i$.
- ✅ **Two basic models** differ *only* in social topology: **gbest (star, global)** and **lbest (ring, local)**.
- ✅ **Velocity update has three terms:** inertia ($w$), cognitive ($c_1 r_1$), social ($c_2 r_2$).
- ✅ **gbest PSO uses $\vec{g}^{best}$** — a single swarm-wide best position. Fast convergence, high risk of premature convergence.
- ✅ **lbest PSO uses $\vec{l}_i^{best}$** — the best in a small neighbourhood (typically ring of 2). Slow convergence, low risk of premature convergence.
- ✅ **Position update:** $\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)$.
- ✅ **Inertia weight $w$** balances exploration vs. exploitation; recommended $0.9 \to 0.4$ (linearly decreasing).
- ✅ **Cognitive coefficient $c_1$** ≈ 2.0–2.05; **Social coefficient $c_2$** ≈ 2.0–2.05.
- ✅ **Velocity clamping $V_{\max}$** prevents particle explosion — typically $0.1$–$0.2 \times$ search range.
- ✅ **Constriction coefficient $\chi$** (Clerc-Kennedy): $\chi = \frac{2}{\varphi - 2 + \sqrt{\varphi(\varphi-4)}}$ where $\varphi = c_1 + c_2$. For $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, $\chi \approx 0.7298$.
- ✅ **gbest = star topology** (all-to-one centre); **lbest = ring topology** (each particle has 2 neighbours).
- ✅ **Random vectors $r_1, r_2 \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$** introduce stochasticity — without them, PSO is deterministic and may get stuck.
- ✅ **Personal best $\vec{p}_i^{best}$** is updated *before* the social term uses $\vec{g}^{best}$ or $\vec{l}_i^{best}$.
- ✅ **Boundary handling** for $\vec{x}_i$ (clamp, reflect, or absorb) is needed when particles leave the feasible region.
- ✅ **Constriction factor modifies** the entire bracket $[v_i + c_1 r_1 (p_i - x_i) + c_2 r_2 (n_i - x_i)]$ — multiply the *sum* by $\chi$, *not* only the previous velocity.
- ✅ **KTU-typical exam weight:** gbest vs. lbest comparison (frequently 7 marks); velocity equation derivation (frequently 7 marks); constriction factor (frequently 3–7 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: gbest versus lbest PSO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** gbest versus lbest PSO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PSO (Particle Swarm Optimization)** is a population-based stochastic metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling, originally proposed by **James Kennedy and Russell Eberhart (1995)**.

In standard PSO, each particle moves through the search space with a velocity that is dynamically adjusted according to two key influences:

1. **Cognitive Component** — its own personal best position (`pbest`).
2. **Social Component** — the best position found by the swarm (or by its neighbors).

The way this "social component" is defined partitions PSO into two principal topological variants:

### **A. Global Best (gbest) PSO**
> [!NOTE]
> In **gbest PSO**, every particle in the swarm is fully connected to every other particle. The social term pulls each particle toward the single best position found by **the entire swarm** so far — denoted as **gbest (global best)**.

This corresponds to a **fully connected (or star) topology**, where information propagates through the population in a single iteration. The gbest particle acts as a single attractor for the entire swarm.

### **B. Local Best (lbest) PSO**
> [!NOTE]
> In **lbest PSO**, each particle is connected only to a **subset of particles** (its *neighborhood*). The social term pulls each particle toward the best position found by its **immediate neighborhood** — denoted as **lbest (local best)**.

Common neighborhood structures include the **ring topology, wheel topology, von Neumann topology, and pyramid topology**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Birds Searching for Food**

Imagine a flock of 20 birds searching a field for the only patch of food.

### **gbest PSO — "The Loud-Mouth Flock"**
Every bird **shouts its findings continuously to every other bird** in the flock at every second. The moment any one bird spots food, **all 20 birds immediately know about it** and rush toward that exact spot. Information travels at the speed of light within the swarm.

> *Result: Fast convergence, but everyone piles on top of one spot — even if it's a fake food patch (local optimum).*

### **lbest PSO — "The Quiet Neighborhood Flock"**
Each bird is only friends with **its 2 immediate neighbors on either side** (a ring). A bird only learns about food finds from its 3 neighbors — not from the bird way across the field.

> *Result: Slower convergence, but the flock spreads out and explores more — better at escaping fake food patches and finding the truly best spot.*

### **Key Trade-off (The PSO Compass):**

| Property | gbest PSO | lbest PSO |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Convergence Speed | **Fast** ⚡ | Slower 🐢 |
| Exploration | Poor (exploits heavily) | **Strong** (explores widely) |
| Risk of Premature Convergence | **High** | Low |
| Information Spread | Entire swarm (1 step) | Neighborhood (multi-step) |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard PSO Parameters (per KTU 2024 syllabus notation):**
> - **w** (inertia weight): typically **0.4 to 0.9** (linearly decreasing)
> - **c₁** (cognitive coefficient): typically **2.0** (self-confidence)
> - **c₂** (social coefficient): typically **2.0** (swarm confidence)
> - **r₁, r₂** : uniform random numbers ∈ **[0, 1]**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard PSO Update Equations (Foundation)

For a particle $i$ at iteration $t$ with position vector $\vec{x}_i(t)$ and velocity vector $\vec{v}_i(t)$ in a $D$-dimensional search space:

### **Velocity Update:**
$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w \cdot \vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \left(\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)\right) + c_2 r_2 \left(\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)\right)
$$

### **Position Update:**
$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

Where:
- $\vec{p}_i(t)$ = best position ever visited by particle $i$ up to iteration $t$ (personal best / **pbest**)
- $\vec{p}_g(t)$ = best position found by **the swarm / neighborhood** (the **only difference** between gbest and lbest)

---

## 2.2 The Crucial Difference: gbest vs lbest

The mathematical distinction lies **entirely** in how $\vec{p}_g(t)$ is computed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **gbest PSO**, $\vec{p}_g(t) = \arg\min_{j \in S} f(\vec{p}_j(t))$, where $S$ = the **entire swarm**.
>
> In **lbest PSO**, $\vec{p}_g(t) = \arg\min_{j \in N_i} f(\vec{p}_j(t))$, where $N_i$ = the **neighborhood of particle $i$** (a strict subset of $S$).

### **Logical Step-by-Step (How gbest Works):**

1. Initialize $N$ particles with random positions $\vec{x}_i(0)$ and velocities $\vec{v}_i(0)$.
2. Evaluate fitness $f(\vec{x}_i(0))$ for every particle.
3. Set $\vec{p}_i(0) = \vec{x}_i(0)$ for all $i$.
4. Determine $\vec{p}_g(t) = $ best $\vec{p}_j(t)$ over **all** $j = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.
5. Update velocity using the formula above with this global $\vec{p}_g(t)$.
6. Update position. Apply clamping ($v_{max}$, $x_{max}$) if needed.
7. Repeat from Step 2 until convergence.

### **Logical Step-by-Step (How lbest Works):**

1. Initialize $N$ particles with random positions and velocities.
2. Evaluate fitness. Set $\vec{p}_i(0) = \vec{x}_i(0)$.
3. **Define topology**: assign each particle a fixed neighborhood $N_i$ (e.g., 2 neighbors on each side in a ring).
4. For each particle $i$, determine $\vec{p}_g^{(i)}(t) = $ best $\vec{p}_j(t)$ over $j \in N_i$ (a **per-particle** local best).
5. Update velocity of each particle using its **own** $\vec{p}_g^{(i)}(t)$.
6. Update position. Repeat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: In lbest, each particle may have a **different** social attractor. In gbest, **all** particles share the **same** social attractor.

---

## 2.3 Common Neighborhood Topologies for lbest PSO

| Topology | Structure | Neighbors per Particle | Information Spread Speed |
|----------|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Ring** | Particles in a circle | 2 (or $k$ on each side) | Slowest (best exploration) |
| **Star** | One central hub connected to all | All connect through hub | 2 hops (moderate) |
| **Wheel** | One central node, others are leaves | All via hub | 2 hops |
| **Von Neumann** | 2D grid, 4 neighbors (N,S,E,W) | 4 | Faster than ring |
| **Pyramid** | Triangular pyramid structure | Variable | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb**: Larger neighborhoods → behavior approaches gbest; smaller neighborhoods → stronger exploration but slower convergence.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | PSO Velocity Update | $\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_g - \vec{x}_i)$ | Universal |
| 2 | PSO Position Update | $\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)$ | Universal |
| 3 | gbest Definition | $\vec{p}_g = \arg\min_{j \in S} f(\vec{p}_j)$ | $S$ = entire swarm |
| 4 | lbest Definition | $\vec{p}_g^{(i)} = \arg\min_{j \in N_i} f(\vec{p}_j)$ | $N_i \subset S$ |
| 5 | Inertia Weight Bounds | $w \in [0.4, \text{ } 0.9]$ (linearly decreasing) | Controls exploration/exploitation |
| 6 | Clamping Velocity | $v_{ij} \in [-v_{max}, \text{ } v_{max}]$ | Prevents explosion |
| 7 | Ring Topology Size | $\vert N_i \vert = 3$ (or odd $k$) | Symmetric neighbors |
| 8 | Convergence Speed (qualitative) | gbest: $O(1)$ step propagation; lbest: $O(\text{diameter})$ | Diameter = max hops |
| 9 | Recommended Population | 20–50 for gbest; 40–100 for lbest | lbest needs more particles |
| 10 | Convergence Criterion | $\vert f_{best}(t) - f_{best}(t-1) \vert < \epsilon$ | Standard stopping rule |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **gbest PSO** is preferred when the objective function is **smooth, unimodal, or computationally cheap**, and rapid convergence to a good solution is desired (e.g., **real-time control tuning, embedded optimization in antennas/phased arrays**).
- **lbest PSO** is preferred for **multimodal, noisy, or deceptive landscapes** (e.g., **neural network training, structural design optimization, job-shop scheduling, antenna array synthesis with multiple local minima**) where escaping local optima is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the gbest Velocity Update Equation

The standard PSO velocity update can be derived conceptually from three behavioral forces:

### **Step 1: Inertia Term (Momentum)**
The particle tends to continue moving in its current direction. This is the "memory" of its previous flight.

$$
\vec{v}_i^{(1)}(t+1) = w \cdot \vec{v}_i(t)
$$

> **Logic**: A particle cannot instantly change direction; momentum carries it forward (Newton's First Law analogy).

### **Step 2: Cognitive Term (Self-Experience)**
The particle is pulled toward the best position it has personally discovered (`pbest`).

$$
\vec{v}_i^{(2)}(t+1) = c_1 r_1 \left(\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)\right)
$$

> **Logic**: $\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)$ is the vector from the particle's current position to its personal best. The constants $c_1$ (confidence factor) and $r_1$ (randomness in [0,1]) scale this pull stochastically.

### **Step 3: Social Term (Swarm Experience)**
The particle is pulled toward the best position found by **the swarm (gbest)** or **its neighborhood (lbest)**.

$$
\vec{v}_i^{(3)}(t+1) = c_2 r_2 \left(\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)\right)
$$

> **Logic**: $\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)$ is the vector from the particle's current position to the best-known location. The social term represents imitation of successful neighbors.

### **Step 4: Combine the Three Forces**
By vector addition (linear superposition of forces):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{v}_i(t+1) &= \vec{v}_i^{(1)}(t+1) + \vec{v}_i^{(2)}(t+1) + \vec{v}_i^{(3)}(t+1) \\
&= w \cdot \vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \left(\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)\right) + c_2 r_2 \left(\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5: Position Update**
The particle's new position is its old position plus its new velocity (Euler integration with unit time step):

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

> **Logic**: This is a first-order discrete integration analogous to $x = x_0 + v \cdot \Delta t$ with $\Delta t = 1$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: gbest vs lbest (5 Particles in Ring)

**Problem Setup:**
- 5 particles arranged in a ring: $\vec{x}_1, \vec{x}_2, \vec{x}_3, \vec{x}_4, \vec{x}_5$.
- Each particle's neighborhood $N_i$ = itself + immediate neighbors (size 3).
- Ring topology: $N_1 = \{5, 1, 2\}$, $N_2 = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $N_3 = \{2, 3, 4\}$, $N_4 = \{3, 4, 5\}$, $N_5 = \{4, 5, 1\}$.
- Suppose at iteration $t$, the personal best fitness values are:
  - $f(\vec{p}_1) = 10$, $f(\vec{p}_2) = 5$, $f(\vec{p}_3) = 8$, $f(\vec{p}_4) = 12$, $f(\vec{p}_5) = 7$.

### **Computation for gbest PSO:**

**Step 1:** Identify the global best across the entire swarm.
$$
f_{gbest} = \min\{10, 5, 8, 12, 7\} = 5 \quad \text{(achieved at } \vec{p}_2\text{)}
$$

**Step 2:** Every particle uses $\vec{p}_g = \vec{p}_2$.

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_2 - \vec{x}_i) \quad \forall i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
$$

> **Result**: All 5 particles are pulled toward $\vec{p}_2$. Convergence is rapid but particles cluster together.

### **Computation for lbest PSO (Ring, k=1):**

**Step 1:** Compute local best for each particle.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{lbest}^{(1)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_5), f(\vec{p}_1), f(\vec{p}_2)\} = \min\{7, 10, 5\} = 5 \quad (\vec{p}_2) \\
f_{lbest}^{(2)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_1), f(\vec{p}_2), f(\vec{p}_3)\} = \min\{10, 5, 8\} = 5 \quad (\vec{p}_2) \\
f_{lbest}^{(3)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_2), f(\vec{p}_3), f(\vec{p}_4)\} = \min\{5, 8, 12\} = 5 \quad (\vec{p}_2) \\
f_{lbest}^{(4)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_3), f(\vec{p}_4), f(\vec{p}_5)\} = \min\{8, 12, 7\} = 7 \quad (\vec{p}_5) \\
f_{lbest}^{(5)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_4), f(\vec{p}_5), f(\vec{p}_1)\} = \min\{12, 7, 10\} = 7 \quad (\vec{p}_5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Each particle uses its own local best in the velocity update.

$$
\vec{v}_1(t+1) = w\vec{v}_1 + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_1 - \vec{x}_1) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_2 - \vec{x}_1)
$$

$$
\vec{v}_4(t+1) = w\vec{v}_4 + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_4 - \vec{x}_4) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_5 - \vec{x}_4)
$$

> **Result**: Particles 1, 2, 3 are pulled to $\vec{p}_2$; particles 4, 5 are pulled to $\vec{p}_5$. Two distinct clusters form — the swarm is preserving diversity and exploring more of the search space.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | gbest PSO | lbest PSO |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Topology | Fully connected (star) | Ring / Von Neumann / Wheel |
| Best particle shared | One single `gbest` | Per-particle `lbest` |
| Convergence speed | **Fast (1 iteration)** | **Slow (Diameter iterations)** |
| Exploration capability | Weak | **Strong** |
| Premature convergence risk | **High** | Low |
| Swarm diversity preservation | Poor | **Good** |
| Information propagation time | 1 step | O(diameter) steps |
| Best for | Unimodal, smooth problems | Multimodal, deceptive problems |
| Susceptibility to false peaks | High | Low |
| Population size needed | Smaller (20–30) | Larger (40–100) |
| Memory requirements | Stores 1 best | Stores $\vert N \vert$ neighborhood bests |
| Standard coefficient settings | $c_1 = c_2 = 2.0$ | Often $c_1 > c_2$ (e.g., $c_1=2.05, c_2=1.05$) |

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: gbest PSO Topology (Fully Connected Star)

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1["Particle 1"]
    p2["Particle 2"]
    p3["Particle 3"]
    p4["Particle 4"]
    p5["Particle 5"]

    p1 --- gbest
    p2 --- gbest
    p3 --- gbest
    p4 --- gbest
    p5 --- gbest
    p1 -.inform.-> p2
    p1 -.inform.-> p3
    p1 -.inform.-> p4
    p1 -.inform.-> p5
    p2 -.inform.-> p3
    p2 -.inform.-> p4
    p2 -.inform.-> p5
    p3 -.inform.-> p4
    p3 -.inform.-> p5
    p4 -.inform.-> p5
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Solid lines indicate pull toward gbest. Dashed lines indicate mutual information sharing between all particles. Every particle is a neighbor of every other particle.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: lbest PSO Ring Topology (k=1)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["Particle 1"]:::local
    L2["Particle 2"]:::local
    L3["Particle 3"]:::local
    L4["Particle 4"]:::local
    L5["Particle 5"]:::local
    L1 --- L2
    L2 --- L3
    L3 --- L4
    L4 --- L5
    L5 --- L1
    N1["lbest for Particle 1 from neighbors 5 1 2"]:::note
    N2["lbest for Particle 2 from neighbors 1 2 3"]:::note
    N3["lbest for Particle 3 from neighbors 2 3 4"]:::note
    N4["lbest for Particle 4 from neighbors 3 4 5"]:::note
    N5["lbest for Particle 5 from neighbors 4 5 1"]:::note
    L1 -.- N1
    L2 -.- N2
    L3 -.- N3
    L4 -.- N4
    L5 -.- N5
    classDef local fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Solid edges form a ring. Each particle communicates only with its 2 immediate neighbors. Each particle has its **own** `lbest`. Information must traverse the ring to reach distant particles.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: PSO Iteration Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initialize N particles random positions and velocities"] --> B["Evaluate fitness fxi for all i"]
    B --> C["Update pbesti for each particle"]
    C --> D{"Topology Type"}
    D -- gbest --> E["Compute single GLOBAL BEST across all particles"]
    D -- lbest --> F["Compute LOCAL BEST for each particle from its neighborhood Ni"]
    E --> G["Update velocity vi using gbest for all i"]
    F --> H["Update velocity vi using its lbest for each i"]
    G --> I["Update position xi = xi plus vi"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Apply velocity clamping and boundary constraints"]
    J --> K{"Convergence criterion met"}
    K -- No --> B
    K -- Yes --> L["Report final best solution gbest or best lbest"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between gbest PSO and lbest PSO in terms of topology and convergence behavior.**

**Model Answer:**

> **gbest PSO** uses a **fully connected (star) topology** where every particle is a neighbor of every other particle. All particles are attracted to a **single global best (gbest)** position found anywhere in the swarm. This leads to **fast convergence** but high risk of **premature convergence** to local optima.
>
> **lbest PSO** uses a **partial topology** (e.g., ring, von Neumann) where each particle communicates only with a **subset (neighborhood)** of particles. Each particle is attracted to the **local best (lbest)** of its own neighborhood. This results in **slower convergence** but better **exploration** and lower risk of getting stuck in local optima.

*(3 marks — KTU rubric: 1 mark for topology, 1 mark for convergence, 1 mark for trade-off)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Write the velocity update equation for PSO and explain the role of the social component in gbest and lbest variants.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t))
$$

> The **social component** is $c_2 r_2 (\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t))$, which pulls the particle toward a best-known position. In **gbest PSO**, $\vec{p}_g(t)$ is the best position across the **entire swarm**. In **lbest PSO**, $\vec{p}_g(t)$ is the best position within the particle's **neighborhood $N_i$** only. Hence the social pull is "global" in gbest but "local" in lbest.

*(3 marks — KTU rubric: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for gbest social term, 1 mark for lbest social term)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Writing $\vec{p}_g$ without specifying it is the **fitness-best position**, not a particle index. Always distinguish between "best particle" and "best position".
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Confusing **neighborhood topology** (fixed during initialization) with **dynamic grouping** (changes per iteration). Ring topology is **static**.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Forgetting that **all** particles use the **same** gbest in gbest PSO — many students incorrectly treat it as a per-particle quantity.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Not mentioning the **stochastic terms** $r_1, r_2$ — they are critical for PSO's exploratory behavior.
> - **Mark Loss**: Failing to discuss the **exploration-exploitation trade-off** explicitly costs ~3 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Explain the concept of gbest PSO with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
gbest PSO is a variant of Particle Swarm Optimization in which **every particle in the swarm shares information with every other particle**. The social component of each particle's velocity update is computed using a **single global best position (`gbest`)** that is the best position ever discovered by any particle in the entire swarm.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t))
$$

where
$$
\vec{p}_g(t) = \arg\min_{\vec{p}_j, \, j \in S} f(\vec{p}_j(t)), \quad S = \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}
$$

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $N$ particles with random $\vec{x}_i(0)$ and $\vec{v}_i(0)$.
2. Evaluate fitness $f(\vec{x}_i)$ for all $i$.
3. Initialize $\vec{p}_i(0) = \vec{x}_i(0)$.
4. Identify $\vec{p}_g(0) = \arg\min_i f(\vec{p}_i(0))$.
5. Update velocities and positions.
6. Update $\vec{p}_i$ and $\vec{p}_g$ if better positions are found.
7. Repeat until convergence.

**Topology Diagram (Star / Fully Connected):**

```
       P2
        |
   P1---GB---P3
        |
       P4
   (P5 also connects to GB directly)
```

All particles connect to the central global best; all-to-all information flow.

**Advantages (3 marks):**
- **Fast convergence**: Information spreads in one iteration.
- **Simple implementation**: One global best to track.
- **Strong exploitation**: Refines the best-known region aggressively.
- **Low memory overhead**: Only one $\vec{p}_g$ stored.

**Limitations (3 marks):**
- **Premature convergence**: Easily trapped in local optima.
- **Poor exploration**: Swarm loses diversity rapidly.
- **Sensitive to outliers**: A misleading early gbest can mislead the entire swarm.
- **Deceptive landscapes**: Fails on multimodal problems with many false peaks.

**Concluding Statement (1 mark):**
gbest PSO excels on **smooth unimodal functions** but requires modifications (e.g., inertia weight decay, constriction factor) or replacement by lbest for complex multimodal problems.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Explain lbest PSO with ring topology. Show how the local best is computed for each particle in a swarm of 6 particles with neighborhoods of size 3.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
In **lbest PSO**, each particle is influenced only by its **neighbors**, not the entire swarm. The social term uses the **local best (`lbest`)** — the best position found within the particle's neighborhood.

**Ring Topology with Neighborhood Size 3:**
For 6 particles arranged in a ring, the neighborhood of particle $i$ contains itself and its 2 immediate neighbors (one on each side).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= \{6, 1, 2\} \\
N_2 &= \{1, 2, 3\} \\
N_3 &= \{2, 3, 4\} \\
N_4 &= \{3, 4, 5\} \\
N_5 &= \{4, 5, 6\} \\
N_6 &= \{5, 6, 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Suppose the personal best fitness values at iteration $t$ are:
- $f(\vec{p}_1) = 12$, $f(\vec{p}_2) = 8$, $f(\vec{p}_3) = 15$, $f(\vec{p}_4) = 6$, $f(\vec{p}_5) = 10$, $f(\vec{p}_6) = 9$.

**Local Best Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{lbest}^{(1)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_6), f(\vec{p}_1), f(\vec{p}_2)\} = \min\{9, 12, 8\} = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(1)} = \vec{p}_2 \\
f_{lbest}^{(2)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_1), f(\vec{p}_2), f(\vec{p}_3)\} = \min\{12, 8, 15\} = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(2)} = \vec{p}_2 \\
f_{lbest}^{(3)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_2), f(\vec{p}_3), f(\vec{p}_4)\} = \min\{8, 15, 6\} = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(3)} = \vec{p}_4 \\
f_{lbest}^{(4)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_3), f(\vec{p}_4), f(\vec{p}_5)\} = \min\{15, 6, 10\} = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(4)} = \vec{p}_4 \\
f_{lbest}^{(5)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_4), f(\vec{p}_5), f(\vec{p}_6)\} = \min\{6, 10, 9\} = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(5)} = \vec{p}_4 \\
f_{lbest}^{(6)} &= \min\{f(\vec{p}_5), f(\vec{p}_6), f(\vec{p}_1)\} = \min\{10, 9, 12\} = 9 \quad \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(6)} = \vec{p}_6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Velocity Update for Particle 3 (sample):**

$$
\vec{v}_3(t+1) = w\vec{v}_3(t) + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_3 - \vec{x}_3) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_4 - \vec{x}_3)
$$

Note that particle 3 is attracted to $\vec{p}_4$, **not** to the global best (which is $\vec{p}_4$ in this case, but in general it could differ).

**Key Observations:**
- Each particle has a **different** social attractor.
- Particles 1, 2 cluster near $\vec{p}_2$; particles 3, 4, 5 cluster near $\vec{p}_4$; particle 6 clusters near $\vec{p}_6$.
- This **preserves diversity** and supports **parallel exploration** of multiple optima.

**Advantages of lbest PSO (1 mark):**
- Better exploration; resists premature convergence.
- Suitable for **multimodal, dynamic, and noisy** problems.
- Robust to changes in problem landscape.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Slower convergence (information must propagate through ring).
- Requires larger population to compensate.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Compare gbest and lbest PSO on the basis of: (i) topology, (ii) convergence speed, (iii) exploration vs exploitation, (iv) susceptibility to local optima, and (v) suitable problem types. Draw the topology diagrams for both.**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Criterion | gbest PSO | lbest PSO |
|---|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| (i) | **Topology** | Fully connected (star); all-to-all communication | Ring / Von Neumann / Wheel; partial connectivity |
| (ii) | **Convergence Speed** | Fast (1-iteration propagation) | Slow (multi-step propagation via neighborhood hops) |
| (iii) | **Exploration vs Exploitation** | Heavy exploitation, weak exploration | Balanced; stronger exploration |
| (iv) | **Susceptibility to Local Optima** | **High** — easily trapped | **Low** — multiple sub-swarms preserve diversity |
| (v) | **Suitable Problem Types** | Unimodal, smooth, cheap fitness | Multimodal, deceptive, noisy, dynamic |

**Topology Diagrams (2 marks):**

```
GBEST PSO (Star):                LBEST PSO (Ring):
       P2                              P1---P2
       |                                \  /
  P1---GB---P3                          P6  P3
       |                                  \/
       P4---...---Pn                       P5---P4
```

**Detailed Explanations (5 marks):**

- **Topology (1 mark):** gbest uses $N(N-1)/2$ connections (complete graph). lbest uses only $k \cdot N$ connections (e.g., $k=2$ in ring).
- **Convergence (1 mark):** Because gbest propagates the best position in **one iteration**, all particles immediately realign toward it. In lbest, a new optimum may take $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ iterations to traverse the ring.
- **Exploration (1 mark):** gbest collapses the swarm toward a single point — losing diversity. lbest allows multiple local attractors to coexist, enabling the swarm to **search different regions simultaneously**.
- **Susceptibility (1 mark):** gbest fails on the **deceptive** Rosenbrock or Schwefel functions because it locks onto a false peak early. lbest maintains separate exploration fronts.
- **Suitable problems (1 mark):** gbest is appropriate for **antenna array weight optimization, PID controller tuning** (smooth landscapes). lbest is preferred for **traveling salesman, neural architecture search, feature selection** (rugged landscapes).

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Derive the velocity and position update equations for PSO. Explain how the social component differs between gbest and lbest variants. Use a 1-D example to illustrate.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Velocity Update Derivation:**

The PSO velocity update is constructed from three behavioral forces:

**(i) Inertia Term** — tendency to continue in current direction:
$$
\vec{v}_i^{(I)}(t+1) = w \cdot \vec{v}_i(t)
$$

**(ii) Cognitive Term** — pull toward personal best:
$$
\vec{v}_i^{(C)}(t+1) = c_1 r_1 \left(\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)\right)
$$

**(iii) Social Term** — pull toward best-known position (gbest or lbest):
$$
\vec{v}_i^{(S)}(t+1) = c_2 r_2 \left(\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)\right)
$$

**Combining via superposition:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{v}_i(t+1) &= \vec{v}_i^{(I)} + \vec{v}_i^{(C)} + \vec{v}_i^{(S)} \\
&= w\vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_g(t) - \vec{x}_i(t))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Position Update (Discrete Integration):**
$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

This corresponds to a forward Euler discretization of $\frac{d\vec{x}}{dt} = \vec{v}$ with step size $\Delta t = 1$.

**Step 3 — Difference Between gbest and lbest:**

In **gbest PSO**:
$$
\vec{p}_g(t) = \arg\min_{j \in \{1,\ldots,N\}} f(\vec{p}_j(t))
$$

In **lbest PSO**:
$$
\vec{p}_g^{(i)}(t) = \arg\min_{j \in N_i} f(\vec{p}_j(t)), \quad \text{where } \vert N_i \vert < N
$$

**Step 4 — 1-D Numerical Illustration:**

Consider 4 particles in 1-D at positions $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 5, x_3 = 8, x_4 = 11$ (minimizing $f(x) = (x-6)^2$). Let their pbests be $p_1 = 3, p_2 = 4, p_3 = 7, p_4 = 10$. Compute fitness $f(p_i) = (p_i - 6)^2$:

- $f(p_1) = 9$, $f(p_2) = 4$, $f(p_3) = 1$, $f(p_4) = 16$.

**gbest PSO:** Single global best = $\vec{p}_g = p_3 = 7$ (lowest fitness = 1). Every particle's social term uses $p_3 = 7$.

**lbest PSO (Ring, $k=1$):** Neighborhoods:
- $N_1 = \{4, 1, 2\}$: $\min\{16, 9, 4\} = 4 \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(1)} = p_2 = 4$.
- $N_2 = \{1, 2, 3\}$: $\min\{9, 4, 1\} = 1 \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(2)} = p_3 = 7$.
- $N_3 = \{2, 3, 4\}$: $\min\{4, 1, 16\} = 1 \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(3)} = p_3 = 7$.
- $N_4 = \{3, 4, 1\}$: $\min\{1, 16, 9\} = 1 \Rightarrow \vec{p}_g^{(4)} = p_3 = 7$.

**Observation:** In this symmetric case, lbest particle 1 is attracted to $p_2 = 4$ instead of $p_3 = 7$. This demonstrates **divergent social influence** in lbest — particle 1 explores a different region.

**Conclusion:** The social term is **identical for all particles** in gbest (one attractor), but **differs per particle** in lbest (multiple local attractors). This is the single mathematical distinction that drives the radically different convergence behaviors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — gbest vs lbest PSO**

- ⭐ **Core Definition**: gbest = global best across **entire swarm**; lbest = local best within a particle's **neighborhood**.
- ⭐ **Topology**: gbest uses **star / fully connected** graph; lbest uses **ring / von Neumann / wheel** graph.
- ⭐ **Velocity Formula** (universal):
  $$\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2 r_2(\vec{p}_g - \vec{x}_i)$$
- ⭐ **Position Formula**: $\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)$.
- ⭐ **Three PSO components**: **Inertia** ($w\vec{v}_i$), **Cognitive** (own pbest), **Social** (gbest or lbest).
- ⭐ **Convergence speed**: gbest **faster**, lbest **slower**.
- ⭐ **Exploration**: lbest **stronger**; gbest exploits heavily.
- ⭐ **Risk**: gbest → **premature convergence**; lbest → slower but escapes local optima.
- ⭐ **Standard parameters**: $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$, $c_1 = c_2 = 2.0$, $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$.
- ⭐ **Neighborhood sizes** in lbest: typically 2–5; symmetric on both sides for ring topology.
- ⭐ **Recommended populations**: 20–30 (gbest); 40–100 (lbest).
- ⭐ **Information propagation**: gbest = 1 step; lbest = O(diameter) steps.
- ⭐ **When to use gbest**: unimodal, smooth, low-cost objective functions.
- ⭐ **When to use lbest**: multimodal, deceptive, dynamic, or noisy problems.
- ⭐ **Hybrid approach**: some algorithms (e.g., CLPSO, FIPS) combine both ideas dynamically.
- ⭐ **Historical note**: gbest is the **original** PSO by Kennedy & Eberhart (1995); lbest was introduced shortly after to address premature convergence.
