# Velocity Update Equations — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)**, the **Velocity Update Equation** is the mathematical rule that governs how each particle in the swarm adjusts its search velocity at every iteration of the algorithm. It determines the *direction* and *magnitude* of movement of a candidate solution through the decision space, drawing influence from three distinct memory components: the particle's own inertia, its individual cognitive memory, and the swarm's collective social memory.

The canonical formulation, as presented in the **Shi & Eberhart (1998) inertia-weight PSO**, is expressed as:

$$v_{i,j}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 \left( pbest_{i,j} - x_{i,j}(t) \right) + c_2 r_2 \left( gbest_j - x_{i,j}(t) \right)$$

where every symbol carries a strict engineering meaning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Update Equations** form the **algorithmic heart** of PSO. Without them, the swarm would have no mechanism to propagate information between particles or to balance exploration against exploitation. Under KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation, this equation accounts for the majority of marks in any PSO-based numerical/analytical question.

## 1.2 Symbol Glossary (Standard KTU Reference)

| Symbol | Engineering Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $v_{i,j}(t)$ | Velocity of particle *i* in dimension *j* at iteration *t* | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $x_{i,j}(t)$ | Position of particle *i* in dimension *j* at iteration *t* | Search space bounds |
| $w$ | **Inertia weight** | $0.4 \leq w \leq 0.9$ |
| $c_1$ | Cognitive acceleration coefficient | $1.5 \leq c_1 \leq 2.5$ |
| $c_2$ | Social acceleration coefficient | $1.5 \leq c_2 \leq 2.5$ |
| $r_1, r_2$ | Uniformly distributed random numbers | $r \sim U(0,1)$ |
| $pbest_{i,j}$ | Best personal position of particle *i* in dimension *j* | Search space bounds |
| $gbest_j$ | Global best position in dimension *j* | Search space bounds |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bird in a Flock"

Imagine a flock of birds searching for food in a foggy field (you cannot see far). Each bird has three pieces of information guiding its next flap of wings:

- **Memory (Cognitive Component)**: "I personally remember finding worms at that oak tree last Tuesday" → pulls the bird toward its own best known spot, $pbest$.
- **Testimony (Social Component)**: "My neighbour Pedro just chirped that he saw a juicy worm pile near the big rock" → pulls the bird toward the group's best known spot, $gbest$.
- **Momentum (Inertia Component)**: "I was flying north at a certain speed; I'll keep that momentum but slightly damped" → the inertia term $w \cdot v(t)$ keeps the bird from making jerky, inefficient micro-turns every second.

The **velocity update equation** is precisely the vector addition of these three influences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters for KTU valuation:** Examiners often award full marks only if a student can verbally articulate the *role* of each term, not merely reproduce the formula. The bird-flock analogy (or an equivalent one) is a known high-scoring answer pattern in the **3-mark conceptual questions** of the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstruction of the Three Components

The velocity update equation is a **linear superposition** of three vector influences acting on every particle at every iteration. Each component serves a unique algorithmic purpose.

### 2.1.1 Inertia Component — $w \cdot v_{i,j}(t)$

- **Purpose**: Controls *exploration* vs. *exploitation* trade-off.
- **Engineering role**: A large $w$ (close to **0.9**) encourages wide exploration of the search space; a small $w$ (close to **0.4**) encourages fine-grained exploitation near promising regions.
- **Adaptive strategy (Shi & Eberhart Linear Decay)**:

$$w(t) = w_{max} - \frac{(w_{max} - w_{min}) \cdot t}{T_{max}}$$

where $w_{max} = 0.9$, $w_{min} = 0.4$, and $T_{max}$ is the maximum iteration count.

### 2.1.2 Cognitive Component — $c_1 r_1 (pbest_{i,j} - x_{i,j}(t))$

- **Purpose**: Returns the particle toward its own historically best location.
- **Engineering role**: Maintains *particle individuality* and prevents premature convergence to the swarm's current best.
- **Why $c_1$ matters**: $c_1 = 0$ produces a purely social particle (loses diversity); excessively high $c_1$ produces a roaming particle (never converges).

### 2.1.3 Social Component — $c_2 r_2 (gbest_j - x_{i,j}(t))$

- **Purpose**: Pulls the particle toward the best location found by any particle in the swarm (or a neighbourhood, in *local-best PSO*).
- **Engineering role**: Enables *information sharing* and rapid convergence.
- **Why $c_2$ matters**: $c_2 = 0$ produces a purely cognitive particle (independent search, no swarm benefit); excessively high $c_2$ causes swarm collapse to a single point.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference

| Formula | Name | Key Use |
|---|---|---|
| $v_{i,j}(t+1) = w v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest - x) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x)$ | Inertia-weight PSO velocity update | Standard PSO |
| $x_{i,j}(t+1) = x_{i,j}(t) + v_{i,j}(t+1)$ | Position update | Always paired with velocity update |
| $w(t) = w_{max} - (w_{max} - w_{min}) t / T_{max}$ | Linearly decreasing inertia weight | Adaptive PSO |
| $\chi = \frac{2k}{\mid \phi - 2 \mid + \sqrt{\phi(\phi - 4)}}$, $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$ | Constriction coefficient (Clerc & Kennedy) | Stability-guaranteed PSO |
| $v_{max}$ clamping: $\mid v_{i,j} \mid \leq v_{max,j}$ | Velocity boundary control | Prevents explosion |
| $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ | Stochastic perturbation | Diversity injection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the vertical bar in formulas:** The KTU 2024 LaTeX rendering system often breaks when the pipe character `|` is used as absolute value within markdown tables. Always prefer `\mid` or `\vert` (e.g., $\mid v_{i,j} \mid$) when constructing or transcribing these tables into your own answer scripts.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Velocity update equations are not purely academic. They power real-time decision-making in:

- **Power Systems**: Economic dispatch and unit commitment where each "particle" represents a candidate generator scheduling profile.
- **Robotics**: Swarm-robot path planning for search-and-rescue operations in unknown terrains.
- **Antenna Design**: Optimising array element positions for desired radiation patterns.
- **Machine Learning Hyperparameter Tuning**: Selecting learning rate, batch size, and architecture hyperparameters.
- **Structural Engineering**: Truss weight minimisation under stress constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> The **cognitive–social balance** (the $c_1$ vs $c_2$ trade-off) is one of the most frequently tested concepts in KTU Module 4. Memorise the parameter ranges: $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$, $c_1, c_2 \in [1.5, 2.5]$, and the condition $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$ for the constriction variant.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Original PSO (1995) to Inertia-Weight PSO (1998)

The original PSO velocity update proposed by Kennedy & Eberhart (1995) had no inertia term:

$$v_{i,j}(t+1) = v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest_{i,j} - x_{i,j}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (gbest_j - x_{i,j}(t))$$

**Step 1 — Identification of the velocity-explosion problem.**
Yuhui Shi observed that the original equation allowed velocities to grow unbounded, causing particles to leave the search space.

**Step 2 — Insertion of the inertia weight as a velocity-scaling factor.**
Shi & Eberhart (1998) introduced the scalar $w$ multiplied directly with the previous velocity:

$$v_{i,j}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest_{i,j} - x_{i,j}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (gbest_j - x_{i,j}(t))$$

**Step 3 — Justification via convergence analysis.**
For $0 \leq w < 1$ and bounded random perturbations, the velocity trajectory asymptotically converges to a stable region around the weighted centroid of $pbest$ and $gbest$.

**Step 4 — Empirical parameter calibration.**
Through benchmark testing on the Sphere, Rosenbrock, and Griewank functions, the parameter range $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ was established as a robust default.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constriction Coefficient PSO (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002)

**Step 1 — Rewrite the velocity update in compact vector form.**

Let $\phi_1 = c_1 r_1$ and $\phi_2 = c_2 r_2$. The expected values are $\mathbb{E}[\phi_1] = c_1$ and $\mathbb{E}[\phi_2] = c_2$, with $\phi = \phi_1 + \phi_2$.

**Step 2 — Define the constriction multiplier.**

$$\chi = \frac{2k}{\mid \phi - 2 \mid + \sqrt{\phi(\phi - 4)}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the entire RHS by $\chi$.**

$$v_{i,j}(t+1) = \chi \left[ v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest_{i,j} - x_{i,j}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (gbest_j - x_{i,j}(t)) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Establish convergence guarantee.**
For $\phi > 4$ and $k = 1$, the system is guaranteed to converge without requiring $v_{max}$ clamping, a stronger analytical result than inertia-weight PSO.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Exam Pattern

**Problem (14-Mark Pattern):** Consider a 2-D search space. A particle has:

- Current position $x(t) = (2, 3)$
- Current velocity $v(t) = (1, -1)$
- Personal best $pbest = (4, 2)$
- Global best $gbest = (5, 6)$
- $w = 0.6$, $c_1 = 2.0$, $c_2 = 2.0$
- $r_1 = 0.5$, $r_2 = 0.8$

Compute $v(t+1)$ and $x(t+1)$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the velocity update equation for dimension $j = 1$:

$$v_{i,1}(t+1) = 0.6(1) + 2.0(0.5)(4 - 2) + 2.0(0.8)(5 - 2)$$

$$= 0.6 + 1.0 \cdot 2 + 1.6 \cdot 3 = 0.6 + 2.0 + 4.8 = 7.4$$

Step 2 — Compute the velocity for dimension $j = 2$:

$$v_{i,2}(t+1) = 0.6(-1) + 2.0(0.5)(2 - 3) + 2.0(0.8)(6 - 3)$$

$$= -0.6 + 1.0 \cdot (-1) + 1.6 \cdot 3 = -0.6 - 1.0 + 4.8 = 3.2$$

Step 3 — Compose the updated velocity vector:

$$v(t+1) = (7.4, \ 3.2)$$

Step 4 — Apply the position update equation for both dimensions:

$$x_{i,1}(t+1) = 2 + 7.4 = 9.4$$

$$x_{i,2}(t+1) = 3 + 3.2 = 6.2$$

Step 5 — Compose the updated position vector:

$$x(t+1) = (9.4, \ 6.2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's insight:** A common student error is forgetting to apply the position update *after* the velocity update, or mixing up the dimension index. Always write the dimension index explicitly ($j = 1, 2$) to gain the **process marks** KTU examiners award for clearly delineated working.

## 3.4 Derivative Insight — Why $c_1 = c_2$ is Often Recommended

Setting $c_1 = c_2$ produces a balanced pull toward personal and global memory. Empirically, $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ with $\phi \approx 4.1$ yields a constriction factor $\chi \approx 0.729$, which Clerc & Kennedy demonstrated as a robust convergence-promoting configuration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow of the Velocity Update Mechanism

The following diagram illustrates the internal data-flow of a single PSO iteration, highlighting the role of the velocity update equation as the central transformation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInit["Particle Initialisation: x_i and v_i"]
    nodeEval["Objective Function Evaluation: f x_i"]
    nodePbest["Update Personal Best: pbest_i"]
    nodeGbest["Update Global Best: gbest"]
    nodeRead["Read v_i t, x_i t, pbest, gbest"]
    nodeInertia["Inertia Component: w times v_i t"]
    nodeCognitive["Cognitive Component: c1 r1 times pbest minus x"]
    nodeSocial["Social Component: c2 r2 times gbest minus x"]
    nodeSum["Vector Sum: v_i t plus 1"]
    nodeClamp["Velocity Clamping: check v_max bound"]
    nodePos["Position Update: x_i t plus 1 equals x_i t plus v_i t plus 1"]
    nodeCheck{"Convergence Criterion Met"}
    nodeStop["Terminate and Return gbest"]
    nodeLoop["Increment t and Return to Particle Initialisation"]

    nodeInit --> nodeEval
    nodeEval --> nodePbest
    nodePbest --> nodeGbest
    nodeGbest --> nodeRead
    nodeRead --> nodeInertia
    nodeRead --> nodeCognitive
    nodeRead --> nodeSocial
    nodeInertia --> nodeSum
    nodeCognitive --> nodeSum
    nodeSocial --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeClamp
    nodeClamp --> nodePos
    nodePos --> nodeCheck
    nodeCheck --> nodeStop
    nodeCheck --> nodeLoop
    nodeLoop --> nodeEval
```

## 4.2 Component Decomposition Diagram

The following schematic shows how the three velocity components combine vectorially to produce the new velocity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Inertia Vector: w times v_old"]
    nodeB["Cognitive Vector: c1 r1 toward pbest"]
    nodeC["Social Vector: c2 r2 toward gbest"]
    nodeD["Resultant Velocity: v_new"]

    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD
```

## 4.3 Parameter Influence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeW["Inertia Weight w"]
    nodeC1["Cognitive Coefficient c1"]
    nodeC2["Social Coefficient c2"]
    nodeR["Random Numbers r1 r2"]
    nodeV["New Velocity v t plus 1"]
    nodeX["New Position x t plus 1"]
    nodeExp["Exploration Phase"]
    nodeExp2["Exploitation Phase"]
    nodeCon["Convergence Behaviour"]

    nodeW --> nodeV
    nodeC1 --> nodeV
    nodeC2 --> nodeV
    nodeR --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeX
    nodeW --> nodeExp
    nodeW --> nodeExp2
    nodeC1 --> nodeCon
    nodeC2 --> nodeCon
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU valuation:** KTU 2024 Scheme question papers under Module 4 often include *"Explain the velocity update mechanism with a suitable block diagram"* as a 7-mark sub-question. A well-labelled flow diagram showing the inertia, cognitive, and social components separately is worth at least **2 of those 7 marks** purely on visual clarity, even before the textual explanation begins.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the velocity update equation in PSO. Explain the role of the inertia weight $w$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The velocity update equation in PSO is given by

$$v_{i,j}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest_{i,j} - x_{i,j}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (gbest_j - x_{i,j}(t))$$

The inertia weight $w$ controls the influence of the particle's previous velocity on its current velocity. A larger value of $w$ (close to 0.9) promotes global exploration, while a smaller value (close to 0.4) promotes local exploitation. It therefore balances the exploration–exploitation trade-off in the search process.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the difference between the cognitive and social components in the PSO velocity update?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The cognitive component $c_1 r_1 (pbest - x)$ drives a particle toward its own historically best position, preserving individual search experience. The social component $c_2 r_2 (gbest - x)$ drives the particle toward the best position found by the entire swarm, enabling collective knowledge sharing. The cognitive term prevents premature convergence; the social term accelerates convergence.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the canonical velocity update equation used in inertia-weight Particle Swarm Optimization. Clearly state the role of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A particle in a 3-D search space has:
- $x(t) = (1, 2, 3)$
- $v(t) = (0.5, -0.5, 1.0)$
- $pbest = (2, 3, 4)$
- $gbest = (4, 5, 6)$
- $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = c_2 = 2.0$, $r_1 = 0.6$, $r_2 = 0.4$

Compute $v(t+1)$ and $x(t+1)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

The canonical PSO velocity update equation is:

$$v_{i,j}(t+1) = w v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest_{i,j} - x_{i,j}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (gbest_j - x_{i,j}(t))$$

**Term 1 — Inertia term $w \cdot v_{i,j}(t)$:** This term carries forward the particle's previous velocity, scaled by the inertia weight $w$. It maintains momentum and prevents abrupt direction changes. **(2 Marks)**

**Term 2 — Cognitive term $c_1 r_1 (pbest - x)$:** This term measures the displacement between the particle's current position and its personal best. It encodes the particle's individual memory. **(2.5 Marks)**

**Term 3 — Social term $c_2 r_2 (gbest - x)$:** This term measures the displacement between the particle's current position and the swarm's global best. It encodes collective intelligence. **(2.5 Marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Dimension $j = 1$:

$$v_{i,1}(t+1) = 0.7(0.5) + 2.0(0.6)(2 - 1) + 2.0(0.4)(4 - 1) = 0.35 + 1.2 + 2.4 = 3.95$$

Dimension $j = 2$:

$$v_{i,2}(t+1) = 0.7(-0.5) + 2.0(0.6)(3 - 2) + 2.0(0.4)(5 - 2) = -0.35 + 1.2 + 2.4 = 3.25$$

Dimension $j = 3$:

$$v_{i,3}(t+1) = 0.7(1.0) + 2.0(0.6)(4 - 3) + 2.0(0.4)(6 - 3) = 0.7 + 1.2 + 2.4 = 4.3$$

Therefore, $v(t+1) = (3.95, 3.25, 4.3)$.

Position update:

$$x_{i,1}(t+1) = 1 + 3.95 = 4.95$$

$$x_{i,2}(t+1) = 2 + 3.25 = 5.25$$

$$x_{i,3}(t+1) = 3 + 4.3 = 7.3$$

Therefore, $x(t+1) = (4.95, 5.25, 7.3)$.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the inertia-weight PSO and the constriction-coefficient PSO formulations. Under what conditions does each perform better? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with a suitable diagram, the role of the cognitive and social components in the velocity update mechanism. How does the choice of $c_1$ and $c_2$ affect convergence? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Inertia-weight PSO (Shi & Eberhart, 1998):** Uses an explicit inertia weight $w$ multiplying the previous velocity. The equation is

$$v_{i,j}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest - x) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x)$$

with $w$ typically decayed linearly from 0.9 to 0.4. **(3 Marks)**

**Constriction-coefficient PSO (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002):** Removes $w$ as a separate parameter and instead multiplies the entire right-hand side by $\chi$:

$$v_{i,j}(t+1) = \chi \left[ v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest - x) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x) \right]$$

with $\chi = \dfrac{2}{\mid \phi - 2 \mid + \sqrt{\phi(\phi - 4)}}$ and $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$. **(3 Marks)**

**Comparative analysis:** Constriction PSO has a theoretical convergence guarantee without requiring $v_{max}$ clamping; inertia-weight PSO is more intuitive and allows adaptive tuning of exploration/exploitation via $w$. Inertia-weight PSO is preferred for dynamic, time-varying objective functions; constriction PSO is preferred for static, high-precision benchmark optimisation. **(1 Mark)**

**(b) Component Roles (7 Marks):**

The cognitive component $c_1 r_1 (pbest - x)$ represents the particle's "memory" — the pull toward its own best-known location. The social component $c_2 r_2 (gbest - x)$ represents "testimony" — the pull toward the swarm's collective best. **(3 Marks)**

**Effect on convergence:**
- If $c_1 \gg c_2$: Excessive independent roaming, slow convergence.
- If $c_2 \gg c_1$: Premature convergence to a local optimum (swarm collapse).
- If $c_1 \approx c_2$: Balanced exploration and exploitation; recommended default. **(3 Marks)**

**Diagram requirement:** The answer must include a labelled block diagram showing inertia, cognitive, and social components combining into the resultant velocity vector. **(1 Mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $v_{max}$ with $v_{i,j}$ direction:** $v_{max}$ is a scalar bound, not a vector. Applying it requires clamping *each component* independently.
> 2. **Forgetting the position update:** The velocity equation alone does not move the particle. The position update $x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)$ is a separate, mandatory step.
> 3. **Mixing up $c_1$ and $c_2$ roles:** Cognitive ($c_1$) = *personal*; Social ($c_2$) = *global*. Reversing these is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Using $w > 1$:** Causes velocity explosion and divergence. Always stay within $[0.4, 0.9]$.
> 5. **Omitting dimension indices:** Writing $v(t+1) = ...$ without $j$ loses process marks for multi-dimensional problems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation:** $v_{i,j}(t+1) = w v_{i,j}(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest - x) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x)$
- **Three Components:** Inertia ($w v$), Cognitive ($c_1$ with $pbest$), Social ($c_2$ with $gbest$).
- **Position Update Pair:** Always $x_{i,j}(t+1) = x_{i,j}(t) + v_{i,j}(t+1)$.
- **Parameter Defaults:** $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = c_2 = 2.0$, $r_1, r_2 \in U(0,1)$.
- **Inertia Decay Formula:** $w(t) = 0.9 - (0.5 \cdot t) / T_{max}$.
- **Constriction Condition:** $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$ with $k = 1$.
- **Velocity Clamping:** $\mid v_{i,j} \mid \leq v_{max,j}$.
- **Cognitive–Social Balance:** $c_1 \approx c_2$ is recommended for most problems.
- **Bird-Flock Analogy:** Personal memory, swarm testimony, momentum — remember all three.
- **Block Diagram:** A clear component-decomposition diagram scores visual clarity marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position Update Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Position Update Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Metaheuristic Optimization**, a **Position Update Equation** is the governing mathematical rule that determines how each candidate solution (called an *agent*, *particle*, *individual*, or *searcher*) modifies its coordinates in the $D$-dimensional search space from one iteration to the next. Formally, given an agent $i$ at iteration $t$ with position vector $\vec{X}_i^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^D$, the position update equation produces the new position vector $\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)}$ by combining **inertial retention**, **cognitive exploitation** (the agent's own best experience), and **social exploration** (the swarm's collective best experience), possibly augmented with stochastic perturbation operators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> A *Position Update Equation* is the iterative mapping function $\vec{X}_i^{(t)} \rightarrow \vec{X}_i^{(t+1)}$ that encodes the exploitation–exploration trade-off of a metaheuristic algorithm. Every modern metaheuristic is uniquely characterized by its update rule.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Homing Pigeons

Imagine a flock of **homing pigeons** released from an unknown location trying to locate a hidden food source on a vast terrain:

- Each pigeon (agent) remembers the **best place it has personally visited** (cognitive component).
- The flock shares information about the **single best place any pigeon has found so far** (social/global component).
- The pigeon also has **momentum** from its previous flight path (inertial component).
- At every step, the pigeon adjusts its heading by combining these three influences weighted by randomness.

This is precisely how **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** updates particle positions. Other algorithms (GWO, WOA, DE, Firefly, GSA) extend or replace these components with analogous biologically/physically inspired operators such as *encirclement*, *bubble-net attack*, *mutation crossover*, or *gravitational attraction*.

## 1.3 Key Parameters and Constants

The following stochastic coefficients appear repeatedly in position update equations across all metaheuristics:

- **Inertia Weight ($w$)**: Controls momentum retention. Typical range $\mathbf{w \in [0.4,\ 0.9]}$.
- **Cognitive Coefficient ($c_1$)**: Pull toward personal best. Typical $\mathbf{c_1 \approx 2.0}$.
- **Social Coefficient ($c_2$)**: Pull toward global best. Typical $\mathbf{c_2 \approx 2.0}$.
- **Random Vectors ($r_1, r_2$)**: Uniformly distributed in $\mathbf{[0, 1]^D}$.
- **Convergence Factor ($\chi$)**: Typically $\mathbf{\chi \approx 0.729}$.
- **Decay Coefficient ($\alpha$)**: Controls stochastic parameter decay across iterations.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, you must be able to **write down, differentiate, and explain** at least four distinct position update equations — typically one from each major family: **Swarm-based, Evolution-based, Physics-based, and Human/Social-based**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of Any Position Update Equation

Every position update equation contains three conceptual building blocks:

1. **Current Position Term** — anchors the agent and provides continuity.
2. **Perturbation / Velocity Term** — generates directional movement using one or more of:
   - Inertia from previous iteration
   - Cognitive learning (pbest-based)
   - Social learning (gbest/neighborhood-based)
   - Differential mutation (between two or more agents)
   - Attraction–repulsion forces (physics-inspired)
3. **Boundary Enforcement / Clamping** — projects the new position back into the feasible search domain $\vec{X} \in [L_b, U_b]^D$.

The general form is universally expressible as:

$$
\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} \;=\; \vec{X}_i^{(t)} \;+\; \Delta \vec{X}_i^{(t+1)}
$$

where $\Delta \vec{X}_i^{(t+1)}$ is the *displacement vector* whose definition is **algorithm-specific**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Position Update Equations of Major Metaheuristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Master every equation and its parameter meaning.

| **Algorithm** | **Velocity / Perturbation Update** | **Position Update** | **Key Parameters** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PSO** (Inertia Weight) | $\vec{v}_i^{(t+1)} = w\vec{v}_i^{(t)} + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2 r_2(\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i)$ | $\vec{x}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{x}_i^{(t)} + \vec{v}_i^{(t+1)}$ | $w, c_1, c_2, r_1, r_2$ |
| **PSO** (Constriction) | $\vec{v}_i^{(t+1)} = \chi[\vec{v}_i^{(t)} + c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2 r_2(\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i)]$ | $\vec{x}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{x}_i^{(t)} + \vec{v}_i^{(t+1)}$ | $\chi = 0.729$ |
| **GWO** | $\vec{D}_\alpha = \vert C_1 \cdot \vec{X}_\alpha - \vec{X} \vert$ etc. | $\vec{X}^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{\vec{X}_{\alpha} + \vec{X}_{\beta} + \vec{X}_{\delta}}{3}$ | $A, C$ decay $2 \to 0$ |
| **WOA** (Encircling) | $\vec{D} = \vert \vec{C} \cdot \vec{X}^* - \vec{X}^{(t)} \vert$ | $\vec{X}^{(t+1)} = \vec{X}^* - \vec{A} \cdot \vec{D}$ | $A, C, l, p$ |
| **WOA** (Bubble-net) | Spiral path | $\vec{X}^{(t+1)} = \vec{D}' \cdot e^{bl} \cos(2\pi l) + \vec{X}^*$ | $b$ constant, $l \in [-1, 1]$ |
| **WOA** (Prey Search) | — | $\vec{X}^{(t+1)} = \vec{X}_{rand} - \vec{A} \cdot \vert \vec{C} \cdot \vec{X}_{rand} - \vec{X} \vert$ | $\vert A \vert \ge 1$ |
| **DE / rand/1/bin** | $\vec{V}_i = \vec{X}_{r1} + F \cdot (\vec{X}_{r2} - \vec{X}_{r3})$ | $\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{V}_i$ if $f(\vec{V}_i) \le f(\vec{X}_i)$ | $F \in [0.4, 1.0]$ |
| **Firefly** | $\beta = \beta_0 e^{-\gamma r^2}$ | $\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{X}_i + \beta(\vec{X}_j - \vec{X}_i) + \alpha \vec{\epsilon}$ | $\gamma, \beta_0, \alpha$ |
| **GSA** | $a_i = G(t) \cdot \dfrac{M_i}{R_{ij} + \epsilon}$ | $\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{X}_i^{(t)} + \vec{v}_i^{(t+1)}$ | $G(t), M_i, R_{ij}$ |
| **Cuckoo Search** | Lévy flight: $L(s) \sim s^{-1.5}$ | $\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{X}_i^{(t)} + \alpha \oplus L(\lambda)$ | $\alpha$ step size |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Equation — Engineering Utility

- **PSO's velocity equation** converges rapidly on unimodal functions (PID tuning, neural network weight optimization, antenna array design).
- **GWO's average-of-three position** mimics a *democratic leadership* and is excellent for engineering design problems with mixed continuous–discrete variables.
- **WOA's spiral update** captures both spiral trajectory behavior of humpback whales and is suited for global numerical optimization with unknown landscape curvature.
- **DE's mutation-based update** is exceptionally robust on non-differentiable, noisy, or discontinuous objective functions found in real-world signal processing.
- **GSA's gravitational acceleration** exploits the analogy of mass concentrations and excels in clustering, feature selection, and data mining tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: The choice of position update equation directly determines the *convergence speed*, *diversity preservation*, and *robustness to local optima* of the algorithm. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **justify the algorithm selection** for a given problem type.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PSO Position Update Equation

### 3.1.1 Objective

Derive the canonical Particle Swarm Optimization position update from the underlying stochastic gradient-free optimization principle.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the agent's three information sources**:
At iteration $t$, particle $i$ has:
- Its current position $\vec{x}_i^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^D$.
- Its own historically best position $\vec{p}_i \in \mathbb{R}^D$.
- The global best position discovered by the swarm $\vec{g} \in \mathbb{R}^D$.

**Step 2 — Construct velocity as a weighted stochastic sum**:

$$
\vec{v}_i^{(t+1)} = w \vec{v}_i^{(t)} \;+\; c_1 r_1 \bigl( \vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i^{(t)} \bigr) \;+\; c_2 r_2 \bigl( \vec{g} - \vec{x}_i^{(t)} \bigr)
$$

**Explanation of each term**:
- $w \vec{v}_i^{(t)}$ → inertial momentum (continuation of past motion).
- $c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i^{(t)})$ → cognitive pull toward personal best.
- $c_2 r_2(\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i^{(t)})$ → social pull toward global best.

**Step 3 — Update the position via explicit Euler integration**:

$$
\vec{x}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{x}_i^{(t)} + \vec{v}_i^{(t+1)}
$$

This is mathematically identical to **forward Euler discretization** of a second-order dynamical system with damping coefficient $(1 - w)$ and spring constants $c_1, c_2$.

**Step 4 — Constriction Coefficient Variant (Clerc–Kennedy)**:
To guarantee convergence, replace $w$ with a constriction factor $\chi$:

$$
\chi = \dfrac{2k}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi(\phi - 4)} \vert}, \quad \phi = c_1 + c_2, \quad k \in [0, 1]
$$

For $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, we obtain $\phi = 4.1$, giving $\chi \approx 0.729$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — GWO Position Update Equation

### 3.2.1 Objective

Derive the Grey Wolf Optimizer update based on the social hierarchy ($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\delta$, $\omega$).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Encircling the prey** (modeled as uniform circular motion):

$$
\vec{D} = \vert \vec{C} \cdot \vec{X}_p(t) - \vec{X}(t) \vert
$$

$$
\vec{X}(t+1) = \vec{X}_p(t) - \vec{A} \cdot \vec{D}
$$

where the coefficient vectors are:

$$
\vec{A} = 2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{r}_1 - \vec{a}, \quad \vec{C} = 2 \vec{r}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Linear decay of $\vec{a}$** across iterations:

$$
\vec{a}(t) = 2 - \dfrac{2t}{T_{max}}
$$

**Step 3 — Hunting guided by the top three wolves**:

For each wolf, compute distances from all three leaders:

$$
\vec{D}_\alpha = \vert \vec{C}_1 \cdot \vec{X}_\alpha - \vec{X} \vert, \quad \vec{D}_\beta = \vert \vec{C}_2 \cdot \vec{X}_\beta - \vec{X} \vert, \quad \vec{D}_\delta = \vert \vec{C}_3 \cdot \vec{X}_\delta - \vec{X} \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Compute candidate positions**:

$$
\vec{X}_1 = \vec{X}_\alpha - \vec{A}_1 \cdot \vec{D}_\alpha, \quad \vec{X}_2 = \vec{X}_\beta - \vec{A}_2 \cdot \vec{D}_\beta, \quad \vec{X}_3 = \vec{X}_\delta - \vec{A}_3 \cdot \vec{D}_\delta
$$

**Step 5 — Final averaged position update**:

$$
\vec{X}^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{\vec{X}_1 + \vec{X}_2 + \vec{X}_3}{3}
$$

This averaging yields a robust **democratic leadership** update that prevents premature convergence.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential Evolution (DE/rand/1/bin) Position Update

### 3.3.1 Objective

Derive the mutation–crossover–selection pipeline that defines DE's position update.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Mutation**: Pick three distinct random agents $r_1 \neq r_2 \neq r_3 \neq i$. Form the donor vector:

$$
\vec{V}_i^{(t)} = \vec{X}_{r_1}^{(t)} + F \cdot \bigl( \vec{X}_{r_2}^{(t)} - \vec{X}_{r_3}^{(t)} \bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Binomial Crossover** with crossover rate $CR \in [0, 1]$:

$$
U_{i,j}^{(t)} = \begin{cases} V_{i,j}^{(t)} & \text{if } \text{rand}_j \le CR \text{ or } j = j_{rand} \\ X_{i,j}^{(t)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Greedy Selection** — final position update rule:

$$
\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} = \begin{cases} \vec{U}_i^{(t)} & \text{if } f(\vec{U}_i^{(t)}) \le f(\vec{X}_i^{(t)}) \\ \vec{X}_i^{(t)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This is essentially **only the position is updated** — DE has no explicit velocity. The mutation vector serves as both perturbation and new candidate.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Algorithm** | **Has Velocity?** | **Uses Population Best?** | **Uses Random Partners?** | **Stochastic Operator** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PSO | ✅ Yes | ✅ gbest + pbest | ❌ No | Uniform random $r_1, r_2$ |
| GWO | ❌ No | ✅ Three leaders | ❌ No | Linear decay $\vec{a}(t)$ |
| WOA | ❌ No | ✅ Best agent | ✅ Yes (if $\vert A \vert \ge 1$) | Spiral + uniform |
| DE | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ $r_1, r_2, r_3$ | Differential mutation |
| GSA | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ All agents | Gravitational force |
| Firefly | ❌ No | ✅ Brighter individual | ✅ Yes | Attractiveness $\beta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix matters**: In KTU 14-mark questions, you may be asked to **compare two algorithms**. This table gives you a ready-made structural comparison that earns full marks when each cell is justified.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unified Position Update Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize population X_i at t=0] --> B[Evaluate fitness f X_i]
    B --> C[Update pbest, gbest or leaders]
    C --> D[Compute perturbation vector using algorithm specific rule]
    D --> D1[PSO: velocity = w v + c1 r1 pbest - x + c2 r2 gbest - x]
    D --> D2[GWO: average of X1 X2 X3]
    D --> D3[WOA: encircling or spiral or prey search]
    D --> D4[DE: mutant donor vector V_i]
    D1 --> E[Apply position update X_i t+1]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Enforce boundary clamping]
    F --> G{Convergence criterion met?}
    G -->|No| C
    G -->|Yes| H[Report best solution]
```

## 4.2 GWO Hierarchical Update Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Alpha wolf: best fitness] --> M1[Compute distance D_alpha]
    L2[Beta wolf: second best] --> M2[Compute distance D_beta]
    L3[Delta wolf: third best] --> M3[Compute distance D_delta]
    M1 --> O1[Candidate position X1]
    M2 --> O2[Candidate position X2]
    M3 --> O3[Candidate position X3]
    O1 --> AVG[Average the three positions]
    O2 --> AVG
    O3 --> AVG
    AVG --> OUT[Final omega wolf position X at t+1]
```

## 4.3 DE Mutation–Crossover–Selection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Population X at iteration t] --> B[Pick r1 r2 r3 distinct]
    B --> C[Mutant donor V = X_r1 + F times X_r2 minus X_r3]
    C --> D[Binomial crossover with probability CR]
    D --> E[Trial vector U]
    E --> F{f U is better than f X_i?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Replace X_i with U]
    F -->|No| H[Retain X_i unchanged]
    G --> I[Next generation X at t+1]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Exploitation vs Exploration Trade-off Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Position Update Equation] --> B[Exploitation component]
    A --> C[Exploration component]
    B --> B1[Personal best pull: c1 r1 pbest minus x]
    B --> B2[Global best pull: c2 r2 gbest minus x]
    B --> B3[Local search: small step size alpha]
    C --> C1[Inertia: w v at t]
    C --> C2[Lévy flight jumps]
    C --> C3[Random agent perturbation]
    C --> C4[Spiral exploration in WOA]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip**: Always include a **flow diagram of the update rule** in 14-mark answers. Examiners explicitly reward visualization for system-design questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** What is a position update equation in a metaheuristic algorithm? Why is it considered the "heart" of any population-based optimizer?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A position update equation is the iterative mathematical rule that defines how each candidate solution in a metaheuristic algorithm modifies its coordinates in the search space from one iteration to the next. It is typically expressed as $\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{X}_i^{(t)} + \Delta \vec{X}_i^{(t+1)}$, where the displacement vector $\Delta \vec{X}_i^{(t+1)}$ encodes the algorithm's exploitation–exploration balance.

It is considered the "heart" of the optimizer because:
1. It determines **how** the population explores the search landscape (exploration).
2. It determines **how** the population converges toward optima (exploitation).
3. Its stochasticity directly governs **diversity preservation** and **premature convergence resistance**.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Differentiate between the position update mechanisms of PSO and Differential Evolution.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | PSO | DE |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity concept | Explicit velocity $\vec{v}_i$ updated first | No velocity; uses donor vector |
| Stochastic sources | $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ | Difference of three agents |
| Update rule | $\vec{x}_i + \vec{v}_i$ | Greedy select between $\vec{X}_i$ and trial $\vec{U}_i$ |
| Memory | Uses pbest and gbest | No memory of past bests |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in 14-mark questions on position update equations:
> 1. **Forgetting to include random vectors** $r_1, r_2$ in PSO — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Writing the WOA spiral equation without the $\cos(2\pi l)$ term** — incomplete (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Conflating "velocity update" with "position update"** in PSO — examiners expect **both equations**.
> 4. **Omitting boundary clamping** discussion in DE — partial mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation of parameter decay** (e.g., $\vec{a}(t)$ in GWO) — loses 2 marks.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a) Derive the complete position update equation of Particle Swarm Optimization with inertia weight. Explain each term with its role in the exploitation–exploration trade-off. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define agent state**:
Each particle $i$ has position $\vec{x}_i^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^D$ and velocity $\vec{v}_i^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^D$.

**Step 2 — Identify memory terms**:
- $\vec{p}_i$ = personal best position found by particle $i$.
- $\vec{g}$ = global best position found by the swarm.

**Step 3 — Write the velocity update equation**:

$$
\vec{v}_i^{(t+1)} = w \vec{v}_i^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 \bigl( \vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i^{(t)} \bigr) + c_2 r_2 \bigl( \vec{g} - \vec{x}_i^{(t)} \bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Explain each term (2 marks)**:
- $w \vec{v}_i^{(t)}$ — inertia, controls exploration.
- $c_1 r_1(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i)$ — cognitive component, individual learning.
- $c_2 r_2(\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i)$ — social component, swarm learning.

**Step 5 — Write the position update**:

$$
\vec{x}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{x}_i^{(t)} + \vec{v}_i^{(t+1)}
$$

**Step 6 — Discuss trade-off (2 marks)**:
- Higher $w$ promotes exploration.
- Higher $c_1$ promotes local refinement (individual learning).
- Higher $c_2$ promotes swarm convergence (social learning).
- Balance typically: $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$, $c_1 = c_2 \approx 2.0$.

---

#### **(b) With a neat flowchart, explain the position update mechanism of the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO). Derive the encircling, hunting, and attacking equations. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Social hierarchy**: Wolves classified as $\alpha$ (best), $\beta$ (second), $\delta$ (third), and $\omega$ (rest).

**Step 2 — Encircling equations**:

$$
\vec{D} = \vert \vec{C} \cdot \vec{X}_p(t) - \vec{X}(t) \vert
$$

$$
\vec{X}(t+1) = \vec{X}_p(t) - \vec{A} \cdot \vec{D}
$$

with $\vec{A} = 2\vec{a}\vec{r}_1 - \vec{a}$, $\vec{C} = 2\vec{r}_2$, and decay $\vec{a}(t) = 2 - 2t/T_{max}$.

**Step 3 — Hunting guided by three leaders**:

For each $\omega$-wolf:

$$
\vec{D}_\alpha = \vert \vec{C}_1 \cdot \vec{X}_\alpha - \vec{X} \vert, \quad \vec{D}_\beta = \vert \vec{C}_2 \cdot \vec{X}_\beta - \vec{X} \vert, \quad \vec{D}_\delta = \vert \vec{C}_3 \cdot \vec{X}_\delta - \vec{X} \vert
$$

Candidate positions:

$$
\vec{X}_1 = \vec{X}_\alpha - \vec{A}_1 \cdot \vec{D}_\alpha, \quad \vec{X}_2 = \vec{X}_\beta - \vec{A}_2 \cdot \vec{D}_\beta, \quad \vec{X}_3 = \vec{X}_\delta - \vec{A}_3 \cdot \vec{D}_\delta
$$

**Step 4 — Final attack update**:

$$
\vec{X}^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{\vec{X}_1 + \vec{X}_2 + \vec{X}_3}{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Flowchart** (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes).

**Step 6 — Attacking condition**: When $\vert \vec{A} \vert < 1$, wolves converge (exploitation). When $\vert \vec{A} \vert \ge 1$, wolves diverge (exploration).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Explain the Whale Optimization Algorithm (WOA) with its three position update phases: encircling prey, bubble-net attacking (spiral), and search for prey. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Encircling prey**:

$$
\vec{D} = \vert \vec{C} \cdot \vec{X}^*(t) - \vec{X}(t) \vert
$$

$$
\vec{X}(t+1) = \vec{X}^*(t) - \vec{A} \cdot \vec{D}
$$

with $\vec{A} = 2\vec{a}\vec{r} - \vec{a}$, $\vec{C} = 2\vec{r}$, $\vec{a}$ decays from 2 to 0.

**Phase 2 — Bubble-net attack (spiral)**:
Choose probability $p \in [0, 1]$:

$$
\vec{X}(t+1) = \begin{cases} \vec{X}^*(t) - \vec{A} \cdot \vec{D} & \text{if } p < 0.5 \\ \vec{D}' \cdot e^{bl} \cos(2\pi l) + \vec{X}^*(t) & \text{if } p \ge 0.5 \end{cases}
$$

where $\vec{D}' = \vert \vec{X}^*(t) - \vec{X}(t) \vert$, $b$ is a constant defining spiral shape, and $l \in [-1, 1]$.

**Phase 3 — Search for prey (exploration)**:
When $\vert \vec{A} \vert \ge 1$, update using a random agent:

$$
\vec{D} = \vert \vec{C} \cdot \vec{X}_{rand}(t) - \vec{X}(t) \vert
$$

$$
\vec{X}(t+1) = \vec{X}_{rand}(t) - \vec{A} \cdot \vec{D}
$$

**Phase 4 — Mechanism summary**: Encircling → shrinking (via $\vec{a}$ decay); Spiral → exploitation by helix path; Random search → exploration to escape local optima.

---

#### **(b) Derive the position update rule of Differential Evolution (DE/rand/1/bin). Discuss the role of the scaling factor $F$ and crossover rate $CR$. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mutation**: Pick $r_1 \neq r_2 \neq r_3 \neq i$ randomly:

$$
\vec{V}_i^{(t)} = \vec{X}_{r_1}^{(t)} + F \cdot \bigl( \vec{X}_{r_2}^{(t)} - \vec{X}_{r_3}^{(t)} \bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Crossover** (binomial):

$$
U_{i,j}^{(t)} = \begin{cases} V_{i,j}^{(t)} & \text{if } \text{rand}_j \le CR \\ X_{i,j}^{(t)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $j_{rand}$ ensures at least one dimension is inherited from the donor.

**Step 3 — Selection (greedy)** — final position update:

$$
\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} = \begin{cases} \vec{U}_i^{(t)} & \text{if } f(\vec{U}_i^{(t)}) \le f(\vec{X}_i^{(t)}) \\ \vec{X}_i^{(t)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Role of $F$** (scaling factor):
- Small $F$ ($\approx 0.1$): small steps, slow convergence, good for separable problems.
- Large $F$ ($\approx 0.9$): large steps, better diversity, good for multi-modal problems.
- Typical range: $F \in [0.4, 1.0]$.

**Step 5 — Role of $CR$** (crossover rate):
- $CR \approx 0.1$: very low diversity, rotation-invariant behavior.
- $CR \approx 0.9$: high diversity, biased toward donor vector.
- Typical choice: $CR \in [0.1, 0.9]$.

**Step 6 — Why greedy selection?**: It guarantees that the population fitness is monotonically non-increasing across generations — a unique feature of DE among metaheuristics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Position Update Equations**

- ✅ The general form of any position update is $\vec{X}_i^{(t+1)} = \vec{X}_i^{(t)} + \Delta \vec{X}_i^{(t+1)}$.
- ✅ **PSO** has both velocity and position updates; uses $w, c_1, c_2, r_1, r_2$.
- ✅ **Constriction factor** $\chi \approx 0.729$ ensures PSO convergence.
- ✅ **GWO** uses three leaders ($\alpha, \beta, \delta$) and averages their candidate positions.
- ✅ **GWO's $\vec{a}(t)$** decays linearly from 2 to 0 to switch from exploration to exploitation.
- ✅ **WOA** has three phases: encircling, spiral bubble-net, random prey search.
- ✅ **WOA's spiral** uses $e^{bl} \cos(2\pi l)$ — memorize this!
- ✅ **DE** has no velocity; uses mutation, binomial crossover, and greedy selection.
- ✅ **DE donor vector** $\vec{V}_i = \vec{X}_{r_1} + F(\vec{X}_{r_2} - \vec{X}_{r_3})$.
- ✅ **Greedy selection** guarantees monotonically non-increasing population fitness.
- ✅ **GSA** uses gravitational force and mass-weighted acceleration.
- ✅ **Firefly** uses light-intensity-based attractiveness $\beta = \beta_0 e^{-\gamma r^2}$.
- ✅ **Cuckoo Search** uses **Lévy flights** with $L(s) \sim s^{-1.5}$.
- ✅ Always **enforce boundary clamping** after position update.
- ✅ Compare algorithms using: velocity presence, memory usage, randomness source, and trade-off behavior.
- ✅ Examiners reward **diagrams and flowcharts** — always include one in 14-mark answers.

---

topics.title: Velocity Clamping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Velocity Clamping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Clamping** is a fundamental parameter control strategy in **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** that constrains the magnitude of each particle's velocity vector within a predefined bounded range, typically $[-V_{max}, +V_{max}]$, on a per-dimension basis. It serves as the primary mechanism to prevent **swarm explosion** — the uncontrolled divergence of particles beyond the feasible search space.

The technique was formally introduced by **Shi and Eberhart (1998)** as a stabilization enhancement to the original Kennedy–Eberhart PSO (1995) formulation. It addresses the well-known **"swarm divergence problem"** where particles' velocities can grow without bound due to the recursive nature of the velocity update equation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Speed Governor on a Car Engine**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car without a speed limiter. Every time you press the accelerator, the car keeps gaining speed. In the early days of motoring, this was a real safety hazard — engines would "blow up" because there was no upper bound on RPM. The **velocity governor** was invented to cap the maximum RPM, ensuring the engine operates within safe mechanical limits.
>
> **Velocity Clamping in PSO works identically.** Each particle is like a car, and its velocity is like engine speed. Without clamping, particles can accelerate without limit due to compounding inertia + cognitive + social terms, causing them to "fly out" of the valid search space and miss optimal solutions. Velocity clamping acts as a **soft speed governor**, allowing exploration but preventing catastrophic divergence.

### 1.3 Key Constants and Metrics

- **$V_{max}$**: The maximum allowable velocity magnitude per dimension (a **user-defined hyperparameter**).
- **$X_{max}$**: The upper bound of the search space in each dimension.
- **Typical heuristic**: $V_{max} = k \cdot X_{max}$, where $k \in [0.1, 1.0]$.
- **Units**: Dimensionless (normalized to the search space), though conceptually expressed in units of *position per iteration*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Velocity Clamping Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The standard PSO velocity update equation **before** clamping is:

$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = w \cdot v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{id}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{d}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)})$$

Where:
- $v_{id}^{(t)}$ = velocity of particle $i$ in dimension $d$ at iteration $t$
- $w$ = inertia weight
- $c_1, c_2$ = cognitive and social acceleration coefficients
- $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ = random numbers
- $p_{id}^{(t)}$ = personal best position of particle $i$
- $g_{d}^{(t)}$ = global best position found by the swarm
- $x_{id}^{(t)}$ = current position of particle $i$

**After computation**, velocity clamping is applied:

$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = \begin{cases} V_{max,d} & \text{if } v_{id}^{(t+1)} > V_{max,d} \\ -V_{max,d} & \text{if } v_{id}^{(t+1)} < -V_{max,d} \\ v_{id}^{(t+1)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is then followed by the position update:

$$x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + v_{id}^{(t+1)}$$

### 2.2 The 'Why' — Why Velocity Clamping is Necessary

1. **Prevents Swarm Explosion**: Without bounds, velocities can grow exponentially across iterations, especially when particles overshoot good regions and get pulled back strongly.
2. **Maintains Search Space Confinement**: Ensures particles remain inside or near the feasible region $[-X_{max}, X_{max}]$.
3. **Controls Exploration vs. Exploitation**: A small $V_{max}$ favors **exploitation** (local search); a large $V_{max}$ favors **exploration** (global search).
4. **Ensures Convergence Stability**: Provides a Lipschitz-like bound that helps in formal convergence proofs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Range |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $v_{id}^{(t+1)} = w v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{id} - x_{id}) + c_2 r_2 (g_d - x_{id})$ | Standard velocity update | — |
| 2 | $v_{id}^{(t+1)} = \min(\max(v_{id}^{(t+1)}, -V_{max}), V_{max})$ | Compact clamping rule | $\vert v_{id} \vert \leq V_{max}$ |
| 3 | $x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + v_{id}^{(t+1)}$ | Position update | — |
| 4 | $V_{max} = k \cdot X_{max}$ | Heuristic setting rule | $k \in [0.1, 1.0]$ |
| 5 | $V_{max} = \alpha \cdot (X_{max} - X_{min})$ | Range-based setting | $\alpha \in [0.1, 0.5]$ |
| 6 | $\text{Stagnation: } v_{id} \to 0$ | Convergence indicator | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Velocity clamping is widely used in real-world PSO deployments including **optimal power flow in smart grids**, **neural network weight training** (PSO-NN), **UAV swarm trajectory planning**, **PID controller tuning**, and **feature selection in high-dimensional ML pipelines**.

### 2.4 Limitations of Velocity Clamping

- **Premature Convergence**: Too small a $V_{max}$ traps particles in local optima.
- **Dimension Dependency**: A scalar $V_{max}$ may be too restrictive for some dimensions and too loose for others.
- **No Directional Information**: Clamping treats all dimensions uniformly, ignoring problem-specific scaling.
- **Alternative Approaches**: Adaptive velocity clamping, constriction coefficient (Clerc & Kennedy), and dynamic $V_{max}$ strategies address these limitations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Clamped Velocity Update Rule

We begin with the **unbounded** velocity recurrence:

$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = w v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{id}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_d^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)})$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Stability Condition**

For swarm stability (bounded particle trajectories), we require $\vert v_{id}^{(t)} \vert < \infty$ for all $t$. Without intervention, the recurrence can yield unbounded growth because each term on the RHS depends on past positions, which themselves depend on past velocities. This creates a feedback loop.

**Step 2 — Define the Maximum Allowable Velocity**

Introduce a hard constraint $V_{max,d} > 0$ for each dimension $d$ such that:

$$\vert v_{id}^{(t+1)} \vert \leq V_{max,d} \quad \forall i, d, t$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Saturation Function**

Define the **saturation (clipping) operator** $\sigma(\cdot)$:

$$\sigma(v) = \begin{cases} V_{max} & \text{if } v > V_{max} \\ -V_{max} & \text{if } v < -V_{max} \\ v & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is equivalent to: $\sigma(v) = \text{sgn}(v) \cdot \min(\vert v \vert, V_{max})$

**Step 4 — Compose the Final Clamped Update**

$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = \sigma\left( w v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{id}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_d^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)}) \right)$$

And the position evolves as:

$$x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + v_{id}^{(t+1)}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style)**: Consider a 2D PSO with $V_{max} = 2.0$ per dimension. At iteration $t$, a particle has $v_{i}^{(t)} = (1.5, -0.8)$. After applying the velocity update with $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.5$, $r_1 = 0.4$, $r_2 = 0.6$, $p_i = (3, 4)$, $g = (5, 2)$, $x_i^{(t)} = (2, 3)$. Compute the clamped velocity $v_i^{(t+1)}$.

**Step-by-Step Solution**:

**Step 1** — Compute cognitive component $c_1 r_1 (p_i - x_i)$:

$$
c_1 r_1 (p_i - x_i) = 1.5 \times 0.4 \times (3-2, \; 4-3) = 0.6 \times (1, 1) = (0.6, 0.6)
$$

**Step 2** — Compute social component $c_2 r_2 (g - x_i)$:

$$
c_2 r_2 (g - x_i) = 1.5 \times 0.6 \times (5-2, \; 2-3) = 0.9 \times (3, -1) = (2.7, -0.9)
$$

**Step 3** — Compute inertia term $w v_i^{(t)}$:

$$
w v_i^{(t)} = 0.7 \times (1.5, -0.8) = (1.05, -0.56)
$$

**Step 4** — Sum all components (raw velocity):

$$
v_i^{(t+1),\, \text{raw}} = (1.05 + 0.6 + 2.7, \; -0.56 + 0.6 - 0.9)
$$

$$
v_i^{(t+1),\, \text{raw}} = (4.35, \; -0.86)
$$

**Step 5** — Apply velocity clamping with $V_{max} = 2.0$:

- Dimension 1: $\vert 4.35 \vert > 2.0 \Rightarrow$ clamp to $+V_{max} = +2.0$
- Dimension 2: $\vert -0.86 \vert \leq 2.0 \Rightarrow$ keep as $-0.86$

$$
v_i^{(t+1)} = (2.0, \; -0.86)
$$

**Step 6** — Update position:

$$
x_i^{(t+1)} = x_i^{(t)} + v_i^{(t+1)} = (2 + 2.0, \; 3 + (-0.86)) = (4.0, \; 2.14)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Without clamping, the particle would have moved to $(6.35, 2.14)$, potentially exiting the search space if the bounds were $[-5, 5]$ — but more critically, future iterations would compound this overshoot.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Impact of $V_{max}$ on PSO Behavior

| $V_{max}$ Setting | Behavior | Convergence Speed | Risk |
|-------------------|----------|-------------------|------|
| $V_{max} \to 0$ | Pure local search | Very fast | Premature convergence |
| $V_{max} \approx 0.1 \cdot X_{max}$ | Exploitation-heavy | Fast | Local optima trapping |
| $V_{max} \approx 0.5 \cdot X_{max}$ | Balanced | Moderate | Generally optimal |
| $V_{max} \approx X_{max}$ | Exploration-heavy | Slow | Wasted evaluations |
| $V_{max} \to \infty$ | Unbounded (no clamp) | Divergent | Swarm explosion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PSO Loop with Velocity Clamping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Swarm Particles] --> B[Evaluate Fitness Function]
    B --> C[Update Personal Best pbest]
    C --> D[Update Global Best gbest]
    D --> E[Compute Raw Velocity v_raw]
    E --> F{V_raw exceeds V_max}
    F -->|Yes| G[Apply Clamping: v = sign v times min v abs V_max]
    F -->|No| H[Keep v_raw unchanged]
    G --> I[Update Position x_new = x_old + v_clamped]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Convergence Criteria Met}
    J -->|No| B
    J -->|Yes| K[Output Global Best Solution]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Clamping Decision Logic (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Computed Velocity v_id] --> C1{v_id greater than V_max}
    C1 -->|True| R1[Set v_id equals plus V_max]
    C1 -->|False| C2{v_id less than minus V_max}
    C2 -->|True| R2[Set v_id equals minus V_max]
    C2 -->|False| R3[Keep v_id unchanged]
    R1 --> OUT[Output Clamped Velocity]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Particle Position x_id]
        I2[Particle Velocity v_id]
        I3[Personal Best pbest]
        I4[Global Best gbest]
        I5[Hyperparameters w c1 c2]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Velocity Computation Engine]
        C1[Inertia Term w times v_id]
        C2[Cognitive Term c1 r1 times pbest minus x]
        C3[Social Term c2 r2 times gbest minus x]
        C4[Vector Summation Unit]
    end

    subgraph CLAMP[Velocity Clamping Module]
        K1[Saturation Operator sigma]
        K2[Bound V_max per Dimension]
        K3[Sign Preservation Logic]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Clamped Velocity v_id_new]
        O2[Updated Position x_id_new]
        O3[Fitness Re-evaluation Queue]
    end

    I1 --> C2
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C2
    I4 --> C3
    I5 --> C1
    I5 --> C2
    I5 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> K1
    K2 --> K1
    K3 --> K1
    K1 --> O1
    I1 --> O2
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define velocity clamping in PSO. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Velocity clamping is a parameter control mechanism in Particle Swarm Optimization that restricts each particle's velocity to a bounded range $[-V_{max}, +V_{max}]$ along every dimension. The unbounded velocity update equation can cause velocities to grow without limit across iterations due to compounding inertia, cognitive, and social terms — a phenomenon known as **swarm explosion**. Velocity clamping prevents this divergence by saturating the velocity at a user-defined maximum $V_{max}$, ensuring particles remain within or near the feasible search space and enabling stable convergence. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. How is the parameter $V_{max}$ typically chosen in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The parameter $V_{max}$ is a critical hyperparameter that balances exploration and exploitation. It is commonly set using the heuristic rule:
$$V_{max} = k \cdot X_{max}$$
where $X_{max}$ is the bound of the search space and $k \in [0.1, 1.0]$. Empirical studies (Shi & Eberhart) suggest $k \approx 0.5$ provides a good balance. A small $V_{max}$ favors exploitation (fine local search) but risks premature convergence, while a large $V_{max}$ favors exploration but may waste function evaluations and cause oscillation. Advanced variants use **adaptive** or **time-varying** $V_{max}$ schedules. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the velocity update equation of PSO with inertia weight. Explain the role of each term in the equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of velocity clamping with a suitable mathematical formulation. Discuss the impact of choosing a very large and very small value of $V_{max}$ on the convergence behavior of PSO. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Velocity Update Equation with Inertia Weight (7 marks):**

The original PSO velocity equation (Kennedy & Eberhart, 1995) lacked an inertia term, causing erratic behavior. **Shi and Eberhart (1998)** introduced the inertia weight $w$:

$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = w \cdot v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{id}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{d}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)})$$

**Role of each term:**
- **Inertia term** $w v_{id}^{(t)}$: Controls the particle's tendency to continue in its previous direction. Larger $w$ encourages global exploration; smaller $w$ encourages local exploitation. Typically $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ with linear decay.
- **Cognitive term** $c_1 r_1 (p_{id} - x_{id})$: Pulls the particle toward its personal best position, representing the particle's "memory" or individual learning.
- **Social term** $c_2 r_2 (g_d - x_{id})$: Pulls the particle toward the swarm's global best, representing "social cooperation" and collective knowledge.

**Position update:**
$$x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + v_{id}^{(t+1)}$$

**(b) Velocity Clamping — Mathematical Formulation and Impact (7 marks):**

**Mathematical Formulation:**

After computing the raw velocity, the saturation operator is applied per dimension:

$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = \begin{cases} V_{max,d} & \text{if } v_{id}^{(t+1)} > V_{max,d} \\ -V_{max,d} & \text{if } v_{id}^{(t+1)} < -V_{max,d} \\ v_{id}^{(t+1)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Equivalently: $v_{id}^{(t+1)} = \text{sgn}(v_{id}) \cdot \min(\vert v_{id} \vert, V_{max,d})$

**Impact of $V_{max}$ on Convergence:**

| $V_{max}$ Value | Behavior | Convergence |
|----------------|----------|-------------|
| Very Small ($V_{max} \ll X_{max}$) | Restricted movement; particles cannot escape local neighborhoods | Premature convergence to local optima |
| Very Large ($V_{max} \gg X_{max}$) | Particles fly past good regions; oscillate or diverge | Slow or no convergence (swarm explosion) |
| Balanced ($V_{max} \approx 0.5 X_{max}$) | Healthy exploration-exploitation tradeoff | Reliable convergence to global optimum |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A particle in a 2D PSO has current position $x = (2, 4)$, velocity $v = (1.2, -0.5)$, personal best $p_{best} = (3, 5)$, and global best $g_{best} = (6, 3)$. Given $w = 0.8$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.4$, $r_1 = 0.5$, $r_2 = 0.7$, and $V_{max} = 1.5$ per dimension, compute the new velocity (with clamping) and the new position. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare velocity clamping with the **constriction coefficient** approach (Clerc & Kennedy) for ensuring PSO convergence. Which is preferred and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Cognitive Component:**
$$c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x) = 1.4 \times 0.5 \times (3-2, 5-4) = 0.7 \times (1, 1) = (0.7, 0.7)$$

**Step 2 — Social Component:**
$$c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x) = 1.4 \times 0.7 \times (6-2, 3-4) = 0.98 \times (4, -1) = (3.92, -0.98)$$

**Step 3 — Inertia Component:**
$$w v = 0.8 \times (1.2, -0.5) = (0.96, -0.4)$$

**Step 4 — Raw Velocity:**
$$v_{raw} = (0.96 + 0.7 + 3.92, \; -0.4 + 0.7 - 0.98) = (5.58, -0.68)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Clamping ($V_{max} = 1.5$):**
- Dim 1: $\vert 5.58 \vert > 1.5 \Rightarrow$ clamp to $+1.5$
- Dim 2: $\vert -0.68 \vert \leq 1.5 \Rightarrow$ keep as $-0.68$

$$v_{new} = (1.5, -0.68)$$

**Step 6 — Position Update:**
$$x_{new} = x + v_{new} = (2 + 1.5, \; 4 + (-0.68)) = (3.5, 3.32)$$

**(b) Velocity Clamping vs. Constriction Coefficient (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Velocity Clamping | Constriction Coefficient |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Hard saturation at $V_{max}$ | Multiplicative scaling factor $\chi$ on velocity |
| **Origin** | Shi & Eberhart (1998) | Clerc & Kennedy (2002) |
| **Mathematical Form** | $\sigma(v)$ operator | $\chi = \frac{2k}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$ where $\phi = c_1 + c_2$ |
| **Convergence Guarantee** | Empirical only | Provable stability when $\phi > 4$ and $k \in [0, 1]$ |
| **Parameter Sensitivity** | Highly sensitive to $V_{max}$ tuning | Less sensitive; $\chi$ derived analytically |
| **Exploration Control** | Discrete truncation | Smooth, continuous dampening |
| **Implementation Simplicity** | Very simple | Requires computation of $\chi$ |

**Preferred Method**: The **constriction coefficient** approach is theoretically preferred because it provides **formal convergence guarantees** without requiring an externally tuned $V_{max}$. However, velocity clamping remains popular in practice due to its simplicity and intuitive parameterization. In modern implementations, both techniques are often **combined** for robustness.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Exam Valuation Warning):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the clamping step**: Many students compute the raw velocity but forget to apply $\min/\max$ bounds — losing 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up signs**: When velocity exceeds $-V_{max}$, students often write $+V_{max}$ instead of $-V_{max}$. Always preserve the sign of the original velocity.
> 3. **Skipping position update**: PSO is a **two-stage** update (velocity, then position). Omitting the position update loses 1-2 marks in numerical questions.
> 4. **Confusing $V_{max}$ with $X_{max}$**: $V_{max}$ is a velocity bound; $X_{max}$ is a position bound. They are related but distinct.
> 5. **Not explaining "swarm explosion"**: When asked *why* clamping is needed, students must explicitly mention divergence or explosion — vague answers lose marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Velocity Clamping** = saturation of velocity to $[-V_{max}, +V_{max}]$ per dimension.
- ✅ It is the primary remedy for the **swarm explosion problem** in PSO.
- ✅ The update is a **two-step process**: compute raw velocity → apply clipping → update position.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb**: $V_{max} \approx 0.5 \cdot X_{max}$ for balanced search.
- ✅ **Small $V_{max}$** → exploitation → risk of premature convergence.
- ✅ **Large $V_{max}$** → exploration → risk of divergence.
- ✅ **Constriction coefficient** is a theoretically superior alternative.
- ✅ Clamping is **empirical**, not provably convergent (unlike constriction).
- ✅ Works dimension-wise: each $d$ has its own $V_{max,d}$.
- ✅ Sign of velocity is **preserved** during clamping.
- ✅ Modern variants include **adaptive** and **time-decreasing** $V_{max}$.
- ✅ Real-world applications: power systems, neural network training, UAV swarms, PID tuning, feature selection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inertia Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT632 - METAHEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inertia Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inertia Weight (w)** is a critical control parameter introduced into the velocity update equation of **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** by Shi and Eberhart (1998) to balance the **global exploration** (searching new areas) and **local exploitation** (refining known solutions) capabilities of the swarm.

Mathematically, the inertia weight is a scalar multiplier that scales the particle's previous velocity before it is combined with the cognitive (personal best) and social (global best) components. The modified velocity equation becomes:

$$v_{i}^{t+1} = w \cdot v_{i}^{t} + c_1 r_1 (pbest_{i} - x_{i}^{t}) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_{i}^{t})$$

where:
- **$w$** = Inertia Weight (typically in the range $[0, 1.2]$)
- **$v_{i}^{t}$** = Velocity of particle $i$ at iteration $t$
- **$c_1, c_2$** = Cognitive and Social acceleration coefficients
- **$r_1, r_2$** = Random numbers uniformly distributed in $[0, 1]$
- **$pbest_i$** = Personal best position of particle $i$
- **$gbest$** = Global best position in the entire swarm

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Skydiver with a Parachute**
> Imagine a skydiver falling through the sky searching for the lowest valley (optimum). His previous momentum (velocity) carries him forward. The **inertia weight (w)** acts like the **drag/parachute setting**:
> - **High w (≈ 1.0+):** A small parachute — he keeps flying long distances, exploring new regions (global search).
> - **Low w (≈ 0.4):** A huge parachute — he slows down and carefully examines the spot he landed on (local search).
> - **w = 0:** No momentum at all — he teleports based purely on where he and his friends have been before (memory only).

This single parameter determines whether the swarm behaves like an aggressive **explorer** or a meticulous **refiner**.

### 1.3 Key Highlights

> [!TIP]
> **Three Critical Ranges to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **$w \in [0.9, 1.2]$** → Strong global exploration (search is broad, particles diverge).
> - **$w \approx 0.8$** → Balanced trade-off (most commonly used default).
> - **$w \in [0.4, 0.6]$** → Strong local exploitation (converges fast, refines solution).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin and Motivation

The original PSO algorithm proposed by Kennedy and Eberhart (1995) lacked the inertia weight. The velocity update was:

$$v_{i}^{t+1} = v_{i}^{t} + c_1 r_1 (pbest_{i} - x_{i}^{t}) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_{i}^{t})$$

**Problems observed:**
- Particles gained too much velocity over iterations.
- The swarm failed to converge in many optimization problems.
- No mechanism to control the "momentum" of particles.

Shi and Eberhart's 1998 modification introduced **w** as a velocity damping coefficient, transforming the algorithm into a convergent, controllable optimizer.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Velocity Equation

1. **Inertia Component ($w \cdot v_i^t$):** Retains past directional motion. Provides momentum.
2. **Cognitive Component ($c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i^t)$):** Pulls the particle toward its historically best position. Represents *individual learning*.
3. **Social Component ($c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i^t)$):** Pulls the particle toward the swarm's best-known position. Represents *social learning / imitation*.

### 2.3 Role of Inertia Weight in Convergence

| Inertia Weight Value | Algorithmic Behavior | Convergence Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **$w > 1.2$** | Particles accelerate indefinitely | **Diverges** — no convergence |
| **$w = 1.0$** | Maintains full previous velocity | Slow / unstable convergence |
| **$w \in [0.9, 1.0]$** | Strong global exploration | Good for early iterations |
| **$w \approx 0.8$** | Balanced exploration/exploitation | **Recommended default** |
| **$w \in [0.4, 0.6]$** | Strong local exploitation | Good for late iterations |
| **$w = 0$** | No momentum; pure memory search | Fast convergence, premature |

### 2.4 Classification of Inertia Weight Strategies

#### (a) Constant Inertia Weight
A single fixed value used throughout the run.
- Common choice: **w = 0.7298** (Clerc's constriction coefficient recommendation).

#### (b) Linear Decreasing Inertia Weight (LDIW)
The most widely used variant. Starts high (exploration) and decreases linearly (exploitation):

$$w(t) = w_{max} - \left(\frac{w_{max} - w_{min}}{T_{max}}\right) \cdot t$$

Standard values: **$w_{max} = 0.9$**, **$w_{min} = 0.4$**.

#### (c) Non-Linear Decreasing Inertia Weight

$$w(t) = w_{min} + (w_{max} - w_{min}) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{\lambda \cdot t}{T_{max}}\right)$$

Provides faster initial decay for rapid transition to exploitation.

#### (d) Adaptive / Fuzzy Inertia Weight
$w$ is dynamically adjusted based on swarm performance metrics such as:
- Distance to global best
- Diversity of the swarm
- Improvement rate of $gbest$

#### (e) Random Inertia Weight

$$w = 0.5 + \frac{Rand()}{2}$$

where $Rand()$ is a uniform random number in $[0, 1]$. Gives $w \in [0.5, 1.0]$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Typical Values |
|---|---|---|
| $v_i^{t+1} = w v_i^t + c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i^t) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i^t)$ | Standard PSO velocity update with inertia | — |
| $x_i^{t+1} = x_i^t + v_i^{t+1}$ | Position update | — |
| $w(t) = w_{max} - \dfrac{(w_{max} - w_{min})}{T_{max}} \cdot t$ | Linear Decreasing Inertia Weight (LDIW) | $w_{max}=0.9, w_{min}=0.4$ |
| $w(t) = w_{min} + (w_{max} - w_{min}) \cdot e^{-\lambda t / T_{max}}$ | Exponential / Non-linear decreasing | $\lambda \approx 3$ |
| $w = 0.5 + \dfrac{Rand()}{2}$ | Random inertia weight | $w \in [0.5, 1.0]$ |
| $\chi = \dfrac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}, \quad \phi = c_1 + c_2$ | Clerc's constriction factor equivalent inertia | $\chi \approx 0.7298$ when $\phi = 4.1$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Optimization:** Tuning PID controllers in real-time.
- **Neural Network Training:** Replacing gradient descent with PSO + inertia for non-differentiable loss surfaces.
- **Antenna Design:** Optimizing electromagnetic arrays (high-dimensional, noisy landscapes).
- **Robotics Path Planning:** Adaptive inertia enables dynamic obstacle avoidance.
- **Supply Chain & Scheduling:** LDIW finds good-enough schedules quickly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Inertia weight is the *single most influential* PSO parameter. Empirical studies by Shi & Eberhart (1998) showed that using LDIW from 0.9 to 0.4 outperformed both the original PSO and Genetic Algorithms on a wide range of benchmark functions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Linear Decreasing Inertia Weight (LDIW)

**Objective:** Find $w(t)$ such that:
- At $t = 0$: $w(0) = w_{max}$
- At $t = T_{max}$: $w(T_{max}) = w_{min}$
- $w(t)$ is a linear function of $t$.

**Step 1:** Assume a linear form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(t) &= a \cdot t + b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(0) = b &= w_{max} \\
w(T_{max}) = a \cdot T_{max} + b &= w_{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $a$ and $b$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{w_{min} - w_{max}}{T_{max}} \\
b &= w_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into the linear form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(t) &= \left(\frac{w_{min} - w_{max}}{T_{max}}\right) \cdot t + w_{max} \\
&= w_{max} + \frac{w_{min} - w_{max}}{T_{max}} \cdot t \\
&= w_{max} - \frac{(w_{max} - w_{min})}{T_{max}} \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

> This matches the canonical LDIW formula. **Derivation complete.**

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (LDIW Calculation)

**Problem:** A PSO algorithm runs for $T_{max} = 100$ iterations with $w_{max} = 0.9$ and $w_{min} = 0.4$. Calculate the inertia weight at iteration $t = 30$.

**Step 1:** Identify the given parameters.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{max} &= 0.9 \\
w_{min} &= 0.4 \\
T_{max} &= 100 \\
t &= 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the LDIW equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(30) &= 0.9 - \frac{(0.9 - 0.4)}{100} \cdot 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the difference and ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(30) &= 0.9 - \frac{0.5}{100} \cdot 30 \\
w(30) &= 0.9 - (0.005)(30)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final numerical value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(30) &= 0.9 - 0.15 \\
w(30) &= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** At iteration 30, the inertia weight is **w = 0.75**, indicating a balanced exploration–exploitation regime.

### 3.3 Stability Analysis — Why $w > 1$ Causes Divergence

**Consider a simplified single-particle, one-dimensional PSO (no cognitive/social randomness):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v^{t+1} &= w \cdot v^{t} + \phi \cdot (p - x^{t}) \\
x^{t+1} &= x^{t} + v^{t+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $y^t = p - x^t$ (distance from optimum). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y^{t+1} &= -w \cdot v^t + (1 - \phi) y^t \\
v^{t+1} &= -w \cdot v^t + \phi y^t
\end{aligned}
$$

For convergence, the eigenvalues $\lambda$ of the system matrix must satisfy $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$.

The characteristic equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda^2 - (1 + w - \phi)\lambda + w &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving and applying the Jury stability criterion (assuming $\phi = c_1 + c_2$ small):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &< w < 1
\end{aligned}
$$

is necessary for convergence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** Any $w \geq 1$ causes the eigenvalues to have magnitude $\geq 1$, leading to **non-convergent oscillations or divergence**. This is why most implementations cap $w \leq 1$.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Inertia Weight vs. Constriction Factor

| Aspect | Inertia Weight ($w$) | Constriction Factor ($\chi$) |
|---|---|---|
| Introduced by | Shi & Eberhart (1998) | Clerc (1999) |
| Mathematical form | Multiplies velocity directly | Multiplies entire velocity update |
| Typical value | 0.7298 (constant) | 0.7298 |
| Dynamic variation | Easy (LDIW, adaptive) | Less common |
| Convergence guarantee | Depends on $w$ and $c_1, c_2$ | Mathematically guaranteed when $\chi$ is chosen correctly |
| Equivalence | $\chi$ with $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ yields $w_{eff} \approx 0.7298$ | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Velocity Update Block Diagram (PSO with Inertia Weight)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Previous Velocity vt"] --> M["Multiply by w"]
    B["Random r1 in 0 to 1"] --> N["Multiply by c1"]
    C["Personal Best pbest"] --> D["Subtract Current Position xt"]
    D --> N
    N --> P["Cognitive Component c1 r1 pbest minus xt"]
    E["Random r2 in 0 to 1"] --> Q["Multiply by c2"]
    F["Global Best gbest"] --> G["Subtract Current Position xt"]
    G --> Q
    Q --> R["Social Component c2 r2 gbest minus xt"]
    M --> S["Sum Aggregator"]
    P --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T["New Velocity vt plus 1"]
    T --> U["Update Position xt plus 1"]
```

### 4.2 Inertia Weight Variation Strategies (LDIW, Random, Adaptive)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Initialize Swarm and Parameters"] --> Init["Set Initial Positions and Velocities"]
    Init --> Loop{"Iterate t = 1 to T max"}
    Loop --> |Yes| ComputeW["Compute Inertia Weight w t"]
    ComputeW --> Choice{"Inertia Weight Strategy"}
    Choice --> |LDIW| L["w t = wmax minus wmax minus wmin divided by Tmax times t"]
    Choice --> |Random| R["w = 0.5 plus Rand divided by 2"]
    Choice --> |Adaptive| A["w t depends on swarm diversity and gbest improvement"]
    L --> Update["Update Velocity and Position"]
    R --> Update
    A --> Update
    Update --> Eval["Evaluate Fitness"]
    Eval --> UpdatePB["Update pbest and gbest"]
    UpdatePB --> Loop
    Loop --> |No| End["Return gbest as Optimal Solution"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Effect of $w$ on Swarm Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH_W["High Inertia Weight w greater than 0.9"]
        H1["Strong Momentum"] --> H2["Large Velocity Updates"]
        H2 --> H3["Wide Search Space Coverage"]
        H3 --> H4["Global Exploration"]
    end
    subgraph MID_W["Balanced w equals 0.7 to 0.8"]
        M1["Moderate Momentum"] --> M2["Balanced Velocity Updates"]
        M2 --> M3["Mix of Search and Refinement"]
        M3 --> M4["Exploration plus Exploitation"]
    end
    subgraph LOW_W["Low Inertia Weight w less than 0.5"]
        L1["Weak Momentum"] --> L2["Small Velocity Updates"]
        L2 --> L3["Focused Local Search"]
        L3 --> L4["Local Exploitation"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Algorithmic Flow: PSO with Inertia Weight

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Start PSO Algorithm"] --> P1["Initialize n particles with random positions xi and velocities vi"]
    P1 --> P2["Evaluate fitness f xi for all particles"]
    P2 --> P3["Set pbest i = xi and gbest = best of all xi"]
    P3 --> P4{"t less than T max"}
    P4 --> |Yes| P5["Compute inertia weight w t"]
    P5 --> P6["For each particle update velocity using w"]
    P6 --> P7["Update position xi"]
    P7 --> P8["Evaluate new fitness"]
    P8 --> P9["Update pbest i and gbest if improved"]
    P9 --> P10["t = t + 1"]
    P10 --> P4
    P4 --> |No| P11["Output gbest as optimal solution"]
    P11 --> P12["End"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define inertia weight in PSO. Mention any two strategies used to vary it dynamically.**

**Model Answer:**
