# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Velocity-Based PSO Iteration

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *flow of information* through a single PSO iteration, highlighting where the velocity model sits in the computational pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Swarm] --> B[Evaluate Fitness f x_i]
    B --> C{Is f x_i < f pbest_i}
    C -- Yes --> D[Update pbest i = x_i]
    C -- No --> E[Retain Old pbest]
    D --> F[Identify Global Best g]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Sample r1 r2 from U 0 1]
    G --> H[Apply Velocity Model]
    H --> H1["Momentum: w * v i t"]
    H --> H2["Cognition: c1 r1 pbest - x"]
    H --> H3["Social: c2 r2 gbest - x"]
    H1 --> I[Compute v i t+1]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J{Apply Vmax or Constriction}
    J --> K[Update Position x i t+1]
    K --> L{Iter < Tmax}
    L -- Yes --> B
    L -- No --> M[Return gbest as Optimum]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map: The Three Forces of a Velocity Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Behavioural Forces"
        F1["Inertia / Momentum\nw * v i t\n(Exploration)"]
        F2["Cognitive Memory\nc1 r1 pbest - x i\n(Self-Learning)"]
        F3["Social Influence\nc2 r2 gbest - x i\n(Group-Learning)"]
    end
    F1 --> SUM(("+"))
    F2 --> SUM
    F3 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT["v i t+1\n(New Velocity)"]
    OUT --> POS["x i t+1 = x i t + v i t+1\n(New Position)"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Velocity Model to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need proven\nconvergence?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> M1[Constriction Factor\nchi = 0.729\nc1 = c2 = 2.05]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Problem is\nmultimodal\nwith many optima?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M2[High Inertia w = 0.9\nTVAC cognitive decay]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need fast\nconvergence to\nsingle optimum?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> M3[LDW w 0.9 to 0.4\nTVAC social ramp]
    Q3 -- No --> M4[Adaptive w via\nfuzzy feedback]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The three behavioral forces (Momentum, Cognition, Sociality) are *additively* combined in *all* standard velocity models. The **difference between models** is *only* in (a) whether a constant $\chi$ or $w$ multiplies the sum, and (b) how $w$ and $c_1, c_2$ vary with time.

## 4.4 Stability Region Plot (Schematic)

The following text-based diagram shows the **stability region** in the $(c_1, c_2)$ plane for the deterministic PSO (Clerc & Kennedy 2002):

```
          c2
          |
       4 -|        ___________
          |       /           \
          |      /  STABLE     \
       2 -|     /    REGION     \
          |    /                 \
          |   /                   \
          |__/_____________________\______ c1
          0      2        4
          |
        c1 + c2 > 4
        c1, c2 > 0
```

The *unbounded* region above the curve $c_1 c_2 = 4$ (approximately) is where eigenvalues satisfy $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ after applying the constriction factor. KTU exam answer should include this schematic in rough form for full marks on stability questions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define the inertia weight in PSO. How does it affect the balance between exploration and exploitation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The inertia weight $w$ is a multiplicative coefficient applied to a particle's previous velocity in the PSO update rule:
$$\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w \vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2 r_2 (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i)$$

- **High $w$ (close to 1):** preserves momentum, encourages **exploration** (global search).
- **Low $w$ (close to 0):** dampens velocity, encourages **exploitation** (local search).
- **Typical value:** $w = 0.7298$ (constriction) or $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ with linear decrease.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for high-$w$ effect, 1 mark for low-$w$ effect)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the constriction factor in PSO? Why is it preferred over $V_{max}$ clipping?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The constriction factor $\chi$ is a theoretically derived multiplier that ensures the PSO dynamics converge without ad-hoc velocity clamping. It is computed as:
$$\chi = \frac{2\kappa}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}, \quad \phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$$

**Why preferred over $V_{max}$:**
1. **Provable convergence** — guaranteed by eigenvalue analysis (Jury's test).
2. **No user tuning** of $V_{max}$ needed — the parameter is self-regulating.
3. **Canonical setting** $\chi = 0.729, c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ works across problems.
4. **Prevents velocity explosion** without artificially truncating the search direction.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q.A (a) Derive the constriction coefficient formula starting from the deterministic PSO recurrence and apply Jury's stability criterion. Clearly state the canonical values of $c_1, c_2, \chi$ for stable PSO. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the simplified deterministic PSO velocity update (single particle, single dimension):
$$v(t+1) = v(t) + c_1 r_1 (p - x(t)) + c_2 r_2 (g - x(t))$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $v(t) = x(t) - x(t-1)$ and $x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)$ to obtain a second-order recurrence in $x$:
$$x(t+1) = (1 + \chi - \phi_1 - \phi_2) x(t) - \chi x(t-1) + \phi_1 p + \phi_2 g$$
where $\phi_1 = \chi c_1/2$ and $\phi_2 = \chi c_2/2$ (using $E[r] = 1/2$ and including $\chi$ as in constriction model).

**Step 3:** Form the characteristic equation by substituting $x_h(t) = \lambda^t$:
$$\lambda^2 - (1 + \chi - \phi_1 - \phi_2) \lambda + \chi = 0$$

**Step 4:** Apply Jury's test — three conditions for asymptotic stability:
- $P(1) = 1 - (1 + \chi - \phi) + \chi = \phi - 1 > 0 \Rightarrow \phi > 1$
- $P(-1) = 1 + (1 + \chi - \phi) + \chi = 2 + 2\chi - \phi > 0 \Rightarrow \phi < 2 + 2\chi$
- $\vert a_2 \vert = \vert \chi \vert < 1 \Rightarrow 0 < \chi < 1$

**Step 5:** Combine the conditions. To ensure the *entire* parameter space $(\phi, \chi)$ is stable (not just at boundary), we set the discriminant to zero and solve:
$$\chi = \kappa(1 - \sqrt{\phi - 1})^2 = \frac{2\kappa}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$$

**Step 6:** Canonical values: $\phi = 4.1 \Rightarrow \chi \approx 0.729$, with $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, $\kappa = 1$.

**(Marks: 2 for setup, 2 for characteristic equation, 2 for Jury's test application, 1 for canonical values)**

---

**Q.A (b) Compare the Inertia Weight Model and Constriction Factor Model of PSO in terms of (i) convergence guarantee, (ii) parameter count, (iii) sensitivity to $V_{max}$, and (iv) typical engineering application where each is preferred. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| **Criterion** | **Inertia Weight Model (Shi & Eberhart 1998)** | **Constriction Factor Model (Clerc & Kennedy 2002)** |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Convergence Guarantee** | Empirical only; depends on $w$ schedule | **Provable** via eigenvalue analysis |
| **(ii) Parameter Count** | 3 base + 1 schedule ($w, c_1, c_2, w(t)$) | 3 base + 1 derived ($\chi, c_1, c_2$, $\phi$) |
| **(iii) $V_{max}$ Sensitivity** | Optional but recommended; high sensitivity | **Not required**; $V_{max}$ becomes redundant |
| **(iv) Preferred Application** | Dynamic, time-varying problems (e.g., MPPT, online tuning) | Static, offline optimization (e.g., truss design, NN training) |

**Verdict (2 marks):** The constriction factor model is theoretically superior but the inertia weight model offers more *tunability* and is therefore preferred in **adaptive engineering applications** where the problem landscape evolves over time. For benchmarking on static benchmark functions (Sphere, Rosenbrock, Rastrigin), constriction factor is the gold standard.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q.B (a) Explain the Linearly Decreasing Weight (LDW) strategy in detail. Show how $w$ is updated and discuss its effect on the exploration–exploitation balance across iterations. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of LDW (Shi & Eberhart 1998):**

LDW is an adaptive inertia weight scheme where $w$ is **linearly decreased** from $w_{max}$ to $w_{min}$ over the course of the run:

$$w(t) = w_{max} - \frac{(w_{max} - w_{min}) \cdot t}{T_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Parameter values (KTU benchmark):**
- $w_{max} = 0.9$ (start with high exploration)
- $w_{min} = 0.4$ (end with strong exploitation)
- $T_{max}$ = total iteration budget (e.g., 1000)

**Step 3 — Worked example:**

For $t = 250$ and $T_{max} = 1000$:
$$w(250) = 0.9 - \frac{(0.9 - 0.4) \cdot 250}{1000} = 0.9 - 0.125 = 0.775$$

**Step 4 — Effect on exploration–exploitation balance:**

| **Phase** | **Iterations** | **$w$ value** | **Behaviour** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Early | $t \in [0, 0.3 T_{max}]$ | $\approx 0.8$–$0.9$ | **Exploration** dominant | Scouting the design space |
| Mid | $t \in [0.3, 0.7] T_{max}$ | $\approx 0.5$–$0.8$ | **Balanced** transition | Convergence toward optima |
| Late | $t \in [0.7 T_{max}, T_{max}]$ | $\approx 0.4$–$0.5$ | **Exploitation** dominant | Fine-tuning solution |

**Step 5 — Intuition:** The swarm "scouts" broadly at the start (high momentum, big jumps), then "settles" into a local refinement near the end (low momentum, small adjustments). This mimics the *annealing* behaviour of Simulated Annealing, but with a deterministic schedule.

**Step 6 — Limitations:** LDW is monotonic — it does not adapt to swarm diversity or fitness stagnation. A premature decrease can trap the swarm in a local optimum.

**(Marks: 1 for formula, 1 for parameter values, 1 for example, 2 for exploration–exploitation table, 2 for intuition + limitations)**

---

**Q.B (b) Discuss Time-Varying Acceleration Coefficients (TVAC) by Ratnaweera et al. (2003). Why is $c_1$ decreased and $c_2$ increased with time? (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of TVAC:**

TVAC modulates the cognitive and social coefficients linearly with the iteration counter:

$$c_1(t) = c_{1i} + \frac{(c_{1f} - c_{1i}) \cdot t}{T_{max}}, \quad c_2(t) = c_{2i} + \frac{(c_{2f} - c_{2i}) \cdot t}{T_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Canonical parameters (KTU exam values):**
- $c_{1i} = 2.5$ (initial cognitive pull)
- $c_{1f} = 0.5$ (final cognitive pull)
- $c_{2i} = 0.5$ (initial social pull)
- $c_{2f} = 2.5$ (final social pull)

**Step 3 — Behavioural logic for the trend:**

| **Coefficient** | **Trend** | **Phase Intuition** |
|---|---|---|
| $c_1$ (cognitive) | $2.5 \to 0.5$ (decreasing) | Early: trust own memory; Late: trust group |
| $c_2$ (social) | $0.5 \to 2.5$ (increasing) | Early: less reliance on swarm; Late: converge to global best |

**Step 4 — Worked example at $t = 500, T_{max} = 1000$:**

$$c_1(500) = 2.5 + \frac{(0.5 - 2.5) \cdot 500}{1000} = 2.5 - 1.0 = 1.5$$
$$c_2(500) = 0.5 + \frac{(2.5 - 0.5) \cdot 500}{1000} = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5$$

The swarm reaches a "balanced" state at the mid-point. **Note:** $c_1(t) + c_2(t) = 3$ *constant* in this canonical TVAC setting — but this is *less than 4*, meaning the basic TVAC alone is **not** provably convergent. It is usually combined with an inertia weight.

**Step 5 — Engineering advantage:** TVAC + LDW combination gives **complementary adaptation** — early iterations favour individual exploration (high $c_1$, high $w$), late iterations favour group convergence (high $c_2$, low $w$).

**Step 6 — Real-world deployment:** TVAC is preferred in **dynamic optimization** (e.g., mobile robot path re-planning) where the optimum drifts over time and the swarm must continually re-converge.

**(Marks: 1 for formula, 1 for canonical values, 2 for the directional logic table, 1 for example, 2 for engineering use-case)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Velocity Model Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting $E[r_1] = E[r_2] = 1/2$** when doing deterministic analysis. Examiners expect this substitution explicitly. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing $w$ with $\chi$.** The inertia weight $w$ is a *free parameter*; the constriction factor $\chi$ is a *derived* coefficient. Mixing them up costs **(-2 marks)**.
> 3. **Not stating $\phi > 4$** when using the constriction factor. Without this, the eigenvalue argument collapses. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Writing $c_1 = c_2 = 2$ instead of 2.05** in canonical PSO. While $c = 2$ is common, the provably convergent setting is $2.05$ with $\chi = 0.729$. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **Omitting the position update** $x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)$. Velocity update alone is incomplete. **(-1 mark)**
> 6. **Writing $V_{max}$ clipping in the constriction model.** This is **redundant and penalized** — explicitly state that $V_{max}$ is *not required*. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Models — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Three behavioural forces:** Inertia (momentum), Cognition (self-best pull), Sociality (group-best pull). Memorize this triad.
- **Original PSO (1995):** No $w$; $V_{max}$ clamping is **mandatory**. Not provably convergent.
- **Inertia Weight PSO (1998):** Adds $w$ as a momentum governor. Empirical, but flexible.
- **Constriction Factor PSO (2002):** $\chi \approx 0.729, c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$. **Only** provably convergent model.
- **LDW (1998):** $w(t) = w_{max} - (w_{max} - w_{min}) t / T_{max}$; typical $[0.9, 0.4]$.
- **TVAC (2003):** $c_1$ decreases $2.5 \to 0.5$; $c_2$ increases $0.5 \to 2.5$.
- **Random numbers:** $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0, 1)$ are essential for stochasticity — never set them to constants.
- **Stability requirement:** $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$ for constriction model; Jury's test on the characteristic polynomial.
- **Steady-state:** A particle's equilibrium is a *weighted mean* of its own pbest and the global gbest — never exactly $\vec{g}$ unless $c_1 = 0$.
- **Exploration ⇔ high $w$, high $c_1$, high $V_{max}$.** Exploitation ⇔ low $w$, high $c_2$, no $V_{max}$ clipping.
- **Use Constriction** for static benchmark problems and theory. **Use Inertia + TVAC** for adaptive/dynamic real-world problems.
- **Engineering applications:** PID tuning, MPPT, truss design, NN training, antenna array design.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) Parameters** are the tunable control variables that govern the search behavior, convergence velocity, exploration–exploitation balance, and trajectory stability of particles traversing a multi-dimensional solution hyperspace.

The **Basic PSO Parameters** collectively form the algorithmic control panel of the swarm intelligence framework. They dictate *how* each particle updates its velocity and position, and they directly determine whether the algorithm will converge prematurely, oscillate unstably, or successfully locate the global optimum.

The canonical set of basic PSO parameters is:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Inertia Weight | $w$ | $[0.4,\ 0.9]$ |
| Cognitive Acceleration Coefficient | $c_1$ | $[1.5,\ 2.5]$ |
| Social Acceleration Coefficient | $c_2$ | $[1.5,\ 2.5]$ |
| Maximum Velocity | $V_{max}$ | Problem-dependent |
| Swarm Size (Population) | $N$ | $20$–$100$ |
| Maximum Iterations | $T_{max}$ | $500$–$2000$ |
| Uniform Random Numbers | $r_1,\ r_2$ | $U(0,1)$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Flock of Birds Searching for Food"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a flock of hungry birds scattered across a foggy valley. They cannot see the entire valley, but each bird remembers (a) the best food location it has personally found, and (b) the best food location any bird in the flock has found.**

Every bird flies in a direction that is a weighted combination of:
- **Its own momentum** (the *inertia weight* $w$).
- **Its pull toward its personal best memory** (the *cognitive coefficient* $c_1$).
- **Its pull toward the flock's best-known spot** (the *social coefficient* $c_2$).

Now translate "bird" to **"particle"** and "food location" to **"candidate solution"** — and you have the essence of PSO parameters. Each parameter acts as a knob that adjusts how strongly each of these three influences shapes the particle's next move.

## 1.3 The Two Governing Update Equations

$$v_i^{t+1} = w \cdot v_i^t + c_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot (pbest_i - x_i^t) + c_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot (gbest - x_i^t)$$

$$x_i^{t+1} = x_i^t + v_i^{t+1}$$

Every parameter in these two equations belongs to the **Basic PSO Parameters** set. Mastering them is equivalent to mastering the *steering wheel* of the swarm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parameter-by-Parameter Functional Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Inertia Weight ($w$) — The *Momentum Governor*

- **Role:** Scales the particle's previous velocity, thereby preserving momentum across iterations.
- **High $w$ (≈ 0.9):** Strong exploration, particles fly across wide regions, but convergence becomes sluggish and unstable.
- **Low $w$ (≈ 0.4):** Strong exploitation, particles refine local solutions quickly, but risk premature convergence.
- **Linear Decreasing Strategy (Shi & Eberhart, 1998):**

$$w(t) = w_{max} - \left(\frac{w_{max} - w_{min}}{T_{max}}\right) \cdot t$$

where $w_{max}=0.9$, $w_{min}=0.4$, $t$ = current iteration, $T_{max}$ = maximum iterations.

### 2.1.2 Cognitive Coefficient ($c_1$) — The *Self-Confidence Factor*

- **Role:** Pulls each particle toward its own best-known position ($pbest_i$).
- Represents the *individual learning* or *nostalgia* of the particle.
- High $c_1$ encourages particles to wander excessively (over-exploration).
- Typical default: $c_1 = 2.0$.

### 2.1.3 Social Coefficient ($c_2$) — The *Swarm Conformity Factor*

- **Role:** Pulls each particle toward the global best position found by the entire swarm ($gbest$).
- Represents *social knowledge transfer* or *herd behavior*.
- High $c_2$ causes premature convergence to local optima.
- Typical default: $c_2 = 2.0$.

### 2.1.4 Constriction Coefficient ($\chi$) — Clerc & Kennedy (2002)

- An alternative single-parameter formulation that guarantees convergence:

$$\chi = \frac{2 \cdot \kappa}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$$

with $\phi = c_1 + c_2$, $\phi > 4$, and $\kappa \in [0,1]$.

The simplified form often cited:

$$\chi \approx 0.7298 \quad \text{when } c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$$

This pair $(\chi=0.7298,\ c_1=c_2=2.05)$ is the **most-cited stable configuration** in KTU board exam solutions.

### 2.1.5 Maximum Velocity ($V_{max}$)

- **Role:** Hard upper bound on $\vert v_i^{t+1} \vert$ in each dimension.
- Prevents particles from "exploding" out of the search space.
- Often set as $V_{max} = k \cdot (X_{max} - X_{min})$, where $k \in [0.1, 1.0]$.

### 2.1.6 Swarm Size ($N$) and Iteration Limit ($T_{max}$)

- **$N$** controls parallelism; more particles = better coverage, but higher computational cost.
- **$T_{max}$** controls total computational budget.
- Trade-off rule of thumb: $N \times T_{max} \approx \text{constant}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity Update | $v_i^{t+1} = w v_i^t + c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i^t) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i^t)$ | dimensionless | All PSO applications |
| 2 | Position Update | $x_i^{t+1} = x_i^t + v_i^{t+1}$ | problem domain | Trajectory integration |
| 3 | Inertia (Linear Decay) | $w(t) = w_{max} - \dfrac{(w_{max}-w_{min})}{T_{max}} \cdot t$ | $[0.4,\ 0.9]$ | Adaptive PSO |
| 4 | Constriction Coefficient | $\chi = \dfrac{2\kappa}{\vert 2-\phi-\sqrt{\phi^2-4\phi} \vert}$ | $\chi \in [0,1]$ | Stable convergence |
| 5 | Stability Condition | $\phi = c_1+c_2 > 4$ | scalar | Convergence proof |
| 6 | Random Numbers | $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ | probability | Stochastic perturbation |
| 7 | Velocity Clipping | $v_i = \min(v_i, V_{max})$ | bound constraint | Numerical safety |
| 8 | Stopping Criterion | $\vert f(gbest^{t}) - f(gbest^{t-1}) \vert \le \epsilon$ | tolerance | Termination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Basic PSO parameters are tuned in real engineering design of *antenna arrays* (electrical), *structural truss weight minimization* (civil), *PID controller tuning* (instrumentation), *neural network training* (CSE), and *job-shop scheduling* (industrial). Every application simply adjusts $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$, and $N$ for its domain.

## 2.3 The Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off — KTU's Favourite Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Exploration** = Searching *new* regions of the search space (global search).
> **Exploitation** = Refining the *current best* region (local search).

The fundamental role of every PSO parameter is to balance these two forces:

| Dominant Force | Parameter Setting | Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| **Exploration** | High $w$, High $c_1$, Low $c_2$ | Particles scatter, slow convergence |
| **Exploitation** | Low $w$, Low $c_1$, High $c_2$ | Fast convergence, premature trapping |
| **Balanced** | $w\approx 0.7$, $c_1=c_2\approx 2.05$, $\chi=0.7298$ | Recommended default |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Constriction Coefficient ($\chi$) Formula

Starting from the simplified 1-D PSO update for particle $i$ at iteration $t$:

$$v_i^{t+1} = v_i^t + c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i^t) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i^t)$$

### Step 1 — Replace Random Variables with Their Expectation

Since $E[r_1] = E[r_2] = \frac{1}{2}$, the *expected* velocity update becomes:

$$v_i^{t+1} = v_i^t + \frac{c_1}{2}(pbest_i - x_i^t) + \frac{c_2}{2}(gbest - x_i^t)$$

### Step 2 — Define the Combined Attractor $p$

Assume the particle tracks a weighted average attractor:

$$p = \frac{c_1 \cdot pbest_i + c_2 \cdot gbest}{c_1 + c_2}$$

Then:

$$v_i^{t+1} = v_i^t + \frac{c_1 + c_2}{2}(p - x_i^t)$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Inertia Weight $w$

Multiply $v_i^t$ by $w$ and define $\phi = c_1 + c_2$:

$$v_i^{t+1} = w \cdot v_i^t + \frac{\phi}{2}(p - x_i^t)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Into Position Equation

Position update:

$$x_i^{t+1} = x_i^t + v_i^{t+1}$$

Expand:

$$x_i^{t+1} = x_i^t + w v_i^t + \frac{\phi}{2}(p - x_i^t)$$

### Step 5 — Replace $w$ with Constriction Coefficient $\chi$

Let $w \equiv \chi$. The convergence analysis (Clerc & Kennedy) requires the second-order characteristic equation of the discrete dynamical system to have all roots within the unit circle. The derived bound is:

$$\chi = \frac{2\kappa}{\vert 2-\phi-\sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Canonical Stable Value

Set $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, hence $\phi = 4.10$, and $\kappa = 1$:

$$\chi = \frac{2 \cdot 1}{\vert 2 - 4.10 - \sqrt{(4.10)^2 - 4(4.10)} \vert}$$

Compute the discriminant:

$$\phi^2 - 4\phi = (4.10)^2 - 4(4.10) = 16.81 - 16.40 = 0.41$$

$$\sqrt{0.41} \approx 0.6403$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\vert 2 - 4.10 - 0.6403 \vert = \vert -2.7403 \vert = 2.7403$$

Therefore:

$$\chi = \frac{2}{2.7403} \approx 0.7298$$

### Step 7 — Final Stable Velocity Equation

$$v_i^{t+1} = 0.7298 \cdot v_i^t + 2.05 \cdot r_1 (pbest_i - x_i^t) + 2.05 \cdot r_2 (gbest - x_i^t)$$

This is the **canonical Clerc–Kennedy stable PSO update**, frequently appearing in KTU 14-mark derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Decreasing Inertia Weight at a Specific Iteration

Given $w_{max} = 0.9$, $w_{min} = 0.4$, $T_{max} = 1000$.

Find $w(t)$ at $t = 250$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Linear Decay Formula

$$w(t) = w_{max} - \frac{w_{max} - w_{min}}{T_{max}} \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$w(250) = 0.9 - \frac{0.9 - 0.4}{1000} \cdot 250$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Difference

$$w(250) = 0.9 - \frac{0.5}{1000} \cdot 250$$

### Step 4 — Multiply

$$w(250) = 0.9 - 0.5 \cdot 0.25 = 0.9 - 0.125$$

### Step 5 — Final Value

$$w(250) = 0.775$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on PSO Behavior

| Parameter $\uparrow$ (Increase) | Exploration | Exploitation | Convergence Speed | Stability | Premature Convergence Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $w$ (Inertia) | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ (oscillates) | $\downarrow$ |
| $c_1$ (Cognitive) | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| $c_2$ (Social) | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (initially) | $\uparrow$ |
| $V_{max}$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| $N$ (Swarm Size) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| $T_{max}$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | — | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The table above is a *high-yield visual* often reproduced in KTU module-end examinations under sub-question (a) of 7-mark questions.

```mermaid
    E --> J

    H --> K["Velocity Update Engine"]

    J --> K
    F --> K

    K --> L["New Velocity v_t+1"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Define the role of the **inertia weight** $w$ in Particle Swarm Optimization. How does it affect exploration versus exploitation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The inertia weight $w$ is a control parameter introduced by Shi and Eberhart (1998) that multiplies the previous velocity of a particle in the velocity update equation:

$$v_i^{t+1} = w \cdot v_i^t + c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i^t) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i^t)$$

A **high value of $w$** (close to 0.9) preserves the particle's previous momentum and promotes **exploration** of the search space. A **low value of $w$** (close to 0.4) reduces momentum and promotes **exploitation** of the current best region. A commonly adopted strategy is the **linear decreasing inertia weight (LDIW)** that gradually reduces $w$ from $0.9$ to $0.4$ across iterations, balancing global and local search. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Differentiate between the **cognitive coefficient** $c_1$ and the **social coefficient** $c_2$ in PSO.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Cognitive Coefficient $c_1$ | Social Coefficient $c_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Attractor** | Particle's own best position $pbest_i$ | Swarm's global best position $gbest$ |
| **Interpretation** | Individual memory / self-confidence | Group knowledge / herd behavior |
| **High value causes** | Excessive wandering, slow convergence | Premature convergence to local optima |
| **Typical value** | $1.5$–$2.5$ | $1.5$–$2.5$ |

Both control the relative pull toward personal memory and swarm-wide best respectively. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail all the **basic PSO parameters** including inertia weight, cognitive and social coefficients, maximum velocity, swarm size, and the constriction coefficient. Discuss their effect on the exploration–exploitation trade-off. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Derive the **constriction coefficient** $\chi$ expression used in the Clerc–Kennedy stable PSO formulation. Compute its value for $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ and $\kappa = 1$, and state the final stable velocity update equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Inertia weight $w$** — Multiplies previous velocity; controls momentum. High $w$ → exploration, low $w$ → exploitation. Linear decrease from $0.9$ to $0.4$ is standard. *(2 marks)*

2. **Cognitive coefficient $c_1$** — Pulls particle toward its own $pbest_i$; represents self-confidence. Typical value $c_1 \approx 2.0$. *(1 mark)*

3. **Social coefficient $c_2$** — Pulls particle toward swarm's $gbest$; represents conformity. Typical value $c_2 \approx 2.0$. *(1 mark)*

4. **Maximum velocity $V_{max}$** — Caps particle velocity to prevent explosion; set as a fraction of the search range $V_{max} = k(X_{max}-X_{min})$. *(1 mark)*

5. **Swarm size $N$ and iteration limit $T_{max}$** — $N$ particles; common choice $N \in [20,100]$. $T_{max}$ controls budget. *(1 mark)*

6. **Constriction coefficient $\chi$** — Clerc–Kennedy formula guaranteeing convergence; canonical value $\chi = 0.7298$ with $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$. *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1: Express velocity update with expected random numbers:

$$v_i^{t+1} = v_i^t + \frac{c_1}{2}(pbest_i - x_i^t) + \frac{c_2}{2}(gbest - x_i^t)$$

Step 2: Combine attractors. Let $\phi = c_1 + c_2$:

$$v_i^{t+1} = v_i^t + \frac{\phi}{2}(p - x_i^t)$$

Step 3: Apply inertia $w \equiv \chi$:

$$v_i^{t+1} = \chi \cdot v_i^t + \frac{\phi}{2}(p - x_i^t)$$

Step 4: Stability analysis of the discrete dynamical system yields:

$$\chi = \frac{2\kappa}{\vert 2-\phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$$

*(2 marks)*

Step 5: Substitute $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05 \Rightarrow \phi = 4.10$:

$$\phi^2 - 4\phi = 16.81 - 16.40 = 0.41, \quad \sqrt{0.41} \approx 0.6403$$

$$\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - 4.10 - 0.6403 \vert} = \frac{2}{2.7403} \approx 0.7298$$

*(3 marks)*

Step 6: Final stable velocity update equation:

$$v_i^{t+1} = 0.7298 \cdot v_i^t + 2.05 \cdot r_1 (pbest_i - x_i^t) + 2.05 \cdot r_2 (gbest - x_i^t)$$

*(2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **velocity update** and **position update** equations in PSO. Identify and explain **each parameter** appearing in the velocity update equation. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A PSO algorithm uses $w_{max} = 0.9$, $w_{min} = 0.4$, and $T_{max} = 500$ iterations with a **linearly decreasing inertia weight** strategy. Compute $w(t)$ at iterations $t = 100$ and $t = 400$. Also explain what happens algorithmically when $w$ becomes very small ($w \to 0$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The two governing equations are:

$$v_i^{t+1} = w \cdot v_i^t + c_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot (pbest_i - x_i^t) + c_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot (gbest - x_i^t)$$

$$x_i^{t+1} = x_i^t + v_i^{t+1}$$

Parameter-by-parameter explanation:

| Symbol | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| $v_i^t$ | Current velocity | Direction & magnitude of motion |
| $w$ | Inertia weight | Momentum preservation |
| $c_1$ | Cognitive coefficient | Attraction to $pbest_i$ |
| $c_2$ | Social coefficient | Attraction to $gbest$ |
| $r_1, r_2$ | Random numbers | Stochastic perturbation in $[0,1]$ |
| $pbest_i$ | Personal best | Best position particle $i$ has seen |
| $gbest$ | Global best | Best position any particle has seen |
| $x_i^t$ | Current position | Location in search space |

*(7 marks — distributed as 2 for equations, 4 for parameter table, 1 for diagram)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Iteration $t = 100$:**

$$w(100) = 0.9 - \frac{0.9 - 0.4}{500} \cdot 100 = 0.9 - \frac{0.5}{500} \cdot 100 = 0.9 - 0.1 = 0.8$$

*(2 marks)*

**Iteration $t = 400$:**

$$w(400) = 0.9 - \frac{0.5}{500} \cdot 400 = 0.9 - 0.4 = 0.5$$

*(2 marks)*

**Effect of $w \to 0$:** *(3 marks)*

When $w$ becomes very small, the particle loses its previous momentum almost entirely. The velocity update effectively reduces to:

$$v_i^{t+1} \approx c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i^t) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i^t)$$

The swarm shifts into a **pure exploitation phase** — particles converge rapidly but risk becoming trapped in **local optima**. The algorithm loses global search ability, which is why a small but non-zero lower bound (typically $0.4$) is maintained.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the units/range** of parameters ($w \in [0.4, 0.9]$, $c_1, c_2 \in [1.5, 2.5]$) → loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $pbest_i$ with $gbest$** in the cognitive/social coefficient explanation → loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps** in the constriction coefficient derivation (especially the discriminant calculation) → loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Using $\chi$ and $w$ interchangeably without justification** — they are mathematically equivalent only under the Clerc–Kennedy framework.
> 5. **Failing to mention the stability condition** $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$ in derivations.
> 6. **Drawing the velocity update without labeling all four components** (inertia, cognitive, social, and position difference terms).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Basic PSO parameters** = $\{w,\ c_1,\ c_2,\ V_{max},\ N,\ T_{max},\ r_1,\ r_2\}$.
- **Velocity update** has **three additive forces**: momentum, cognitive, social.
- **Inertia weight $w$** controls **momentum**; linearly decrease from $0.9 \to 0.4$.
- **Cognitive coefficient $c_1$** = pull to **$pbest_i$** (individual memory).
- **Social coefficient $c_2$** = pull to **$gbest$** (swarm memory).
- **Canonical Clerc–Kennedy stable PSO**: $\chi = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, $\kappa = 1$.
- **Stability condition**: $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$.
- **Linear decreasing $w(t)$**: $w(t) = w_{max} - \dfrac{(w_{max}-w_{min})}{T_{max}} \cdot t$.
- **High $w$** → exploration (global search); **Low $w$** → exploitation (local refinement).
- **$V_{max}$** prevents velocity explosion; **$N$** controls parallelism.
- **Most-cited default values in KTU answers**: $w=0.7$, $c_1=c_2=2.0$, $N=30$, $T_{max}=1000$.
- **Engineering applications**: antenna design, PID tuning, neural network training, truss optimization, feature selection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
